His tale described youthful infatuation tempered by secrecy: outward indifference to conceal their connection, exchanged signals through the flowers she carried, and the shared thrill of concealment.A turning point came when illness forced him to send a note cancelling a meeting. Tormented by jealousy, he nevertheless decided to visit her and found her with another man. Her calm demeanor and tender concern briefly quelled his doubts, but the sight ignited a storm of emotion. Confronted with betrayal, de Marsay experienced a profound internal shift—the end of innocence and the rise of a more cynical view of love.He wrestled with thoughts of revenge and the complexities of passion, ultimately concluding that true love must rise above the desire for vengeance. His account moved those present, prompting reflection on love, jealousy, and the social expectations that shape relationships. De Marsay’s story lingered in the salon as a poignant reminder of trust’s fragility and love’s transformative power.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Cured of his cold and his pure love, the narrator begins an affair with a charming heroine whose beauty sharply contrasts with a previous deceptive angel. He avoids quarrels with this clever woman, suspecting that true love might not match the pleasures of her skilled deceit — refined hypocrisy, he muses, can be as good as virtue.He visits a hair artist in the Rue Boucher to commission a keepsake. The artisan shows him astonishing hair mementos and mentions passing trends like marking linen with hair. Suspicion flares when the narrator recognizes a handkerchief the artist has worked on, confirming his doubts about the authenticity of women’s affections. He leaves resolved to enjoy pleasure but skeptical of love.Two months later, in the heroine’s boudoir, he holds her hand and probes her sentiments. Charlotte answers with professions of exclusive love, claiming he is the only man in her world and that she sought ways to bind him eternally. But when he bluntly asks, “When are you to marry the Duke?” her startled reaction betrays guilt. She feigns astonishment; he persists, suggesting she should accept the Duke—rich, devout, and proper.Charlotte falls to her knees, swearing before God that she loves only him. He presses her, confessing doubts about her fidelity; she counters that he no longer loves her and tries to escape, but curiosity draws her back to ask what he has seen. He reveals he has noticed her attending mass with the Duke several times; she dismisses his jealousy. He advises her to seize the Duke’s offer, since marriage would elevate her and the Duke expects propriety. In tears she laments that had he spoken sooner they might have been happy, but he insists it is too late—she has gone too far.She fears the Duke’s suspicions; he reassures her it’s unlikely but urges her to show devotion. As she alternates demeanor and supplication, she vows never to forget him. He plays the distressed lover until she asks what will become of him; he then admits to having made advances to another woman. This surprises Charlotte, who accuses him of unfaithfulness; he replies that her wit exceeds her heart’s suffering. She proclaims disillusionment with love, asserting that women can never truly be loved; he retorts that taking love seriously in society is like romanticizing an actress. She accuses him of betrayal; he feigns submission and asks whether she will remember her grievances once she is a duchess. Her lingering bitterness convinces him he has successfully distanced himself—he has studied how successful men withdraw and believes he has mastered the art of retreat.The other women express sympathy for Charlotte while de Marsay smiles slightly. Conversation shifts to the erosion of social distinctions. Lady Dudley notes women often do not view their marriages as acts of inconstancy. De Marsay laments the aristocracy’s decline, mourning that duchesses and marquises are disappearing and that the Civil Code has leveled social hierarchies. Blondet quips that aristocracy now begins with viscountesses; de Marsay hopes countesses may survive in some form. They recall how the last great ladies vanished with the previous century’s splendor, and how the Code inadvertently created a new class of women.The Comte de Vandenesse observes that the current order lets anyone claim nobility; Lord Dudley adds that unsettled property arrangements accelerate the aristocracy’s fall. Joseph Bridau laments a decline in womanly beauty and the broken fan of the great lady; Daniel d’Arthez says the aristocracy’s retreat has deprived society of women who might have shaped it.Discussion turns to the modern “perfect lady,” a creature of the era: tasteful, graceful, and poised but lacking the grandeur of past great ladies. Mademoiselle des Touches questions whether this is progress; Madame de Nucingen argues that a woman’s worth now lies in character and presentation rather than lineage. Count Adam Laginski asks for a definition; Émile Blondet describes the perfect lady as effortlessly elegant and refined, commanding respect and admiration by bearing and demeanor across settings.The dialogue underscores modern femininity’s complexities: the perfect lady skillfully navigates societal expectations with charm, blending beauty and intellect. Guests debate whether this new type compensates for the vanished greatness of earlier aristocratic women or simply reflects a society where merit and presentation have replaced inherited splendor.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“You will not talk for half an hour with a bourgeoise without her mentioning her husband,” Blondet said gravely. “A lady, though married, makes her husband hard to find. At a ball you may only notice her glance at a middle-aged man with a decoration who bows and goes; she has ordered her carriage and departs.“You are not the rose, but you have been with the rose; you go to bed under a golden canopy of a delightful dream that may linger after Sleep opens the ivory gates.“At home she sees no one before four; she keeps you waiting. Her house is tastefully arranged; her luxury is for daily use and regularly renewed. There are no dusty shades or remnants of wrappings. The staircase is warm, flowers abound—her only accepted gifts, and only from chosen people, since they symbolize a promise. Fashionable trinkets lie about, but not like museum pieces. She sits by the fire, not rising to greet you. At the ball she was our creditor; here she owes you the pleasure of her wit. She seeks a man to widen her circle, an object for her attentions. To draw you into her drawing-room she will be charming. This shows how isolated women are and why they want a little world to shine in. Conversation there often lives on generalities.“Yes,” de Marsay agreed, “you have caught our age’s flaw. The epigram—a volume in a word—no longer strikes at people or things but at sordid events, fading quickly.”“Hence a lady’s intelligence, if she has any, lies in doubting everything. Compare two women: the townswoman is virtuous; the lady hesitates and contends with her virtue. This uncertainty is one of the last graces left her. She rarely goes to church but will discuss religion; if you express free thought she will try to convert you, sliding into the familiar phrases and gestures of such women: ‘For shame! I thought you had too much sense to attack religion. Society is tottering, and you deprive it of support. Religion means you and me; it is property and the future of our children! Let us not be selfish! Individualism is the disease of the age, and religion is the only remedy; it unites families, which your laws put asunder,’ and so on. She drifts into a neo-Christian discourse laced with political notions—neither Catholic nor Protestant—but very moral: a muddle of modern doctrines at odds with each other.”The women laughed at Blondet’s satire.“This sketch, Count Adam,” Blondet told the Pole, “shows that the ‘perfect lady’ embodies intellectual and political confusion, surrounded by flashy yet fleeting products of an industry that replaces its work with something new. When you leave her you think she had superior ideas; you believe it because she has delicately probed your heart and asked your secrets; she feigns ignorance to learn everything. There are things she never knows, even when she does. You alone feel uneasy, knowing nothing of her heart. The great ladies of old flaunted their affairs; today’s lady arranges her passions like a musical score, with notes and rests, pauses and sharps. A merely weak woman fears compromising her love, husband, or children’s future. Name, position, and fortune no longer shield her. The aristocracy no longer protects her. Unlike the great lady of the past, she lacks lofty antagonism; she cannot crush; rather, she would be crushed. Thus she masters Jesuitical compromises, moving between propriety and anonymous passion. She fears her servants as an Englishwoman fears a divorce trial. So free at a ball, so charming on a walk, she is a slave at home; she is never independent except in privacy or theoretically. Her task is to preserve her position as a lady.“For today, a woman repudiated by her husband, reduced to a meager allowance, without carriage, luxury, or opera box, is neither wife, maid, nor townswoman; she is adrift, a chattel. The Carmelites will not accept a married woman; it would be bigamy. Would her lover still want her? That is the question. Thus your perfect lady may give rise to calumny, but never to slander.”“It is all so horribly true,” said the Princesse de Cadignan.“And so,” Blondet continued, “our ‘perfect lady’ exists between English hypocrisy and eighteenth-century frankness—a bastard system symptomatic of an age where nothing that emerges resembles what has vanished; transition leads nowhere, everything is a matter of degree; all great figures fade into the background; distinction becomes purely personal. I am convinced it is impossible for a woman, even if born near a throne, to acquire before twenty-five the encyclopedic knowledge of trifles, the practice of maneuvering, the important small things, the musical tones of coloring, the angelic mischief and innocent cunning, the speech and silence, the seriousness and banter, the wit and obtuseness, the diplomacy and ignorance that make the perfect lady.”“And where, according to your sketch,” Mademoiselle des Touches asked, “would you classify the female author? Is she a perfect lady, a woman comme il faut?”“When she has no genius, she is a woman comme il n’en faut pas,” Blondet replied, with a look that might praise Camille Maupin. “This epigram is not mine, but Napoleon’s.”“You need not hold a grudge against Napoleon for that,” Canalis interjected. “He was weak in being jealous of literary genius—he had his flaws. Who can explain or depict Napoleon? He was a man of paradoxes: arms folded, he did everything; the most concentrated, caustic force, a singular genius who spread armed civilization without fixing it anywhere. He could do everything because he willed everything; he was a phenomenal will, conquering illness through battle, yet destined to die of disease in bed after living amid balls and bullets. He had a code and a sword in his brain, word and deed; a clear-sighted spirit that foresaw everything but his own fall; a capricious politician who risked men for economy yet spared three heads—Talleyrand, Pozzo de Borgo, and Metternich—whose deaths might have saved the Empire. Nature, as a rare privilege, gave a heart in a bronze frame; mirthful and kind at midnight among women, and the next morning manipulating Europe as a child might splash in her bath. Hypocritical and generous; loving tawdriness and simplicity; devoid of taste yet protecting the arts; and despite these contradictions, truly great by instinct or temperament: Caesar at twenty-five, Cromwell at thirty. He improvised public works, empires, kings, codes, verses, a romance—with more range than precision. He aimed to make all Europe France, and after changing the laws of gravitation he left us poorer; he who had taken an empire by his name lost his name on the frontier of his empire in a sea of blood and soldiers. A man of thought and action, who understood Desaix and Fouché.”“All despotism and all justice at the right moments. The true king!” de Marsay declared.“Ah! what a pleasure it is to digest what you talk about,” Baron de Nucingen remarked.“But do you think the treat we are giving you is common?” Joseph Bridau asked. “If you had to pay for the charms of conversation as you do for dancing or music, your fortune would be inadequate! There is no second performance of the same flash of wit.”“And are we really so much diminished as these gentlemen think?” the Princesse de Cadignan asked, smiling at the women. “Under a regime that makes everything small, you prefer small dishes, small rooms, small pictures, small articles, small newspapers, small books—does that prove women have grown smaller? Why should the human heart change because you change your coat? In all ages passions remain the same. I know of beautiful devotion and sublime sufferings that lack the publicity that once gave luster to the errors of some women. But even if one has not saved a King of France, one is not less an Agnès Sorel. Do you believe our Marquise d’Espard is not the equal of Madame Doublet or Madame du Deffant, in whose rooms so much evil was spoken and done? Is not Taglioni a match for Camargo? Or Malibran the equal of Saint-Huberti? Are not our poets superior to those of the eighteenth century? If, at this moment, due to the Grocers who govern us, we lack a style of our own, did not the Empire have its distinguishing stamp as the age of Louis XV did, and was not its splendor fabulous? Have the sciences lost anything?”“I completely agree, madame; the women of this age are truly great,” the Comte de Vandenesse replied. “When posterity looks back, will not Madame Récamier appear in as fine a proportion as the most beautiful women of the past? We have made so much history that historians will be lacking. The age of Louis XIV had but one Madame de Sévigné; we have a thousand now in Paris who certainly write better and do not publish their letters. Whether the Frenchwoman is called ‘perfect lady’ or great lady, she will always be the woman among women.“Blondet has painted the fascinations of a woman of the day; but when needed this creature who bristles or preens, who echoes the ideas of this or that man, can be heroic. And your faults, mesdames, are all the more poetic because they are surrounded by greater perils. I have seen much of the world—perhaps too late; but where the illegality of your feelings might be excused, I have always observed the effects of what you may call Providence, inevitably overwhelming those we consider light women.”“I hope,” Madame de Vandenesse said, “that we can be great in other ways——”“Oh, let the Comte de Vandenesse preach to us!” Madame de Serizy cried.“With all the more reason because he has preached a great deal by example,” the Baronne de Nucingen added.“On my honor!” General de Montriveau said, “in all the dramas—a word you are very fond of,” he said, glancing at Blondet—“in which the finger of God has been visible, the most frightful I ever knew was almost by my act——”“Well, tell us all about it!” Lady Barimore cried. “I love to shudder!”“It is the taste of a virtuous woman,” de Marsay replied, glancing at Lord Dudley’s lovely daughter.“During the campaign of 1812,” General Montriveau began, “I was the involuntary cause of a terrible disaster which may interest you, Doctor Bianchon,” turning to me, “since while you devote yourself to the body, you concern yourself with the mind; it may help solve some problems of the will.“I was on my second campaign; I loved danger and laughed at everything, like the young and foolish lieutenant of artillery I was. When we reached the Beresina, the army had lost all discipline. It was a medley of men of many nations, instinctively making their way from north to south. Soldiers would drive away a ragged, barefoot general if he brought neither wood nor food. After crossing the river disorder did not lessen. I had come quietly and alone, without food, from the marshes of Zembin, wandering in search of a house where I might be taken in. Finding none or being driven away, I finally saw a wretched Polish farm, like the wooden houses of Lower Normandy or the poorest Beauce farms. These dwellings consist of a single room, one end divided by a partition as a forage store-room.“In the twilight I saw faint smoke above the house. Hoping to find comrades more compassionate than those I had met, I approached. Inside, the table was laid. Several officers, with a woman—a common sight—were eating potatoes, some horseflesh broiled over charcoal, and frozen beetroots. I recognized artillery captains from my first regiment. I was welcomed with shouts; my comrades were warm, they had food, and the straw-strewn floor promised a night’s rest. We did not ask for much then. My comrades could be philanthropists for free—one of the commonest ways of being philanthropic. I sat to eat on a straw bundle.“At the end of the table, by the door to the hay-filled division, sat my old colonel, one of the most extraordinary men I ever met among our mixed collection. He was Italian. Whenever southern human nature is truly fine it is sublime. My colonel was tall, admirably proportioned, perhaps a little stout but prodigiously powerful, active, and clean-limbed like a greyhound. His abundant black curls contrasted with a complexion as white as a woman’s; he had small hands, a shapely foot, a pleasant mouth, and an aquiline nose that would pinch and turn white when he was angry, which happened often. His irascibility was beyond belief; no one could remain calm in his presence. I alone perhaps was not afraid of him; he had such a fancy for me that he thought everything I did was right. When angry, his brow knit and the muscles of his forehead formed a horseshoe; his whole frame quivered, and his strength, great at rest, became almost unbounded.“He spoke with a strong guttural roll that, when excited, lent incredible force to his commands. When calm his blue eyes were angelically sweet and his smooth brow charming. On parade or with the army of Italy, no man could compare with him. Indeed, d’Orsay himself was eclipsed by our colonel at the last review Napoleon held before the invasion of Russia.“Everything about this man was contrast. Passion thrives on contrast. He had the irresistible influence that our nature yields to such men, yet by singular fatality he was neither a lady-killer nor indifferent to success.“To illustrate his violence: we were dragging guns up a narrow road when we met another artillery regiment. Their colonel wanted our captain to yield but our captain refused. The other colonel ordered his battery forward; the wheel of the first gun struck our captain’s right leg, breaking it and throwing him over on the near side of his horse. Our Colonel, nearby, galloped up, calling ‘Help!’ No, our Italian colonel was no longer human! Foam rose to his lips; he roared like a lion. Incapable even of a word or cry, he made a terrifying signal, drew his sword, and in two seconds his opponent lay with his skull split. The soldiers backed away.“The captain, nearly crushed, had an Italian wife, a beautiful Sicilian from Messina, who was not indifferent to our Colonel. This aggravated his rage. He felt pledged to protect the husband as he would the woman herself.“In the hovel beyond Zembin where I was so well received, this captain sat opposite me, and his wife was at the other end facing the Colonel. This Sicilian, Rosina, was very dark, with the sun’s fire in her almond-shaped eyes. She was terribly thin; her face dusted like fruit exposed on a highway. Scarcely clothed in rags, exhausted by marches, her hair disheveled under a shawl, she still had a woman’s graces; her small rose mouth, white teeth, and figure retained charms that misery had not erased. Rosina seemed fragile but wiry and springy. Her husband, a Piedmontese gentleman, had an ironical simplicity that betrayed cruelty beneath phlegm and indolence. He seemed unaware of any connection between his wife and the Colonel for three years; yet a thin, restless underlip turning down at the corners filled me with distrust.“The conversation was not lively when I arrived. My comrades ate in silence; they asked questions, mixed reflections on the campaign, and lamented the cold. A minute after I arrived, the colonel, having finished his meager meal, wiped his mustache, bade us goodnight, shot a black look at the Italian woman, saying, ‘Rosina?’ and went into the little barn full of hay to sleep. The meaning of the call was clear. The young wife responded with an indescribable gesture, expressing the annoyance she could not feel at seeing her thralldom flaunted without decency, and the offense to her dignity as a woman and to her husband. Yet there was in her rigid features a sort of presentiment; perhaps she foresaw her fate. Rosina remained quietly in her place.“A minute later, apparently when the Colonel was settled in straw, he repeated, ‘Rosina?’ The tone was even more brutally demanding. Rosina turned pale, rose, passed behind us, and went to the Colonel. All sat in utter silence; I, unfortunately, began to laugh, and then they all laughed too.“‘Tu ridi?—you laugh?’ said the husband.“‘On my honor, old comrade,’ I replied seriously, ‘I confess I was wrong; I ask your pardon a thousand times, and if you are not satisfied with my apologies, I am ready to give you satisfaction.’“‘Oh! it is not you who are wrong, it is I!’ he replied coldly.“Thereupon we lay down in the room, and before long all were sound asleep.“Next morning, each one, without waking his neighbor or seeking companionship, set out again on his way, with that selfishness which made our rout one of the most horrible dramas of self-seeking, melancholy, and horror ever enacted under heaven. Nevertheless, about seven or eight hundred paces from our shelter, most of us met again and walked on together, urged by the same necessity.“Having reached a knoll where we could still see the farmhouse, we heard sounds like a roar, mingled with a woman’s feeble scream. We looked: the farmhouse had been barricaded and was in flames. Swirls of smoke carried hoarse cries and an indescribable pungent smell. A few yards behind, the captain was quietly approaching to join our caravan; we gazed at him in silence; he, understanding our curiosity, pointed to his breast with his right forefinger and, waving his left toward the fire, said, ‘Son’io.’ We all walked on without a word to him.”“There is nothing more terrible than the revolt of a sheep,” de Marsay remarked.“It would be frightful to leave with this horrible picture in our memory,” Madame de Montcornet said. “I shall dream of it——”“And what was the punishment of Monsieur de Marsay’s ‘First’?” Lord Dudley asked, smiling.“When the English jest, their foils have the buttons on,” Blondet replied.“Monsieur Bianchon can tell us, for he saw her dying,” de Marsay said, turning to me.“Yes,” I replied; “and her end was one of the most beautiful I ever witnessed. The Duke and I had spent the night by the dying woman’s pillow; pulmonary consumption, in its last stage, left no hope; she had taken the sacrament the day before. The Duke had fallen asleep. The Duchess, waking about four in the morning, signed to me with a friendly smile to leave him to rest, while she was about to die. She had become incredibly thin, but her face retained its sublime outline and features. Her pallor made her skin look like porcelain with a light within. Her bright eyes contrasted with this languidly elegant complexion, and her face was full of expressive calm. She seemed to pity the Duke, and this feeling sprang from a lofty tenderness that, as death approached, seemed boundless. The silence was absolute. The room, softly lit by a lamp, resembled every sickroom at the hour of death.“At this moment the clock struck. The Duke awoke, despairing at having fallen asleep. I did not see the impatient gesture he made, expressing regret for having lost sight of his wife for a few of the last granted moments; but it is certain anyone but the dying woman might have misunderstood it. A busy statesman, always thinking of France’s interests, the Duke had a thousand odd surface ways which often lead a genius to be mistaken for a madman, explainable by the exacting needs of their intellect. He came to sit in an armchair by his wife’s side and looked fixedly at her. The dying woman reached out, took her husband’s hand and clasped it feebly; in a low but agitated voice she said, ‘My poor dear, who is left to understand you now?’ Then she died, looking at him.”“The stories the doctor tells us,” the Comte de Vandenesse remarked, “always leave a deep impression.”“But a sweet one,” Mademoiselle des Touches said, rising.PARIS, June 1839–42.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy (selected cross-references):- Bianchon, Horace: Father Goriot; A garden slopes to the river where once-clipped box shrubs now run wild; willows rooted in the stream form a half-hidden fence. Weeds have taken the bank; fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, their suckers forming a thicket; espaliers look like a little copse. The once-graveled paths are overgrown—indeed there is no trace of a path. From the hilltop where the ruins of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, one can see that this spot must once have been the pride of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture. An arbor’s wreck and a partially standing table testify to that bygone care. The garden’s aspect suggests provincial peace—the epitaph of a worthy tradesman. To complete the mournful air, one wall bears a sundial with the motto “Ultimam cogita” (think on your last).The house itself is dreadfully dilapidated: the roof leaking and warped, shutters always closed, swallows nesting on balconies, doors shut. Grasses green the flagstones of the steps; ironwork is rusty; moon, sun, winter, and snow have eaten into the wood, peeling paint and warping boards. The silence is broken only by birds, cats, polecats, rats, and mice; to the observer an invisible hand has inscribed one single word over it all: Mystery.From the street you see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of children's holes; the gate, as the narrator later learned, had been blocked for ten years. Through breaches you view the courtyard: the same ruinous state as the garden side—paving-stones choked with weeds, cracked walls, blackened coping laced with pellitory, disjointed stone steps, a rotten bell-cord, broken gutter-spouts. What fire from heaven, what decree, has struck this dwelling? Has God been mocked, or was France betrayed? Reptiles crawl over it but give no answer. The empty, deserted house is a vast enigma.It was formerly a small fiefed estate called La Grande Bretèche. During the doctor’s stay in Vendôme—where he had been left to attend a rich patient—this odd dwelling became one of his keenest pleasures. Better than a ruin, it carried a secret, an unrevealed thought: the slow destruction of a standing house seemed like an avenging hand at work. Many evenings he climbed the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure, braved scratches to enter the ownerless garden, and lingered for hours in its disorder. He would weave romances and indulge in melancholia there, relishing the unwritten poetry of ignorance; had he known the reason for the neglect, the charm would have been gone. To him the place represented various phases of human misfortune: sometimes a graveyard without the dead, sometimes a lepers’ home, sometimes the house of the Atridae—but above all provincial life, contemplative and like an hour-glass. He often wept there; he never laughed.Occasionally he felt involuntary terrors at the dull hum of a passing wood-pigeon overhead. The damp earth required watching for lizards, vipers, and frogs; a cold would seem to settle on your shoulders like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck. One evening a weathercock turned in the wind, its creaking like a cry from the house just as the doctor was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this enormous air of woe. He returned to his inn in a pensive mood. After supper, the hostess came into his room with a mysterious air to announce, “Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.”“Who is Monsieur Regnault?” the doctor asked.“What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,” she said and left.A tall, slim man in black appeared: hat in hand, receding forehead, small pointed head, colorless face like dirty water—a sort of usher. His coat was old, worn at the seams, but he wore a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.“Monsieur,” the doctor asked, “whom have I the honor of addressing?”The man sat by the fire, rubbed his hands and answered, “Dear me, it is very cold. I am Monsieur Regnault.”“My curiosity is piqued,” the doctor said.“I am notary at Vendôme,” Regnault replied. He quickly informed the doctor that the latter’s habit of walking in La Grande Bretèche’s garden constituted a misdemeanor since the estate was enclosed property, and as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret he begged the doctor to desist. He insisted he meant no offense and would be glad to allow free access if he were not bound by the testatrix’s instructions: he had never set foot in the house since the will was read. They had done nothing there but verify doors and windows to assess taxes; administering Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life. His account was long and voluble, but the doctor pressed him for the reason that La Grande Bretèche was left to ruin.Regnault delighted in relating his own history and the tale of Madame de Merret. He had been head-clerk in a Paris office that went bankrupt; unable to buy a practice in Paris in 1816, he came to Vendôme, bought his predecessor’s business, and married into local relations. Three months after being licensed, just before his marriage, he was sent for to the Chateau of Merret by Madame la Comtesse. The Count had gone to Paris and died two months previously after falling into dissipation. On the day he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted La Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it; some said she had burned the furniture and hangings, perhaps in the Merret meadow. Regnault had been summoned: the Countess was very ill and seemed unlikely to survive the night, having received the Sacrament. The maid who met him at the door was now a servant in the inn.When Regnault reached the chateau about eleven, he climbed the great staircase, crossed large, lofty, dark rooms—cold and damp—and reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. Rumors had painted her as a coquette; reality confounded that image. The enormous, old-fashioned room, papered with brown and filled with dust, had only an old Argand lamp for light. The bed was antique, the tester hung with flowered chintz; on a small table lay an Imitation of Christ and the lamp; a deep armchair for the maid and two small chairs formed the whole furnishing; there was no fire. If you had seen that vast room you would have felt transported into a romance; it was icy, funereal.Madame de Merret lay propped up, waxen and narrow-faced. Her hair, abundant but white as linen, was shown by a lace cap; her large black eyes, dimmed by fever, hardly moved beneath bony brows. Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands looked like bones covered with soft skin; veins and muscles showed—she must have once been striking, but disease had consumed her to a phantom. Her lips, pale-violet, scarcely moved when she spoke. Though Regnault’s work had exposed him to many deaths and last wishes, this solitary figure in her vast chateau was the most affecting: no sound, almost no breath, yet an immense presence. When her eyes moved, she tried to raise a hand, then, with difficulty, drew from under her pillow a sealed paper. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” she uttered, then clutched a crucifix, kissed it, and died. Regnault remembered a joy stamped on her last glance, an expression that made him shudder, and he took away the will: she had appointed him her executor.The will proved extraordinary. She left most of her property to the Vendôme hospital, with a few legacies. As for La Grande Bretèche, she ordered that, for fifty years from her death, it should remain precisely in the state it was at her death: nobody, whoever he might be, should enter the apartments, no repairs were to be made, and watchmen were even to be paid if necessary to secure this injunction. At the expiration of the term, if the will had been dutifully executed, the house would become the executor’s heirs’ property; otherwise it would revert to the heirs-at-law under terms set in a codicil not to be opened for fifty years. The will had not been contested. Regnault’s account thrilled him and he enjoyed the triumph of such a legal contrivance.The doctor, vivid imaginings spinning into a gothic romance, pressed Regnault for the specifics of the Countess’s eccentric injunctions, but the notary affected comical reticence. Eventually he regaled the doctor further with town gossip—diffuse, contradictory opinions that nearly put the doctor to sleep. Regnault’s monotone and self-satisfaction were too much for curiosity, and the notary took his leave, teasingly suggesting that many would wish to live fifty years longer, and hinting cryptically about age.After Regnault left, the doctor, still stirred, sat in his armchair when his landlady, Madame Lepas, entered. A buxom, florid Flemish woman with the keen scrutiny of an innkeeper, she had overheard the notary’s history. The doctor repeated the story of Madame de Merret, and Madame Lepas leaned close, an instinctive mix of constable, spy, and dealer in her look.When asked about Monsieur de Merret, she described him as extremely tall, a good gentleman from Picardy, taciturn and hot-tempered but generous in paying his debts, and well-liked by the common folk despite his haughty manner. He and Madame de Merret had once been a handsome couple; she was pretty, sweet, and wealthy—about twenty thousand francs a year. They were a splendid match and their wedding had filled the town. Were they happy? “So-so,” Madame Lepas said—the common folk were not intimate with their life. Madame de Merret was kind and pleasant but must sometimes have endured her husband’s temper; being of noble birth, his ways passed as his place required.“But there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?” the doctor pressed.“I did not say there was any catastrophe,” she replied. Yet she then confided what she knew. During the Emperor’s time, when Spaniards and prisoners of war were sent here, she was required to lodge a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Despite the parole, he had to present himself daily to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee with a fanciful name—Bagos de Férédia or something alike—which she had entered in her books. For a Spaniard he was notably handsome though short—no more than five feet two or three—but well made, with small, fastidious hands; he kept his hands, she said, as carefully as a woman keeps brushes. He had thick black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion the hostess admired, and the finest linen she had ever seen—finer than anything even princesses had worn at her inn, despite having housed personages like General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. The Spaniard ate little but had such polite, amiable ways that he was impossible to dislike; he spoke scarcely at all, answering rarely if spoken to—a manner the hostess guessed might be a national affectation.He was devout: he read his breviary like a priest and went to mass regularly. Most notably, he always took his station within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. At his first attendance there no one suspected intent; he never raised his eyes above his book, poor young man. In the evenings he walked the hill among the ruins of the old castle—a pastime that must have reminded him of Spain, which, they said, is all hills.Thus the scene closes on the picture of La Grande Bretèche, the Countess’s strange last will that sealed the house for half a century, and the shadow of a Spanish exile whose daily position near the Countess’s chapel and his solitary walks among the ruins suggest a link between foreign passion and domestic catastrophe—an implication the doctor, Regnault, and Madame Lepas allow the listener to supply, while the dilapidated house stands mute and enigmatic by the Loir.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening shortly after he arrived, he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait for him. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys thought he had seen the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he returned I warned him about the weeds; he seemed annoyed at being seen.Eventually, one morning we found he hadn’t come back. Searching his things I discovered a note in his drawer, fifty doubloons—about five thousand francs—and a sealed box of diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note said that if he didn’t return he left the money and diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.My husband went searching for him and returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He came early, unseen. After reading the letter he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced he had escaped. The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas thought the Spaniard had drowned. I suspected otherwise, believing him implicated in Madame de Merret’s affair; Rosalie had told me the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was ebony and silver, and early in his stay Monsieur Férédia had owned a similar crucifix that I never saw again. “Should I feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs?” my hostess asked. “Aren’t they truly mine?”“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread—like entering a dark church at night with a faint light under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, suddenly appeared vividly in my mind. I felt compelled to explore the house and uncover the heart of this solemn story that had claimed three lives.Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme. Observing her, I sensed some inner thought beneath her blooming health—a sorrow or hope; she gave the impression of keeping a secret, like devout souls who pray excessively or a woman haunted by a past crime. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of criminality; one would deem her innocent seeing the large checked kerchief tucked into her lilac-and-white-striped gown. “No,” I resolved, “I will not leave Vendôme without knowing la Grande Bretèche’s full story. To achieve this, I will make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.”“Rosalie!” I called one evening.“Your servant, sir?”“You’re not married?” She flinched.“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, then regained composure; every woman, from the highest lady to an inn-servant, possesses presence of mind.“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”Her response seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to the affair; she seemed tied to its core. It wasn’t a matter for ordinary courtship; this girl contained the final chapter of a romance. From that moment my attention fixed on her. Observing her further, I noticed admirable qualities; she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning I said to Rosalie:“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a particular attitude of confidence is necessary for storytelling. If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence a whole volume would scarcely suffice; as her account lies between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s version, I will summarize it concisely.Madame de Merret’s bedroom at la Grande Bretèche was on the ground floor; a small cupboard in the wall, about four feet deep, served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question Madame de Merret had been seriously ill; her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. One night he returned later than usual from the club, delayed by animated discussions and losses at billiards. He had previously relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; that evening he decided to check on her, perhaps to share his misfortunes or seek comfort. He entered thinking he heard a closet door shut; Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace, looking anxious.“You’re very late,” she said huskily.Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing, arms crossed.“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He didn’t answer.“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She wanted to be alone with her husband. Once Rosalie left—or lingered in the passage—Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, saying coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”That “No” pierced him; he didn’t believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand and said with strange emotion, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”Her extraordinary dignity filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve like a legend: “No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” She looked haggard. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in there; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, observing the ebony-and-silver crucifix, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before.”“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.”“Indeed,” he said, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.He waited for her to arrive. “Go to bed, all of you,” he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss billiards and the club. When Rosalie returned she found them conversing amiably.Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the ground-floor reception rooms. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme; he had ordered a large quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.“Tell him to come in,” he replied aloud.Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason. “Gorenflot,” said her husband, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered: “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, to be paid on your wedding day—contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!”“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”Her husband quietly paced the room, watching the door, the mason, and his wife, without showing suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise; Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”Both husband and wife remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door; the silence on Monsieur’s part was deliberate—he did not want to let his wife speak ambiguously. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason broke one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to him. They all then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.Before her husband turned around the poor woman nodded to the stranger the signal of “Hope.”At four in the morning in September the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.The next morning he casually mentioned, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy—she thought he would go to Duvivier’s.As soon as he left Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”In an instant Rosalie brought a cleaver and began demolishing the wall with an intensity beyond words. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”“No, monsieur.”“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. “Jean,” he added to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he said without allowing her to speak, “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”After Bianchon finished this story, all the ladies rose from the table, shaken by the last words.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################During the Restoration the Archbishop of Besançon was a regular at Baronne de Watteville’s drawing-room because of her devoutness.Monsieur de Watteville, a descendant of the infamous Watteville, was gentle and unassuming. After a quiet life in La Franche-Comté he married Mademoiselle de Rupt, heiress of the Rupt family; the match was arranged in 1802 and formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. Following her grandparents’ deaths, Madame de Watteville grew more pious and influenced Besançon’s upper circles.Thin and plain, Monsieur occupied himself as a turner and collector of local geological specimens. Many saw a nobility of soul in him: accepting he would not dominate his wife, he threw himself into work and hobbies.The Rupt residence reflected both families’ standing—grand rooms, elegant furnishings, fine cuisine—and occasionally Monsieur acted as his own butler. Madame’s fortune was considerable; his estate modest. Their intimacy with the Archbishop attracted several notable abbés.At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834 guests welcomed Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable lawsuit verdict. The Vicar-General, noting the result was unexpected in the present political climate, credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron. When Madame de Watteville asked about him, the Vicar-General said Savaron lived nearby but his origins were unknown, which intrigued the guests. Amedée de Soulas, present that evening, would later prove significant.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often skirting customs: what is "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual borrowing. For example, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts, once derided, became a dominant European fashion. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, yet French styles soon shifted in response.More recently England supplied French with terms like "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset’s poetry, inspired a trend of stylish young men in Paris that spread to the provinces. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt ill at ease in the dull, military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but cultivated an ostentatious appearance, keeping a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and lavished expense on his wardrobe while subsisting on scant income.Though seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was adept at economizing, often dining out to avoid household costs. His costly grooming and clothes were a façade aimed at impressing the local aristocracy and winning Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His Parisian polish and travel tales helped him appear well-informed despite financial strain.Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy, "the Colony," maintained strict social hierarchies. Amedee’s unusual fashions—such as wearing trouser-straps—set him apart and bolstered his status as a "lion." The town’s nobles wished to seem modern to visiting Parisians, and Amedee enabled that image.Amedee’s chief ambition was to marry Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict, religious household with little exposure to the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye and trained more in domestic skills than social polish. Despite a passive exterior, Rosalie had a resolute spirit inherited from her lineage. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake; this produced tension as Amedee courted Rosalie and complicated his efforts.Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus illustrates the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraints as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville’s heart amid rigid social structures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Madame,” Monsieur de Soulas began as he waited for his soup to cool, “one morning a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took rooms on the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the Mairie and had his name—‘ALBERT SAVARON’—entered on the list of barristers at the Court, leaving his card with local officials.”“The Savaron name is well known,” Mademoiselle de Watteville observed, “one of the oldest and richest families in Belgium.”“He’s a Frenchman and no man’s son,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he claims the Savaron arms he must add a bar-sinister. The only surviving Brabant is a rich heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”“The bar-sinister denotes a bastard,” Rosalie chimed in, “but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble.”“Enough,” the Baroness cut in.“You insisted she learn heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville said; “and she knows it well.”“Please continue,” the Baroness urged Monsieur de Soulas.“In a town like Besancon, where everything is catalogued, Albert Savaron was soon accepted by the local lawyers. They wondered who had sent him and what he aimed to do—he sent his card to the judges rather than calling. Three days later he was forgotten. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. No one saw him much.”“Does he go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“He attends the early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two in the morning, works until eight, breakfasts, works again, walks in his garden in slight circles, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven.”“How do you know all this?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“I live on Rue Neuve and overlook his house; my servant speaks with Jerome.”“And you gossip with Babylas?”“What else is there to do while riding?”“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar-General.“The President assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man’s innocence and exposed the true culprits, leading to their arrest. His defense impressed the Court and jury. A merchant later entrusted him with a difficult case, which he won. Given our matter, Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ Savaron. Upon meeting him, I felt confident—and was right.”“Is he extraordinary?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.“Certainly,” the Vicar-General replied.“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.“The first time I met him,” Abbe de Grancey recounted, “he received me in an outer room lined with law books, simply furnished—an old writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, a green carpet. He was younger than I expected. He wore a black dressing gown with a red cord, red slippers, a red waistcoat, and a red smoking cap.”“The devil’s colors!” Madame de Watteville exclaimed.“Yes,” the Abbe continued, “but he has a magnificent head. Black hair streaked with gray, thick and curly; a round white throat; a splendid forehead furrowed by deep lines of thought; an olive complexion; a square nose; fiery eyes. He has passion but a calm demeanor and a sweet voice that can thunder when needed.”“Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate.”“The second time I visited, he received me in his shabby bedroom. The third time, I found him having a frugal breakfast in his library. I observed him closely. He is no ordinary man; there’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face. He seems to carry a heavy burden.”“Why would so eloquent a man leave Paris for Besancon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”“After studying his head and hearing him answer a prominent Parisian lawyer,” the Abbe replied, “I believe he will make a significant impact.”“Why discuss him? You’ve won your case and paid him,” Madame de Watteville remarked, noting her daughter’s rapt attention.The Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron captivated Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone exceptional, unlike the mundane Count who had courted her. Albert Savaron presented enigmas. “Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him, replaying the Abbe’s words and fixating on his description. “What a fine forehead!” she thought, comparing him in her mind to the men at the table.“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she answered, lost in thought about the Abbe’s prelate-like hands. She recalled a lighted window seen from her bed and realized it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.“Monsieur de Grancey, is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” Rosalie asked during a lull.“What does that matter to you?” her mother replied.“It might go to the Court of Appeal, but our adversaries will hesitate,” the Abbe answered.“I never thought Rosalie would think about a lawsuit during dinner,” Madame de Watteville remarked.“Nor I,” Rosalie said dreamily, prompting laughter. “But Monsieur de Grancey was so engrossed that I became interested.”After dinner Rosalie listened for any mention of Albert Savaron, but aside from congratulations to the Abbe, he was not discussed. Eager for bedtime, she planned to wake between two and three to glimpse his windows. When the hour arrived, she felt a thrill watching candlelight through the trees. She saw him writing and imagined the color of his furniture.“While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.The education of girls poses delicate questions: how much should they know? Too much enlightenment can force premature maturity; too little produces stunted minds. Rosalie’s crisis was triggered by the Abbe’s portrait of Savaron.The next morning, while dressing, she saw Albert walking in the garden next to the Hotel de Rupt. “What if he lived elsewhere? At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered.Having seen him, Rosalie resolved to enter his house, uncover his mystery, hear his voice, and glimpse his eyes. Throughout the day she embroidered with the concentration of someone appearing idle yet deeply reflective. She decided to go to confession. The next morning, after Mass, she arranged to confess at half-past seven, just before the eight o’clock service, hoping to encounter Albert.She visited her father in his workshop, suggesting he turn larger pieces and build a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. As he delighted in the idea, she cautioned him not to tell her mother, fearing a scolding.“Don’t worry!” he replied, sharing her frustration with his wife.Rosalie envisioned a charming observatory from which she could see into Albert’s private room. Young girls often go to great lengths for the sake of a man, even if he remains unaware.Sunday arrived. Rosalie dressed with such care that her maid Mariette smiled.“It’s the first time I’ve seen you so fidgety,” Mariette remarked.“Perhaps you too are more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing.Crossing the courtyard her heart raced; she had never walked like this before, feeling as if her mother could read her thoughts and forbid her confession. She felt invigorated, as if walking on fire. She arrived at church before Mass, prayed briefly, and checked if Abbe Giroud was in his confessional, positioning herself to see Albert as he entered.No man could seem unattractive to Rosalie in her curious state. Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a profound impression. His solemn demeanor suggested a heavy burden, and his deep gaze conveyed intense thought. Rosalie now fully understood the Vicar-General’s words. Those eagle-like eyes revealed a hidden ardor. In a bold moment she stood in his path to exchange glances, igniting a rush of warmth within her.After Albert took a seat, Rosalie found a place to observe him during her confession. When Mariette announced Monsieur Giroud’s arrival, the time in the confessional felt fleeting. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended and Albert had gone.“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besancon? I must know everything! But how?”With this new desire, Rosalie concentrated on her embroidery, masking her thoughts with an innocent demeanor to deceive her mother.From that Sunday, after their glance, Rosalie eagerly supported the plan for the Belvedere.“Mamma,” she said one day, “my father has a unique idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”“I approve of everything your father does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms, and he’ll see us—perhaps remarks will be made—”“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie, thinking you know more about life and propriety than we do?”“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentioned a cool room in the grotto where we can take coffee.”“Your father has had an excellent idea,” Madame de Watteville said, going to inspect the columns.She approved a site in the garden not visible from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but commanding a view into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was commissioned to construct a grotto with a path lined with periwinkles, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior was to be rustic, with a mirror, an ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor; Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier.“The Wattevilles are creating something lovely in their garden,” rumors spread in Besancon.“They’re wealthy and can afford such whims—”“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas replied. “A man from Paris is coming to rusticate the interior, and it will be very pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”“Berquet is making a cellar beneath it,” an Abbe added.“No,” the young man corrected, “he’s raising the kiosk on a concrete foundation to prevent dampness.”“You know the least about what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt said, glancing at her daughter, who had been waiting to marry for a year.Mademoiselle de Watteville felt a flush of pride at the Belvedere’s success, sensing superiority over others. No one suspected that the girl thought to be a witless goose had devised the plan to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s private study.Albert Savaron’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon forgotten amid envy from other lawyers. He remained secluded, avoiding social gatherings and thereby increasing the likelihood of being overlooked as a stranger. Nevertheless, he successfully argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal, gaining four prominent merchants as clients who recognized his legal skill.On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was launching another enterprise. Through quiet connections among local merchants he was starting a fortnightly paper, the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, convinced of the need to promote Besancon as a trade hub.To compete with Strasbourg, Besancon needed to become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Albert’s vision was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon and bring light to Eastern France. His ideas appealed to merchants eager to believe in their own enterprise.Savaron wisely kept his name out of the spotlight, letting his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, handle finances while he retained editorial control and a share of the profits. The Review appealed to various regions, and with a low subscription price it quickly garnered five hundred subscribers.To avoid offending local pride, Albert invited the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to manage the literary side. Albert maintained control, subtly guiding Alfred’s contributions while letting him believe he was gaining. The commercial community, pleased with the Review’s success, only had to pay a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers, the publication would yield dividends for shareholders, while the editor remained unpaid.After the third issue, the Review gained recognition among French publications, which Albert began reading at home. The third number featured a tale attributed to him, sparking discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room, though higher circles of Besancon dismissed it for liberal views.“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besancon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, as mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you can lend it to me.”Monsieur de Watteville, eager to please Rosalie who had shown him so much affection, subscribed and lent her the four issues already published. That night Rosalie devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited experience, the impact on her was profound. She hoped to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert. From the first pages she became convinced it reflected him, as critics noted Albert had drawn from his own life in this confession.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1823 two young men set out from Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland, planning to tour the lake of the Four Cantons. As they passed the hamlet of Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay while his friend Leopold continued the tour.Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together. Leopold was destined to be a notary in Paris; Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more troubled background. His mother had sacrificed everything for his education, hoping to secure his future. Rodolphe was passionate and sensitive, often driven by desire, in contrast to Leopold’s calm demeanor.In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with a couple who took him in and learned the woman was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. They were of modest means; Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.The next day Rodolphe sought permission to visit the gardens of the Bergmanns, who rented to the Lovelaces. After hesitation he was allowed to explore and noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, who seemed to have an unusual background. Eager to meet Fanny, he encountered resistance from the protective Bergmanns.That evening Rodolphe heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later encountered her and Gina, but their conversation was cut short when Rodolphe was attacked and wounded by an unknown assailant. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken back to safety.While he recovered Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.Fanny later revealed that her husband was not as old as he had seemed and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his feelings and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing he had great potential.Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past, arrived. Tito’s presence stirred rivalry and fear of losing her. Though they dreamed of freedom and a future together, Rodolphe’s ambition, Fanny’s ties to her husband, and social constraints loomed, forcing them to weigh love against duty and sacrifice. The beauty of their surroundings mirrored their intense emotions even as life’s realities threatened their hopes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For twenty days Rodolphe stayed at the Bergmanns’, watching Francesca with a mixture of fascination and confusion. He observed in her both youthful imprudence and a resolute, sometimes resistant, character. The old man treated her like a father; her gratitude revealed an innate nobility that deepened the enigma she presented to him.Those days were a blend of secret joys and melancholy, quarrels as sweet as harmony. Rodolphe grew more captivated by Francesca’s constant tenderness and became jealous over trifles. One evening, when she declared she wished to leave Gersau for its lack of luxuries, he asked if she valued luxury. She answered passionately, equating a love of luxury with an appreciation for art and beauty, while insisting she had endured hardship without complaint. Her playful suggestion that ordinary people aspire to greatness revealed her charm.Francesca then insisted Rodolphe not accompany them to Geneva, citing town gossip and the need to protect her husband’s reputation. She professed love for Rodolphe but demanded secrecy, kissed him, and left him astonished.The next day, after the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed, Rodolphe, restless, went to Vevay and then to Geneva. To avoid local discomforts he rented a room in Eaux-Vives and inquired about Italian refugees—hoping to find a bookseller named Lamporani—but learned the only recent arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini. His first walk took him to Villa Diodati; there he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady and felt dizzy. Learning she was at the Colonna residence lifted a veil: the intimacy he had felt now collided with the reality of her noble rank, and he feared he had been a mere whim.He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by a false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from requesting to see the Princess. That evening his passion intensified and he felt driven to raise his social standing. When he finally saw Francesca at the concert she was radiant; their eyes met with a charged recognition. Her beauty and grace convinced him she had remained true to their past despite her rank.Afterward he was introduced to the Colonnas and Francesca, who teasingly called herself “Princess Rodolphini,” reviving memories of Gersau. As days passed Francesca acknowledged Rodolphe’s deep love and spoke of a faithful, persistent affection despite distance. Rodolphe, however, was motivated by ambition—he wanted to achieve greatness so she could be proud.Francesca’s life was marked by contradiction: born to be cloistered, family tragedies forced her into marriage with Prince Gandolphini to preserve status, yet she preferred independence, liberal ideas, and sympathized with revolutionary sentiments. Winter in Geneva flew by in her intelligent, beautiful company, but with spring Rodolphe grew restless; her calm contrasted with his need for more demonstrative feeling.One day on a terrace, Rodolphe bitterly lamented others’ happiness, particularly that of a noblewoman. Francesca, wounded, withdrew; he found her in tears and, in a moment of passion, kissed her. She rebuffed him, asserting her dignity, and ordered him to leave—declaring they would not meet again until Naples. He, recognizing the strength of her will, complied.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Returning to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, who had refused to paint several women. Rodolphe doubted the Prince could persuade the artist, but Francesca charmed him into producing an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio. She announced this in a delightful letter, beginning a regular correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca—their sole indulgence.Driven by ambition and love, Rodolphe invested all his resources in a venture that failed through deceit, costing him three years of struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics, but first traveled to Naples to heal at the Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored. Three months there revived him and brought calm before new trials.He then tried to rebuild his fortune. His talents were recognized, and a high post was promised for his past efforts, but the storm of July 1830 again thwarted his ambitions.Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave efforts of this young man—endowed with fine qualities but without fortune's favor. Sustained by love, he returned to face new challenges, guided by a faithful heart.Lovers! Pray for him!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her tale, Rosalie flushed and wept. The novel—an early example of a man narrating his own love—was her first encounter with that voice, and it stirred a jealous conviction that Francesca Colonna was Albert’s beloved.Curiosity turned to a fierce resolve: despite insecurities about her looks and lack of musical talent, Rosalie wanted to win Albert or at least learn the truth. Her imagination devised schemes typical of a sheltered young woman—sneaking into Albert’s garden, writing to him, visiting her father’s land to engineer a meeting.From Albert’s window she saw something that promised revelation: Jerome, Albert’s servant, being helped over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie noted Mariette’s plainness but also her relative financial security, which explained Jerome’s interest. The scene taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson—her mother’s restrictions could be skirted by servants.The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the chance to question her about Jerome. She confronted Mariette about their secret meetings and, unexpectedly, offered help in exchange for access to letters Albert sent by Jerome. Relieved by Rosalie’s discretion, Mariette agreed.Rosalie’s obsession deepened when she read a letter Albert had sent to his friend Leopold. It revealed his noble lineage, past struggles, ambition, and steadfastness—an image of a man striving for success while remaining devoted to the woman he loved, Francesca. Absorbed by his character, Rosalie read more correspondence and became increasingly aware of her own inadequacies and the moral wrong of invading Albert’s privacy. She justified it as proof of love, and her passion hardened into a perilous infatuation that she punished with penances and self-imposed suffering to prove her devotion.One evening Mariette brought another letter destined for Francesca. Torn between respect and the urge to know, Rosalie yielded and read it. The letter confirmed Albert’s ongoing love for Francesca and outlined his hopes for their future. The revelation filled Rosalie with aching sorrow: she admired the fidelity between Albert and Francesca yet was painfully aware of her unrequited longing.Ultimately she resolved, in a clear moment, to step back—recognizing the nobility of their bond and her own shortcomings. As she prepared to return the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on love’s nature, feeling sorrow and resignation but also a yearning for the selfless, enduring love she had witnessed.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil; by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. Alone, that struggle fueled her passion and dark plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist; this is presented as a cautionary tale.That winter Albert de Savarus advanced his position in Besançon, eager for the Chamber’s dissolution. He secured support from a wealthy contractor linked to the moderates. The Romans had long supplied towns with water; Besançon drew water from Arcier, a distant hill, and restoring a Roman aqueduct for drinking water seemed absurd given the Doubs river. Yet if citizens could be persuaded, they would authorize large expenditures favoring the contractor. Savarus argued the river water was unfit and promoted Arcier’s water. The local Review echoed him, and townspeople of many parties accepted the idea of “Roman” water. The issue became a focal point in Besançon, driven by hidden interests: opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the previously stagnant town, gaining influence without broad popularity.Throughout the winter he also won several lawsuits for local priests; ambition consumed him. His apparent disinterest in personal gain masked a different form of moral usury: he anticipated a much larger reward. In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, a move that also qualified him for election. The Abbé de Grancey, who discerned Savarus’s designs, remarked on his remarkable nature and suggested he had taken a wrong turn in life.Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, at first content merely to observe him. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, yielded ten thousand francs a year but was poorly managed by the Baron, who left it to an old steward, Modinier. The Baron’s indifference sprang from reliance on his wife’s income; the family often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot improved by the famous Watteville. Watteville had constructed a dam and lake, enhancing the area’s beauty, but his heirs maintained a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, whose inhabitants long held rights to the lands. The Baron's death left a legacy of usurpation, and his descendants continued to assert control over the village.Rosalie’s maneuvers were subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey, captivated by the lake and associating it with love. On a boat trip her father confided about legal troubles with Riceys over land rights; Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for their estate. She argued that a lawsuit would add excitement and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey, clearly aiming to fix her future there and create gardens.Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing that alignment would compromise his neutrality; the Abbé pressed political advantages, especially potential votes. Rosalie, determined to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election, wrote to the préfet revealing Savarus’s ambitions, but the préfet disregarded her.
