She understood the political landscape and sought to manipulate it.As the election approached, a meeting of influential men unexpectedly nominated Savarus. Surprised, he had been unaware of the political machinations. The Abbé warned him of potential betrayals and advised transparency about his past. Savarus’s passionate speech captivated his audience, yet the préfet worked to undermine him, eroding his support. On the day of the first voting the results disappointed Savarus; he returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and an uncertain future. Then the Abbé arrived with news of hope: he had labored behind the scenes to secure support, promised the Rouxey case would be resolved in time, and suggested that marriage to Sidonie de Chavoncourt might consolidate votes. Savarus, initially resistant, recognized the advantage and prepared to navigate the coming choices of love and politics.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By nine o’clock the next evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were full of Besançon’s aristocracy, assembled to debate the surprise decision to present a candidate for the daughter of the Rupts. It was known the former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, would be put forward. Madame de Chavoncourt arrived with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed; her elder sister, secure in her relationship, had not bothered to come. In a small town such choices were noticed. The Abbe de Grancey circulated, listening and steering the talk with sharp remarks.He asked an elderly statesman what politicians the Elder Branch would find; the statesman said Berryer, isolated on his bench, might upset the Government if he rallied support. They mentioned the Duc de Fitz-James’s nomination in Toulouse and worried that voting for Monsieur Savarus could align Republicans with them instead of the Moderates.By nine-thirty Albert had not appeared; Madame de Watteville took his tardiness as an insult. Madame de Chavoncourt urged her not to be distracted by trifles, suggesting Albert might be delayed by a consultation. Rosalie, watching her mother exchange with Madame de Chavoncourt, noted her leniency toward Albert and hinted at a possible match between him and Sidonie.At ten the impatience grew; some gentlemen began to play cards. The Abbe, perplexed, went to the window where Rosalie had withdrawn and cried, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville, and Rosalie went to Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent. Calling to the servant Jerome, they learned Albert had left in a post-chaise. The Abbe proclaimed, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s face betrayed joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but affected not to see.Back in the drawing-room Monsieur de Watteville announced Albert’s unexplained departure. By half-past eleven only about fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the two Chavoncourt girls, the Abbes de Godenars and de Grancey, Monsieur de Vauchelles, Rosalie, Amedee de Soulas, a retired magistrate who had supported Albert, and a few others. The Abbe de Grancey stationed himself near the Baroness to watch Rosalie, whose usual pallor had given way to agitation.When Madame de Chavoncourt asked what could have happened, a servant brought a letter for the Abbe on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it; as he did he saw Rosalie turn pale and recognized the handwriting. He folded the letter and slipped it into his pocket after exchanging a telling glance with Rosalie. “She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie stopped him: the letter was from Albert, she confessed passionately, and begged him to keep silent when he revealed that Albert was withdrawing from the election—knowing that the withdrawal would end any chance of her sister’s marriage to him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette how Albert had suddenly vanished. An elderly Parisian had visited Monsieur Savaron; Savaron turned pale at the card, met him for over an hour, and the visitor left with a large package. Savaron, distressed, went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage with post horses, left his keys, and told his servant to seek other work. The Prefect quickly granted him a passport, and Savaron’s abrupt departure inflamed local prejudice against outsiders. Most soon forgot Albert—except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville pressed Rosalie to marry, and the Baron promised the family estate, les Rouxey, though the Baroness fretted over finances. A match with Monsieur de Soulas was arranged; Rosalie refused, insisting she was content. Tensions escalated between mother and daughter during a fraught summer at les Rouxey. After Mariette and Jérôme married, the Baron invested in the estate—fuel for the Baroness’s resentment. The Vicar-General tried to mediate.Rosalie then confessed to the Vicar-General that she had sabotaged Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo, by intercepting their letters, causing their separation. Horrified by the moral weight of her action, the Vicar-General urged her to seek forgiveness and understand the consequences. Her guilt deepened when a Parisian clerk, acting for Albert, came to sell his belongings and revealed that Albert had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received Albert’s letter: he forgave Rosalie and wished to remain in solitude.The Vicar-General urged Rosalie to reconcile with her mother and accept Monsieur de Soulas; Rosalie remained defiant, unwilling to sacrifice her mother’s happiness. After the Vicar-General’s death, Madame de Watteville married de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support. Now of age and financially independent, Rosalie entered Parisian society resolved to confront the Duchess. At a ball she publicly admitted her part in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken. She returned to Besançon having exposed the truth, but the emotional cost weighed heavily. The story closed on love, betrayal, and the lasting consequences of ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her les Rouxey estate—riding, hunting, and improving the land—making her notable in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, mother of two, looks younger than her years, while Monsieur de Soulas has grown considerably older; he told young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”Known for her eccentric “vagaries,” Mademoiselle de Watteville visits the walls of the Grande Chartreuse annually, perhaps inspired by a grand‑uncle who once broke monastery walls to gain his freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon, said to be marrying, though the real motive was unclear. She returned after a steamboat boiler explosion on the Loire that cost her her right arm and left leg and scarred her face. With failing health and constant pain, she now devotes herself to religious practices at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.PARIS, May 1842.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot; The Deserted Woman- Genovese: Massimilla Doni- Hannequin, Leopold: Beatrix; Cousin Betty; Cousin Pons- Jeanrenaud: The Commission in Lunacy- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman- Rhetore, Duc Alphonse de: A Bachelor’s Establishment; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Letters of Two Brides; The Member for Arcis- Savaron de Savarus; Savarus, Albert Savaron de: The Quest of the Absolute- Schinner, Hippolyte: The Purse; A Bachelor’s Establishment; Pierre Grassou; A Start in Life; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; The Imaginary Mistress; The Unconscious Humorists- Tinti, Clarina: Massimilla Doni############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyLETTERS OF TWO BRIDESBy Honore de BalzacTranslated by R. S. ScottDEDICATIONTo George SandYour name, dear George, lends a reflected radiance to my book, not from self-interest but as a tribute to our enduring friendship, which has outlived the world's malice. The names attached to my works give me pleasure amid the vexation of their multiplying. I take pride in a friendship like ours rather than in fickle popularity. Always your friend,DE BALZAC.  PARIS, June 1840.LETTERS OF TWO BRIDESFIRST PARTI. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBE. PARIS, September.Sweetheart, I too am free! I am the first to write, unless you have already written to Blois.Do not be alarmed; my escape from the convent was simple. The conscience that had alarmed me yielded to tenderer reasons: my aunt feared I would waste away, and my mother relented. I am in Paris thanks to you. If you had seen me when I left you, you would have felt proud of your influence on my young heart. We had been so intimate at the convent—our imaginations fused and turned the closed world into a playground—that when you left I fell into a deep melancholy. I was like a turtle-dove widowed of her mate; death seemed sweet, and I nearly succumbed. To be at Blois, consumed by dread of taking vows and without you, made me ill.My aunt could not understand the private life we led, nor how ill-suited the strict religious routine was for girls of our temperament. She had sacrificed herself for a brother; to expect the same self-denial from us would be too much. For a fortnight I suppressed everything, but letter-writing became my lifeline. I began a journal picturing your own, and dreamed of Gemenos as you will do of Paris.One morning a lady companion and Philippe, my grandmother's last valet, arrived to take me off. My aunt kissed me and spoke of the pride she saw in me, which might lead to heaven or hell. I kissed her hands and promised I would not return unless my heart was broken.At Beaugency I still felt stunned. No one was at home to receive me—my mother at Bois de Boulogne, my father at council, my brother late to dress. Philippe led me to the suite that had belonged to my beloved grandmother, the Princess de Vauremont, whose fortune had been left to me. Entering the rooms where she had died brought a wave of grief; though she died in 1817, the place seemed recently occupied. It pained me that so little had been done since her death; the panels were empty, marbles broken. Philippe told me the Princess's furniture had been left to me in her will, and my father was waiting for a law that would restore lost property before renovating—an estimate of three hundred thousand livres had been set.At first despair gripped me: had my father meant to send me to the convent, sparing no expense on my household? But these memories of the Princess softened me; the rooms preserved her spirit. I set Philippe to polishing and rearranging, giving the old furniture a youthful air.The bedroom and drawing-room, decorated in yellow damask and scarlet and filled with family relics—Savonnerie tapestry, a timepiece from Maréchal de Saxe, china from Maréchal de Richelieu, my grandmother's portrait—breathed of another century and of her coquetry. I remembered how she tended her appearance to the end; her drawing-room showed traces of nocturnal visitors, disarranged furniture, card-tables, snuff.I recalled how, when I was sent to the convent in 1816, she bid me go without display, promising I should remain independent and marry whom I pleased. Six months later she died, her will entrusted to the Prince de Talleyrand, who hinted through a friend that she disapproved of the vows. I hope to meet the Prince and learn more.Thus, even though no one in flesh met me initially, the Princess's presence comforted me, and I resolved to keep our pact: to tell each other the smallest domestic details. Send me every minute picture of your life.October 19th.At half-past five, Rose announced my mother's return. My mother's suite corresponds to mine, and we share a secret staircase. I descended anxious about our reunion, wondering if I should feign affection. Her tact surprised me: she was neither distant nor effusive, but greeted me like a friend and kissed my forehead.She said she preferred my living with family to dying at the convent, and wished to be more sister than mother. I found her charming at thirty-eight—exquisitely beautiful, with dark-blue eyes, smooth forehead, a marvelous figure and hands—and I responded kindly. Her manner warmed; she took me in and said we had guests for dinner, advising I not make my first appearance in society before visiting the dressmaker.My father then arrived and greeted me affectionately, calling me his rebel daughter and praising my looks; he predicted pride in my future. My brother, Duc de Rhetore, arrived leisurely and kissed me; I was conscious of his indifference to have not come to Blois when I was there.I tidied my rooms and set to write you. Thus ended the return of an eighteen-year-old after nine years at a convent, to a noble household where the old ways linger. Tired, I kept convent hours and slept early.II. THE SAME TO THE SAME November 25th.The next morning the rooms were made ready; Philippe had put flowers in the vases. Breakfast was troublesome as convent habits left me hungry early. About one, my father knocked and entered to find me writing to you. He gave me a purse containing twelve thousand francs as the annual allowance he intended for me, and said my grandmother left me five hundred thousand francs in government stock, now yielding about forty thousand francs a year. He had planned to use this to set up my younger brother but now left decisions to me, trusting my good sense. He warned that a Chaulieu daughter must comport herself with pride; any slander might have dire consequences. His frankness pleased me and spared my pride.Philippe spent the morning arranging my transformation: dressmaker Victorine, corset-maker, shoemaker, glove-maker—all to fashion me out of convent attire. I was impatient to see my new self; even a glovemaker was called. At lunch, my mother proposed we go to the milliner; I felt dazzled by this independence—like a blind man who had recovered sight. I began to sense how far I had been from society.My father seemed absent at lunch; he is deeply engaged in state affairs and had refused an appointment as Minister of State, preferring an embassy. At home, family life is fragmentary: my brother and father are often away, my mother occupies her mornings dressing, and keeps a busy social calendar of drives, receptions, and evenings at theatres and concerts. We meet mainly at lunch and dinner; otherwise each pursues his own life. I see now why family affection there is so seldom: in Paris people find no time for private intimacy.Miss Griffith, my governess—recommended by the English ambassador—is a Scotch clergyman's daughter, thirty-six, well-bred and meant to teach me English and chaperon me. She is cautious but accommodating; I quickly perceive she will be governed by me. She advised against being dazzled by society and against being seduced by what attracts me most—her moral lectures are brief and repetitive.I have not yet seen the streets; I must first be made presentable. The Italian opera opens soon, and my mother has a box. I read too much indiscriminately; modern literature often dwells on love—the theme that once obsessed us at the convent. Corinne and Adolphe delighted me. When I asked if I might meet Mme. de Staël, my parents laughed and informed me she was dead.III. THE SAME TO THE SAME December.My Darling—tomorrow I make my debut. I have rehearsed all my follies, tried on stays, shoes, curls, dress, ornaments—performed a private inspection. Griffith alone witnessed my little play. In short, I have left the convent and been reborn as a frivolous society girl. I may be raw in some ways—shoulder lines not yet rounded—but there is strength, health, and a clear, vivid complexion. My features are correct, my hair pale gold with darker center, my eyes bright and insolent, the mouth expressive. I have grace of gait and voice; my grandmother's manner and voice are in me. If I wished, I can be innocent, witty, or melancholy enough to command admiration.My father has provided me with a trousseau—ribbons, shoes, gloves, dressing-case, scent-box, fan, sunshade, prayer-book, cashmere shawl—and promised riding lessons. I can dance; tomorrow evening I am to appear in white muslin, with a Greek garland of white roses, and I intend to play the simpleton to win the other women. My mother underestimates me and sees me as a simpleton; my brother treats me with polite contempt. I suspect Alphonse harbors secret discontent and jealousy toward our father, which none of the household understands.We are so unlike my parents: my father absorbed in public life and pleasures, my mother devoted to her toilette and society. They both know how to wear their parts; the show of unity in the family conceals indifference. It amazes me how Paris life can consume people from nine until the small hours, draining both energy and money. I am newly curious about this spell of society that keeps people enslaved and separate. For now I have sixteen hours a day to think, while those around me barely have minutes for reflection. There is much to learn and plumb in this world I sought; I am eager and anxious at once.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yesterday at two o'clock I drove through the Champs-Élysées and the Bois de Boulogne on a beautiful autumn day that reminded me of the Loire. I finally experienced Paris. The Place Louis XV impressed me as a testament to human artistry.I was impeccably dressed, pensive, and poised, yet no one smiled. The carriage moved slowly, matching my demeanor. Only one person, a duke—my father—acknowledged me with satisfaction; my mother sent a butterfly kiss. Griffith, ever observant, scanned indiscriminately. A young woman should know where to look.I was furious. A man inspected my carriage without seeing me—probably a carriage-maker. I had misjudged my appeal; beauty, it seems, is common in Paris. Hats were tipped to the less deserving while my mother drew admiration. I resolved to understand this.The men were generally unattractive, with only poor imitations among them. Their fashion lacked the elegance, color, and comfort of previous generations. Hats, like truncated columns, sat awkwardly on heads. It seems easier to incite a revolution than to create a stylish hat. Despite their fickle reputation, Frenchmen cling to ridiculous styles.Overall they felt unappealing, their worn faces reflecting thwarted ambition and bruised vanity. A fine forehead was rare. "And these are the products of Paris!" I said to Miss Griffith. "Most cultivated and pleasant men," she replied. I was silent; a thirty-six-year-old spinster's heart is a well of tolerance.In the evening I went to a ball, staying close to my mother, who received all attention. I was merely a pretext for compliments aimed at her. She found me partners who all spoke of the heat and beauty of the ball as if I were freezing and blind; each man claimed it was his first time seeing me.My dazzling dress paled beside the extravagant attire of married women, each surrounded by admirers and watching one another closely. A few shone with real beauty, my mother among them. At a ball a girl is merely a dancing machine—insignificant.The men impressed me no more at the ball than on the Champs-Élysées: exhausted, bland, uniform. The proud, vigorous men of our ancestors seem vanished. One man, however, stood out for his beauty yet failed to stir me. He was not noble; no matter his genius, a commoner could not move me. He seemed absorbed in himself, as if intellectuals view us as objects. When they love, they should abandon their writing; otherwise their love is not genuine. I learned he is a professor and author, driven by ambition.I resolved not to quarrel with society for failing to recognize my merits and instead embraced the simple joy of dancing, which I thoroughly enjoyed. I overheard trivial gossip about people I did not know; perhaps my ignorance spared me. Society is a web of intrigue, and I am perceptive.Returning home pleasantly tired, I began sharing my experiences with my mother. She interrupted, advising me to confide only in her. "My dear child," she said, "it requires as much tact to know when to be silent as when to speak."Her advice revealed feelings that should remain concealed, even from a mother. I realized the vast realm of feminine duplicity. In our innocence we would seem astute gossipers; a mere gesture or glance can teach profound lessons. Suddenly I felt overwhelming shyness. Must I never again express my joy in dancing? What of deeper emotions?I went to bed sorrowful, reeling from the clash between my candid nature and society's harsh rules. Already the hedges of my life are flecked with white wool! Farewell, beloved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################How deeply your letter moved me, especially when I reflect on how our paths diverge: you into Parisian brightness, I into a quiet retreat.At the Castle of Maucombe my room remains as I left it, now opening on a splendid view of the Gemenos valley I once saw without understanding. Soon after my arrival my parents took me to dine with M. de l'Estorade, a wealthy, frugal old gentleman. He lost his only son to Bonaparte’s conscription in 1813; the youth evaded the draft, joined the Emperor’s bodyguard, and vanished after the Battle of Leipzig. The Baron clung to hope and hoarded his income for him. Only later did I learn that my future husband had in fact been making his slow return from Russia, Poland, and Germany, his fortune turning in Berlin where the French Minister helped him home. The Baron, a small Provençal landowner with an income near ten thousand livres, remains obscure enough in Provence despite these adventures.The combined sum from the late Mme. de l'Estorade’s estate and the father’s savings is roughly two hundred and fifty thousand livres—excluding property—a considerable fortune here. The Baron has just bought a poorly run estate he plans to plant with ten thousand mulberry trees. Now that his son is found, his one desire is to marry him to a girl of good family.My parents approved once they learned Renée de Maucombe would be accepted without a dowry and that her prospective inheritance would be acknowledged in the marriage contract. My younger brother Jean signed a document recognizing an advance on his inheritance. This is how the nobles of Provence work within Bonaparte’s Civil Code, which otherwise pushes many girls into convents rather than marriage.That dinner was the first meeting of your sweetheart and the exile. My father-in-law-to-be has no castle, only a simple country house at the entrance of a beautiful valley: unpretentious, four pebble walls and a red-tiled roof, with a typically Provençal garden enclosed by low pebble walls and a touch of neglect. Still, the house did its best for the dinner, serving us on old silver.The exile — my dear — is like the railing: emaciated and pale, marked by suffering. At thirty-seven he looks much older, streaks of white in once-beautiful hair; he is a little deaf, like the Knight of the Sorrowful Countenance. Despite his ravaged appearance, he seems pleased to be marrying the fair Renée de Maucombe.I have graciously agreed to become Mme. de l'Estorade, bringing the two hundred and fifty thousand livres, on condition that I may transform the grange into a park. I have asked my father for a water grant to help this. In a month I will be Mme. de l'Estorade, having made a good impression.While you prepare to enjoy Paris’s vibrant life, I descend from our shared dreams into the humble realities of country existence. I am determined to bring joy to this young man, who knew little youth, going straight from his mother’s arms to war and the harshness of Siberia. I will take pleasure in the quiet, improving the little oasis around our home with fine trees and greenery: a hillside park with perhaps a kiosk from which I can glimpse the Mediterranean, orange and lemon trees, and the poetry of the surrounding nature. My father-in-law and the Chevalier share my religious sentiments.My life will run like a smooth highway, free from the turmoil of another Bonaparte. If I have children they will be mine to nurture and guide; if you remain true to your destiny, your children will have in me a devoted protector. I bade farewell to the romances and adventures we once imagined: my milestones will be the care of the young Masters de l'Estorade and the mischief they bring. I will embroider their caps and be cherished by a frail man in the Gemenos valley. Perhaps one day I may visit Marseilles; otherwise provincial dangers are minimal. We will interest ourselves in the silkworms whose mulberry leaves we sell and share in Provence’s ordinary ups and downs. Quarrels seem unlikely: M. de l'Estorade has promised to leave household matters to me, and I shall see that he does.You, dear Louise, will provide the romance in my life. Tell me of your balls, your dresses, and the men you meet; your other self will always be with you, listening and feeling the excitement. If only I could enjoy some Parisian fun while you managed La Crampade, the name of our grange—poor M. de l'Estorade thinks he is marrying one woman; will he discover two?I must conclude—I am foolish only through you. One kiss on each cheek; my lips remain untouched, he has only taken my hand. P.S. I have just opened your third letter. I have about one thousand livres to spend; buy me pretty things we cannot find here or in Marseilles. Think of the recluse at La Crampade; neither family has anyone in Paris with taste to buy what we need. I will answer your letter later. Goodbye, dear.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################VI. DON FELIPE HENAREZ TO DON FERNAND PARIS, September.The heading of this letter shows the head of your house is safe. The massacre of our ancestors in the Court of Lions forced us into double identity, but it also taught us an Arab cunning that may have saved my life.Ferdinand’s fear made his acting convincing enough for Valdez to believe him. Had I not intervened, the Admiral would have perished. The Liberals still fail to understand kingship. I know this Bourbon well; his repeated assurances of protection confirm my suspicions. A true Spaniard need not repeat promises; excess words often signal deceit.Valdez left on an English ship. As soon as I saw the cause of my beloved Spain falter in Andalusia, I set in motion my escape. I instructed my steward, sent coral fishers to meet me on a coast, and while Ferdinand sought to seize me, I was already in my barony of Macumer, among brigands who defy authority.The last Hispano-Moorish family of Granada has found refuge in an African desert, even a Saracen horse on an estate inherited from Saracens. The brigands, who once feared my authority, now shield me from the King of Spain's vendetta, proud to defend the Duc de Soria, a Henarez—the first to visit them since the Moors ruled.I had hoped to live on the estate’s income, but Quevedo’s reports were right: he had twenty-two lives at my disposal but not a real one; vast prairies without houses, virgin forests with no furniture. A million piastres and a resident master for fifty years would be needed to make these lands profitable. I must address this.The conquered have time to reflect. Seeing my noble country reduced to a corpse for monks to exploit moved me to tears; it foreshadows Spain’s grim future.In Marseilles I learned of Riego's fall. It struck me that my life would be a prolonged, unnoticed martyrdom. Is existence worth living when one can neither die for country nor live for a woman? To love and conquer—this was our law. Yet my sabre is broken, the palace in ruins, the living spring dried in barren sand.Here is my last will and testament.Don Fernand, you now see why I restrained your enthusiasm and urged loyalty to the rey netto. As your brother and friend I implore you to obey; as your master I command. You will approach the King and claim my dignities, property, office, and titles. He may hesitate, but tell him you are loved by Marie Heredia and she can marry none but a Duc de Soria. This will please the King: the Heredia fortune has long been a barrier to my ruin. Your proposal will seem to deprive me of a final resource, and he will gladly hand over my spoils.You will then marry Marie. Her secret love for you was no secret to me; I have prepared the old Count for your ascension. Marie and I discharged our family duties; everything favors you. You are handsome and possess Marie's heart. I am an ill-favored grandee for whom she harbors an unconfessed aversion; any reluctance from her due to my misfortunes you will soon overcome.As Duc de Soria, you will not regret my predecessor nor lose a maravedi. I will keep my mother's diamonds for my independence; the gap they leave can be filled by Marie's jewels. Send them with my nurse, old Urraca—the only servant I shall retain. No one else makes my chocolate as she does.During our short revolution, my toil was reduced to bare necessities, covered by my salary. You will find the income from the last two years with your steward. This sum is mine, but a Duc de Soria cannot marry without expenses, so we will share it. You will not refuse this wedding gift from your brigand brother; I intend it so.The barony of Macumer, not Spanish territory, remains mine. Thus I still have a country and a name should I wish to re-enter the world.Thank Heaven, this concludes our business, and the house of Soria is saved!Just as I resign myself to being merely Baron de Macumer, the French cannon announce the arrival of the Duc d'Angouleme. You will understand why I must stop here.October.When I arrived I had not ten doubloons. A poor leader finds means to live within calamities he cannot avert: for the vanquished Moor, a horse and the desert; for the frustrated Christian, the cloister and a few gold pieces.My resignation is weariness. I am not ready to abandon all thoughts of life. Ozalga gave letters of introduction, including one to a bookseller who serves our compatriots as Galignani does the English. He found me eight pupils at three francs a lesson. I teach every other day, earning twelve francs—more than I need. When Urraca arrives I will pass on my connection to another Spanish exile.I lodge in Rue Hillerin-Bertin with a poor widow who takes boarders. My room faces south, overlooking a small garden. It is quiet; I have green trees to gaze on, and I spend one piastre a day. I am amazed at the calm pleasure of this life, reminiscent of Dionysius at Corinth. From sunrise until ten I smoke and enjoy chocolate, contemplating two Spanish plants—a broom rising from a clump of jessamine—gold on white, colors that thrill any scion of the Moors. At ten I give lessons until four, return for dinner, then read and smoke until bed.I can endure this life of work, meditation, solitude, and society for a long time. Be happy, Fernand; my abdication brings no regrets. Five days have passed since I wrote my will; they feel like five centuries. Honor, titles, wealth—as if they never existed.Now that the conventional barriers of respect are down, I open my heart to you, dear boy. Though outwardly grave, my heart is full of tenderness and devotion—never granted to any object, not even she who was to be my bride since childhood. This is the source of my political enthusiasm. Spain took the place of a mistress and received my homage. Now Spain is lost. Beggared of all, I gaze upon the ruins of my former self and ponder the mystery of my existence.Why was life given this carcass, and when will it depart? Why did a race famed for chivalry infuse all its virtues into this last offshoot only for the seed not to sprout—no stem, no flower to scatter its Eastern perfumes? What crime did I commit before birth to inspire no love? Was I destined to be stranded, a useless hulk on a barren shore? In my soul I see deserts where my ancestors roamed under a scorching sun that shrivels life. Proud remnant of a fallen race, vain force, love wasted—an old man in the prime of youth—I shall await death's last grace. Alas! under this murky sky no spark will rekindle these ashes. Thus my last words may echo Christ's: "My God, Thou hast forsaken me!" A cry of agony and terror whose depth few mortals have explored.You can now understand, Fernand, the joy I find in living through you and Marie. I will watch you with the pride of a creator satisfied with his work. Love each other well and continue to do so; any discord between you would pain me more than it would you.Our mother had a premonition that events would one day fulfill her wishes. Perhaps a mother's longing is a pact with God. Was she not one of those beings who converse with Heaven and return with visions? How often I read in her brow that she coveted for Fernand the honors and wealth of Felipe! When I mentioned this she would weep, revealing a heart that rightly belonged to both her sons, yet was given to you alone by an irresistible passion.Her spirit will hover above you as you bow at the altar. Mother, have you no caress for your Felipe now that he has yielded to your favorite even the girl you regretfully thrust into his arms? What I have done pleases our womenfolk, the dead, and the King; it is God's will. Do not resist, Fernand; obey and remain silent.P.S. Tell Urraca to call me nothing but M. Henarez. Do not mention me to Marie. You must be the sole living soul to know the secrets of the last Christianized Moor, whose veins carry the blood of a great family that began in the desert and is now about to die out in a solitary exile.Farewell.VII. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO RENEE DE MAUCOMBEWhat! To be married so soon? This is unheard of. After a month you become engaged to a stranger about whom you know nothing. He may be deaf—there are many kinds of deafness! He could be sickly, tiresome, insufferable!Don’t you see, Renée, what they want from you? You’re needed to carry on the glorious stock of the l’Estorades, that is all. You will be buried in the provinces. Are these the promises we made each other? Were I you, I’d rather set off to the Hyères islands in a caique, risk capture by an Algerian corsair, and be sold to the Grand Turk. Then I could be a Sultana and make a stir in the harem while I’m young—yes, and afterwards too!You are leaving one convent for another. I know you; you are a coward, and you will submit to family life with docility. But I will guide you; you must come to Paris. There we shall drive men wild and hold court like queens. Your husband may join the Chamber in three years. I know how those members work; you will work that machine well; you can live in Paris and become, as my mother calls it, a woman of fashion. Oh! do not think I will leave you in your grange!Monday.For a fortnight I have been living the life of society: one evening at the Italiens, another at the Grand Opera, and always a ball afterward. Society is bewitching. The Opera’s music enchants me; while my soul is immersed, I am the focus of admiration and opera glasses. But a single glance makes the boldest youth drop his eyes.I’ve seen charming young men, yet none have stirred me; not one has evoked the emotion I feel when listening to García in his splendid duet with Pellegrini in Otello. How jealous Rossini must have been to express jealousy so well! What a cry in "Il mio cor si divide!" I speak Greek to you, for you’ve not heard García, but you know how jealous I am!What a wretched dramatist Shakespeare is! Othello wins battles and leaves Desdemona meekly neglected. Such a sheep deserves slaughter. Let the man I love beware of thinking of anything but loving me!I prefer the long trials of old chivalry. That lout of a young lord who took offense because his sovereign lady sent him among the lions to fetch her glove was impertinent. Doubtless the lady had some exquisite prize for him, which he lost—the puppy!But I ramble. My father is likely to represent our master the King in Madrid. I say "our" master, for I shall be part of the embassy. My mother wishes to remain; my father will take me to have a woman with him.This seems simple, but there are complications. In a fortnight I have probed the household’s secrets. My mother would accompany my father to Madrid if he took M. de Canalis as secretary. But the King appoints secretaries; the Duke cannot offend the King, who hates opposition. The cunning diplomat thinks he cut the knot by leaving the Duchess here. M. de Canalis, the great poet of the day, cultivates my mother’s society and studies diplomacy with her from three to five. Diplomacy must be a fine subject, for he keeps at it as regularly as a gambler at the Stock Exchange.The Duc de Rhetore, our elder brother, solemn and capricious, will remain in Paris. Miss Griffith has discovered Alphonse is in love with a ballet girl at the Opera. How can one fall in love with legs and pirouettes? Our brother only attends when Tullia dances; he applauds and leaves. Two ballet girls in a family are more destructive than the plague. My second brother is with his regiment; I have not yet seen him. Thus I must act as the Antigone of His Majesty's ambassador. Perhaps I may marry in Spain; perhaps my father imagines a marriage without a dowry. He asked if I’d accompany him and offered me the use of his Spanish master."Spain, the country for castles in the air!" I exclaimed. "Perhaps you hope it will mean marriages for me!"He only gave a knowing look. For days he has teased me at lunch; he watches me, and I dissemble, playing cruelly with him as father and ambassador in petto. He asked my opinion of some young men and girls. I answered with inane remarks about hair, faces, and figures. He seemed disappointed by my crassness and inwardly blamed himself for asking."Still, father," I added, "don’t suppose I’m saying what I really think: mother made me afraid the other day that I had spoken too frankly about my impressions.""With your family, you can speak quite freely," said my mother."Very well, then," I continued. "The young men I’ve met are too self-centered to excite interest; they’re taken up with themselves. They put on an expression to talk to us, then drop it, apparently satisfied that we don’t use our eyes. The man as he converses is the lover; silent, he is the husband. The girls are so artificial that it’s impossible to know them, except from their dancing; their figures are not a sham. But the alarming thing is society’s brutality. Small incidents when supper is announced give one an idea—comparing small things with great—of what a popular uprising might be. Courtesy is a thin veneer over general selfishness. I imagined society to be very different. Women count for little in it; perhaps this is a survival of Bonapartist ideas.""Armande is coming on extraordinarily," said my mother."Mother, did you think I would never get beyond asking to see Mme. de Staël?"My father smiled and rose.Saturday.I left out one thing. The love we pictured is extremely well hidden; I have seen no trace. True, quick exchanges of glances occur in drawing-rooms, but how colorless it all is! Love as we imagined it—a world of wonders, glorious dreams, charming realities, sorrows that awaken sympathy, smiles bringing sunshine—does not exist. The bewitching words, constant interchange of happiness, misery of absence, joy of the beloved’s presence—where are they? What soil produces these radiant flowers of the soul? Which is wrong? We or the world?I have seen hundreds of men; not one has moved me. No proof of admiration or devotion on their part, not even a sword drawn in my defense, would have touched me. Love is so rare that one might live a lifetime without meeting the being who can make one happy. The thought chills; if found too late, what then?For days I have trembled at the thought of women’s destinies and begun to understand why so many wear sad faces beneath social excitement. Marriage is mere chance. Look at yours. A storm of wild thoughts swept me. To be loved every day the same, yet with a difference, to be loved as much after ten years as on the first day—such love demands years. The lover must languish; curiosity must be piqued and satisfied, feelings stirred and reciprocated.Is there a law for the inner fruits of the heart, as for nature's visible fruits? Can joy be made lasting? In what measure should love mix tears with pleasure? The convent’s monotony seems the only real life; while wealth, splendor, tears, delights, triumph, joy, and the satisfaction of an equal, shared, sanctioned love appear idle visions.I see no room in this city for the gentle ways of love—precious walks, the full moon on water, the suitor pleading in vain. Rich, young, and beautiful, I have only to love, and love would be my life; yet in three months nothing has appealed to me among the bright, covetous eyes here. No voice has thrilled me, no glance brightened my world.Music alone has filled my soul; it has even taken the place of our friendship. At night I linger by my window, summoning the future from its mystery. Sometimes, setting out to drive, I dismount and walk the Champs-Élysées imagining the man who will awaken my slumbering soul is near. Instead I meet mountebanks, vendors, jugglers, and secret lovers. I am tempted to stop them, ask, "You who are happy, tell me what is love." But I repress the impulse and return to my carriage, swearing to die an old maid. Love is an incarnation, and how many conditions must be met before it can occur! We are not certain we shall never quarrel with ourselves; how much less certain with another? Only God can solve this.I begin to think I’ll return to the convent. If I stay in society I may do foolish things, for I cannot reconcile what I see with my sense of delicacy and principle.Ah! my mother is the happiest, adored by Canalis, her great little man. I sometimes crave to know what goes on between them. Griffith tells me she has passed through such moods; she has longed to lash out at women whose happiness is evident and blackened their characters. According to her, virtue is burying savage instincts in one’s heart. But then what becomes of the heart? It becomes a sink for the worst in us.It is humiliating that no admirer has yet appeared for me. I am marriageable, but having brothers and a family sensitive about honor may keep men away. If that is why, they are cowards.The parts of Chimène in the Cid and the Cid himself delight me. What a marvelous play! Farewell.VIII. THE SAME TO THE SAME January.Our master is a poor refugee, hiding because of his part in the revolution quelled by the Duc d’Angouleme—a triumph that has led to splendid fêtes. Though a Liberal and likely a man of the people, he has piqued my interest; I suspect he was condemned to death. I make him talk to uncover his secret, but he has a truly Castilian taciturnity, proud as if he were Gonsalvo di Cordova, yet angelic in patience and gentleness. His pride is not irritable like Miss Griffith’s; it belongs to his nature, compelling civility through perfect manners and keeping us at a distance with the respect he shows.My father says much nobility dwells in Señor Henarez, whom he humorously calls Don Henarez. A few days ago I took the liberty of thus addressing him. He raised his eyes, usually cast down, and flashed a look that abashed me; he certainly has the most beautiful eyes imaginable. I asked if I had offended, and he replied in grand, rolling Spanish:"I am here only to teach you Spanish."I blushed and felt snubbed. I nearly retorted sharply, but remembering what our dear mother used to say, I instead replied:"It would be kind to tell me if you have any complaints."A tremor passed through him; the blood rose in his olive cheeks. He answered with some emotion:"Religion must have taught you, better than I can, to respect the unhappy. Had I been a don in Spain and lost everything in Ferdinand VII.'s triumph, your witticism would be unkind; but if I am only a poor language teacher, is it not heartless satire? Neither is worthy of a young lady of rank."I took his hand, saying:"In the name of religion, I beg you to pardon me."He bowed, opened my Don Quixote, and sat.This little incident disturbed me more than a harvest of compliments and glances. During the lesson I watched him closely, which I could do safely, as he never looks at me.From my observations, the tutor, whom we assumed to be forty, is under thirty. My governess remarked on the beauty of his black hair and pearly teeth. His eyes are velvet and fire, but he is plain and insignificant. Though Spaniards are often called unclean, this man is meticulously groomed, with hands whiter than his face. He stoops slightly and has an odd, large head. Smallpox scars give his ugliness a piquancy; his prominent forehead and shaggy brows give a harsh, frowning, plaintive look, like a sickly child whose life depends on superhuman care. As my father noted, his features are a shrunken Cardinal Ximenes. The natural dignity of his manners disconcerts the dear Duke, who dislikes him and never feels at ease before superiority.Once my father knows enough Spanish we will go to Madrid. When Henarez returned two days after his reprimand, I said to show gratitude:"I have no doubt you left Spain because of political events. If my father is sent there, as seems likely, we might help you obtain a pardon, if you are under sentence.""It is impossible for anyone to help me," he replied."But," I pressed, "is that because you refuse help or because the situation is impossible?""Both," he said, bowing, in a tone that closed the matter.My father's blood boiled. I was offended by this haughty posture and promptly dropped Señor Henarez.Yet something admirable lay in his refusal of aid. "He would not accept even our friendship," I thought while conjugating a verb. Suddenly I stopped and spoke to him in Spanish. Henarez replied that equality of sentiment is necessary between friends, which did not exist here, making the matter pointless."Do you mean equality in feeling, or equality in rank?" I demanded, eager to provoke him.He raised those awe-inspiring eyes, and mine fell. Dear, this man is a hopeless enigma. He seemed to ask whether my words implied love; the mixture of joy, pride, and agonized doubt in his glance moved me. It was clear that advances, taken lightly in France, might complicate with a Spaniard, and I retreated, feeling foolish.The lesson ended; he bowed, and his eyes expressed a humble prayer: "Don't trifle with a poor wretch."This sudden variance from his usual grave dignity made a deep impression. It seems horrible to think and say, but I can’t help believing there are treasures of affection in that man.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################All is over, my dear child, and it is Mme. de l'Estorade who writes to you. Between us there is no change; it is only a girl the less.Do not be troubled; I did not give my consent recklessly. My life is now mapped out, and the certainty suits me. A great moral power has removed chance from my life. We have property to develop and a home to beautify; I have a household to manage and a husband to reconcile to life. I shall likely have children to care for and educate.Everyday life cannot be cast in a heroic mould, yet I can still explore the infinite within our familiar craft. My humble duties allow for passion; restoring faith in happiness to those who have suffered is a noble task that will relieve the monotony of existence. I see little room for suffering and much good to be done. My love for Louis de l'Estorade does not make my heart race, but there is nothing in him that offends me.You may wonder about my instincts for greatness and the mental energies that bind us. That thought has troubled me, but these faculties remain mine, even if concealed and employed in secret for the welfare of our family. The time for a woman to seek admiration is short; if my life is not grand, it will at least be calm and free from shocks.Nature has favored our sex with a choice between love and motherhood. I have chosen motherhood: my children will be my gods, and this spot of earth my Eldorado.I can say no more today. Thank you for all you have sent. Please look at my needs on the enclosed list. I am determined to live with refinement and luxury, taking from provincial life only what adds charm. Solitude never vulgarizes a woman—she remains herself. My father-in-law is delighted, refuses me nothing, and is sending workmen from Paris to renovate the house.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE January.Oh, Renée, you have made me miserable! Your beauty, proud features, graceful manner, rich soul, and exquisite heart will be wasted in a dull marriage you will come to despise. I already dread your children; they will be monsters!You seem resigned, neither hopeful nor afraid. Yet what if a glorious morning brings the man who could awaken you? The thought chills me. At least you will have a friend; you will become the guardian angel of your valley and learn to cherish its beauty until it is part of you. As you walk with your husband and children I foresee your letters mirroring the valley’s monotony. I will remain a heart untouched by social pettiness, all your own.Monday.My Spaniard bewilders me with a calm, severe dignity. His solemnity and silence provoke curiosity; how odd that a language teacher should fascinate me more than any nobleman or officer. His coldness piques me—he keeps distance, wrapped in pride and mystery, while I am bold. This amuses me; it is nothing, yet it irks.Yesterday I made a remark to M. Henarez that seems to have struck him; he did not answer, and the lesson ended with a bow implying he might not return. That would suit me; I do not want a sentimental catastrophe. I’ve been reading Rousseau and find his passion that moralizes detestable. Richardson’s Clarissa, praised by my father, better captures English women; Rousseau reads like a sermon.Love is for me a subjective poem. Everything written about it is both false and true. Since you will be an authority on married love, I feel called to remain unmarried and seek a grand passion, to enrich our shared experience. Tell me every detail of your first days with that strange creature called a husband; I promise to do the same if I ever find love.Farewell, poor martyred darling.XI. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU La Crampade.Your Spaniard and you make me shudder. Dismiss him. Everything you describe fits a dangerous adventurer who risks all. He cannot be your husband or lover. I will write more about my married life when my heart is not so anxious from your letter.XII. MLLE. DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE February.This morning my father visited, more thoughtful than usual. He asked me to sit and, after flattering me for a moment and admitting he’d misjudged my intelligence, unfolded a political lecture: France stands in a perilous place, its Revolution still active in law and thought; the King is a head without arms, nobles lack authority over elected men, and the family has ceased to exist—individuals and State treasury have replaced the family spirit. He argued a state cannot stand without paternal authority, and warned of calamity if our party fails.He then asked me, half-joking, to help persuade him to sacrifice my inheritance for my younger brother’s estate. I asked how I could still enjoy life if I surrendered my fortune. He called me a little hussy and laughed, but the request was serious: my future husband would not take me without a dowry. We parted in good humor.Your letter made me reflect on the abyss you warned about. I took precautions concerning Henarez, whose gaze unsettles me. I hesitated to tell my father I wished no more lessons, yet find myself drawn to him. His voice thrills me; his manners are simple and genuine. When he left after a lesson he almost reached to kiss my hand, then stopped, terrified. I smiled, understanding the courage it takes for a plebeian to love a noblewoman. My smile encouraged him; his eyes were wet with unshed tears, and I felt a strange happiness. He has fascinated me.Friday.He is handsome and speaks admirably, revealing intellectual power and a new world of thought. He told a story of a valet who died for a queen’s glance and I was captivated. At a ball M. de Vandenesse hinted Henarez was of noble descent, the Duke de Soria, whose titles were confiscated by King Ferdinand. Suspense followed until our next lesson.During that lesson I suspected him of being the Duke de Soria. He denied it, saying he was merely the servant of the King of Spain. His reticence provoked me; I devised a practical test: I asked him to translate a letter I wrote describing my ideal lover. He was visibly moved and asked to keep the letter as a remembrance. He left asking to speak with my father; from my father I learned Henarez is now the Baron de Macumer, with property in Sardinia.Two weeks have passed since I last saw him; I wonder what he is doing. He has become necessary to me, and I am torn between love and the reality of my situation.Sunday at the Italian Opera, I felt his fiery gaze. He has a genius for love.XIII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO MLLE. DE CHAULIEU LA CRAMPADE, February.My dear Louise, I waited to write until I had learned many things for your happiness. The difference between a girl and a married woman is vast; one cannot fully understand the other. I chose to marry Louis de l'Estorade rather than return to the convent. The seriousness of marriage terrified me, yet I realized friendship might be the key to a happy marriage. I accepted his love believing it would not be slavery but a reign.We agreed to separate marriage from certain marital rights to preserve independence. I watched Louis to see if suffering had softened or hardened him. His love proved a passion, and I judged I could risk much. I proposed a friendship in which we share life’s burdens; I asked to be left free, promising to attend to his feelings. He was overjoyed, and we married soon after.I am nearly happy now, though I must conceal my full contentment from him, for he is sensitive. For three months we lived much as before; I studied our relationship and saw how my presence changed him. I could bring new life to my husband before bearing him children. His happiness brightened our home; even my father became generous. I manage the household with economy—seeking pleasure without excess.I harbor ambitious plans for Louis, hoping to see him in politics; he has embraced my designs, showing the depth of his affection. To conclude, here is a letter he wrote, expressing his love: he sees me as a treasure, and I feel proud of our relationship.XIV. THE DUC DE SORIA TO THE BARON DE MACUMER MADRID.My dear brother, I did not make you Duc de Soria to betray the name. I cannot tolerate your exile while we enjoy happiness. Marie and I will delay marrying until you accept the money Urraca offers. We have prayed for your happiness; I believe Heaven will send you the love you seek. Marie and I will always be grateful to you. Write to us through the Nuncio. Farewell; take pride in the happiness you have brought us.XV. LOUISE DE CHAULIEU TO MME. DE L'ESTORADE March.Ah, my friend, marriage has made a philosopher of you! Your prudent calculations trouble me. Do you think you can convert me to matrimony with cautious arithmetic? We left Blois innocent, armed with meditation, and now your weapons have turned against you! If I did not know you were pure, I would think your calculations were vice.You have made a holocaust of your youth—becoming a miser before your time. If Louis loves you, he will never realize you act from necessity. Love is the first principle of all virtues; I cannot accept you justifying your married position as a calculation. You act a man's part; Louis must play the woman!Your letter plunged me into thought; I see now the convent cannot replace a mother. Please, do not turn away from my cries!Monday.Last night I looked at the stars and pictured my Spaniard in a tree by my window. I felt both joy and fear. He blew a letter to me through the window, but I burned it unread—it felt improper to read such an offering. At the opera he seemed sad; I learned he had renounced a rich heiress for another man’s sake. I was wretched for burning his letter—what might he have confessed? To be loved in secret, to know a man is devoted—how can I resist?When I returned home I reopened my window, hoping he might be there. He was, and I received another letter, which I read and cherished.XVI. THE SAME TO THE SAME March.I am dressed in white, with camellias in my hair. I feel an odd desire to delay my decision and make him wait. What a vision of beauty—driving my barouche, seeing him, the thrill. I complimented him on his horse; he said it was a gift from his steward. Henarez is no longer just a simple man; he grows more distinguished. Do my love affairs amuse you? I am a little off my head and must stop.XVII. MME. DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE CHAULIEU April.My angel, you have grieved me. You do not realize the force of the word indissoluble in marriage. Each marriage has laws shaped by place and character: a wife of a leader must resign to his guidance; a wife of a fool must take control. You speak of vice, but passion can be admirable when it springs from the heart. We are answerable to God and ourselves for our happiness.You will find a wife’s calculation necessary to maintain happiness. I have reflected on the duties of wife and mother, and I believe a sublime hypocrisy may be required to reach the ideal of a perfect woman. I persist in my principles, convinced virtue varies with surroundings. If I wished to make Louis unhappy, I would leave him the helm entirely.I may not have married a man of distinction, but I hope to further him toward it. Five years from now we will see if I was mistaken. You taught me what a woman sacrifices in marriage; regret is not remorse, but I feel the loss of freedom. You say marriage has made you a philosopher; I feel far from it. I have read Bonald, who revealed the strength of the family.Farewell, my dear friend.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Well, my Renee, you are a wonderful woman, and I now agree that we can only be virtuous by cheating. Will that satisfy you? The man who loves us is our property; we may make a fool or a genius of him as we please—though the former is common. You will make yours a genius and keep the secret—two heroic acts, if you will!Ah, if there were no afterlife, how nicely you would be sold, for this is the martyrdom you are choosing. You want to make him ambitious and keep him in love! Child, surely keeping him in love alone is punishment enough.Tell me, to what extent is calculation a virtue, or is virtue calculation? You won’t say? We won’t quarrel; we have Bonald to refer to. We are, and intend to remain, virtuous; still, I believe you, with all your little knavery, are a better woman than I.Yes, I am shockingly deceitful. I love Felipe and conceal it with hypocrisy. I long to see him leap from his tree to my balcony—and if he did, how I would wither him with my scorn! I am frank with you.What restrains me? Where is the power that prevents me from telling Felipe how supremely happy he has made me with his pure, absolute, overflowing love?Mme. de Mirbel is painting my portrait, which I intend to give him. Love animates life; every hour, every trifle matters, blurring past, present, and future. Is it still so at the heights of happiness? What is happiness—soothing or exciting? I am restless, dragged to him despite reason and propriety. At least I can better understand your feelings.Felipe’s happiness rests in feeling himself mine; his aloofness and strict obedience irritate me, just as his profound respect did when he was my Spanish master. I want to cry out, “Fool, if you love me as a picture, what will it be when you know the real me?”Oh, Renee, you burn my letters, don’t you? I will burn yours. If anyone else read these thoughts, I would send Felipe to silence them; perhaps even kill the owners for security.Monday.How can one fathom the human heart? My father should introduce me to M. Bonald; I would ask him. I envy God, who can read the heart’s undercurrents. Does Felipe still worship me? That is the question.If I ever detect the slightest fall in the respect he used to show me as my instructor, I believe I would have the strength to end it. “Why these fine words, these grand resolutions?” you will say. Dear, I will tell you.My charming father, devoted as an Italian cavaliere servente, had my portrait painted by Mme. de Mirbel. I managed to get a copy for the Duke and sent the original to Felipe with these lines:“Don Felipe, your single-hearted devotion is met by a blind confidence. Time will show whether this is to treat a man as more than human.”It was a rich reward—both promise and challenge—given without fully committing. If his reply included “Dear Louise!” or even “Louise,” he would be lost!Tuesday.No, he is not lost. The constitutional minister is perfect as a lover. Here is his letter:“Every moment away from you has been filled with ideal pictures, my eyes closed to the outside world, fixed on your image. Henceforth my gaze will rest upon this wondrous ivory—this talisman—since your blue eyes sparkle with life, and paint passes into flesh and blood. If I delayed writing, it was because I could not tear myself from your presence, which wrung from me all I was bound to keep secret.“Yes, closeted with you all last night and today, I have, for the first time, given myself up to complete happiness. Could you see yourself where I have placed you, between the Virgin and God, you might understand the agony of my night. But I would not offend you by speaking of it; one glance from your eyes, robbed of their tender sweetness, would be full of torture. Queen of my life and soul, oh that you could grant me but a fraction of the love I bear you!“This was the burden of my prayer; doubt wrecked my soul as I oscillated between belief and despair. A criminal awaiting his verdict is not more racked with suspense than I. The smile on your lips, which alone can calm my dread of displeasing you, haunts me. From my birth, no one, not even my mother, has smiled on me. The beautiful girl designed for me rejected my heart for my brother. In politics, my efforts have been thwarted; in the eyes of my king I have read only thirst for vengeance—he has been my enemy since childhood, and the Cortes that placed me in power was seen as an insult.“Less than this might breed despondency in the stoutest heart. I have no illusions; I know the gracelessness of my person and how hard it is to do justice to the heart within. To be loved had ceased to be more than a dream when I met you. Thus, when I bound myself to your service, I knew that devotion alone could excuse my passion.“But as I look upon this portrait and hear your smile that whispers of rapture, rays of hope pierce the gloom only to be obscured by mists of doubt and fear of your displeasure. It is impossible you should love me yet—I feel it; but in time, as you see the strength, constancy, and depth of my affection, you may yield me some foothold in your heart. If my daring offends you, tell me without anger and I will return to my former role. But if you consent to try and love me, be merciful and break it gently to one who has placed his happiness in the thought of serving you.”My dear, as I read these words, he seemed to rise before me, pale as the night when the camellias told their story and he knew his offering was accepted. These humble words, unlike usual lover’s flourishes, washed over me; it was the breath of happiness.The weather has been atrocious; impossible to go to the Bois without arousing suspicion. Even my mother, who often goes out regardless of rain, remains at home.Wednesday evening.I have just seen him at the Opera; he was another man. He came to our box, introduced by the Sardinian ambassador.Having read in my eyes that his audacity was well taken, he seemed awkwardly conscious and addressed the Marquise d’Espard as “mademoiselle.” A light brighter than the chandeliers flashed from his eyes. He left with the air of a man unsure of his next move.“The Baron de Macumer is in love!” exclaimed Mme. de Maufrigneuse.“Strange, isn’t it, for a fallen minister?” replied my mother.I kept my composure outwardly, regarding Mmes. de Maufrigneuse and d’Espard and my mother with curiosity as if they spoke a foreign language, while inwardly my soul sank in waves of intoxicating joy. There is only one word for what I felt: rapture. Such love as Felipe’s makes him worthy of mine. I am the very breath of his life; my hands guide his thoughts. I have a mad longing to see him clear every obstacle and stand before me, boldly asking for my hand. Then I would know whether this storm would calm at a glance from me.Ah, my dear, I stopped here, and I am still trembling. As I wrote, I heard a noise outside and rose to see. From my window I saw him coming along the wall at the risk of his life. I went to the bedroom window and made him a sign; it was enough. He leaped from the wall—ten feet—and then ran along the road, showing me he was not hurt. That he thought of my fear moved me so much that I am still crying; I don’t know why. Poor ungainly man! What was he coming for? What had he to say to me?I dare not write my thoughts and will go to bed joyful, imagining all we would say if together. Farewell, fair silent one. I have no time to scold you for not writing; it has been over a month since I heard from you! Does this mean you are finally happy? Have you lost the “complete independence” you were so proud of, which nearly played me false tonight?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################If love is life’s essence, why do philosophers dismiss it in marriage? Society demands that women sacrifice for family, which breeds marital discord. Anticipating this, men devised defenses against women; without them, their theories might be overturned by love or aversion.Marriage now embodies two opposing forces the law should reconcile. Your letter challenges foundations laid by the sage of Aveyron, under which I once found comfort. Laws made by men accept conjugal love without passion, allowing women to yield without love’s sanction, reflecting Nature’s emphasis on procreation.Once a person, I now feel like property. I have swallowed many tears alone, aching for a comforting smile. Why are our fates so different? You thrive in lawful love where virtue aligns with pleasure, while I face an uncertain future.You are loved and adored. Embrace this noble romance we long dreamed of. Your beauty, refined and spiritual, was made to enchant. Guard your heart’s secret and test Felipe’s love—and, more importantly, test your own. Novelty and the illusion of happiness easily mislead.You remain independent, dear friend; I urge you not to rush into marriage without assurance. In intimate conversation, a gesture or word can reveal hidden depths. You have the courage to explore where others falter.I watch you anxiously from afar; my heart is in sync with yours. Write to me about everything; your letters ignite passion in my otherwise mundane life. I oscillate between despair and hope—perhaps expecting too much. In youth we often believe ideals and reality will align.Seated beneath a rock in my park, I concluded that love in marriage is a fortunate accident, not a universal rule. The philosopher rightly views the family as society’s unit, placing women in subservience as history shows. The solution to our dilemma lies in motherhood, which I yearn for to channel my energy and heart.Louis remains cheerful; his love is active while mine is passive. He enjoys the flowers of my affection without acknowledging the toil behind them. I play the devoted mother, sacrificing for his happiness, and that sacrifice gives me joy. I await the rewards of my efforts, hoping motherhood will fulfill my aspirations and compensate for what I surrender.---Dear wedded friend, your letter arrived just as I contemplated a bold step. A strange yearning for the unknown revealed the conflict between societal and natural laws within me. I want to speak with Felipe alone, under the lime trees in our garden. This suits the modern girl I’ve become, my father observes.There is method in this seeming madness: I wish to reward Felipe for his nights beneath my window while testing his reaction to my boldness. If he respects my folly, he may imagine me as his wife; if not, farewell, Don Felipe.Meeting him this way seems less risky than encounters in society with prying eyes. I longed for your company to share the anticipation of our first clandestine meeting. I sent Felipe a tracing of the garden key with a note urging him to stop his reckless behavior, warning that if he fell it would tarnish my reputation. Yesterday I took Griffith to the garden, and after a delicious moment of tension, I saw Felipe approach like a shadow.When he reached the garden, I introduced him to Griffith, who withdrew a few steps. The night was mild and heavy with scent; I felt intoxicated by the thrill of being almost alone with him. We savored the moment before I urged him to abandon his reckless climbing, fearing scandal.His pallor revealed fear; I reminded him that if he had been caught, suspicion would fall on my family. He apologized, and I reassured him my actions were not as mad as they seemed.After a few moments of silence he expressed joy and reverence for me, acknowledging that my actions were sacred. I told him I was on the path to loving the man who understands me, but that my heart would belong only to someone who truly knows it.He expressed gratitude for my acceptance; I noted he should prove himself worthy of my affection. We walked and talked; his tenderness reminded me of divine love. His low, melodic voice conveyed his feelings beautifully.I dismissed him, aware of our meeting’s implications. Griffith remarked on my commitment, but I assured her that in France my intentions were merely exploratory.Once again I met Felipe; he appeared timid and reverent, which I found appealing. He seemed to sense my power, yet remained unaware of the depth of my feelings. I am still fond of him, but I keep my composure.Love appears in two forms: one that commands and one that obeys. Can both coexist? Will Felipe ever become my master? These questions unsettle me.He remains oblivious to my true nature. If I were in his position, I would see Mlle. de Chaulieu as a calculating coquette. I still care for Felipe, but I feel calm and in control now. The thrill of uncertainty has faded, and I fear I may have lost the excitement of our initial encounters.Is suspense always more pleasurable than fulfillment? Do we exaggerate our feelings by letting imagination run wild? These thoughts haunt me as I consider another secret meeting in the garden—this time without Griffith. How far can I go? Imagination knows no bounds, but pleasure has its limits. How can a woman reconcile these two aspects of her existence?############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I am displeased with you. If you did not cry over Racine's Berenice and find it a profound tragedy, we have no connection and should part. Forget me; unless you answer satisfactorily I will forget you. You will be M. le Baron de Macumer to me, or cease to exist.At Mme. d'Espard's yesterday your self-satisfied air disgusted me; your confidence of success alarms me. You have lost the humility of your first letter and, instead of the absent-mindedness of a lover, you polished epigrams. That is not the behavior of a true believer, who should always stand in awe of his divinity.If you do not see me as the very breath of your life, a being above others, then I must be less than a woman in your eyes. You have bred a spirit of mistrust that drowns my tenderness. Reflecting on our past, I feel justified in my suspicions. Understand, Prime Minister of all the Spains, that I have contemplated the vulnerable state of our sex. My innocence has lit my path, and I have emerged unscathed.Let me reiterate what my youthful experience taught me: in most matters deceit and broken promises are punished, but love follows its own code—the victim is also accuser and judge. The most heartless betrayals remain secret, known only to the two hearts involved. How can the victim reveal them without harming herself? Love administers its own justice, which the world ignores.I have resolved never to forgive a serious offense; in love, what is not serious? Yesterday you looked like a man who had already succeeded. If that certainty has stripped you of the charming uncertainty that once characterized you, I would blame you. I want you neither timid nor presumptuous; I do not want you to fear losing my affection—that would be an insult—yet I also do not want you to treat your love lightly. Your heart should never be freer than mine. If you cannot grasp the torment a single stab of doubt can inflict, beware the lesson I might teach you.In a single glance I entrusted my heart to you, and you understood it. The purest feelings of a young girl are yours. My reflections have enriched my mind; yet if a wounded heart seeks counsel from reason, it may ally itself with the darker aspects of knowledge and daring.I swear to you, Felipe, if you love me as I believe you do, and if I sense even the least decline in the fear, obedience, and respect you have shown, or if the pure flame that ignited my heart grows dim, expect no reproaches. I would not burden you with letters of weakness, pride, or rage. But if I became silent, my face would announce that death was near. I would not perish, however, until I had branded you with infamy and sown eternal sorrow in your heart; you would behold the girl you loved dishonored and lost, condemned to suffering.Therefore, I implore you, do not make me envy the old, happy Louise, the object of your pure worship, whose heart thrived in happiness, as Dante wrote: Senza brama, sicura ricchezza! I have searched the Inferno for the most terrible punishment, a mental torture to link with divine vengeance.Yesterday, as I observed you, doubt pierced me like a cold dagger. I mistrusted you, and the pain was unbearable. If my service is too exacting, leave it; I would not retain you. I do not need proof of your cleverness. Keep your wit for me. Do not present a polished façade to others for flattery or praise. Come to me burdened with scorn or hatred, the target of calumny, told that no woman loves you; then you will discover the treasures of Louise's heart.We are truly rich only when our wealth lies so deep the world could trample it without knowing. If you were merely handsome, I doubt I would have noticed you or found the myriad reasons for my love. We understand little of those reasons, as little as we know why the sun makes flowers bloom and ripens fruit. Yet I can reveal one cause dear to me.The character and individuality that give meaning to your face are a mystery to all but me. My love transforms you into the most lovable of men, and that is why I wish to keep your mental gifts for myself. To others they should be as meaningless as your eyes and features. Let it be mine alone to ignite your intelligence, as I bring light to your eyes. I envision you as a proud Spanish grandee, cold and haughty, a monument to be admired from a distance—ruins of barbaric power—rather than a figure of Parisian affability.Have you forgotten my ideal portrait? Your excessive cheerfulness betrayed your love. Had I not restrained you, you would have revealed to the sharpest salons of Paris that your inspiration came from Armande‑Louise‑Marie de Chaulieu.I believe too much in your greatness to think your love is merely strategic; yet if you did not show childlike simplicity with me, I could only pity you. Despite this initial fault, you are still deeply admired by LOUISE DE CHAULIEU.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When God sees our faults, He also sees our repentance. My beloved mistress, you are right; I felt I had displeased you but did not know how. Now that you have explained, I find new reasons to adore you. Like the God of Israel, your jealousy is a holy and precious sentiment. Be jealous of your servant, Louise; the more you strike, the more contrite I will be, proving my devotion to you.If the effort it took to overcome my timidity was invisible to you, will Heaven reward it? It was no small feat to show you my true self, as I was before love. In Madrid, I was seen as a good talker, and I wanted you to witness my few gifts. If this was vanity, I have been punished.Your last glance unnerved me. I quailed, even more than when I faced peril at Cadiz. I sought the cause of your displeasure; the lack of sympathy between us was terrible. I wish only to act by your will, think your thoughts, and share your joys and sorrows. The anguish lay in the breach of our shared feelings."I have vexed her!" I repeated, distraught. My noble, beautiful Louise, your words have illuminated my feelings. If this is chastisement, what sweetness lies in your rewards?For me, it was happiness enough to be your servant. You have given me a life I thought lost. I no longer draw a useless breath; I have something to devote myself to, even if it means suffering for you. I will always be as I was when I first offered myself as your bondman. If you were dishonored, my heart would cling to you even more, seeking to heal your wounds and pray for your innocence.Did I not tell you my feelings for you are not just those of a lover? I will be your father, mother, sister, brother—anything or nothing, as you wish. Is it not your desire that has confined my devotion to a lover's feelings?
