Forgive me if the father and brother sometimes fade behind the lover; they are still there, though hidden. If you could read my heart when you appear before me, radiant in beauty, you would know how free from selfishness my pride is when I hear praises of your loveliness. When you greet me, I feel as if God's blessing rests upon me, and the joy lingers long after.Can you be unaware of how you are loved? After seeing you, I return to my study, and the glitter of its ornaments pales before your portrait. I lose myself in thoughts of you, linking my happiness to verse. Have you never heard the whispers of my prayers reaching you?Through silent contemplation of your face, I have deciphered every feature and its connection to your spirit's grace, penning sonnets in Spanish about your dual beauty. You will never see them, for my poetry is unworthy of you. Not a moment passes without thoughts of you; my whole being is bound to you, and without you, I would ache.Now, Louise, can you realize the torture of knowing I displeased you without knowing why? My ideal life was cut short, and I feared you had ceased to love me. Your letter turned my despair into joy. Oh, might I listen forever to such chiding!Once, a child, after falling, turned to his mother and said, "Forgive me." Hiding his hurt, he sought pardon for the pain he caused her. Louise, I am that child, and I remain so. Here is the key to my character, which I humbly place in your hands.But fear not, there will be no more stumbling. Keep tight the chain that binds me to you, so that a touch may communicate your lightest wish to your devoted slave, FELIPE.---My dear friend, how can you, who married a broken-down invalid to mother him, understand the shifting drama of love, where death lies in a glance or a light reply?I reserved one last test for Felipe to see if his love was like a Royalist's for his King, who can do no wrong. He walked with me all night, and not a shadow of suspicion crossed his mind. The next day, he loved me more, but with reverence and humility. He is a true Spaniard, scaling my wall to kiss my hand from my balcony, risking his life.But this is nothing; Christians suffer for heaven. I spoke to my father, the royal ambassador-to-be, with a smile, saying some friends claim he is marrying Armande to an ambassador's nephew for wealth and title. Your daughter weeps but bows to her father's authority. Some say she hides a worldly soul behind her tears.We are going to the gentleman's box at the Opera tonight, and M. le Baron de Macumer will visit us there."Macumer needs a spur," my father said, smiling at me."You mistake Clarissa Harlowe for Figaro!" I replied, scornfully. "When you see me with my right hand ungloved, you will mark your displeasure at this gossip.""I can rest easy about your future. You have no more a girl's head than Jeanne d'Arc had a woman's heart. You will be happy, love no one, and allow yourself to be loved."This was too much; I laughed."What is it, little flirt?" he asked."I tremble for my country's interests..."Seeing him blank, I added, "At Madrid!""You have no idea how this little nun has learned to make fun of her father," he told the Duchess."Armande makes light of everything," my mother replied."What do you mean?" I asked."You are not even afraid of rheumatism on these damp nights," she said, glancing at me meaningfully."Oh!" I answered, "the mornings are so hot!""It's high time she were married," my father said. "It had better be before I go.""If you wish it," I replied demurely.Two hours later, my mother, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, and Mme. d'Espard were blooming in the box. I sat sideways, unseen, to observe everything.Macumer arrived, putting his opera-glasses to look at me comfortably.In the first interval, the young man I call "king of the profligates," Comte Henri de Marsay, entered with an epigram in his eyes and a smile. He greeted my mother and the others, then turned to me, saying, "I do not know whether I shall be the first to congratulate you on an event that will make you the envy of many.""Ah! a marriage!" I cried. "Is it left for me, a girl fresh from the convent, to tell you that predicted marriages never come off?"M. de Marsay whispered to Macumer, and I sensed he was warning him about me.Macumer treated him to a glance of noble scorn, replying he was "not in love with any little coquette." His demeanor delighted me, and I caught sight of my father, ready to act.Felipe showed no fear or doubt. His faith is in me; society's lies cannot hurt him. Not a muscle of his face stirred, nor did his olive cheek flush.The two young counts left, and I laughed at Macumer, saying, "M. de Marsay has been treating you to an epigram on me.""He did more," he replied. "It was an epithalamium.""You speak Greek to me," I said, smiling at him.My father was talking to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, exclaiming about the scandalous gossip surrounding me. He intended to take a turn in the promenade to avoid suggesting the marriage.The Duchess and Mme. d'Espard exchanged glances, sensing something. Love is the least mysterious of all mysteries; it exudes from women like perfume.Having enjoyed Felipe's response, I signaled him to come to my window by the dangerous route. Later, I found him, upright as a statue, studying the light in my room."My dear Felipe," I said, "You acquitted yourself well tonight; you behaved as I would have if told you were marrying.""I thought you would hardly tell others before me.""And what right have you to this privilege?""The right of one who is your devoted slave.""In truth?""I am, and shall ever remain so.""But suppose this marriage was inevitable; suppose I had agreed..."His eyes flashed, calculating the danger of a fall. A power stronger than passion held him back."An Arab cannot take back his word," he said. "I am your slave; my life is not mine to destroy."I placed my hand on his as I said, "Felipe, my beloved, from this moment I am your wife in thought and will. Go in the morning to ask my father for my hand. He wishes to retain my fortune, but if you acknowledge receipt of it in the contract, his consent will be given. I am no longer Armande de Chaulieu. Leave me; no breath of scandal must touch Louise de Macumer."He listened, pale and trembling, then cleared the ten feet to the ground in safety. It was agonizing, but he waved goodbye and disappeared."I am loved then," I thought, "as never woman was before." I fell asleep, content, my destiny fixed.The next day, my father summoned me to his private room, where I found the Duchess and Macumer. After civilities, I said that if my father and M. Henarez agreed, I had no reason to oppose their wishes. My mother invited the Baron to dinner, and we drove in the Bois de Boulogne, where I smiled ironically at M. de Marsay as he passed.Felipe has reprinted his cards as follows:HENAREZ(Baron de Macumer, formerly Duc de Soria.)Every morning, he brings me a splendid bouquet, hidden within which is a letter containing a Spanish sonnet in my honor.To keep this letter concise, I send you only the first and last of these sonnets, translated for your benefit:FIRST SONNETMany a time I've stood, clad in thin silken vest,  Drawn sword in hand, with steady pulse,  Waiting the charge of a raging bull,  And the thrust of his horn, sharper-pointed than Phoebe's crescent.I've scaled, on my lips the lilt of an Andalusian dance,  The steep redoubt under a rain of fire;  I've staked my life upon a hazard of the dice  Careless, as though it were a gold doubloon.My hand would seek the ball out of the cannon's mouth,  But now meseems I grow more timid than a crouching hare,  Or a child spying some ghost in the curtain's folds.For when your sweet eye rests on me,  Any icy sweat covers my brow, my knees give way,  I tremble, shrink, my courage gone.SECOND SONNETLast night I fain would sleep to dream of thee,  But jealous sleep fled my eyelids,  I sought the balcony and looked towards heaven,  Always my glance flies upward when I think of thee.Strange sight! whose meaning love alone can tell,  The sky had lost its sapphire hue,  The stars, dulled diamonds in their golden mount,  Twinkled no more nor shed their warmth.The moon, washed of her silver radiance lily-white,  Hung mourning over the gloomy plain, for thou hast robbed  The heavens of all that made them bright.The snowy sparkle of the moon is on thy lovely brow,  Heaven's azure centers in thine eyes,  Thy lashes fall like starry rays.What a gracious way to say to a young girl that she fills your life! Tell me what you think of this love, which lavishes treasures of earth and soul. Only in the last ten days have I grasped the meaning of that famous Spanish gallantry.Ah, dear, what is happening at La Crampade? How often I stroll there, inspecting our crops! Do you have news of our mulberry trees and last winter's plantations? Does everything prosper as you wish? While buds open on our shrubs, have they also blossomed in the bosom of the wife? Does Louis continue his policy of madrigals? Do you enter each other's thoughts? I wonder if your wedding peace is better than the raging torrent of my love! Has my sweet lady professor taken offense? If so, I would send Felipe post-haste to plead for my pardon or her head. Sweet love, my life here is a splendid success, and I want to know how it fares with life in Provence. We have just added a Spaniard with the complexion of a Havana cigar to our family, and your congratulations still await.Seriously, my sweet Renee, I am anxious. I fear you may be eating your heart out in silence, afraid to cast a gloom over my sunshine. Write to me at once, naughty child! Tell me your life in every detail; tell me whether you still hold back, whether your "independence" stands erect, or has fallen on its knees, or is sitting comfortably, which would indeed be serious. Can you suppose the incidents of your married life are without interest for me? I muse over all you have said. Often, at the Opera, while absorbed in watching the dancers, I think, "It is half-past nine; perhaps she is in bed. What is she doing? Is she happy? Is she alone with her independence, or has it gone the way of other dead and cast-off independences?"A thousand loves.---Saucy girl! Why should I write? What could I say? While your life is varied by social festivities and the passions of love, mine is as monotonous as a convent.We go to bed at nine and rise with daybreak. Meals are served with maddening punctuality. Nothing ever happens. I have adjusted to this routine, which may be the nature of things. Where would the universe be without the fixed laws that govern it?I have imposed certain rules of dress, which occupy my mornings. I feel it is my duty as a wife to look charming, and it pleases both Louis and the good old man. After lunch, we walk. When newspapers arrive, I disappear to manage household affairs or read—often writing to you. I return an hour before dinner, and after, we play cards or receive visits. Thus, my days pass between a contented old man, who has done with passions, and the man who owes his happiness to me. Louis' happiness is so radiant that it has warmed my heart.For women, happiness cannot consist solely in satisfying desire. In the evenings, when I can relax, I imagine your life, its riches and passions, and wonder what such a stormy preface can lead to. May I not swallow the book itself? For you, my darling, love's illusions are possible; for me, only the facts of homely life remain. Your love seems a dream!I find it hard to understand why you insist on romanticizing it. Your ideal man must have more soul than fire, more nobility and self-command than passion. You demand sacrifices for the pleasure of rewarding them, testing Felipe to see if desire, hope, and curiosity endure. But behind your stage scenery lies the altar where everlasting bonds are forged. The day after your marriage, the graceful structure raised by your strategy may fall before the reality that transforms a girl into a woman, a gallant into a husband. Remember, there is no exemption for lovers. For them, as for ordinary folk like Louis and me, the great "Perhaps" of Rabelais lurks beneath wedding rejoicings.I do not blame you for your rashness in speaking with Felipe in the garden or spending a night with him; but you play with life, and I fear it may turn the tables. I dare not give you the counsel my experience suggests, but let me repeat from my valley that the viaticum of married life lies in resignation and self-sacrifice. Despite your tests, I see that marriage will mean to you what it has been to me. The greater the passion, the steeper the precipice we have hewn for our fall—that is the only difference.Oh, how I wish to see the Baron de Macumer and talk with him! Your happiness lies so near my heart.---As Felipe has generously acknowledged my fortune, my mother has become even friendlier, calling me sly-boots and a woman of the world."But, dear mama," I said before the contract was signed, "you attribute cunning to what is truly the outcome of the most unselfish love! I assure you I am not the 'woman of the world' you think.""Come, Armande," she said, drawing me close, "you did not want to return to the convent or die an old maid. Like a noble Chaulieu, you recognized the need to build up your father's family." I glanced at my father, who was listening, and said, "I love Felipe de Soria with all my soul. Though this love sprang up without my knowledge, I swear I never yielded until I recognized in him a character worthy of mine.""But, my dear," she interrupted, "he is as ugly as...""As anything you like," I retorted, "but I love his ugliness.""If you love him, Armande," my father said, "and can master your love, do not risk your happiness. Happiness in marriage depends largely on the first days—""Days only?" my mother interrupted. "You had better leave us to talk.""You are to be married in three days," the Duchess began, "so I must give you the serious advice every mother owes her daughter. You are marrying a man you love, and there is no reason for pity. I have only known you for a year, but it is long enough to love you. You have gratified maternal pride and shown yourself a sweet daughter. I promise you will always find a staunch friend in me.""You smile? Alas! it often happens that a mother who has lived well with her daughter as a girl comes to cross purposes with her as women.""I want your happiness, so listen. The love you feel is that of a young girl, natural to us all, for it is a woman's destiny to cling to a man. Unfortunately, there is but one man for a woman! Sometimes, the man fate has marked for us is not the one we choose as husband. If we cannot love the man we have chosen, the fault lies with both or circumstances beyond our control. Yet, there is no reason why the man chosen by our family should not be the one we can love. The barriers that arise between husband and wife often stem from a lack of perseverance on both sides. Transforming a husband into a lover is as delicate as making a lover into a husband, which you have just proved marvelously successful."Your happiness is my object. Never forget that the first three months of marriage may bring misery if you do not submit to the yoke with the same forbearance, tenderness, and intelligence you showed during courtship. You have indulged in the innocent pleasures of clandestine love. If the culmination of your love begins with disappointment, come and tell me. Do not hope for too much from marriage; it may bring more discomfort than joy. The happiness of your life requires as much patient cherishing as the early shoots of love."If you lose the lover, you will find in his place the father of your children. This is the secret of social life. Sacrifice everything to the man whose name you bear, whose honor cannot suffer without involving you in frightful consequences. Such sacrifice is not only a duty for women of our rank; it is also wise policy. This is the mark of great moral principles—they hold good from every aspect. But I need say no more."I fancy you are jealous, and if you knew how jealous I am! But do not be foolish about it. To publish your jealousy is like playing politics with your cards on the table; those who let their game be seen learn nothing of their opponents. Whatever happens, we must know how to suffer in silence."She added that she intended to have a serious talk with Macumer the evening before the wedding.I kissed her hand, dropping a tear, moved by her words. In her advice, I saw high principles worthy of us both, true wisdom, and a tenderness unspoiled by society's narrow code. Above all, I saw she understood my character. Her words summed up the lessons life had taught her, perhaps at a heavy price."Dear little girl, you've got a nasty crossing before you. Most women, in their ignorance or disenchantment, are as wise as the Earl of Westmoreland!"We laughed, but I must explain the joke. A Russian princess had told us of this gentleman, who suffered from sea-sickness and turned back from Italy upon hearing of "crossing" the Alps. "Thank you; I've had quite enough crossings already," he said.You will understand, Renee, that your gloomy philosophy and my mother's lecture revived fears that had disturbed us at Blois. As marriage approached, I needed all my strength, resolution, and affection to face this passage from maidenhood to womanhood. All our conversations returned to my mind, and I re-read your letters, sensing a vague sadness.This anxiety had one advantage; it helped me adopt the regulation expression for a bride. Consequently, on the day of signing the contract, everyone said I looked charming and quite right. This morning, at the Mairie, it was informal, with only witnesses present.I write this tail to my letter while they prepare my dress for dinner. We shall be married at midnight at the Church of Sainte-Valere, after a very gay evening. I confess my fears give me a martyr-like air to which I have no right, but which will be admired—why, I cannot conceive. I am delighted to see that poor Felipe is as timid as I; society grates on him, and he feels like a bat in a glass shop."Thank Heaven, the day won't last forever!" he whispered innocently.In his bashfulness, he wished for no one there.The Sardinian ambassador presented me with a pearl necklace linked with six splendid diamonds—a gift from my sister-in-law, the Duchess de Soria. Along with it was a sapphire bracelet engraved with the words, "Though unknown, beloved." I could not accept these without consulting Felipe."For," I said, "I would not want you to wear ornaments from anyone but me."He kissed my hand, moved, and replied, "Wear them for the inscription and the sincere feeling."Here, then, my poor Renee, are the last words of your girl friend. After the midnight Mass, we set off for an estate Felipe bought in Nivernais, on the way to Provence. My name is now Louise de Macumer, but I leave Paris in a few hours as Louise de Chaulieu. However I am called, there will always be but one Louise for you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I have not written to you, dear, since our marriage nearly eight months ago, and not a line from you! You are inexcusable.We set off in a post-chaise for the Castle of Chantepleurs, which Macumer has bought in Nivernais, on the Loire, sixty leagues from Paris. Our servants, except my maid, were there before us; we arrived the next evening after a rapid journey. I slept from Paris to beyond Montargis, my head on Felipe's shoulder. His tenderness moved me; he kissed my forehead twice and looked at me with unchanging passion.At Briare we breakfasted in the carriage, talking like in our old Blois days and admiring the Loire. By half-past seven we entered the beautiful avenue to Chantepleurs, dined at eight, and retired to a charming Gothic bedroom, made comfortable with modern luxuries. Felipe, often thought ugly, seemed beautiful to me in his kindness. He felt like an old friend, though he later confessed to storms of passion hidden beneath a calm exterior."So far, I have found nothing terrible in marriage," I said, gazing at the moonlit park."Why fear it?" he replied. "Have I ever proved false to my promise? Why should I in the future?"His words and glances had mastery and thrilled my heart. I thought of duty and the unfair constraints placed on a woman's delicate feelings. What force seeks to crush a woman's sensitivity under obligation? Love should guard its own rights and bloom freely under its warmth.Oh, my noble Renee! I understand you now. The woman who has not used marriage to legalize her heart's choice has only motherhood left to turn to. When earthly ties fail, the soul seeks the divine.A hard truth: only truly great men know how to love. They balance sensual and spiritual forces; weaker men confuse them. Great souls express instinct with grace, tempering passion with reverence. The depth of feeling follows the caliber of intellect; hence only men of genius possess a woman's delicacy. When mind, heart, and senses unite in rapture, we briefly soar to heaven.This was the philosophy of my first three months of marriage. Felipe is angelic, another self; I can think aloud with him. His greatness surpasses my understanding, and our bond deepens with possession. I foresee years of marriage will only strengthen his affection and our connection. What joy!Pleasure invigorates me; its sunshine becomes part of my being. The intervals between ecstasies are like short nights between long summer days. What happy chance has given me such a destiny? My mother's fears proved unfounded; your fears and mine dissolved.We spent seven and a half months at Chantepleurs like runaway lovers in blissful solitude. One morning, in particular happiness, I reflected on my life and thought of your prosaic marriage. I grasped the meaning of your life. Oh, my sweet, why do we speak different languages? Your marriage of convenience and my love match are worlds apart. You walk the earth, while I soar in the heavens. Love crowned me queen; you reign by duty. My heights are so lofty that a fall would shatter me.But I will not dwell on that. I hesitate to describe the vibrant joy of love's springtime as we know it.For ten days we have been in Paris, staying in a lovely house on the Rue du Bac, prepared by the architect who decorated Chantepleurs. I have been listening to Rossini's divine music, which once soothed my restless heart. They say I am more beautiful now, and I take childish pleasure in being called "Madame."Friday morning.Renee, my dear, my happiness draws me back to you. I feel I can be more to you than ever. I have studied your married life alongside my own, and you emerge as great, noble, and splendid in your goodness. I declare myself your humble admirer and friend. My marriage has been a joy; I would have perished had it been otherwise. And you live! What feeds your heart? I will never mock you again. Mockery comes from ignorance; we jest at what we do not understand. "Recruits laugh where veterans are grave," said the Comte de Chaulieu.I suspect you have not told me everything. You hide wounds; I believe you suffer. From afar I wove romantic theories: perhaps you merely experimented with marriage, and what brought me happiness brought you pain. Your sacrifice seems unrewarded, wrapped in social axioms. If self-denial is noble, what of my joy, sanctified by the church and the mayor's seal? Is it unnatural?For the honor of the law and to complete my happiness, I long to see you content. Oh, tell me you see a dawn of love for Louis, who adores you! I return to this midday blaze, fearing it may consume me.Dear Renee, do you remember how you once said, "I love you so, Louise, that if God appeared to me, I would pray for all your sorrows to be mine"? I take up your prayer, imploring Heaven to grant you a share in my happiness.I have an idea. I suspect you are nurturing plans for Louis de l'Estorade. Very well; help him get elected deputy in the upcoming election. He will be nearly forty then, and the Chamber won't meet for six months, so he will qualify for a seat. You will come to Paris—wouldn't that be enough? My father and my friends will know and admire you. If your father-in-law consents to a family, we could secure the title of Comte for Louis. That would be something! And we shall be together.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, your letter left me dizzy with joy and a little envy. I held it at sunset, tears glistening, beneath a barren rock overlooking the Mediterranean where I’ve set climbers to one day cover it—a spot I call Louise's seat. It symbolizes my solitude yet reminds me I am not alone.Reading you stirred the child within me: a composite of warning, delight, pain, promise, and fulfillment that convinced me I will one day know laughter and love. I now understand my purpose: to bring forth life. The certainty of motherhood has turned my sacrifices into deep joy, surpassing any personal passion.For you, love reflects Felipe’s ardor; my love will return in my children. Your golden harvest may fade, but my delayed motherhood will be enduring and renewing. Love can be a beautiful gem taken from Nature, but motherhood is Nature’s purest joy. A smile has dried my tears; while love makes Louis happy, marriage has made me a mother, and in that I find happiness.I returned to my white grange to take these thoughts in. For five months the miracle of nature has been unfolding within me, yet I remain emotionally detached. My family is excited; I feel cold and hesitant to confess my true feelings—motherhood is still abstract.Louis was surprised, which shows how little the father's role seems to matter here. My doctor says childbirth is governed by chance, though children born of passionate love may be luckier. Perhaps there are joys for you I will never feel, since your love for Felipe differs from my sense of duty.These reflections accompany my expectation of family life, along with physical discomfort: nausea and odd cravings. While Louis brings home the finest oranges, I crave the half-rotten ones, finding them delicious despite their smell. This taste has eased my sickness and strengthened me, a natural process common to women.As I grow, I plan to stay on the grounds to avoid being seen. I wonder when the true sense of motherhood will begin and hope it won’t arrive with suffering.Farewell, my fortunate friend—your love story inspires me and I long for every detail. Stay faithful to our bond, and I promise to share everything.Yet after rereading your letter I felt dread; your happiness seems to challenge Providence and I fear calamity might take offense. Remember to pray and do good: religion has grown more important to me since my marriage. I hope you and Felipe still invoke God in your happiness. My concern comes from deep affection. You deserve happiness, and I cherish our bond. God bless you!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame,—My wife wishes you to hear first about an event that has brought us immense joy. Renée has given birth to a healthy boy; we are postponing his baptism until your return to Chantepleurs and sincerely hope you will come to La Crampade to be godmother to our firstborn, whom we have named Armand‑Louis de l’Estorade.Renée endured considerable pain but showed remarkable patience; the thought of bringing happiness to us all helped her through this trial. Without new‑father exaggeration, little Armand is a beautiful infant, resembling Renée in features and eyes, which promises intelligence.The physician assures us Renée is out of danger, and as she proves an excellent nurse—thanks to Nature—we freely express our joy. I hope this happiness, which has revitalized our home and transformed my wife’s life, will soon be yours as well.Renée has prepared a suite of rooms for you; while I wish they were more splendid, I trust our warm welcome will compensate for any lack of luxury.I thank you for your kind thoughts, so timely now. The birth of a grandson has reconciled my father to heavy sacrifices: he recently purchased two estates, and La Crampade now yields an annual rental of thirty thousand francs. He intends to seek the King’s permission to establish it as an entailed estate; if you could assist him in obtaining the title you mentioned, it would greatly benefit your godson.I will follow your suggestions to facilitate your reunion with Renée during the Chamber sessions. I am working diligently to become a specialist, and nothing would encourage me more than knowing you will take an interest in little Armand. We earnestly invite you to come and, with your beauty, grace, playful spirit, and noble heart, be a good fairy to my son. In doing so you will earn our everlasting gratitude.Your very humble, obedient servant,LOUIS DE L’ESTORADE.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################XXX. LOUISE DE MACUMER TO RENEE DE L'ESTORADEJanuary 1826.Macumer woke me with your husband's letter. Yes, we'll go to Chantepleurs at the end of April. I am delighted to travel, to see you, and to be godmother to your first child. I insist on having Macumer as godfather; sharing the role would be unbearable — if you could have seen the look he gave me, you would know how dearly he cherishes me.I look forward to visiting La Crampade; I may have a child there too. Can you picture me torn between you and an infant? If anything ever supplanted my place in your heart, I hardly know how I'd bear it — Medea's anger seems less extreme now!So: you have the fruit without the flowers, I the flowers without the fruit. Our lives contrast, yet we have the philosophy to endure. Ten months married is too soon to give up hope.We live happily, as happy people do. Society now calls the Baronne de Macumer prettier than Louise de Chaulieu—love is a wonderful cosmetic. Driving along the Champs-Élysées this January I reflected on how much has changed since last year.If I lack a mother's joys, you can share them with me and I will learn by sympathy. Still, I cannot imagine anything surpassing the rapture of love; often I think it would be better to die at thirty, preserved from later disappointment, than to carry a young heart in an aged body among cold faces. Felipe and I quarreled about this: I wanted him to have the strength to let me slip away at thirty so I might pass from one dream to another; he refused and turned pale. This distinguished statesman can be surprisingly childlike. Now that we share such confidences, we often surprise each other.Felipe and I would like to send you a gift, dear mother — something to bring you joy and to remind you of us. Tell me what you would like. I thought of a luncheon service with baby figures, since lunch is our happiest meal. Let me know soon; we must order it.Farewell, dear nursing mother. May you enjoy all the delights of motherhood. I eagerly await your first letter describing your experiences. Some parts of your husband's letter moved me; I will tell my godson how dearly he must love you. Much love, my sweet one.XXXI. RENEE DE L'ESTORADE TO LOUISE DE MACUMERIt has been nearly five months since the baby was born, and I have found no moment to write. When you become a mother, you will understand. Please write more often! Share your joys; I am happier than you can imagine.I went to the parish church for the Mass of recovery from childbirth, supported by both grandfathers. Kneeling before God, overwhelmed with gratitude, a single image remains: the Virgin on the altar seeming to bow and point to the infant Christ, who smiled at me. With a heart full of love, I held little Armand for the priest to bless, looking forward to his baptism.How sweet the word "child" is! In the last two months of pregnancy I wandered through the gardens, scarcely conscious of the burden I carried. Gloomy forebodings haunted me; my heart did not respond to the thought of the little one, though he reminded me with lively kicks. When labor came, I summoned all my strength and bore the agony; I sank into a stupor, feeling like two beings: my body in pain and my soul at peace. Pain formed a halo; at its climax I screamed, and then the newborn's cries silenced my suffering.They laid me back, and it felt like paradise despite exhaustion. Happy faces pointed to the child, and I cried out in terror, "It looks like a little monkey! Are you sure it's a child?" My mother, now my nurse, reassured me: "You've got the finest baby in the world. Don't worry; be a mother." I slept and left things to nature.Waking from those first days' pain was heavenly. A ray of light pierced the darkness, and my heart opened like a flower. The moment the little one took my breast was pure joy—this felt like the dawn of motherhood.Nursing is a miracle of transformation. The cries go straight to your heart; the child's eyes and lips express everything. What else matters? The father could be forgiven nothing that disturbed the baby. The bond between mother and child is profound; the joy of nurturing is unparalleled.At five months my little one has become the prettiest darling: he cries little and is content in our love. I strive to envelop him in affection.My feelings for Louis have changed into a tender fondness, perhaps surpassing romantic love: unselfish gratitude that lights the future and recalls fond memories. The old L'Estorade and his son have redoubled their devotion; each look from them brings me joy. Louis is ecstatic at having a son—he watches him sleep for hours—and the fear of childlessness has given way to renewed purpose; he works harder, driven by fatherhood.I grow happier by the hour. The bond between mother and child strengthens continually. This love is natural and enduring, unlike romantic passion, which can wane; a mother's love grows with the child's needs and combines passion, duty, and joy. Here a woman's true sphere unfolds, where self-sacrifice flourishes without jealousy.Society reinforces nature's instincts: a mother cherishes the child who opens these delights and teaches her the art of motherhood. The right of the eldest child, once part of natural order, should never have been questioned. A mother learns from her child, and her character deepens through these duties and pleasures; a woman without children is incomplete.Hasten, my sweetest, to fulfill your mission. Your happiness will multiply with mine.23rd.I had to leave you because my godson's cry reached me from the garden. I wanted to say farewell. Rereading this letter, I am surprised at how ordinary my feelings sound; yet every mother must have felt this way. I am as happy now as I once was miserable. This place is my land of promise—the desert is past. A thousand loves, darling. Write to me, for I can now read about your happy love without tears. Farewell.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Do you know, dear, it has been over three months since I last wrote? I must own I failed to answer your last letter, but you won’t mind?Macumer and I took your silence as consent about the baby-wreathed luncheon service; it’s now bound for Marseilles after six months’ work. When Felipe asked me to inspect it before packing, I realized we hadn’t exchanged a word since your letter that showed the heart of a mother.My sweet, I blame dreadful Paris—what is your excuse? Society here is a whirlpool. Didn’t I tell you one must conform? It drives out sentiment, monopolizes time, and can consume the heart. Moliere’s Celimene is the epitome of the society woman—timeless and apt.Without my breastplate—my love for Felipe—I should be lost. This morning I told him he is my salvation. Evenings are full of parties; nights my heart heals in passionate love.I dine at home only with friends; I spend one afternoon a week in the house—Wednesday. I have entered the social fray, competing with Mmes. d’Espard and de Maufrigneuse; my house is lively. I became fashionable for Felipe’s pleasure. My mornings are his; from four in the afternoon until two in the morning, I belong to Paris. Macumer is an admirable host—witty, dignified, courteous. No woman could help loving such a husband.My parents have gone to Madrid. With Louis XVIII gone, the Duchess obtained my father’s appointment as attaché. My brother, the Duc de Rhetore, now acknowledges me. My younger brother, the Comte de Chaulieu, owes me for spending my fortune to secure him an estate and a marriage with Mlle. de Mortsauf, an heiress. The King will confer the de Lenoncourt and de Givry titles on him to preserve their legacy. Mlle. de Mortsauf, sole heiress of the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, is expected to inherit over a hundred thousand livres a year. My father insisted the de Chaulieu arms be placed on the de Lenoncourt escutcheon, making my brother Duc de Lenoncourt. The young de Mortsauf is gravely ill, so the marriage waits until next winter, after mourning.My father’s plan has justified itself, earning me compliments and softening explanations of my marriage to society. The Prince de Talleyrand, kindly toward my grandmother, is now warm to Macumer. Society that once criticized me now admires me.I now reign where I was once insignificant. Macumer is envied as the husband of “the most charming woman in Paris.” Many women vie for that title; men murmur and gaze. This adulation flatters vanity—I see why women pay dearly for such fleeting privileges. This triumph is intoxicating; I understand how women become selfish and frivolous under admiration. Society’s demands consume your spirit; you give yourself for smiles and jealousy and receive only flattery. You know the cost, yet can’t escape the vortex.Ah, how I crave a true friend! Felipe’s love and devotion are my treasure, and my heart longs for you. We are joyfully preparing to move to Chantepleurs, where we can escape the farce of Paris. After rereading your letter, I realize no fashionable woman in Paris can be a good mother.Goodbye for now, dear. We will be at Chantepleurs for only a week and hope to see you around May 10th. We will meet again after more than two years! How we have changed—we are both matrons now, you in motherhood, I in love.If I haven’t written, it isn’t because I’ve forgotten you. How is my little godson? He must be over nine months now; I long to see his first steps, though Macumer says even precocious infants don’t walk at ten months.We will have delightful gossip and see if a child truly spoils a pattern.P.S. If you reply, address it to Chantepleurs. I’m just leaving.---My child, if you become a mother you’ll find it impossible to write during the first two months of nursing. Mary, my English nurse, and I are exhausted. I resolved to do everything myself—sewing baby clothes, embroidering caps. I am a slave, day and night.Master Armand-Louis eats whenever he pleases, and I must constantly change, wash, and dress him. I love watching him sleep, singing to him, and carrying him about, leaving little time for myself. Society has been to you what motherhood is to me!I cannot express how rich my life has become, and I long for your visit to see for yourself. I fear he will soon be teething, and you may find a fussy baby. So far he hasn’t cried much because I’m always attentive. Babies cry when their needs aren’t met, and I’m vigilant. My heart has expanded while yours shrinks under society’s demands. I eagerly await your arrival, wanting your thoughts on l’Estorade just as you’re curious about Macumer.Write to me from your last stop. The gentlemen want to meet our distinguished guests. Come, Queen of Paris, to our humble home, where love will greet you!---The name on this letter shows my petition has been granted: your father-in-law is now Comte de l’Estorade. I wouldn’t leave Paris until I secured this for you; I’m writing in the Keeper of the Seals’ presence—he has just told me the patent is signed.Goodbye for now.---I am ashamed that my sudden departure will surprise you. But I must be honest: I am horribly jealous.Felipe’s eyes rested too often on you. Your little talks at the foot of your rock tortured me, and I became irritable. Your beauty reminded him of his homeland and Marie Heredia; I can be jealous of the past too. Your magnificent features stabbed me in the heart. I tried to enhance my appearance, but everything paled beside you.Felipe doted on the child, whom I began to resent. Yes, I confess—I wanted that exuberance of life for myself. I saw regret in Macumer’s eyes, and I wept for two nights. I was miserable in your home. You are too beautiful and triumphant for me to endure.You complained of your lot, but L’Estorade is presentable, charming, and likely to become a deputy. His exile has given him a calm, dignified air, useful for a politician. Macumer, too, should achieve high place.Having satisfied myself of your happiness, I went to Chantepleurs, where Felipe must pursue his aspirations. I won’t receive you there without a fine baby to match yours.I know I deserve your scorn. I am a fool, a wretch—jealousy is a poor virtue. I am not angry with you, but I was miserable; you will forgive my escape. Two more days and I might have made a scene.Despite my jealousy, I am glad to have seen you in the pride of motherhood. Even in Marseilles I feel proud of you as a splendid mother.You are born for motherhood; I am not. A good mother and a passionate wife both require intelligence and grace. I watched you and admired you. Your children will be happy, not spoiled, by your tenderness and reason.Tell Louis the truth about my departure, but find a decent excuse for your father-in-law and family. Felipe doesn’t know why I left, and he never will. If he asks, I’ll fabricate a pretext—perhaps that you were jealous of me. Forgive my little fib.Goodbye. I write in haste; the postilion waits.Kisses to my dear godson. Come to Chantepleurs in October: I’ll be alone while Macumer is in Sardinia planning estate improvements. That seems to calm him; sharing plans with me unsettles him. Goodbye!---Dear sister, we were astonished at lunch to learn you had left, especially after your letter. You were happy; there was no reason for such objections. My sentence: you return at my first summons. Your letter didn’t say your next stop, so I address this to Chantepleurs.Listen, dear sister of my heart. My focus is your happiness. Your husband commands respect—not only a grave exterior but a revealed power and a fire in his eyes. He has been Prime Minister and adores you, which makes him a profound dissembler. Extracting secrets from such a diplomatic soul requires skill.You know my role is reason while yours is imagination. I am a simple country vicountess, ambitious for family prosperity. Macumer, the Duc de Soria, has a name, and you, a duchess by right, reign in Paris, where it’s hard to maintain power. You have a considerable fortune, which may double if Macumer develops his Sardinian estates. If anyone has the right to be jealous, it’s not you. Thankfully, we both have generous hearts that place our friendship above pettiness.I know you must feel ashamed of fleeing, but your flight won’t spare you from my words. Read carefully; this concerns you more than Macumer.Firstly, you do not love him as a wife should. In two years you may tire of adoration. You will never see Felipe as a husband; he will always be a lover to you. You do not revere him as a woman should her god. I have studied love, and this is not love.You cannot escape the fate of all queens. One day you will long to be treated as a light-o’-love—to be swept off your feet by a strong man who won’t worship you. Macumer loves you too much to resist or criticize. Sooner or later you will scorn this excessive devotion. He spoils you, as I spoiled you at the convent; you are enchanting, and there’s no escaping your charms.Worse, you are candid; our happiness often relies on social hypocrisies you won’t embrace. Society won’t tolerate a wife’s power over her husband. Convention demands that a man not show himself as lover to his wife, nor may a married woman act as a mistress. You both disregard this.The unpardonable sin in society is to be happy; if happiness exists, it must remain hidden. More importantly, equality between lovers shouldn’t appear in marriage, or it undermines social order. It is painful to see a man become a nonentity, and worse to see a capable man reduced to that. You will reduce Macumer to a shadow; your household will lack balance. You will regret it, but by then it may be too late. Women often lack the qualities essential to family life. Macumer senses this; his Sardinia visit suggests he hopes to regain himself.You use the power his love gives you. Your position shows in your gestures and tone. You are the wanton your mother called you! Have you ever heard me contradict Louis? I respect his public authority. If I want to advise him, I wait for private moments; I never assume the role of mentor. If I didn’t present myself as submissive publicly, he would lose confidence. The good we do for others is spoiled unless we erase ourselves so they feel no inferiority. There is sweetness in these hidden sacrifices; it is a triumph when you praise Louis.This is my counsel. Right now, you love Felipe not as a husband but as a reflection of yourself. Your father’s words ring true: beneath your passion lies a selfishness typical of a great lady. How I must love you to speak such bitter truths!If you promise not to tell the Baron, I’ll share the end of our talk. We praised you, and he soon sensed my sisterly love. I ventured, “Louise has never struggled with life; fortune has spoiled her, and she might pay for this if you weren’t there to be father and lover.”“Ah! but is it possible?” he broke off—enough for me. If you had stayed, he would have spoken more freely.Think of the day when your husband’s strength wanes, when pleasure turns to satiety, and he feels diminished before you. The stings of conscience will awaken remorse, painful for you, as you’ll feel responsible and end up despising the man you failed to respect. Remember, scorn can become hatred. You are too noble to forget Felipe’s sacrifices, but what more can he give when he has already given himself fully? Woe to those who leave no desire unsatisfied—women can be insatiable in love.Oh, Louise, change while there’s still time. If you treated Macumer as I treat l’Estorade, you could awaken the hero within him. Wouldn’t you feel pride in using your power to elevate a gifted man, as I do with one of lesser stature?Had you stayed I would have told you this in person, but I know you will read and weigh my warnings. You have everything to be happy; don’t spoil your chances. Return to Paris as soon as Macumer does. The demands of society, which I complain about, are necessary; otherwise you’ll be lost in self-absorption. A married woman shouldn’t be too generous with herself; a mother should reserve sacred hours for herself—children should not take her presence for granted.Farewell, my dear jealous soul! Many women would be flattered by your pangs, but I can only mourn, for what is not mother in me is your dear friend. A thousand loves. Make whatever excuse you must for leaving; if you doubt Macumer, I am sure of Louis.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My beloved,— I was eager to explore Italy and am glad I brought Macumer, since his Sardinia plans are postponed.This country enchants; the churches could make any Protestant yearn for Catholicism. Macumer has been warmly received—everyone delights in his presence. Felipe could secure the Sardinian embassy in Paris for me; I'm well regarded.Send letters to Florence. No time for details—I'll tell you in Paris. We'll stay a week, then go to Florence, stopping at Leghorn; month in Tuscany, another in Naples, aiming for Rome in November. We'll return via Venice—two weeks in December—and reach Paris through Milan and Turin in January.The journey feels like a honeymoon; discovering new places renews our passion. Macumer, never before in Italy, began on the Cornice road, as if by fairies.Goodbye, darling. Don't be upset if I don't write—I'm consumed with seeing and will write when I can reflect.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear,—a full reply to your letter from Marseilles awaits you at Chantepleurs. This honeymoon heightens my concerns; send it from Nivernais.Rumor says the government will dissolve Parliament—unlucky for the Crown, the last session would have strengthened it. This worries us; Louis will not be forty until the end of 1827. My father will stand and resign at the proper moment.Your godson has learned to walk unaided; he is delightful, thoughtful, and his smiles meaningful. I nursed him and will wean Armand in December; a year seems enough—longer nursing, they say, dulls. You must be making an impression in Italy, my fair one with golden locks. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Your dreadful letter has reached me, forwarded by my steward as I asked. Oh, Renée — I will spare you unnecessary complaint and tell you only what it produced.We had just returned from a reception given by the ambassador, where I shone and Macumer was utterly captivated. Then I read your harsh reply to my letter and sobbed. My dear Arab threw himself at my feet, calling you mad. He led me to our balcony; speaking Italian, his declarations were like poetry. He swore that even if your predictions were true, he would not trade one moment of our love for a lifetime of convention—he would choose our love and death over a dull existence with children.While he whispered, a bat's cry startled us and I fainted. I am better this morning, though that sound haunts me. Upon waking I knelt before Felipe, looked into his eyes, and confessed: "My love, I may be naive, and Renée might be right. Perhaps I love only your love, but this is the only feeling in my heart. If anything about me disturbs you, tell me. I want to follow your wishes." Macumer was too choked with emotion to answer.I thank you, Renée; your letter revealed the depths of Macumer's love. Rome is the city of love, where passion should flourish alongside art and faith.In Venice we shall meet the Duc and Duchesse de Soria. If you write, address Paris, as we leave Rome in three days after the ambassador's farewell.P.S. Dear, naive child, your letter shows you know love only in theory. Love is a force that yields diverse fruits beyond any theory's grasp.My father has been elected to the Chamber, my father-in-law has died, and I am near my second confinement; these mark the year’s end. I mention them first to spare you the fright of the black seal.Your letter from Rome chilled me. You and Felipe behave like children. Either he is a deceitful diplomat, or his love is like a man’s for a courtesan—lavishing affection while knowing she is unfaithful. Enough. You say I rave, so I will hold my tongue: from our differing fates I draw a harsh lesson—do not love if you wish to be loved.When Louis joined the provincial Council he received the Legion of Honor cross. Nearly three years on, my father asks the rank of Officer for his son-in-law. I would value your influence with the right person, but do not entangle yourself in my father's affairs. The Comte de Maucombe seeks a marquisate for himself; reserve efforts for me. When Louis becomes a deputy next winter, we will be in Paris to secure him a government post so we may live on his salary. My father sits between center and right; a title will satisfy him. Our family was noble even under King René, and Charles X will likely grant a Maucombe title. My fear is my father using this to favor my younger brother—if a marquisate is dangled, he will focus on himself.January 15th.Ah, Louise, I have been through hell. I tell you because you are like a sister; still, I doubt I shall ever relive those five ghastly days. The word "convulsions" sickens me. Five days felt like five centuries. A mother who has not suffered this cannot comprehend it.The night before, the weather was close; I thought Armand affected by teething. Usually sweet, he grew peevish, crying and breaking his toys. I put him to bed beside me. Toward morning he cried "Mamma!" in a strange voice and twitched. I rose for a drink; as I handed him the cup he was motionless, repeating "Mamma!" in a voice hardly a voice. His hand was stiff. He gulped water convulsively, clung to me, eyes rolling back, limbs rigid. I screamed for Louis."A doctor! Quick!... he is dying," I cried.Louis vanished. Armand gasped "Mamma!" then lost consciousness. Veins on his forehead swelled, and convulsions began. For an hour before doctors arrived I held my lifeless baby—my pride and joy. His eyes! I cannot think of them without horror. My pretty Armand was a mere shell—black, shriveled.Two doctors from Marseilles hovered like birds of ill omen. One blamed brain fever, the other called them ordinary convulsions. Our country doctor offered no opinion. "Teething," said one; "fever," said the other. They agreed on leeches to the neck and ice to the head. To see a child like that—purple, silent—was horrible. They even cut his curls for the ice. The convulsions returned every ten minutes; the child writhed, white then violet, limbs clattering like wood. This unconscious flesh had smiled and said "Mamma!"—the thought pierced my soul.After four days and nights, the doctors recommended a dreadful ointment to create open sores. Sores on my Armand! Five days earlier he had been playing and laughing. I refused, trusting nature. Louis, who believes doctors, scolded me. But in those moments when the disease mimicked death, I felt that remedy might save him. His skin was dry and rough; the ointment would not work. I wept; the doctors were at dinner!Alone with him, I stripped off the medical appliances, pressed him to my bosom, and laid my forehead against his, begging God to spare him. For minutes I held him, longing to die with him. Then his limbs relaxed; the writhings ceased; the ghastly colors faded. I screamed; the doctors rushed in."He is saved!" cried the oldest doctor.What music those words were! Two hours later Armand returned to life. I was crushed, saved only by the joy of his recovery. What tortures bind a mother to her child! Must the nails be driven to fasten him to my heart? Was I not mother enough? I who wept at his first words and steps—why these horrors?Now Armand plays and laughs. What causes this dreadful disease in children? I ponder it, especially as I am again with child. Is it teething? A brain disorder? A nervous susceptibility? Our country doctor blames nervous trouble from teething. I would give every tooth in my head to see Armand's through. One white pearl peeking from swollen gums brings a cold sweat. The heroism with which my angel bore suffering proves he is his mother’s son. A look from him pierces me.Medical science offers no clear explanation for this tetanus that appears and vanishes swiftly, unpreventable and uncured. One thing is certain: it is hell for a mother to see her child in convulsions. How passionately I hold him now! I could walk forever with him in my arms!To have endured this six weeks before my confinement made it worse; I feared for the coming child. Farewell, my beloved. Do not wish for a child—this is the essence of my letter!Poor dear, Macumer and I forgave you upon hearing of your terrible trouble. I felt pain reading of your double agony; there seem compensations in being childless.I write to tell you Louis has been promoted; he may now wear the ribbon of an Officer of the Legion. You are fortunate, Renée—likely to have a little girl, as you wished!My brother's marriage to Mlle. de Mortsauf was celebrated on our return. Our gracious King, who is extraordinarily kind, has granted my brother the reversion of the post of first gentleman of the chamber, presently held by his father-in-law, on condition the Mortsauf scutcheon be placed alongside the Lenoncourt’s."The office ought to go with the title," he told the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry.My father is justified a hundredfold. Without my fortune none of this would have happened. My parents came from Madrid for the wedding and will return after the reception I am hosting for the newlyweds.The carnival will be lively. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria are in Paris, which unnerves me. Marie Heredia is one of Europe’s most beautiful women, and I dislike the way Felipe looks at her. Therefore, I am extra sweet and affectionate. Every look and gesture says what my lips do not: "She could not love like this!" Heaven knows how lovely I am! Yesterday Mme. de Maufrigneuse said, "Dear child, who can compete with you?"I keep Felipe entertained, making his sister-in-law seem dull by comparison. I am less sorry that a little Abencerrage is not on the way, since the Duchess will likely remain in Paris for her confinement and lose her beauty. If the baby is a boy, he will be named Felipe in honor of the exile. Fate decrees I shall be godmother again.Goodbye, dear. I plan to go to Chantepleurs early this year: our Italian tour was shockingly expensive. I will leave around the end of March to economize in Nivenais. Besides, I am tired of Paris. Felipe and I long for the tranquility of the park, cool meadows, and the sparkling Loire. Chantepleurs will be delightful after Italy; splendor becomes tiresome, and a lover's gaze is more beautiful than any opera or painting!We expect you there. Do not fear my jealousy. You are free to test Macumer's heart and expose his doubts. I am utterly indifferent. Since that day in Rome Felipe's love for me has deepened. He just told me (looking over my shoulder) that his sister-in-law, Princess Heredia, his childhood dream, has no brains. Oh, my dear, I am worse than a ballet dancer! If you knew the joy that remark brought me! I pointed out her faulty French—she says "esemple" for "exemple," "sain" for "cinq," "cheu" for "je." She is beautiful but lacks charm and wit. When praised she seems puzzled. Felipe could not have lived two months with Marie after marriage. Don Fernand, the Duc de Soria, suits her perfectly: generous but a spoiled child. I am tempted to be mischievous and make you laugh, but I will refrain. Much love, darling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My little girl is two months old, Jeanne-Athenais; my mother and Louis' grand-uncle are her godparents. I will visit Chantepleurs soon, since you won't mind. Your godson now says Matoumer, mispronouncing c. He's fully teethed, eats meat, and scurries like a mouse. I watch him anxiously, miserable I can't keep him near through my slow recovery. Doctors insist on precautions; childbearing discomforts persist. But don't tell Felipe—this little girl resembles me and may outshine your Armand.My father noticed Felipe thin; you don't seem as blooming either. The Duc and Duchesse de Soria have left—no reason for jealousy. Is something troubling you you're not sharing? Your letter is shorter and less affectionate—another whim?I'm writing too much; my nurse is upset and Mlle. Athenais is hungry. Farewell; please write long letters.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Renée,For the first time I find myself truly alone and in tears. I sat beneath a willow by the Chantepleurs marsh, a pretty scene that feels desolate without the sound of children. Nearly three years of marriage and no child; your household full of little ones makes me survey my own heart. I would brave any suffering, even what you endured at my godson’s birth, to hold a cherub of my own—an Athenais of my own! I imagine her looks and sweetness, yet you tell me nothing—just like you, Renée. I am sure you feel my trouble.Each disappointment drives me into long fits of melancholy, from which I compose mournful elegies. When will I embroider caps and sew lace for a tiny head? Will I never hear “Mamma” or have a little despot tug at my dress? Will my gravel be untouched by carriage wheelmarks, my courtyard free of broken toys? I long to watch a life unfold—another Felipe, only dearer; a son to become my second self. My park and château seem cold and empty. A woman without children feels unnatural; we exist to be mothers. Barrenness is a bleak fate. My life resembles some pastoral picture, meant to be invaded by the wolf—if I cannot be a mother, I must at least indulge in romantic sorrow.I told my Moor of this, and he wept; he cannot bear jokes on his love. Sometimes I dream of pilgrimage, to carry my longings to some shrine or spring. Next winter I shall seek a physician. I am too angry with myself to write more. Farewell.---A whole year has passed with no letter—what does it mean? I am a little hurt. Is your Louis, who visits so often, a sufficient substitute? It is not enough to know you are well; I want your feelings and thoughts. Your silence and seclusion in the country while your husband gains Parliamentary honors alarm me. His eloquence has secured him favor, and he will likely receive a fine post when the session ends. Are you spending your time writing him counsel?Why did you not come to Paris? I could have enjoyed your company for four months. Louis told me you would fetch him and have your third confinement in Paris—what a monstrous Gigogne of a mother! After pestering Louis with questions, I learned his grand-uncle, Athenais’ godfather, is very ill; perhaps you, as a careful mother, wish to secure a legacy from your husband’s last relative. Rest easy, Renée; we are all working for Louis. Martignac will likely place him in the audit department. But if you will not tell me why you hide in the country, I shall be cross.Are you afraid that the political wisdom of the house of l’Estorade should appear to center in you? Or is it the uncle’s legacy? Perhaps you fear you would be less to your children in Paris? Or are you simply too vain to show yourself in the metropolis while in this condition? Vain thing! Farewell.---My dear friend, have you forgotten my two little brown heads, my subjects and tyrants? I could not drag a four-year-old and an almost three-year-old to Paris while expecting again. I would not trouble you or disturb your husband with such a tumult. I detest hotel life. When I come to Paris for the session it will be to my own house: Louis’s uncle, pleased with his grand‑nephew’s prospects, gave me two hundred thousand francs to buy a house near you, and my mother sent thirty thousand for furnishings. I have charged Louis with finding a place; I will do my best not to disgrace you.I appreciate all you have done for Louis at court; yet I do not want him placed too conspicuously. I prefer the Audit Office for its steadiness rather than a post that would commit him too much. Our affairs are in good hands; once the steward understands the details, I will support Louis.As for writing long letters—I cannot. One day is like another here: the children are either well or ill, and that sets the tone of all my hours. In this solitary grange, minutes can become hours depending on their health. My delightful hours come only when they sleep and I am no longer needed to rock or soothe them. When they lie beside me, I think, “Nothing can go wrong now.” Yet every mother is haunted by imagined dangers: razors, fires, ponds, the demon of convulsions. We spend our days on a stage of small dramas, tender and terrible.At night I dream of their future, comforted by the watch of their guardian angel. Sometimes Armand calls in his sleep; I kiss his forehead and his sister’s feet and watch their beauty. Once a night the angel roused me to Athenais’ cradle—her head was too low and Armand’s feet purple with cold. How vital it is to have one’s children at night! A nurse cannot soothe them as a mother can after a nightmare. I have learned to sleep lightly, my ear tuned to every sigh or rustle by their bed.Morning brings their chatter. Nais strains to reach me while Armand climbs up like a little monkey; they transform my bed into a playground and I am at their mercy. The baby tugs my hair and Armand guards my breast like a jealous sentinel. Their antics replace sleep; their laughter repays all care.Then the dressing begins: dressing gown, apron, sleeves rolled up, and Mary’s help in a series of baths and scrubbings. Bath time is a peril of its own—so much crying results from a misjudged temperature or a dropped brush. Amusement is necessary—paper boats and glass ducks—to get them to submit to the bathtub. I contrive all sorts of diversions: prayers, scoldings, promises; motherhood becomes a game of cleverness matched to a child’s cunning. Our triumphs are private: Mary and I rejoice when, after a struggle, the children are at last clean and content. Only God, His angels, and perhaps you can grasp the satisfaction of such small victories.English women excel at arranging material comforts; their nurses and systems are admirable. My children will always be bare-legged in summer and in woolen socks in winter; no swaddling or tight bandages for them. A true mother, though, is never free; the helplessness of many French infants gives nurses a freedom their charge’s mothers would not allow. That is why I do not write: besides managing the estate, I am raising two children, with a third on the way.Motherhood demands silent self‑denial and attention to minute details—a mother blows on the spoon for her child’s soup, minces the chop, endures the annoyances. No servant or foreign nurse can replace a mother in those daily contests where gentleness must soothe every little grief. Louise, caring for these darlings, engages my whole soul. A crying child condemns the nurse or mother unless pain is natural. With two to tend (and soon three), they occupy all my thoughts—sometimes even you, whom I adore, recede to a memory.Often my dressing is not finished until mid-afternoon. I distrust mothers whose rooms are immaculate and who always look perfect. Yesterday, in a lovely April, I planned an outing with the children—an epic for a mother. I had laid out Armand’s little black velvet jacket, a new collar, a Scotch cap; Nais in white and pink with a baby cap. The little heads were to be brushed; Armand’s curls and Nais’ soft border of hair were arranged; my heart was in my mouth. Once outside, proud of my work, I admired Armand’s princely air as he helped Nais along the path. Then a carriage approached and in our scramble the children tumbled into a puddle, wrecking my preparations. We had to return and change: a crying baby and a soaked child leave the mother with no thought for herself.At dinner I have oft achieved nothing. Feeding two, securing bibs, turning up sleeves, cajoling food—these recurring cares, joys, and catastrophes make me neglect my own tidiness. To write you I have let them cut pictures from novels and arrange my silks; they are absorbed and content.Yet I have nothing to complain of. My children are robust and inventive; they amuse themselves more readily than you might think. A little freedom and a few pebbles, shells, and sand do more for them than many toys. Armand talks to flowers, flies, and chickens; he imitates and admires insects and asks “why” about everything. It is touching to see his curiosity—he even thinks of you, his godmother, as a fairy.Let me not sadden you with all my joys. A poor man once followed us begging—beggars know a mother with a child cannot resist. Armand, innocent of hunger or money, offered the beggar the trumpet I had bought him: “Here, take this!” That moment of spontaneous charity was incomparable. The poor man, moved, kept it, saying, “I too, madame, have had children.” I wept at the sweetness.I shudder at the thought of Armand going to school: I have only three and a half years before public institutions tear him away. The bright flowers of his sunny childhood will be cut by the scythe of education; his gracious candor may be lost. What will they do to the thinking being he is, to the curls I brushed and kissed so often? And you—are you still in love with Felipe? I have no fear of the Saracen. Goodbye; Nais has tumbled, and this letter threatens to become a volume.---My sweet, tender Renée, you will have learned from the papers of the terrible calamity that has crushed me. I could not write for twenty days; I did not leave his bedside. I received his last breath, closed his eyes, kept vigil with the priests, and said the prayers for the dead. The pangs I suffered felt like a just punishment; yet when I saw the calm smile on his lips, his final farewell to me, how could I think I had caused his death?He is gone and I am bereft. To you, who knew us both, what more need be said? I would give my share of Heaven to bring him back even for a second. Will you not come soon and soothe me with such impossible promises? You told me beforehand that he would suffer through me. Was it so? Yes; I had no right to all his love. Like a thief I took what belonged to him and in my frenzied grasp crushed the life out of my bliss. The madness is over, but I feel alone and crushed. Merciful God! what torture can surpass the misery in that single word: alone?When they took him away I lay on the same bed hoping to die. There was but a door between us and I thought I could force it. I was too young for death; after forty days of cruel care and attention, I was slowly brought back to life. Now I sit in the country by a window, surrounded by the flowers he had loved and made flourish, looking at the view he admired. Ah, Renée, how new surroundings wound when the heart is dead. His flowers torment me; to walk in my garden is to walk upon him. When I first ventured out I trembled and could not move.My parents are in Spain, my brothers far off, and you detained in the country. But two angels of mercy comfort me: the Duc and Duchesse de Soria hurried to their brother in his illness and have been everything I could wish. The last nights found us gathered in mute grief around this great man—rare in any age, distinguished by mind and heart. Felipe’s calm resignation was angelic. His last words to me were simple and frank about his will and his love; he blessed me on his lips and spoke of my memory more fondly than any other. His brother forgave him, knowing what love is.I owe my life to my brother-in-law and his wife; they wish to take me to Spain. To no one but you can I freely pour out my grief. A sense of my own faults sits heavy: my demands, jealousy, and anxious unrest may have worn him down. My love was dangerous because we were so alike in sensitivity; my careless words pierced him to the heart. You cannot imagine how submissive he was: I only had to tell him to leave me alone, and he obeyed without a murmur. His last breath blessed me, saying that a single morning with me was worth more than a lifetime with another. My tears fall as I write.This is my life now: rising at midday, going to bed early, lingering over meals, standing motionless before a plant, gazing into the trees, finding significance in trifles. I seek shade, silence, and night. I drag through each hour, taking a gloomy pride in surviving them. My park gives me company enough; everything there echoes my vanished joy, visible only to me.If I still live it is because Heaven tempers grief to the strength of those who bear it. Only a woman can know what it is to lose a love that was genuine—one that satisfied spirit and flesh. How rare a man so gifted that worship does not feel debasing! If such fortune befalls us once, we cannot hope to find it again. Men of true greatness, veiled by poetic grace, charm with a high spiritual power—beware of love: it may ruin you and the woman you love. That is my cry as I walk the paths of my wood.And he has left me no child! That love, rich in smiles and tenderness, left no fruit. I feel cursed. Is it that intense, single-hearted passion bears no children? Is only a marriage of reason blessed with progeny? Does Heaven envy our passions? Such wild questions haunt me.You are the one person whose company I could bear. Come to me; none but you should help Louise in her sorrow. The day I first donned my widow’s bonnet—when I first saw myself in black—I fell back and wept until night. I weep again at the memory. Bring your children; you can nurse your baby here without making me jealous; what is gone is gone, and I will be glad to see my godson. Felipe wished for a child like little Armand. Come, then—come and help me bear this woe.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My darling,—By the time you read this I will be near; I leave. We'll be alone, for Louis must stay in Provence to seek re-election against Liberals.I come to comfort and bring my heart to yours to help you. Weep—through tears you'll find the joy of reuniting with him. He is journeying to Heaven; each duty weakens the chain between you.In my arms you will lift your head and move toward him—pure, noble, free of errors, bearing the good you will do in his name.I write amid preparations, interrupted by the children and Armand, who keeps calling, "Godmother, godmother! I want to see her," making me jealous—he could be your child!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Yes, Renée, it is true: I have sold my house, Chantepleurs, and the farms in Seine-et-Marne, but I am neither mad nor ruined.After settling everything I have about twelve hundred thousand francs from my late husband’s fortune. I invested a million in the Three per Cents at fifty, which now yields sixty thousand francs instead of the thirty thousand the property gave. My former life was tedious—six months in the country managing leases and impatient farmers, the rest in Paris plagued by delays and legal troubles. For a twenty-seven-year-old widow it was intolerable.Now my fortune is secured with the State. Instead of paying taxes, I receive thirty thousand francs every six months from the Treasury, handed to me by a pleasant clerk. If the nation failed I should still be as well off as before, and I have time to save a little capital. Besides, the Comte de l’Estorade is a peer of this semi-republic and a friend at Court, so I have no cause for anxiety.What drove me to these calculations? Love.I must tell you plainly why I acted as I did. I am to be married in a village near Paris. I love and am loved. Forgive me for keeping this from you and de l’Estorade; I wished to spare you both anxiety. You know my jealousy; I thought secrecy kinder.The man I love had thirty thousand francs of debts, which I have paid. You may suspect an adventurer, but I have investigated him. He has courted me nearly two years. I am twenty-seven and he twenty-three—yes, a wide gap. He is a poet, who has lived on very little and spent more time dreaming than making a living. Practical people think artists unreliable; I understand that prejudice.But my love is deliberate, not a caprice. He waited patiently; his love is noble and tender. My beloved is Gaston, the illegitimate son of the beautiful Lady Brandon, who died tragically. He was raised by his brother Louis, a sailor, whose letters once encouraged him; now Louis has vanished and Gaston is often despairing.Gaston believed genius would bring fortune, yet his life has been hardship: failure to establish himself in literature, mounting debts, humiliation. I met him at Mme. d’Espard’s and felt an immediate sympathy that has become passion. He has talent and pride—qualities which can intimidate a woman—but his love for me seems genuine. He knows nothing of the depth of my feeling.I am confident of our happiness, though I fear losing him or my charms as he remains young. I mean to nourish our love, to be loved for myself. If his affection should wane, I would blame myself rather than him. My nature inclines to be a lover rather than a mother; should disaster come, Renée, I beg you to care for my children as my legacy. Your tenderness would make my death less bitter.I have resolved to marry quietly. I will retain control of my property; Gaston must not know everything. I propose to assign him a stock yielding twelve thousand francs a year, which I shall place where he will find it on the eve of our wedding. If he refuses it I will break the engagement.This confession has fatigued me; I will continue later. I must spend tomorrow in the country.October 20th.I have taken steps for secrecy. I bought a small property near Ville-d’Avray: twenty acres, a little lake, and a charming garden. It is secluded, surrounded by woods, and beautifully laid out. My chalet, inspired by Swiss designs, is simple and solid, well-equipped and hidden from view. There is a separate house for servants and a concealed entrance; I have chosen discreet, loyal servants. All my cherished belongings from Paris are here, creating a simple but elegant home.The valley’s tranquility is unmatched—birds, wind in the trees, and the hush of water. I, who have suffered much, found myself moved to tears on my first visit and paid the architect without hesitation, insisting he tell no one I was the owner.Now you understand my mysterious actions and the true purpose of my investments. I have cleared financial burdens to focus wholly on him. Gaston knows nothing of my plans; our life at the chalet will be modest, though I shall spare no care in preserving my appearance for him. I want him to love me at the end as at the beginning.In two days I shall be Mme. Gaston. Is it fitting for a Christian to love so deeply?My man of business says he has lost a client. And you, my dear, may feel you are losing a sister.Address future letters to Mme. Gaston, Poste Restante, Versailles. We shall keep our names and our retreat secret from the locals and obtain provisions from Paris. Farewell. Writing this I feel grief and joy together—proof that my love for Gaston is as deep as the sorrow of leaving you.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My Dear Daniel,I need two witnesses for my marriage; please come tomorrow evening with our friend Joseph Bridau. My future wife wishes to live in complete retirement, which explains our infrequent meetings this past year. After the wedding we will be apart for a long time, but I know you will understand. Friendship can endure absence; while I will miss you, I shall not see you in my own home. She has sacrificed the closeness of a childhood friend for me, and I must do the same.This proves our love is deep and genuine, founded on mutual understanding. It gives me immense joy, yet I carry a painful secret. You know how desperate my finances have been, and only through your support have I managed. My wife is wealthy; I am not.
