I once joked about wanting a rich heiress, but now that it is real I feel a bitter humiliation I cannot shake. This shame confirms my love for her, for I do not recoil from it, even if it overwhelms me.Otherwise, my dreams have come true. She is the most beautiful and noble woman, with a charming personality and a sharp mind. At twenty-seven she is a widow without children, full of life yet sometimes melancholy. Despite her aristocratic background, she loves me despite my humble origins. Our love has grown over time; we share a deep emotional connection and avoid the physical aspect of love out of mutual respect. Once she confessed that her marriage was her regret, revealing the depth of her feelings. You, Daniel, are a giant among men, and you love a remarkable woman, just as I love my Armande.Louise, can it be that you intend a life of wedded solitude after sacrificing your first husband? You seem to court future misery. I see you are resolved, and the man who won your heart must possess a rare charm. But have you forgotten your earlier criticisms of young men, when you claimed they had tarnished purity?You are lucky to believe in happiness, and while I cannot blame you, I must warn you. Nature and society conspire against absolute happiness, and I foresee some disaster arising from your boundless joy. I have no objections to your fiancé, but I hope you will describe him to me one day.I have not told Louis about your marriage; it would only invite his ridicule. I will simply say you wish to keep it secret. You are brave to face winter first, and I will be your discreet friend. Your journey into this emotional wilderness is like an explorer’s perilous expedition, though fortunately only a short distance from Paris. I wish you a safe journey and a swift return.What has happened to you, my dear? After two years of silence, Renee has a right to worry about Louise. Is this what love does, scattering friendships like ours? You must admit that, while I am devoted to my children, a mother’s love is expansive and does not diminish other affections. I miss your letters and long to see your face.Regarding our situation, I will be brief. Your last letter contained sharp comments about our political position. You mocked us for keeping our posts in the Audit Department, which Louis owes to Charles X’s favor. But how could we provide for Athenais and Rene on an income of forty thousand livres, of which thirty thousand is entailed? We must live on our salary and save the estate’s income for their future. In twenty years we will have saved enough to provide for both children.Louis has been raised to the Peerage and is now a grand officer of the Legion of Honor. He has made himself useful in the Chamber and was offered an embassy, which I declined for the sake of our children’s education. I will not leave Paris until Rene has finished his schooling.We could not remain loyal to the elder branch of the dynasty without jeopardizing our children’s prospects. A mother is not called to be a Decius, especially now. In fifteen years, Louis will retire on a good pension, and I hope to secure a post for Armand in the Audit Department.I am a happy mother; my children give me endless joy. Armand is now a day scholar at Henry IV’s school. I wanted him in public school but could not bear to part with him, so I arranged for him to attend by day and return home each evening. He is the same charming boy you loved, and his tutor praises his progress.Home education has drawbacks: children raised at home may be exposed too early to the world’s passions and may lack the social skills gained with peers. A mother must keep her children from society’s distractions, as I have done.Now that I am thirty, I feel a duty fulfilled. My children seem to sense my thoughts and respond. Armand, once dull, has lately excelled, winning prizes for translation and essays. I felt immense pride when his name was called and longed to shout, “I am his mother!”Rene, inspired by Armand, wants to attend school too. The three children can be noisy, but I cherish every moment. I am the only one allowed to care for them; the joys of motherhood outweigh the challenges.I must study their characters closely, for a mother alone discerns her children’s natural aptitudes. Home-bred children often develop ease and tact, compensating for lack of innate ability. I believe Armand will become an admirable public servant, while Rene will be a bold sailor.Nais is so dear to me I can hardly see her as separate from myself. I want her to be happy and will only give her to a man who truly loves her. Yet I worry about her future and the risk of a poor marriage. Your silence has reminded me of the anguish marriage can bring a mother. Farewell for now, dear Louise, and please write back.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################So, after two years of silence, you wonder why I have written so little. My dear Renée, words fail to express my happiness. We bear everything with ease; we are in perfect harmony. There has been no discord in our feelings, and each day brings its own joy. We do not fear monotony; rather we regret that our time together may not suffice for all the expressions of our love. We take more pleasure in each other's company than on the first day, and every hour reveals new reasons to love one another.Every evening we speak of visiting Paris, of the new boulevards and the finished Madeleine, but in the morning we linger over breakfast and take a siesta. Gaston gazes at me as if I were a painting, and we both shed tears of joy reflecting on our love. I still play the mistress, affecting to give less than I receive, and I delight in the little coquetries of our life. There is something sweet about passion tempered by tenderness; Gaston, my master, remains a timid suitor at my command.You asked me to describe him, yet it is impossible to portray the man we love without emotion. Society alters men so that the public man can differ from the man of deep feeling. The charming suitor may lack qualities a woman values, while an unrefined man may possess a profound capacity for love. Our ideal is a man whose outward appearance aligns with his true nature, a grace that cannot be acquired. This ideal haunts every woman's imagination, and in Gaston I have found it.Ah, dear, I never knew love could be so intertwined with youth, talent, and beauty. Gaston is unpretentious, moving with an effortless grace. When we walk together our bodies seem to fit perfectly, a single figure gliding through the woods. Our thoughts, desires, and words harmonize. Sometimes we walk in silence, listening to the rain and admiring the sunset, feeling as if in silent prayer for our happiness. A timid kiss exchanged in nature feels like an act of worship.We return home each time more in love than before. Such passion between older married people would scandalize Paris; only in the woods can we express it freely.Gaston is of average height, well-proportioned, and agile. He has a fair complexion, dark hair, and a striking resemblance to Louis XIII. His honorable poverty has spared him the vices that ruin many young men. His teeth are magnificent, and he has a robust constitution. His blue eyes, full of tenderness for me, can flash with intensity at inspiring thoughts.He has a remarkably even temperament. I have heard many tales of women’s troubles with men dissatisfied with themselves, but Gaston is free from such burdens. I have learned that marrying an older man can lead to unequal unions, and I urge you not to let Athénaïs marry one.His smile is a treasure—always fresh, full of unspoken love and gratitude. Our shared experiences have become part of our joy, and even the smallest details of nature remind us of our time together. We share a deep connection, as if we are two editions of the same book. Our ability to view subjects broadly brings constant pleasure, and this unity of thought feels like a pledge of love.Despite these pleasures, life here is laborious. I do my own room, refusing a hired hand to intrude on my personal space. My room is meticulously arranged so that Gaston finds it pleasing. I rise early, bathe in cold water to refresh myself before he wakes, and emerge like a newly opened flower. He appreciates this freshness without knowing the effort behind it.My daily grooming is handled by my maid, and I dress several times a day for Gaston’s delight. We take great interest in our flowers and horticulture, surrounding our chalet with beauty. Our garden is a source of pride, and we eagerly tend our fruits and vegetables.To keep Gaston's mind engaged, I encourage him to finish plays he began during harder times. This literary work requires reflection and inspiration, and I assist him, ensuring our winter evenings are never dull.Our servants have an easy time; they need no reprimand. They have presented us as a traveling couple content with their roles. The gardeners sell surplus produce, and we enjoy an abundance hard to find in Paris.I strive to fill the role of society for Gaston, ensuring his solitude does not become tedious. I have learned true jealousy—the kind born of love. The thought of him ceasing to love me sends shivers through me; I tremble before him.Alas, I am still without a child. I long for the day when our home will be filled with the laughter of children running through our garden. It pains me to see your lovely children, for my life feels narrower without them. Motherhood expands a woman's heart, while love can be selfish. I envy your happiness and the joy you find in your children.As I approach thirty, I feel the limits of my beauty closing in. Though still attractive, I worry about aging while Gaston remains young. I often ask him to promise he will tell me if his love ever fades, and he swears it will not, finding the idea absurd.Goodbye, dear friend. Shall we let years pass without writing again? Happiness can be monotonous, and perhaps that is why Dante seems greater in the Paradiso than in the Inferno. I am not Dante, only your friend, and I do not wish to bore you. Write to me—your children supply endless stories—while I... No more of this. A thousand loves.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My dear Louise, I have read your letter repeatedly; it sounds less like a woman’s than a child’s. You forget that pure love belongs to nature and is distorted by civilization. Society created the family as the foundation of organized life at the cost of individual desires. A married man dedicates himself to his wife, who benefits from his efforts.You have taken a mistaken path: the dream of being adored suits the young, not a mature woman. Vanity may enjoy adoration, but true fulfillment lies in the life of wife and mother. Return to Paris; fear happiness, not misfortune.Life’s simple pleasures—bread, air, silence—sustain us. Passionate love exhausts; motherhood’s affections endure and reward. I chose self-sacrifice and would choose it again. Your life, despite its romantic gloss, is rooted in selfishness; I plead with you as I watch your happiness tested.Life seeks balance; excess brings pain or only fleeting pleasure. You are ill because you cling to a passion a marriage should steady. The glory of home is calmness and shared life, which you overlook. You, more passionate than tender, would be both wife and mistress, undermining marriage’s sanctity.If you could understand a mother’s sweet self-discipline. My proud nature softened into gentle melancholy, rewarded by motherhood’s joys. I fear your life may darken. I wish you could witness family delights, but my arguments seem futile against your illusions. I had hoped time would restore reason, but your appetite for love seems insatiable. Farewell; I despair that my letter, meant to reconcile you with society, only revealed what I see as selfishness.---Renee, calamity has struck—no, a thunderbolt for poor Louise. Doubt is my calamity; certainty would be death. The day before yesterday, after my first toilette, I looked for Gaston to walk but found him gone. In the stable his mare Fedelta was in a lather; it implied he’d been to Paris without telling me. Suspicion seized me; I struggled to compose myself.When Gaston found me I was pale and shaken. He carried me to the parlor; I would not speak and fled to my room to weep. He waited; I said I’d see him when ready. After composing myself he returned, and I asked about mud on Fedelta’s legs. He playfully threatened to withhold explanations; yet I saw no disloyalty in his eyes, though I remained unconvinced.We spent the day together, and my heart clung to him. I hated that mare for my distress. I asked if I bored him; he reassured me. I suggested selling Fedelta; his response crushed me, making me feel inadequate. Alone, I was consumed by despair, surrounded by darkness.Next morning I told him to take Fedelta to Paris; my tone betrayed turmoil. He urged trust; I tried to suppress suspicion. He left, and I watched him go with a sense of doom.When he returned late with a whip as a gift I felt it only distracted from something deeper. I later learned he bought it from Verdier, confirming my suspicions. I resolved to keep watch while staying silent.Days passed; Gaston changed, working harder—seeming to need money. His drawer was empty; he received letters he quickly tore up. Jealousy consumed me; I followed him to the post office and confronted him about a letter from Nathan announcing a play’s acceptance.Despite his success, I couldn’t shake that he hid something. A growing certainty that he had a rival drove me to seek the truth.After investigation I learned Gaston visited a woman in Paris; I followed and found she was his brother’s widow, legally married. The revelation shattered me: he had been providing for her and her children. I returned tormented by thoughts of betrayal and jealousy.Despite the pain, my love for Gaston remained. I struggled with his past and wanted to confront him about his feelings for the widow, but feared the truth would destroy me. I even pondered my own end, unable to bear the suffering.---My dear Louise, I write urgently; your last letter alarmed me. I confided in Louis to save you. Though our methods may seem extreme, the outcome is satisfactory. I even involved the police, kept secret among us.Gaston is not as you feared. His brother Louis died in Calcutta, leaving a widow and two children. Gaston has secretly supported them, using his earnings for his sister‑in‑law and her children. The woman you mistook for a rival is not a threat but a mother in need; Gaston acted from kindness and duty.You should love him more; he has shown sensitivity and care. Welcome the widow and her children into your life—perhaps adopt the boys. This revelation should give you peace, not despair.Ah, my dear friend, what can I say? It is too late for me. My actions have sealed my fate. Tormented by love for Gaston, I cannot bear losing him. I am filled with despair and long for relief. Come, dear friend, and witness my decline.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################My love,—Take the children to Provence without me; I must stay with Louise, who has only a few days left. I cannot leave her or her husband, for whom I worry.You know the letter that sent me rushing to Ville d'Avray, gathering doctors along the way. Since then I have not left my dear friend, sitting up every night for two weeks.When I arrived, Louise and Gaston appeared charming and happy, but it was a façade. She whispered, "He must not know; I am dying." Her hand was burning and her cheeks flushed. I was glad I had left the doctors in the woods until needed."Leave us for a little," she told Gaston. "Two women who have not met for five years have secrets to share."Alone, she collapsed into my arms and wept. I urged her to tell me. "I have brought the best surgeon and physician from the hospital, along with Bianchon," I said."Ah! Have them in at once if they can save me! The passion that drove me to death now cries for life!""But what have you done to yourself?""I have brought myself to the last stage of consumption in just a few days.""But how?""I got myself into a profuse perspiration at night, then lay by the lake in the dew. Gaston thinks I have a cold, but I am dying!""Send him to Paris; I will fetch the doctors myself," I said, rushing out to find them.After the consultation none of the doctors gave hope; they believe Louise will die with the fall of the leaves. Her strong constitution had served her scheme, but a few days of folly made her case desperate.I cannot express my feelings. I have lived in Louise as much as in my own life. I was crushed and could not escort the doctors out. In my bitter thoughts, Louise's clear voice startled me: "So there is no hope for me!"She made me get up and took me to her drawing-room. "Stay with me to the end; I won't have sad faces around me. I must keep the truth from him. I know I have the strength to do it. I am full of youth and spirit, and can die standing! I have no regrets. I am dying as I wished, still young and beautiful, in the perfection of my womanhood.""For him, I can see I would have made his life miserable. My jealousy has already wounded him. When my suspicions met indifference, that would have been my death. I have had my share of life. There are people who have lived sixty years without two years of real life, while my thirty years are doubled by the intensity of my love.""For him and me, the end is happy. But for you, dear Renée, it is different. You lose a loving sister, and that is a loss that cannot be repaired. You alone have the right to mourn my death."After a pause she went on to a grimmer lesson: her death proved, sadly, that marriage cannot rest on passion alone. "Your life is noble," she said to me, "giving your husband growing affection, while my passionate eagerness diminished. Twice I have gone astray, and twice Death has reached for my happiness. The first time he took the noblest of men; now he takes me from my poet husband, with all his beauty and grace.""I would not complain. I have known two men of nobility—one in mind, the other in form. I die adored—what more could I wish? Perhaps time to draw near to God, whom I may have thought too little of. My spirit will fly towards Him, full of love, praying to be reunited with the two who made my life a heaven."She warned that her example might tempt others to expect impossible felicities. Then, with characteristic disdain for the social fuss about women's emancipation, she said women were weak and should yield their wills to their husbands; the clamour they had made was ridiculous, betraying their childishness.She spoke in that gentle, grave fashion that made truths sound pretty, until Gaston entered with his sister-in-law, the children, and the English nurse he had fetched from Paris."Here are the pretty instruments of my torture," she said as her nephews approached. "What a wonderful likeness to their uncle!"She played hostess to Mme. Gaston and insisted the chalet be hers. I wrote to the Duc and Duchesse de Chaulieu, the Duc de Rhetore, the Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, and to Madeleine. It was time. The next day Louise, exhausted, could not go out; she only rose for dinner. Madeleine de Lenoncourt, her brothers, and her mother arrived. The coolness caused by Louise's second marriage faded; her father and brothers visited daily, and duchesses spent evenings at the chalet. Death unites as well as separates; it silences petty feelings.Louise remained charming, graceful, witty, and tender. She kept her perfect tact and lavished her brilliant mind on us—one of Paris's queens to the last."I want to look well even in my coffin," she said with that matchless smile, lying on the bed where she would linger for a fortnight.Her room lacked the usual sick-chamber air; medicines and nursing supplies were hidden."Is not my deathbed pretty?" she asked the priest who came to confess her.We watched her like misers. The anxiety and harsh truths had prepared Gaston for the worst. He is brave, but the blow has struck deep. I would not be surprised if he followed her soon. Yesterday, walking by the lake, he said simply, "I must be a father to those two children," pointing to his sister-in-law with the boys. "But I want your promise that you will be a second mother to them and persuade your husband to accept the role of guardian."He wore smiles for Louise, but I saw through the façade; his courage matched hers.Louise wished to see her godson, but I preferred he remain in Provence; she might wish to remember him generously, putting me in a difficult position.Goodbye, my love.August 25th (her birthday).Yesterday evening Louise was delirious briefly, but even her babble was beautiful—proof that the madness of gifted people is not that of fools. In a faint voice she sang Italian airs from I Puritani, La Sonnambula, Moïse. We stood around her bed in silence; not one of us, not even the Duc de Rhetore, had dry eyes, as we felt her soul departing. She could no longer see us, yet the charm lingered in the melody, sweet and otherworldly.During the night the death agony began. It is now seven in the morning, and I have just raised her from bed. A flicker of strength returned; she wished to sit by the window and asked for Gaston's hand. Then, my love, the sweetest spirit we shall ever see on this earth departed, leaving us with an empty shell.The last sacrament had been administered the evening before, without Gaston's knowledge—he had been sleeping during the ceremony. After she was set at the window, she asked me to read the De Profundis in French, facing the lovely scene she had created. She repeated the words after me and pressed her husband's hands.August 26th.My heart is broken. I have just seen her in her winding-sheet; her face is pale with purple shadows. Oh! I want my children! Bring me my children!############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################AT THE MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Comtesse Bolognini.Madame, remembering the joy your conversation gave a traveler in Milan, I lay this work at your feet, hoping you will protect it with your name as you once shielded the tales of a beloved writer. You have a daughter, Eugenie, whose beauty and intelligence promise a joyful childhood, unlike the rigid upbringing of the Eugenie in these pages. While Frenchmen are often thought fickle, I remain Italian in my constancy and memory. Writing “Eugenie” recalls the laughter and warmth of our time in Milan; I now regard you as a distant, treasured masterpiece.Your devoted servant,De Balzac.A DAUGHTER OF EVECHAPTER I. THE TWO MARIESIn a luxurious blue-velvet boudoir on rue Neuve-des-Mathurins, two young women wept before the fire. The room belonged to Ferdinand du Tillet, a wealthy banker who had married into aristocracy for a dowry but lacked true social standing. Madame du Tillet clasped her sister, Madame Félix de Vandenesse’s, hand as they exchanged intimate confidences. Both had been raised by a strict, pious mother and entered marriage without ever enjoying youthful freedoms, marrying naive about life and love. Their close bond was forged in repression: forbidden the wider world and taught only religion and needlework, they retained innocence but not the knowledge to navigate adult passions. Marriage had seemed an escape, yet they were unprepared for its realities.CHAPTER II. A CONFIDENCE BETWEEN SISTERSMadame de Vandenesse, overwhelmed by marital trouble, poured out her despair over a husband seemingly indifferent to her happiness. Madame du Tillet, feeling equally helpless, revealed the emptiness of her own marriage: a cold husband and a flat domestic life contrasted with their mutual warmth. Each confessed a craving for passion and excitement that society and marital duty suppressed, and each sought consolation and encouragement in the other.CHAPTER III. THE HISTORY OF A FORTUNATE WOMANFélix de Vandenesse, of noble lineage, had married hoping to recreate an idealized love he had known. But the duties and constraints of his class cooled their relationship. Madame de Vandenesse, once content, grew aware of monotony and a desire for deeper emotional fulfilment. Her husband was kind and attentive but could not satisfy her craving for romantic intensity, and she found herself tempted by the notion of love beyond marriage.CHAPTER IV. A CELEBRATED MANRaoul Nathan, an emerging literary figure, entered society with talent, audacity, sharp wit and caustic humor. His unconventional looks and manner made him both attractive and unsettling. At a salon hosted by Madame de Montcornet, Nathan caught Madame de Vandenesse’s attention; their early exchanges carried an unspoken attraction. Nathan, juggling a complex career and private troubles, viewed her as a potential muse, yet social norms and their respective positions threatened any open relationship.CHAPTER V. FLORINEFlorine, a talented actress and Nathan’s long-term companion, represented another side of his life. Their relationship blended affection and practicality: she buoyed his spirits and career, while relying on him for protection and advancement. As Nathan’s admiration for Madame de Vandenesse grew, Florine—alert to the shift—felt threatened and increasingly insecure.CHAPTER VI. ROMANTIC LOVENathan and Madame de Vandenesse’s bond deepened around a shared passion for art and literature. Their encounters became more intimate, bringing exhilaration but also moral peril. Madame de Vandenesse struggled with guilt and desire, torn between loyalty to her husband and an urge for a passionate life, fully aware of the social consequences of yielding to that urge.CHAPTER VII. SUICIDEUnder mounting pressure from career failures and personal despair, Nathan slid toward suicide. A chance encounter with Madame de Vandenesse, however, revived his will to live. Seeing his desperation, she resolved to protect and encourage him, intervening against both his darkest impulses and the destructive bent of his ambitions.CHAPTER VIII. A LOVER SAVED AND LOSTAfter Nathan’s crisis, Madame de Vandenesse became more involved in his life, balancing protection with her own feelings. Their relationship became simultaneously a source of strength and of turmoil as they navigated secrecy and social risk. As his work began to succeed, Nathan found himself torn between Florine’s practical attachment and the countess’s inspiring influence; Florine’s jealousy intensified the strain.CHAPTER IX. THE HUSBAND’S TRIUMPHFélix de Vandenesse, sensing his wife’s attachment to Nathan, acted to preserve their marriage. Determined to discover the truth and shield her from scandal, he moved to defend her honor and the union they shared. The narrative follows characters entangled by love, ambition, loyalty and societal expectation, building to a dramatic conclusion that examines the conflicts between desire and duty.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################On a Sunday morning in April 1813 a luxurious cabriolet drew up before the Tuileries carrying an anxious, aged father and his beautiful daughter, Julie. Proud of the attention she attracted, he accompanied her to the gardens to see the military review commanded by Napoleon, the last before a disastrous campaign. Though the review had not yet begun and he urged them to leave, Julie, afraid of missing the Emperor, insisted on staying. An officer recognized her and admitted them to a prime vantage point. The spectacle—the Old Guard preparing, the crowd’s buzz, the mounting anticipation—was magnificent.When Napoleon appeared, cheers rose. Julie was captivated, but her eye soon fixed on a young officer on the Emperor’s staff, Colonel Victor d’Aiglemont. A pang of jealousy and an unexpected attraction seized her, while her father’s anxious manner reminded her of their precarious situation. As the review ended, he warned her that youthful dreams could lead to sorrow and advised against falling for a man like Victor, whom he deemed unworthy. Despite his cautions, Julie felt drawn to Victor and their attachment deepened.Nearly a year later, in March 1814, Julie and Victor were traveling together. The once vibrant girl had become a shadow; her health faltered as Victor, now a colonel, was absorbed in military duties and later in adapting to the shifting political scene. Isolated and yearning for affection, her need grew while Victor’s attention waned.During their travels they encountered an Englishman, Arthur, detained in France. Julie felt an unexpected kinship with him; Arthur’s quiet admiration and gentleness contrasted sharply with Victor’s indifference, and she was drawn to Arthur as a steady presence. Torn between marital duty and her desires, she wrestled with feelings that deepened her inner conflict.As Napoleon fell and the political landscape shifted, Victor’s ambitions altered—he grew more involved with the new regime and increasingly self-interested. When his infidelities surfaced, Julie’s heartache deepened; she recognized his selfishness and felt the weight of betrayal. In desperation she sought solace in Arthur, though the moral and social consequences of such a choice pressed upon her.Over time Julie’s health and spirits declined; the once radiant woman became trapped in a loveless marriage, isolated and unfulfilled. At last, in a moment of clarity, she resolved to refuse passivity. Determined to reclaim her happiness, she chose to defy societal expectations, confronting the choices that would define her existence. The narrative becomes a poignant exploration of love, sacrifice, duty, and the quest for fulfillment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between the Seine and the Loing lies a flat, dreary country edged by the Forest of Fontainebleau, its southern limits marked by Moret, Montereau, and Nemours. A monotonous horizon seems to foster melancholy. In the center stands the old chateau of Saint-Lange, once a sign of wealth now diminished.In late 1820 a young woman known in Paris for her beauty and wit arrived at Saint-Lange, startling villagers unaccustomed to gentry. The Marquise d’Aiglemont’s frailty and withdrawal fed gossip: some blamed her husband’s gambling in Spain and expected the estate to be sold, prompting locals to eye their own interests.The Marquise lived in isolation, rarely leaving her room and only dining briefly with her daughter. At twenty-six she bore a profound, unacknowledged grief that had extinguished maternal instinct and made her feel like a ghost. Her anguish sprang from a lost love — a man who had died to preserve her honor — and from being bound by social expectations that forbade grieving openly. Her youth and beauty felt meaningless in the absence of the true love and joy she had imagined; her marriage, respectable on the surface, offered no emotional fulfillment.The flat horizon of her surroundings mirrored her desolation. Seasons passed, but her heart stayed heavy with unfulfilled longing; she felt trapped in a life of appearances, consumed by what might have been and by guilt for maintaining a façade of happiness.One day the village priest, M. le Curé, came and, sensing her suffering, spoke of the universality of grief and the sustaining power of faith. At first she dismissed him, convinced her pain unique and insurmountable. His story of a father who had lost all his sons in battle, however, put her suffering in a wider context and offered a small, unexpected solace.Gradually she confided in the priest, revealing her isolation and the emptiness of her motherhood. She saw her daughter Hélène not as the object of maternal love but as a living reminder of the lost man; duty, not affection, defined their relationship. The priest urged her to find strength in maternal duty, but she wrestled with a sense of inadequacy and bitterness toward the social constraints that felt like a prison—an institution forcing women into roles that stifled their true selves.Spring brought distraction: managing the estate provided temporary reprieve from her sorrow. By October she left Saint-Lange with some regained vitality, though the scars of grief remained. She encountered the curé again but avoided him, signaling a desire to move forward without being tied to the chateau’s painful memories.The Marquise d’Aiglemont’s quiet struggle—between public expectation and private desire, between duty and the search for identity—illustrates the costs of rigid social structures and the complex burdens placed on women.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At Madame Firmiani’s ball, M. Charles de Vandenesse, a promising young diplomat about to leave Paris for the Congress of Laybach, came to take his leave. Near thirty and accustomed to a transient life, he treated existence philosophically, favoring ambition over romance and viewing Parisian society as largely superficial and full of empty intrigues.As he surveyed the assembly’s emptiness, Madame Firmiani introduced him to the enigmatic Marquise d’Aiglemont, a woman wrapped in mystery and known for past affairs. The Marquise, also thirty, combined delicate beauty with an air of melancholy that sharply contrasted with the surrounding frivolity. Vandenesse was struck by her depth and the disparity between her sensibility and her husband, a dull man who had profited from her influence. Beneath her calm he sensed profound sadness and unfulfilled dreams.Their conversation moved from light banter to themes of love and sentiment, revealing a connection that both excited and troubled him. Before leaving, he asked to visit again, drawn despite the complexity of her past. In the days that followed she occupied his thoughts; he alternated between hope and doubt and finally visited. Their encounters blended friendship and unspoken desire, with Vandenesse wrestling with the implications of affection for a married woman.The Marquise, too, was drawn to him but divided by loyalty to the memory of a deceased lover and by societal constraints. Though resistant, she could not deny their bond. As the relationship deepened, obstacles multiplied: her history, social expectation, and his ambitions all weighed on them, producing moments of joy and anguish.One evening a tender kiss marked a turning point, only to be shattered by the intrusion of her husband, forcing a confrontation with reality. Their connection ultimately testified to love’s power to cross social boundaries, but it remained fraught with unfulfilled desire and the haunting presence of the past, leaving both to navigate the treacherous waters of duty and feeling.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between the Barrière d’Italie and the Barrière de la Santé, the boulevard toward the Jardin des Plantes offers a stirring view of Paris. From an elevated point, a tree-lined avenue drops into a valley where factories lie among greenery and the brown streams of the Bièvre. Across the slope, the squalor of Faubourg Saint-Marceau contrasts with the majestic cupola of the Panthéon and the somber dome of the Val-de-Grâce rising above dense houses and tall poplars. To the left, the observatory’s windows cast odd shadows; beyond, the lantern tower of the Invalides appears between the Luxembourg and Saint-Sulpice. Architecture and foliage blend and shift with the light.To the right, the canal Saint-Martin runs beside reddish quays and lime trees, flanked by Roman-style public granaries. Farther still, the smoke-laden slopes of Belleville, studded with houses and windmills, meet the clouds. Between that horizon and the valley’s roofs lies a vast, hidden city, alive with sounds that proclaim its presence.When sunlight floods this part of Paris, it transforms: windows gleam, tiles brighten, and the scene acquires a magical atmosphere. Humanity and nature combine into an idyllic spectacle; beneath the cypress trees of Père-Lachaise the capital seems to lie in peaceful splendor.One morning, while admiring this view, I reflected bitterly on those who disdain our homeland, wealthy travelers who mock France. I watched modern Paris with affection until a sound—a kiss—broke my reverie. Nearby, a simply but neatly dressed young woman stood with a handsome young man and a charming little boy. Their joy was immediate and visible in gesture and expression.A second child, a little girl, stood apart. Her demeanor was more troubled. Dark-haired and grave, perhaps seven or eight, she contrasted with her fair, carefree brother. Their clothes matched, but the girl’s plain collar hinted at some hidden parental preference. When the boy approached with a toy, the girl scowled; her gaze suggested thoughtfulness beyond her years.As the couple continued their playful interaction, the girl’s indifference turned darker. When her brother reached out, she pushed him away. In a tragic instant he stumbled and fell into the river, vanishing beneath the murky water. The scene moved with appalling speed; I rushed to help but it was too late. The mother arrived frantic; there was no hope of saving the child.The incident left an indelible mark on the mother, who would carry the consequences home: grief and the silent witness of her daughter’s actions loomed over the family.Years later, the Marquis de Vandenesse, now mourning his father, hosted a dinner that included a notary. The notary, oblivious to the tension, monopolized conversation, unaware of the Marquise’s impatience to leave for the theatre to join their children. Despite hints and the couple’s desire to go, he persisted in long-winded remarks.As the clock struck nine, the Marquis interrupted, insisting the notary leave so he could escort his wife. The notary still failed to grasp the mood, prolonging the talk and prompting a sour exchange. Just then the Marquise’s husband returned unexpectedly with their children, who looked upset; the little girl’s eyes were red from crying, the boy fretful. The Marquise learned the children had left the play in distress; the son recounted a scene that explained their sudden return.The Marquise’s face hardened as she processed her daughter’s emotional state. The notary’s attempted levity—thoughtless comments about family dynamics and preferences—only heightened the tension. Overwhelmed, the Marquise sent her daughter away while the notary, still speaking, remained unaware of the weight of his words. The Marquis, furious, demanded he leave. Confused, the notary departed.Later, the notary reflected superficially on the evening, attributing the disturbance to aristocratic whims. His wife, however, recognized the deeper truth and advised him to stick to business in future dealings; his inability to perceive social nuance threatened more misunderstandings in his professional life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of Napoleon’s orderly staff-officers, known as the General or the Marquis, spent the spring at Versailles and, having amassed a fortune during the Restoration, took a country house near Montreuil seeking solitude close to Paris. The house, once a nobleman’s retreat for passing romances, had broad grounds; its entrance still faced the street, a reminder of a road that had once been quiet.One winter evening the family was alone: the servants had gone to Versailles for a wedding and, with Christmas revelers lingering, the General felt uneasy as the clock struck eleven with no sign of their return. Outside, a deep silence lay over the countryside, broken only by wind and distant footsteps.Inside, the household enjoyed a cozy scene. The General, a rugged soldier, sat by the fire watching his youngest son resist his mother’s attempts to undress him, preferring to play with his sister. Laughter filled the room; the Marquise watched them with contented affection. At a nearby table their thirteen-year-old son was engrossed in the Arabian Nights while Helene, striking and thoughtful, worked on a tapestry. The children’s play contrasted with the older siblings’ more serious reflections, and the General found simple joy in the domestic warmth.As evening wore on, the General voiced concern over the absent servants and the Marquise, sensing tension, urged their son to bed. Then hurried knocks came from the street signaling urgency. The General answered to find a breathless, disheveled man seeking refuge, pleading secrecy and water. With bloodied hands the stranger explained he had been involved in a duel. Despite hesitation, the General felt an inexplicable pull toward him and led him to a hidden room, torn between unease and obligation.The Marquise suspected something amiss and questioned her husband, who insisted on secrecy to protect the family. Helene, curious and uneasy, overheard their low conversation and, compelled, approached the locked room. There she saw the stranger: grim, wounded, yet magnetic. Their eyes met and an unspoken connection formed, leaving Helene both intrigued and unsettled. She returned to the family with her mind racing.As the night advanced the General’s thoughts turned to the implications of harboring a fugitive. He summoned the servants angrily when they returned and prepared for the danger their guest’s presence might invite. Gendarmes soon arrived seeking the fugitive; the General’s protectiveness surged and he steeled himself for confrontation. The stranger remained mostly silent, contemplating his next move as tension built in the household.In the ensuing turmoil Helene’s fascination deepened into something that made her question her desires and the life she had known. The night culminated in a dramatic confrontation that forced the General to confront the moral complexities of his choice and its consequences for his family.Ultimately, the General’s hospitality had unforeseen repercussions: Helene’s connection to the stranger altered the course of their lives. What began as an idyllic evening ended in turmoil, revealing the fragility of their happiness and the heavy cost of a single decision.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It was a June day in 1844 when the Marquise d’Aiglemont, about fifty, walked in the garden of her daughter’s Paris mansion. She paused by a row of windows, sometimes sitting to gaze at the dome of the Invalides and her own grounds. The neighborhood was quiet; the aristocracy was only beginning to stir.Having lost her two sons, Gustave and Abel, the Marquise had given her fortune to her daughter, the Comtesse Moïna de Saint-Héreen. Her devotion to Moïna was profound, a stark contrast to the faint memories she retained of her dead children. Society dismissed the Marquise’s attachment as superstition and largely overlooked both her past and her coolness toward her daughter-in-law.Despite her sacrifices, the Marquise was often alone; Moïna was absorbed in her social life. The Marquise’s love was unmistakable but shaded by sorrow and a sense of loss. Gossip suggested she might regret relinquishing her fortune, yet most praised her devotion. Rarely appearing in society, she felt eclipsed by Moïna’s youthful charm when she did attend.As she sat in the garden, her face showed the weight of experience and beauty faded by sorrow and time. She was aware of Moïna’s flirtation with Alfred de Vandenesse, a man she regarded with disdain, and feared it could end in heartbreak. The Comte de Saint-Héreen had been away for months, and the Marquise felt powerless; her warnings would likely be ignored. Her heart ached at the thought of her daughter on a precipice she could not prevent.A series of small, painful incidents over the years had marked Moïna’s growing distance. The Marquise sought a talk and found indifference; the Countess’s dismissive attitude stung, and the Marquise felt contempt weigh on her. When she finally confronted Moïna, their tense conversation—warnings met with flippant replies—deepened the Marquise’s sorrow. Leaving the room, she felt a sharp pain in her heart, realizing her daughter had lost respect for her.Overwhelmed by grief and a sense of irrevocable estrangement, she collapsed on a bench. Moïna, unaware of the gravity, believed she could mend things with a few gestures. The Marquise was carried inside, lifeless. Servants fetched a physician, but her fate was sealed. Struck by remorse as she watched her mother decline, Moïna suddenly grasped how she had taken that love for granted. In her final moments the Marquise managed a smile at her daughter—a testament to a mother’s enduring love and forgiveness—while Moïna stood hollow with regret, realizing too late the depth of that sacrifice.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ############################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ## MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In the spring of 1822 Parisian doctors sent a young man, M. le Baron Gaston de Nueil, to Lower Normandy to recover from an inflammatory illness likely caused by overwork or excess. Seeking rest, a light diet, and fresh air, he settled in Bayeux, where relatives received him warmly.After a few evenings with his cousin, Mme. de Sainte-Severe, Gaston met the local elite, who embodied provincial French types. One was an ancient but impoverished noble family: a patriarch, a determined sportsman contemptuous of modernity and the new political order; a loud, domineering wife who assumed their daughters’ fortunes rested solely on their name. They clung to obsolete customs, representing a nobility without wealth or influence.In contrast stood a wealthier, more recent family who embraced contemporary Parisian trends. The wife was a society woman—slightly behind the latest fashions—while the husband, a member of the Departmental Council, was shrewd with finances and versed in political gossip. Their Royalism was less zealous than that of the old nobles, engaging with modern life while keeping ancestral ties.The local bishop, a former Vicar-General, mediated between these families: respected for his office but frequently reminded of his plebeian origins. Retired military officers and their families also populated the circle; they preserved a veneer of aristocracy but focused on local affairs rather than national politics. The women dressed as if for court life, yet their days followed routine and tradition.This exclusive set included a few wealthy townspeople who had infiltrated the aristocracy, regarded skeptically by the older generation. Ecclesiastics added wit and a bourgeois element to otherwise staid gatherings.Collectively, the society was marked by antiquated beliefs and resistance to change. Conversations returned to familiar themes, members issued immutable judgments, and intellectual life stagnated. New ideas were unwelcome; any outsider who criticized their way of life was quickly labeled a misfit.Before his arrival, Gaston had already been appraised by the local elite: his cousin had disclosed his fortune and family background. At twenty-three, he was welcomed as a promising young man with an income of eighteen thousand livres from land in the Auge valley; his father was negotiating to buy more woodland, further securing their status. Mme. de Sainte-Severe, however, omitted that his elder brother was consumptive and likely dying.At first Gaston amused himself by observing and sketching their quirks, enjoying their quaintness. But weeks of monotony left him weary and trapped in a cloistered existence that drained his spirits. This confinement produced a crisis: ennui and mounting discontent dimmed the vivid memories of Parisian intellectual life, threatening to turn him into just another fossil among the fossils.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening Gaston de Nueil sat between a dowager and a vicar-general in a gray-paneled drawing-room where family portraits overlooked four card tables and sixteen people playing whist. After a pleasant dinner he took in provincial habits—the old cards, threadbare cloths—and their philosophic indifference to luxury, which made Paris seem remote.His attention fell on a young lady with red hands and a shy, awkward manner. A casual remark about Mme. de Beauseant, however, stirred him like a passage of good music in a tedious opera. An elderly guest mentioned that the Vicomtesse was unwell and had declined an invitation to dinner, which piqued Gaston’s curiosity. The conversation disclosed Mme. de Beauseant’s scandalous liaison with M. d’Ajuda-Pinto and her withdrawal to Courcelles. Though of noble birth, she was shunned after separating from her husband—an exile that intrigued him.He became enthralled by the idea of Mme. de Beauseant, imagining her as a focus of hope and desire amid provincial dullness. This obsession made him picture her as a woman worth pursuing in a stagnant world and turned his thoughts to how to gain access, aware of the barriers her intelligence and rank presented.The next morning he wandered near Courcelles, romanticizing a chance meeting. He thought of writing but feared refusal, so instead he resolved to have M. de Champignelles arrange an introduction. He confided to the Marquis that he had a delicate matter to discuss and asked him to inquire whether the Vicomtesse would receive him. Gaston’s charm convinced the Marquis to oblige.M. de Champignelles hurried to Courcelles; his message, though initially resisted, aroused Mme. de Beauseant’s curiosity. After deliberation she agreed to see the young suitor.Gaston, alternately excited and anxious, prepared carefully, believing his appearance would shape her judgment—unaware that wit and character might matter more than looks. Approaching Courcelles he felt both confidence and trepidation: entering a room with one woman seemed more daunting than a crowd. When announced, he entered with a blend of ease and nervousness, ready to face the enigmatic Vicomtesse.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A great fire burned on the hearth despite mild weather, casting a soft light over a young woman in a high‑backed bergère. The Vicomtesse, with graceful movements, stirred the coals, picked up a fallen glove, and motioned to a chair for M. de Nueil. Her charm and poise—an elegant, fastidious careless­ness—set her apart from the automatons he had met during two months’ exile in Normandy; in her presence he felt transported back to Paris and its sophistication.Mme. de Beauseant had fair hair, dark eyes, and a pale complexion that gave her a regal, almost fallen‑angel air; her hair, arranged in a crown of plaits, heightened that noble bearing. Yet beneath her beauty lay an underlying sadness, traces of past unhappiness and lost love. Alone in her silent salon she seemed cut off from the world, haunted by memories of a once‑vibrant youth now emptied. M. de Nueil felt humbled and captivated by this blend of beauty, nobility, and misfortune.When she acknowledged him he felt the weight of his deception. He had come to deliver a message from M. de Champignelles, but the consciousness of having deceived her made him flush. Summoning his courage he confessed, “Madame, I do not deserve the happiness of seeing you. I have deceived you basely.” Instead of mercy she rang for a servant to escort him out; her manner shifted from gracious to icy.As he left he realized that if he went away now he would never return, and that the Vicomtesse would think him a fool. He hesitated, hoping she might repent. On impulse he turned back, saying he had forgotten something; the servant, unaware of the real reason, followed him into the salon.“Jacques lighted me to the door,” he said with a half‑sad smile, which softened Mme. de Beauseant’s demeanor. She invited him to sit; he did so eagerly, his eyes bright with relief. She felt a thrill at his delight, grateful for what she took as his comprehension of her complex emotions.He began, “Madame, my blunder you know, but you do not know how much I am to blame.” She stopped him playfully, then darkened, reflecting on how her life had changed. “This is all very ridiculous! I cannot do as I like; I am obliged to think. What brings you here? Is it curiosity? If so, I am paying dearly for a fleeting pleasure.”She accused him of disrespect and asked whether he had come to pity her—declaring she would rather be scorned than pitied. Tension mounted as she urged him to recognize the impropriety of his conduct and commanded him not to return, fearing gossip and further complications.M. de Nueil acknowledged his fault, attributing it to youthful enthusiasm and a craving for happiness. He explained that exile’s weariness and his inner fires had driven him; he had idealized her as the woman of his dreams and, in that delirious longing for love, committed a foolish intrusion for which he sought indulgence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################An impassioned voice warmed the chill solitude with youthful charm. Mme. de Beauseant, moved by its sincerity, found herself captivated by M. de Nueil’s noble countenance—Gaston, in the bloom of youth, seemed the ideal man, untouched by ambition or vanity. Both were drawn to one another, reflecting on and concealing their feelings. M. de Nueil saw in the Vicomtesse not only beauty but depth and goodness; Mme. de Beauseant, hearing Gaston’s youthful troubles, recognized the struggles of someone kept pure by effort against society’s corruptions.Gaston’s approach was instinctive; Mme. de Beauseant’s had intent—she probed his views with opposing opinions. Her wit and grace put him at ease, prompting his exclamation, “Oh! madame, how could any man have left you?” The remark struck her, reviving a sense of innocence, condemning the world for her past, and offering a kind of absolution that lifted her above former shame.She glanced at the clock and, with a gentle smile, acknowledged the fleeting nature of their encounter. Gaston, sensing its gravity, wished to prolong the moment. Mme. de Beauseant insisted on preserving her solitary life, fearing further connection would tarnish her reputation. Candidly she spoke of a forsaken love and the lessons solitude had taught her.Her eloquence and beauty captivated Gaston, who longed to declare his feelings but feared rejection. Torn between admiration and dread of appearing foolish, he finally confessed his love and offered to heal her old wounds. Mme. de Beauseant, moved yet resolute, reminded him they must remain strangers and spoke of the pain of being unable to follow the man she loved through life’s changes.Afterward Gaston left feeling more distant, uncertain whether her charm was genuine or mere coquetry. At twenty-three he was hampered by diffidence, feeling unworthy and anxious about expression. That night he was restless, writing unsatisfactory letters. The next day he returned to Courcelles at night to avoid being seen and, after much deliberation, penned a heartfelt letter laying bare his deep feelings and the mark she had left on his life, imploring her not to dismiss him.As he awaited her response, doubts gnawed at him; he feared ridicule and wished to retract his words. Overwhelmed, he longed for friends to distract him. In this despair he confronted the harshness of unrequited passion, bearing the weight of youthful longing and the uncertainty of a future without Mme. de Beauseant.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Nueil received a letter from Mme. de Beauseant, sealed with the arms of Burgundy. He locked himself away and read that she was refusing his advances. She explained she had tried to spare him a rebuff and to be candid about her feelings: she would not be moved by youthful passion. Though she cared for him, she did not share his love and would not yield to a blind impulse of the heart. Nearly thirty while he was just over twenty, she argued, they could not know how their feelings would change with age; vows made lightly might become burdens. She feared he might one day resent the sacrifices she required, and she would rather die than endure desertion. Her consolation was in God, not in earthly attachments; she could offer only the friendship of an older woman, untainted by passion. For their sakes she urged him to stay in his country and not ruin his life for an illusion.Gaston de Nueil answered at once with fervor: if he could cease to love her and accept the ordinary life she suggested, he would deserve his fate; his oath of fidelity would be absolved only by death, and she might take his life if she feared remorse.When the messenger returned, he said he had given Mme. la Vicomtesse’s reply to Mme. la Vicomtesse herself, who was about to leave in a carriage with post-horses. Realizing she was going away, Gaston immediately followed her to Geneva, convinced that if she truly cared she would seek the freedom of Switzerland.Mme. de Beauseant had rented a small house by the lake. One summer evening Gaston surprised her; she dropped her book, then embraced him as he told her how he had followed her. For three years they lived in the villa on Lake Geneva, alone and apparently in dreamlike happiness: a simple home, beautiful views, quiet days treasured together.Weighty obligations soon called Gaston back to France after the deaths of his father and brother. They refrained from selling the villa and instead purchased the family house; Mme. de Beauseant sold her property to buy an estate near Manerville, adjoining Gaston’s lands. She and Gaston lived together; his mother took the family house at Manerville, and they resumed the secluded happiness they had known.For nine years they enjoyed an almost indescribable bliss. Yet the legal obstacle remained: Mme. de Beauseant’s husband, the Marquis, was still alive, so any lawful union was impossible. The situation was always precarious. Gaston’s mother, the dowager comtesse, would never accept Mme. de Beauseant; the Marquise feared the dowager’s influence might one day pull Gaston away. Though tempted to sell her estate and return to Geneva, Mme. de Beauseant could not bring herself to act in a way that might betray distrust.The arrival of a young heiress, Mlle. de la Rodiere, nearby, and Gaston’s meeting with her, disturbed the fragile peace. Mme. de Beauseant responded with a letter that revealed her inner turmoil and pride. She confessed that she had not known love until she met him and that, for nine years, she had felt no jealousy and had experienced infinite happiness. But now she was anguished by the thought that, at forty, she might be a burden to his future: he was thirty and might wish a marriage that would bring children and security. She recoiled at the idea that his devotion could be motivated by pity rather than desire.She admitted sensing his hesitation and being unable to hide her tears, yet she had not reproached him. Too proud to bind his future to her own fading life, she urged him to be frank: if he still loved her, he should come after reading the letter and she would love him more than ever; if he sought freedom, she would not display her suffering, because she loved him enough to put his happiness first.When Mme. de Beauseant learned that her letter had reached Gaston, she was overwhelmed and stunned into numbness. Reading her words, M. de Nueil found himself in a painful dilemma—a crisis of choice and conscience that many young men face when passion, duty, pride, and the prospect of a conventional future collide.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By this time Gaston had nearly yielded to his mother’s entreaties and the attraction of Mlle. de la Rodiere, a plain young woman with a forty-thousand-livre dowry. Mme. de Nueil urged a respectable match, praising the honor of being chosen by a woman who had refused many distinguished suitors and reminding him that future land revenues might one day ease his life to eighty thousand livres. She argued that if Mme. de Beauseant truly loved him she should encourage his marriage. Her arguments left him torn.Just as his affection for Mme. de Beauseant resisted convention, a letter from her changed everything: he resolved to end their liaison and marry. “One must live a man’s life,” he told himself, though he knew the choice would wound her. Vanity and conscience deepened his sorrow; he felt genuine pity for her. He meant to prepare her gently for their separation, at first sacrificing Mlle. de la Rodiere to ease the transition, planning to marry later to honor Mme. de Beauseant’s wishes. He intended to write a letter to quiet her suspicions, though he feared a woman of such perception might be devastated by a mere note.When Jacques delivered the letter, Mme. de Beauseant trembled, dread of loss closing in. If he did not come in person, she feared all was lost; yet a woman in love can endure much pain. As she read, an inner voice cried, “He lies!” She saw the message as calculated rather than true, and in despair she tore the letter and cast it into the fire, feeling betrayed and abandoned.M. de Nueil fetched Jacques, who soon returned with a note saying, “Madame la Marquise has left the chateau.” M. de Nueil opened the note and found it was his own letter from nine years earlier, in which he had sworn eternal fidelity to Mme. de Beauseant; at the bottom Claire de Bourgogne had written, “Monsieur, you are free.” Stunned, he went to his mother and, within three weeks, married Mlle. Stephanie de la Rodiere.If the tale ended there it might seem trivial, but its notoriety resonates with those who have known love’s profound joys and losses. After their parting Mme. de Beauseant never left Valleroy. She lived in seclusion, rarely seen except by servants, and attended mass daily.Soon after his marriage M. de Nueil fell into emotional indifference—interpreted variously as happiness or unhappiness. His mother insisted he was content. Mme. Gaston de Nueil, like many young brides, was sweet but shallow; a month after their wedding she expected a child, fitting social expectations. Two months later, however, M. de Nueil grew increasingly pensive.After seven months a seemingly trivial incident revealed deeper turmoil. Returning from a hunt, he crossed Mme. de Beauseant’s park and asked Jacques if she still enjoyed game; Jacques confirmed, and M. de Nueil paid him to set aside whatever he shot for her. For days M. de Nueil returned with an empty bag while Jacques discreetly delivered the game to the Marquise.Eventually M. de Nueil dared to write Mme. de Beauseant, but his letter was returned unopened. One evening, as his wife played the piano, he suddenly rushed to the Marquise’s room and found her in despair, emptied of hope. When she saw him she threatened to throw herself from the window if he approached. Terrified, M. de Nueil fled.Back home he wrote another letter, insisting it was a matter of life and death, and sent it with his servant. An hour later the servant returned with the unopened note. In despair M. de Nueil retired to another room and shot himself.The tragic end, though at odds with young France’s conventions, may have been inevitable to those who have experienced a profound soul-connection. Love requires time and nurturing; abandoning deep love for a loveless marriage brings either despair or a cold, material life. Mme. de Beauseant likely did not foresee that her friend’s anguish might lead to such an extreme act. She may have believed herself the only sufferer, yet she rightly refused humiliation: a wife may compromise for social reasons, but a mistress cannot submit to degradation, for the purity of her passion demands respect.ANGOULEME, September 1832.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy:- Beauseant, Marquis and Comte de: Father Goriot; An Episode under the Terror- Beauseant, Marquise de: Letters of Two Brides- Beauseant, Vicomte de and Vicomtesse de: Father Goriot; Vicomtesse also in Albert Savarus- Champignelles, De: The Seamy Side of History- Jacques (M. de Beauseant’s butler): Father Goriot- Nueil, Gaston de: The Deserted Woman; Albert Savarus############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################La Grenadiere is a quaint house on the right bank of the Loire, about a mile downstream from the bridge of Tours. The broad river, dotted with green islands, flows between cliffs crowned with white stone country houses, gardens and vineyards. With a favorable southern exposure, generations have terraced the cliffs to grow warm-climate fruit.A church spire marks nearby Saint-Cyr; further on the Choisille joins the Loire. La Grenadiere stands halfway up the hillside, roughly a hundred paces from the church. It is an old-fashioned house of two to three hundred years, typical of Touraine. A fissure in the rock provides steps down to the “dike,” the embankment that keeps the Loire in its bed and serves as the Paris–Nantes causeway. At the top of the steps a gate opens onto a narrow footpath between terraces; trees almost conceal the path until the river view widens.A crumbling Gothic archway leads into a small garden hewn from the rock on the highest terrace. A wooden summer-house draped in jessamine and honeysuckle stands nearby. The house sits centrally above a vine-covered flight of steps, with an arched doorway giving access to extensive cellars carved into the rock. Trellised vines and pomegranate trees—the latter giving the property its name—surround the house. The front has two large windows flanking a rustic door and three dormers in a steep slate roof; the walls are yellowwashed with green doors and shutters.Inside, a crooked staircase rises to a lobby. To the right is a large dining room with white tile floors; to the left, a sitting room of similar size with saffron-coloured walls and exposed walnut rafters. The first floor holds two whitewashed bedrooms with simple stone chimneypieces. Most doors and windows face south, except one opening toward the vineyard. A small timber-framed kitchen, a later addition, contains a rustic pump and well, suggesting La Grenadiere began as a vintner’s retreat for townsfolk of Tours who came for picnics or the grape harvest.The property includes two acres of vineyard, with a steep slope at the back. A vinedresser’s cottage leans against the western gable, and stone walls or espaliers enclose and maximize the land. Neglected corners fill with fig trees, wildflowers or strawberries.Modest yet dignified, La Grenadiere is rich in fruit and fragrances. Its vines yield diverse grapes amid figs, peaches and melons, while Spanish broom and Italian oleanders lend an exotic note. The Loire flows below; evening breezes carry sea-scented air and distant flowers. Clouds alter the light, revealing views of the south bank, Tours and hills dotted with châteaux. Owned by a prosperous burgess of Tours, the house appeals as a peaceful retreat for lovers and dreamers: an atmosphere of contentment where the river’s sounds invite quiet contemplation among perennial flowers and fruit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################If an Englishman rents La Grenadiere for the summer, he pays a thousand francs for six months, excluding vineyard produce. If he wants orchard fruit, the rent doubles; for the vintage, it doubles again. What could La Grenadiere be worth? With its stone staircase, triple terrace, two acres of vineyard, flowering roses, and well-head, it seems priceless. It has not been on the market since it was sold in 1690 for forty thousand francs, remaining a cherished family possession. The view from La Grenadiere embraces three valleys of Touraine and cathedral towers, making it impossible to assign a monetary value to the health and beauty it offers.In the spring of a bright year during the Restoration, Mme. Willemsens arrived in Tours with her housekeeper and two children, aged thirteen and eight. She chose La Grenadiere, perhaps for its distance from town. She converted the drawing-room into a bedroom, gave the children the two rooms above, and the housekeeper slept in a closet. The dining-room served as a combined sitting and drawing room, simply and tastefully furnished with plain walnut furniture; the house's charm lay in neatness and harmony rather than luxury.Mme. Willemsens was hard to classify socially; her plain dress invited assumptions, while her manners suggested higher status. Her reserve piqued local curiosity. Tall and slender with delicate features and a melancholy expression, she had dark eyes that sometimes revealed secret anguish and premature wrinkles. She wore a long black gown, a silk ribbon at the waist, and an English-style bonnet, signaling mourning. Her health seemed fragile, and she walked with her children to the bridge of Tours to enjoy fresh air and sunsets.She visited Tours only twice: once to inquire about the best teachers for her children and again to arrange their lessons. Her occasional appearances on the bridge interested townspeople, but little was known of her status. The owner of La Grenadiere mentioned to friends that she signed the lease as Augusta Willemsens, Countess of Brandon, hinting at her husband's name; this remark remained within the business community.To the local elite she remained a mystery. Her natural manners and sweet voice charmed those around her, but her sadness and beauty made her seem untouchable. Several young men fell in love, yet timidity kept them from approaching. She burned all letters she received, emphasizing her wish for solitude and a life free from outside concerns.The three tutors allowed at La Grenadiere admired the close bond between Mme. Willemsens and her children. The boys, resembling their mother, drew envy from local mothers. Louis-Gaston, the elder, had dark hair and a robust demeanor; Marie-Gaston, the younger, had almost golden hair and a delicate appearance. Their contrasting looks matched their temperaments, Louis showing a protective nature toward his brother.Well-behaved and attentive, the boys rose early, prayed, and observed a scrupulous morning routine. Their interactions with their mother were full of affection, laughter, and an intuitive understanding of her needs. Mme. Willemsens fostered a nurturing atmosphere, ensuring her children felt loved and valued. Louis often took charge to protect his brother and noticed signs of his mother's declining strength—small worries she sought to hide—remaining watchful. Their bond was profound, characterized by a shared life of love largely untouched by external influences.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mme. Willemsens dressed during her children’s early breakfast and play so she would be a pleasing sight for them. She listened to their lessons from ten in the morning until three, with a midday lunch in the summer-house where they all ate together. After the meal the children played while she rested on a sofa, enjoying the Touraine landscape.The children roamed the orchard and garden, chasing lizards and examining plants and insects, often returning with eager questions. Mme. Willemsens embroidered during lessons, following attentively without interrupting. She concentrated on her eldest, Louis, encouraging his ambition and asking little about Marie. When lessons ended she would walk the tutor to the gate and inquire frankly about Louis’ progress; the tutors reported his strengths and weaknesses honestly.Their days were a careful balance of work and play, leaving little room for quarrels. Her steady love smoothed difficulties and taught her sons moderation and submission. She often wept for joy watching them play, reflecting on their happiness and her own contentment. The hours in the garden-house were precious, full of laughter and the children’s voices; they spoke both English and French thanks to their English nurse, and their mother guided their learning so they could understand the world. She provided interesting books about great historical figures and used those stories to teach ambition and success.While the children listened, little Marie often fell asleep on her mother’s knee. Afterwards Mme. Willemsens sometimes grew pensive and tearful. One evening Louis noticed her tears; she confessed fears for their future—that she might leave them alone and without resources—and expressed the deep love that made her sorrow so poignant. Louis, puzzled, promised to take care of Marie. She reassured him that love kept her alive and hoped Touraine’s sunny air might help her recover.As she spoke more of her impending death, Louis grew more focused and adjusted the family’s life to her melancholy. The once vibrant household grew quieter; the old servant Annette watched with growing concern as Mme. Willemsens’ health declined. By August their life had changed markedly: Mme. Willemsens remained beautiful but increasingly frail, making an effort to preserve her appearance; the house felt somber and they rarely ventured far. Louis, feeling close to his mother, noticed her exhaustion and suggested shorter walks.With autumn’s sunny days and ripe fruits, Mme. Willemsens spent her time almost entirely with the children. Meanwhile Louis worked late into the night preparing for the École Polytechnique entrance exam. He frequented the bridge of Tours, where a retired naval officer told him tales of adventure and hardship; encouraged by his mother, Louis began long walks, climbed trees and learned to swim, slowly changing from a boy into a young man whose sunburned face reflected a new purpose.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When October arrived, Mme. Willemsens could only rise at noon. The Loire’s warmth made the air almost like the Bay of Naples, and the faculty had recommended the area. At midday she sat in the shade with her two boys, who never left her side. With lessons over they sought a life of closeness, free from outside worries. The elder boy lay beside her, basking in her affection, while Marie, the more restless child, gathered flowers. Mme. Willemsens, with tired eyes and languid movements, never complained, smiling at her vibrant children—a poignant scene touched by autumn’s melancholy.Eventually the doctor ordered her to stay in her room, brightened daily by her favorite flowers, and her children remained near. One early November day she sat at the piano for the last time, gazing at a Swiss landscape above it and her children outside; her fingers moved with passionate feeling. When the doctor arrived he insisted she stay in bed; she was stunned. After he left she asked Louis to take her to the terrace for one last view of her homeland. As they stepped outside her gaze turned skyward, captivated by the clouds. Anguish crossed her face as she clutched her children’s hands, lamenting their uncertain future and fearing how they would judge her life when older. Seeing Louis and Marie kneel beside her, their love plain, she returned to bed, never to rise again.Days blurred with Annette and Louis keeping vigil. On the fifth day the doctor banned flowers from her room—an ominous sign. As her condition worsened Louis refused to leave. One quiet Sunday night she moved the curtain and solemnly told him tomorrow would be her last day. She instructed him to fetch important papers from her table—birth certificates and a letter she wished to send to Lord Brandon, her estranged husband. As she dictated the letter she hesitated over how to sign it, finally choosing “Louis-Gaston.” She revealed she had twelve thousand francs saved—their remaining funds—and urged him to keep the money safe, as everything would be sealed after her death. Louis vowed to care for Marie, planning to send her to school while he apprenticed at sea, determined to return successful.Her eyes brightened at his resolve. Strength fading, she requested a priest. Louis fetched Annette, who found one; the next morning, surrounded by loved ones, Mme. Willemsens received the sacrament with her children kneeling beside her. As her breathing slowed the room fell silent; she passed away, leaving them stunned.Annette and the vinedresser’s wife prepared the body for burial according to local custom. Only a few mourners attended—far fewer than she might have had. Marie, too young to grasp finality, cried as they lowered the coffin. A simple cross marked the grave with a plea for prayers. Afterwards the children returned to La Grenadiere for a last look before leaving with Annette to her cousin’s house in Tours. Louis entrusted Annette with Marie’s birth certificate and the money. The next day he took Marie to school, gave earnest advice, and parted ways.A month later Louis-Gaston, now an apprentice on a warship, watched the French coast fade.
