Alone on the vast ocean, lost yet comforted by an old sailor’s words, he embraced life at sea with resolve to honor his mother’s memory.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ####################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################I have always wanted to tell a simple true story that would terrify two young lovers and drive them to seek refuge in each other’s hearts, like children clinging together at the sight of a snake. Saying so may spoil the tale and make me seem vain.I played a part in this almost ordinary tragedy, so if it fails to engage you, blame me and the mundane nature of reality; life often lacks interest, and the artist must choose from it those elements with poetic potential.In 1819 I was traveling from Paris to Moulins and, for want of money, took an outside seat on the coach. An Englishman, seemingly better off, joined me and we quickly bonded over fresh air and the landscape. Conversation turned to women and love, and we oddly agreed that a birth certificate meant little—some women at thirty-five to forty were younger, in spirit, than girls at twenty. A poet would have found our exchange rich with passion; our shy fears and interruptions had a youthful charm I no longer possess.We celebrated “the woman of a certain age.” As we described our mistresses—young, charming, devoted—my companion revealed his lady was thirty-eight; I confessed to loving a woman of forty. Freed by this mutual admiration, we traded romantic follies, each trying to outdo the other: one had traveled six hundred miles for a brief meeting; the other had risked danger to meet his in secret. My friend’s countess had lighted a cigar for him; mine made chocolate and wrote daily when we were apart. Both countesses were adored by their husbands, whose simplicity lent just enough danger to heighten our pleasure. Then the wind took away our laughter.At Pouilly I observed my new friend closely. He was well proportioned, expressive, dark-haired, blue-eyed, with a pallor as if ill, shapely hands, and a clever air—worthy of a countess. He was a Vicomte with a respectable income; many girls would have desired him.About a league out of Pouilly the coach overturned. He jumped to escape, miscalculated, and was crushed beneath the vehicle. We carried him to a peasant’s cottage where, in agony, he entrusted me with a sacred task: deliver the news of his death to his mistress so she would not learn it from a newspaper. He asked me to retrieve a key worn around his neck; I found it embedded in his flesh. He gave directions to his home in La Charite-sur-Loire for the love letters, then grew speechless and died. The coachman said it was partly his fault, but I was consumed by the duty before me.At La Charite I fulfilled his wishes. His mother was away; an old servant fainted at the news. I took the sealed letters, burdened by the sorrow of a woman who had lost her last love. The Countess’s chateau lay eight leagues beyond Moulins and I had little money, yet youthful resolve propelled me to walk fast, eager to bring the news before anyone else. Nearing the Chateau de Montpersan, unease grew about my role as bearer of such tragic news.When I reached the chateau, the open doors unsettled me. Inside barking dogs and a servant-maid directed me to the park where Madame was. Feeling lost, I was led by a playful little girl who called for her mother as she guided me through winding paths.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I paused behind the last shrub on the avenue, adjusting my shabby clothes so the lady would not mistake me for a local rate collector. Thinking back now amuses me.I spotted Juliette and her husband among the flowers; at her child's ambiguous remark her pace quickened. Surprised by my presence, she regarded me coolly, disappointment visible. I fumbled for the elegant phrases I had prepared, but that hesitation let her husband approach. I awkwardly asked if they were indeed M. le Comte and Mme. la Comtesse de Montpersan, using the questions to size them up. The husband was a typical provincial nobleman: large, self-important, badly mannered, more pretension than polish, led by his wife while fancying himself master.The Countess was the opposite: petite, graceful, dressed in white muslin with rose-colored accents, youthful and lively — the sort any Romantic reader would recognize. Choosing diplomacy, I took the Count aside with an air of mystery and requested a private word. He obliged, confident his wife could learn his secrets at will, and left her to wander.I told the Count of my traveling companion's death and of his genuine affection. Concerned for his wife, he urged caution. I explained I could not approach the Countess without first informing him, emphasizing the sacredness of the message entrusted to me. Flattered, he allowed me to proceed.As we neared the house, Juliette glanced at us with a mix of curiosity and disdain, likely due to my appearance and our unexpected presence. I recalled her beauty in the sunlit alley and sighed."Alas, madame, I have made a difficult journey solely for you," I began. Her face paled."Is he ill?" she asked."Yes, but control yourself. He entrusted me with secrets concerning you; I am a discreet messenger.""What is wrong with him?" she pressed."What if he no longer loves you?" I replied."That is impossible!" she exclaimed, a faint smile flickering before panic. "Is he alive?"I could only nod. Distress overwhelmed her."Give me an answer!" she cried."Yes, madame.""Is it true? Tell me the truth! Any pain would be better than this suspense."I could only respond with tears; she leaned against a tree, crying out in despair."Your husband is coming!" I warned."Do I have a husband?" she fled.The Count called me to dinner; the Countess was absent. The canon of Saint-Denis inquired after her, but the Count shrugged and hastily served the lavish meal. After a while a maid reported the Countess missing. Alarmed, I rushed out with the canon and the maid to search the garden and park. As we called, I recounted the tragic accident; the maid's concern for her mistress was evident.At last we heard a muffled cry from a granary. Inside we found Juliette buried in hay, sobbing. The maid and I helped her to her room; I instructed that she not be disturbed, claiming she had a headache.Returning to the dining room, I was surprised the Count still ate, seemingly unfazed. His indifference was explained: a serious medical condition had left him ravenous, overshadowing his concern for Juliette. The evening was somber; exhausted, we retired early.Passing the Countess's room, I asked the servant for news. Juliette called me in, tried to speak but could not. I left, feeling the weight of her agitation.Later that night I was awakened by the sound of curtain rings. The Countess sat at the foot of my bed, pale and drawn."Is it true?" she asked, voice trembling. "I don't know how I can live after this blow, but I want to know everything."She had lost her youthful glow; her eyes were red and swollen. I briefly recounted the accident, avoiding distressing details. When I related the dying man's fears and the message he had entrusted to me, her tears dried into a deep pallor. I handed her letters from him; she took them mechanically, lamenting that she had burned all his other letters and now had nothing."I cut this lock of his hair," she said, revealing a precious token. I gave it to her; her tears fell on my hands, a moment of profound gratitude and connection."Ah! You love! May you always be happy," she said, clutching the lock and fleeing with her treasure.The next morning felt surreal. I searched under my pillow for the letters but found nothing. I spent the day with Juliette, witnessing her noble spirit and selflessness.That evening the Comte de Montpersan accompanied me to Moulins and asked if I could deliver a sum of money to a contact in Paris. I agreed, taking a rouleau of twenty-five louis d'or, which covered my journey back. On arrival I appreciated the delicate way Juliette had arranged the loan, revealing the depth of her love and thoughtfulness. What a joy to share this adventure with a woman who cherished me, saying, "Oh, my dear, not you! You must not die!"############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo M. le Baron Barchou de Penhoen.Of all the pupils of the Oratorian school at Vendome, we are, I think, the only two who have since met in mid-career of a life of letters. When we met, you were engaged on your works on German philosophy, and I on this study. So neither of us has missed his vocation; and you, seeing your name here, will feel as much pleasure as I do in inscribing my work to you. Your old schoolfellow, 1840 De Balzac.GOBSECKIt was one o’clock in the morning in the winter of 1829–30, and in the Vicomtesse de Grandlieu’s salon two guests not of her family remained. A young man took his leave; as the carriage died away, the Vicomtesse turned to her daughter Camille, who stood by the chimney-piece ostensibly looking at a fire-screen while listening to courtyard sounds, stirring maternal anxiety.“Camille,” she said, “if you continue to treat young Comte de Restaud as you have this evening, I shall have to prevent him from visiting. Trust my love and let me guide you. At seventeen one cannot judge the past or the future or social standings. M. de Restaud has a mother, Mlle. Goriot, who squandered her father’s fortune and is of no birth. Though he behaves admirably toward her and his siblings, any family would hesitate to entrust their daughter’s future to him while she lives.”“I overheard part of your conversation with Mlle. de Grandlieu,” interposed Derville, a family friend. “I have won, M. le Comte,” he added to the departing man. “I shall assist your niece.”“How could you know what I was saying to Camille?” exclaimed the Vicomtesse.“From your looks,” Derville replied, settling by the fire.Camille’s uncle approached and the Vicomtesse sat on a hearth-stool between them.“It’s time for a story that may change your view of Ernest de Restaud’s prospects,” Derville said.“A story?” Camille cried. “Please begin!”The tale was meant for the Vicomtesse. By birth and fortune she was a leading figure of the Faubourg Saint-Germain; that an attorney could be so intimate with her seemed odd until the facts were known.After returning to France with the Royal family, the Vicomtesse had lived on a pension from Louis XVIII, a state she found intolerable. The Hotel de Grandlieu had been sold during the Revolution. Derville discovered defects in the title, pursued legal action, and reclaimed it for her. He also secured judgments that restored significant property, raising her income to about sixty thousand francs plus indemnities. Derville, a man of integrity and modesty, became a close family confidant.His management of the Vicomtesse’s affairs gained him esteem in the Faubourg and brought many aristocratic clients; yet he did not exploit this popularity for personal advancement. The Vicomtesse urged him to accept a magistracy; he declined, preferring to keep his practice and social ties. His steadfast service revealed his talents, and since Ernest de Restaud’s arrival—especially as Camille showed interest—Derville had been visiting the Hotel de Grandlieu more often.At a recent ball Derville had taunted Camille: “It’s a pity that young man doesn’t have two or three million francs, isn’t it?”“Is it a pity? I don’t think so,” she answered. “M. de Restaud is capable and well educated. He will have as much money as he wishes when he comes into power.”“But what if he were already rich?” Derville persisted.“Rich already?” Camille blushed. “All the girls would be vying for him,” she said, watching the dancers.“Then Mlle. de Grandlieu might not be the one he favors,” Derville teased. “That blush says it all! You like him, don’t you? Speak up.”Camille rose to leave.“She loves him,” Derville thought.Since that evening Camille had been unusually attentive to Derville, who approved of her feelings for Ernest. Though grateful for his services to her family, she had shown respect rather than warmth; gratitude often weighs on the next generation, which may seek to escape it.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“This adventure,” Derville began, “recalls the one romantic episode of my life. It may seem odd that an attorney speaks of romance, yet once, at twenty-five, having seen strange things, I met a man I shall describe—a usurer whom you cannot know.“Imagine a sallow, wan face—what I would call lunar. His iron-gray hair was sleek and carefully combed; his features were as impassive as Talleyrand’s. Little yellow eyes, without eyelashes, peered from under a shabby cap, as if afraid of light. He had thin lips like Rembrandt’s alchemists and a gimlet nose. His voice was low, suave, and never rose. You could not tell his age; he seemed either prematurely aged or unnaturally preserved.“His room matched him—clean but threadbare, like an elderly spinster’s. In winter the fire smoldered in ashes, never making a flame. He moved through his day with a pendulum’s regularity, stopping mid-sentence when a cart rolled by to spare his voice. He hoarded sensation, sparing every pulse. His life flowed soundlessly like hourglass sands; his victims sometimes raged, then fell silent—like a kitchen after a fowl’s neck is wrung.“Yet toward evening this human bill of exchange would become animate: his money turned to a heart, and when he had finished his day’s business he would rub his hands; inward glee creased his face into a silent laugh. Even in joy he spoke in monosyllables, with an inscrutable expression.“He was my neighbor in the Rue de Grès while I was a second clerk finishing my legal studies. The house was damp and dark, all windows facing the street, like an old convent; its gloom smothered even the eldest sons’ gaiety before they reached his door. He and his lodging were alike; the oyster resembles its rock. I was the only one he spoke to: borrowing a light or a book, sometimes permitting me to visit in the evenings. These small courtesies resulted from four years of neighborly contact and my sober conduct. I lived nearly like him from lack of funds. No one could say whether he had relatives or friends, whether he was rich or poor; I never saw money in his room—his capital was in the Bank. He collected debts himself, running all over Paris on skinny legs. Once, when gold fell from his pocket, another lodger returned it.“‘That isn’t mine!’ he cried, surprised. ‘If I were rich, would I live as I do?’“He made coffee every morning on a cast-iron chafing dish; his dinners came from a cookshop, and our old porter’s wife tidied his room on schedule. A whimsical chance named him Gobseck. Later, when I did business for him, I learned he was about seventy-six when we met—born about 1740 near Antwerp, the son of a Dutch father and Jewish mother. His full name was Jean-Esther Van Gobseck. You may recall the murder trial of La belle Hollandaise; when I mentioned it, he said without interest, ‘She is my grandniece.’ That was the only remark he made about the death of his sole surviving relative. She had been called Sara Van Gobseck; when I asked why his grandniece bore his name, he smiled, ‘Women never marry in our family.’“He had never cared to trace relatives beyond four generations of the female line. The thought of heirs disgusted him; the idea of his wealth passing to others after his death was intolerable.“Sent away at ten as a cabin boy to the Dutch Straits, he spent twenty years abroad. The inscrutable lines on his sallow brow concealed adventures, panics, sudden luck, and squandered fortunes. He had known Admiral Simeuse, M. de Lally, Tippoo Sahib, and notorious pirates in St. Thomas, had hunted treasure in Buenos Ayres, and observed the American War of Independence. Yet when he spoke of the Indies or America, he did so reluctantly, as if it were indiscreet. If sociability is a religion, Gobseck was an infidel; despite my efforts, his nature remained opaque.“Was he faithful to his mother’s religion? Had he converted? Did he view Gentiles as prey? I never learned his religious opinions; he appeared indifferent rather than unbelieving.“One evening I visited him. He sat motionless in his armchair, staring at the mantel, as if reading his statements. A lamp burned behind him, making his face look even paler. He gestured to a chair but said nothing. What could such a being think? Did he know God, feeling, women, happiness? I felt pity for him, like pity for a diseased creature. Yet I knew that while millions were at his command, he possessed the world in idea—having explored, ransacked, appraised, and exploited it.“‘Good day, Daddy Gobseck,’ I said.“He turned, contracting his bushy black eyebrows in a look as expressive as a Southern smile. ‘You look as gloomy as when you heard that bookseller you admired had failed, though you were one of his victims.’“‘One of his victims?’ he repeated.“‘Yes. You refused composition at the creditors’ meeting until he promised to pay you in full. He gave you bills accepted by the bankrupt firm. When he resumed business, he paid you the dividend on those bills.’“‘He was sharp, but I got it out of him.’“‘Do you have bills to protest? Today is the 30th, I believe.’“It was the first time I’d mentioned money to him. He looked at me ironically, then said blandly, ‘I am amusing myself.’“‘So you amuse yourself now and again?’“‘Do you think the only poets are those who print their verses?’ he replied, pityingly.“‘Poetry in that head!’ I thought, knowing little of his life.“‘What life can be as glorious as mine?’ he asked, eyes lighting. ‘You are young; your visions are colored by blood. You see women’s faces in the fire; I see coals. You have beliefs; I have none. Keep your illusions if you can. I’ll show you life with the discount taken off. You can go wherever you like or stay with your wife, but eventually you settle into a groove. Happiness consists in applying your faculties to realities. Anything more is false. My principles have varied with different men and latitudes. What we admire in Europe is punishable in Asia; a vice in Paris becomes a necessity elsewhere. There are no hard-and-fast rules, only conventions adapted to climate. Throw a man into various social melting pots and convictions become meaningless. The only constant is self-interest. If you lived as long as I have, you would know the only concrete reality worth caring about is GOLD. Gold means every form of human power. I’ve traveled and found hills or plains everywhere; place is irrelevant. Manners are universal; the battle between poor and rich is inevitable. It is better to exploit than be exploited. Pleasures are the same, and when sensations are exhausted, only Vanity remains. Vanity is satisfied only by gold. Our dreams need time and means to be realized; gold contains everything in embryo; it realizes all things for us.“‘Only fools find pleasure in shuffling cards for a few pence. Only dupes think they’re useful making political rules amid unpredictable events. Only simpletons delight in discussing actors and repeating their sayings. What is all this but Parisian life? Let us seek a higher outlook: happiness in strong emotions that exhaust us or in methodical occupation that makes existence clockwork. A higher happiness is noble curiosity to learn Nature’s secrets, or to imitate Nature. In two words: Science and Art, or passion and calm. Every human passion, heightened by the struggle for existence, parades before me while I live in calm. As for scientific curiosity, I replace it with insight into the springs of action in man and woman. In short, the world is mine without effort and holds no claim on me. Listen,’ he continued, ‘I will tell you about my morning, and you’ll see my pleasures.’“He rose, bolted the door, drew a tapestry across it, and sat again.“‘This morning,’ he said, ‘I had only two amounts to collect; the rest of the bills due I gave away rather than cash yesterday. So much saved—when I discount a bill I always deduct two francs for a hired brougham; it’s absurd for my clients to make me trudge all over Paris for a few francs of discount when no man is my master, and I only pay seven francs in taxes.“‘The first bill for a thousand francs was presented by a dandy—spangled waistcoat, eyeglass, tilbury, English horse. The bill bore the signature of one of the prettiest women in Paris, the wife of a Count, a great landowner. How did that Countess come to sign a bill of exchange, legally worthless but practically sound? Such women, poor things, fear the scandal of a protested bill and would rather pay than fail. I wanted to know what that bill truly meant—stupidity, imprudence, love, or charity?“‘The second bill, signed “Fanny Malvaut,” came from a linen-draper near bankruptcy. No one with bank credit comes to me. The first step to my door means despair; the news of failure will soon break, and he has been everywhere else first. When I see the stag at bay, a pack of creditors follows. The Countess lived in the Rue du Helder, and my Fanny in the Rue Montmartre. I made conjectures as I set out. If these two women could not pay, they would have more respect than their fathers. What tricks would the Countess try for a thousand francs? She would be so nice to me, speaking in that ingratiating tone of endorsers, pouring out coaxing words, while I...’ (he turned his pale eyes on me)—‘and I would remain unmoved, inexorable! So much for hypotheses. I reached the house.“‘“Madame la Comtesse is asleep,” said the maid.“‘“When can I see her?”“‘“At twelve o’clock.”“‘“Is the Comtesse ill?”“‘“No, sir, but she returned home at three o’clock this morning from a ball.”“‘“My name is Gobseck; tell her I’ll call again at twelve,” I said, leaving muddy footprints on the carpet. I like leaving mud on a rich man’s carpet—not spitefully, but as a reminder of Necessity. In the Rue Montmartre, I entered a poor house and looked into a dark courtyard where sunlight never reached. The porter’s lodge was grimy, the window like a shabby gown—greasy and full of holes.“‘“Mlle. Fanny Malvaut?”“‘“She’s gone out; but if you’ve come about a bill, the money is waiting with the porter’s wife.”“‘“I’ll come again,” I said.“‘As soon as I knew the porter had the money, I wanted to know what the girl was like; I pictured her as pretty. I spent the rest of the morning looking at prints along the boulevard; just as it struck twelve, I entered the Countess’s antechamber.“‘“Madame has just rung for me,” said the maid; “I don’t think she can see you yet.”“‘“I’ll wait,” I said, sitting in an easy chair.“‘Venetian shutters opened and the maid returned.“‘“Come in, sir.”“‘From the soft tone I knew the mistress was not ready to pay. What a woman I saw! She had thrown an Indian shawl over her bare shoulders—covering herself while revealing the outline beneath. A loose gown trimmed with ruffles hinted that her laundress’s bills amounted to some two thousand francs a year. Dark curls escaped a bright handkerchief knotted carelessly on her head. The bed lay disorderly, suggesting broken slumber. A painter would have paid to see the scene: a crushed pillow in an eiderdown quilt, satin slippers on a bearskin rug, a crumpled gown, twisted stockings, ribbon garters, a half-open fan on the chimney-piece. Drawers stood open; flowers, diamonds, gloves, a bouquet, a girdle were scattered about. A vague sweet perfume filled the room. Beneath luxury and disorder, I saw Misery lurking—she felt those fangs. Her tired face epitomized the room strewn with relics of past revelry. The scattered gewgaws, pitiable this morning, had turned heads the night before.“‘What efforts to drink from the Tantalus cup of bliss I read in these traces of love struck by remorse—in this visible account of a life of luxury and riot. There were faint red marks on her young face, signs of fine skin; her features were slightly hardened, and dark circles ringed her eyes. Yet Nature was vigorous; these traces of folly did not mar her beauty. Her eyes glittered. She resembled a da Vinci Herodias: magnificently alive and energetic; nothing in her was starved. She awakened desire; it seemed a passion stronger than love burned in her. I was taken with her. It had been long since my heart had throbbed; I was paid then and there—for I would give a thousand francs for a sensation that brought back youth’s memory.“‘“Monsieur,” she said, finding a chair for me, “will you be so good as to wait?”“‘“Until this time tomorrow, madame,” I replied, folding the bill. “I cannot legally protest this bill any sooner.” Inwardly I thought, “Pay for your luxury, your name, your comfort, your monopoly! The rich have invented judges and courts to secure their goods; the guillotine gapes under silk, and remorse grinds teeth beneath smiles; the fantastical lions’ jaws await to sink into your heart.”“‘“Protest the bill! Can you mean it?” she cried, eyes on me. “Could you have so little consideration for me?”“‘“If the King himself owed me money and did not pay, I would summon him even sooner than any other debtor.”“‘While we spoke, someone tapped softly at the door.“‘“I cannot see anyone,” she cried imperiously.“‘“But, Anastasie, I particularly wish to speak to you.”“‘“Not just now, dear,” she answered softer, but without relenting.“‘“What nonsense! You are talking to someone,” said the voice, and in came a man who could be only the Count.“‘The Countess glanced at me. I saw how it was; she was utterly in my power. There was a time when I might have been foolish enough not to protest. At Pondicherry in 1763, I once let a woman off and she repaid me. I deserved it; why trust her?“‘“What does this gentleman want?” asked the Count.“‘I could see the Countess trembling; the white satin of her throat stood rough with gooseflesh. I laughed inwardly without moving a muscle.“‘“This gentleman is one of my tradesmen,” she said.“‘The Count turned his back; I drew the bill halfway from my pocket. After that inexorable movement, she came over and placed a diamond in my hand. “Take it,” she said, “and be gone.”“‘We exchanged values, and I bowed and left. The diamond was worth twelve hundred francs to me. Outside, a swarm of flunkeys brushed their liveries, polishing sumptuous equipages.“‘“This is what brings these people to me!” I thought. “They steal millions with all due formalities to maintain this lifestyle. The great lord and the little man who apes him bathe in mud to save a splash or two when walking through the streets.”“‘At that moment the great gates opened and a cabriolet admitted the young fellow who had brought the bill.“‘“Sir,” I said as he alighted, “here are two hundred francs, which I beg you to return to Mme. la Comtesse, and please tell her I hold the pledge she deposited with me this morning for a week.”“‘He took the two hundred francs and smiled ironically, as if to say, “Aha! So she’s paid, has she? ... Faith, so much the better!” I read the Countess’s future in his face. That fair, handsome young man is a heartless gambler; he will ruin himself, her, her husband, and the children—devouring their portions and wreaking more havoc in Parisian salons than a battery of howitzers.“‘I returned to see Mlle. Fanny in the Rue Montmartre, climbed a steep staircase, and reached a neat two-room dwelling on the fifth floor. Everything was as tidy as a new ducat. In the first room Mlle. Fanny sat—neatly dressed, with a delicate fresh face and a winning look. Her neatly brushed chestnut hair formed a double curve on her forehead, lending refinement to her clear blue eyes. Daylight through short curtains softened her girlish face. A pile of shaped linen showed she was a sempstress. She looked like a spirit of solitude. When I offered the bill, she said the porter’s wife had the money. I pretended not to understand.“‘“You go out early, mademoiselle, it seems.”“‘“I very seldom leave my room; but when you work all night, you must bathe sometimes.”“‘I looked at her. A glance revealed a life of toil and misfortune—honest farmer folk origins, a freckle or two, an air of goodness. There was an indefinable sincerity about her; it was refreshing. Poor child, she had faith in something; a crucifix and a sprig of green box adorned her little painted bedstead. I felt inclined to charge no more than twelve percent, to help her establish a good business.“‘But perhaps she had a cousin who would raise money on her signature and sponge on her,’ I thought.“‘So I restrained my generosity; benevolence, when unchecked, often ruins those who take it. I have often thought Fanny Malvaut would make a nice wife; I compared her pure, lonely life with the Countess’s—already sunk to a bill of exchange; she will sink further before she is done!’ I paused, then he spoke again. ‘Well,’ he said, ‘do you think it nothing to have this power to see into the deepest recesses of the human heart, to embrace many lives, to see the naked truth? There are no two dramas alike: hideous sores, deadly griefs, love scenes, misery soon lying under the Seine, young men’s joys leading to the scaffold, laughter of despair, and sumptuous banquets. Yesterday was a tragedy: a father drowned himself because he could not support his family. Tomorrow will be a comedy: some young man will attempt a theatrical scene. You have heard people praise eloquent preachers; I have wasted time on them; they might change opinions but not conduct. These priests and your Mirabeaus and Vergniauds are mere stammerers compared to my orators.“‘Often a girl in love, a gray-headed merchant on the verge of bankruptcy, a mother concealing a son’s wrongdoing, a starving artist, or a great man whose influence wanes comes to me. Their pleading makes me shudder. Sublime actors play for me, for an audience of one, and they cannot deceive me. I read their inmost thoughts as God reads them. Nothing is hidden from me. The holder of the purse-strings can loose and bind. I am rich enough to buy the consciences of those who control ministers, from office boys to mistresses. Is that not power? I can possess the fairest women, receive soft caresses; is that not Pleasure? Your whole social economy is summed up in Power and Pleasure.“‘There are ten of us in Paris—silent, unknown kings, arbiters of destinies. What is life but a machine set in motion by money? Methods are confounded with results; you cannot separate soul from senses, spirit from matter. Gold is the spiritual basis of society. We ten meet at the Café Thémis near the Pont Neuf; we reveal finance’s mysteries. No fortune deceives us; we possess family secrets in all directions. We keep a Black Book noting important bills, drafts on public credit, or bank instruments. We are the casuists of the Paris Bourse, an Inquisition weighing every man of fortune’s actions; our forecasts are infallible. One of us watches the judicial world, another the financial, another the administrative, another commerce. I keep an eye on eldest sons, artists, high society, and gamblers—on Paris’s most sensational side. Everyone who comes reveals neighbors’ secrets. Thwarted passion and mortified vanity are great babblers. Vice, disappointment, and vindictiveness are the best detectives. My colleagues and I have had all things, are sated, and love power and money for their own sake.“‘Here,’ he said, indicating his bare, chilly room, ‘the most high-mettled gallant who would draw a sword elsewhere will entreat with clasped hands. No city merchant is so proud, no woman so vain, no soldier so bold, but they all entreat me here, with tears of rage or anguish. Here they kneel—the famous artist, the man of letters whose name may go down to posterity. In short, all inheritances and concerns of Paris are weighed in the balance. Do you still think there are no delights behind that blank mask which has so often amazed you?’ he asked, stretching out his livid face that reeked of money.“I returned to my room feeling stunned. The little wizened old man had grown great. He had become a strange visionary symbol—the power of gold personified. I shrank from life and my kind.“Is it really so? must everything be resolved into gold? It was long before I slept that night. I saw heaps of gold surrounding me. My thoughts were filled with the lovely Countess; I confess, to my shame, that she eclipsed another quiet, innocent figure—the woman who toiled in obscurity. But through the clouds of slumber the next morning Fanny’s sweet face rose before me in all its beauty, and I thought of nothing else.”############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Will you take a glass of eau sucrée?” the Vicomtesse asked, interrupting Derville.“I should be glad of it.”“But I see nothing here that concerns us,” Mme. de Grandlieu said, ringing the bell.“Sardanapalus!” cried Derville. “Mademoiselle Camille’s happiness once depended on Daddy Gobseck; but with his death at ninety, M. de Restaud will soon inherit a fortune. This needs explanation. As for poor Fanny Malvaut, you know her; she is my wife.”“Poor fellow, he would admit that, with his usual frankness, in front of a crowd!” said the Vicomtesse.“I would proclaim it to the universe,” said the attorney.“I left you in the Rue du Helder,” the uncle murmured, waking. “You had gone to see a Countess; what became of her?”Derville resumed. After his conversation with the old miser, he submitted his thesis, became a licentiate in law, then an advocate. Gobseck’s opinion of him improved; the old man consulted him free on delicate matters. Derville rose from clerk to head-clerk, moved into his employer’s house, and at last earned 150 francs a month. He went to bid the usurer goodbye; Gobseck showed no sentiment but a glance that implied second sight. Shortly after, his old neighbor continued to consult him as before.Derville’s principal, a man of pleasure, fell into financial trouble and had to sell his practice for 150,000 francs. As the seventh child of a small tradesman from Noyon, Derville had neither money nor connections—only Daddy Gobseck. He resolved not to beg but to face Gobseck boldly. The miser, who now took Derville’s old room and scrutinized visitors through a grated window, guessed why he had come.Derville explained the opportunity: the practice yielded about 20,000 francs a year and might bring 40,000 under his management; with a ten-year repayment plan, he could buy it for 150,000 if Gobseck would lend the money. Gobseck appraised him—no guarantees, age soon twenty-five—then agreed to consider it. The next morning he consented to the loan on his usual terms: while he sometimes made vast returns, for acquaintances he’d be content with twelve and a half percent—thirteen percent, as he corrected himself. Derville accepted, resolved to earn the money, and indignantly refused to pass fees to clients to pay interest.Gobseck promised to pay the seller directly, to secure the lien, and demanded that Derville give bills for the amount—fifteen bills of ten thousand francs each—payable to a third party left unnamed. Derville hesitated over why the birth certificate had been needed; Gobseck, smiling maliciously, answered that youth’s integrity and brains are commodities that can be mortgaged—after thirty, a man’s value becomes uncertain. He then set further conditions: Derville would give him legal advice without fees for life (so long as it cost no outlay), let Gobseck visit him, not live ostentatiously, and see him at dinner on Wednesdays and Saturdays after “Change” at five. Gobseck promised to recommend him, send him business, and teach him “to know men, and what is more—women!” The old man delighted in the idea of investing capital in Derville’s head and watching it prosper.Three months later Derville was an attorney. He took on and won the suit to recover Mme. de Grandlieu’s estates; his name spread. Despite Gobseck’s enormous interest, Derville repaid the loan in under five years. He married Fanny Malvaut, whom he loved; through hard work, shared fortune, and a timely inheritance—one of her uncles left her seventy thousand francs—they cleared the loan. Derville summarized his happiness and then, with a dash of self-deprecation, said nothing was more uninteresting than a happy man, and returned to the other characters.About a year after buying the practice, he was pulled into a bachelor breakfast given as the forfeit of a lost bet. Among the guests was M. de Trailles, the consummate dandy: graceful in clothes and tandem, a master of taste, gambler and consumer of society’s pleasures, spending perhaps a hundred thousand francs a year with no visible capital. A brilliant but frivolous figure, Trailles could charm or ruin, be noble or base, but he most often skated on the surface of life.Derville had tried to avoid Trailles, having heard much about him, but his friend Goriot insisted. A bachelor breakfast, Derville explained, is a display of sumptuousness that dissolves into a chaotic orgy: neatness and refinement give way after hours to broken glasses, ruined linen, loud jesting, songs, quarrels, smashed bottles, and a cacophony of voices—an exhausted Bacchanal. Among this pandemonium Trailles, cool and calculating beneath an affected drunken air, engaged Derville and by evening secured his promise to introduce him to Papa Gobseck. Trailles’s golden tongue had mixed words of honor, virtue, and urgent misfortune; he had portrayed a young woman’s reputation and marriage at stake unless fifty thousand francs could be procured that morning.The next day Derville, uneasy but bound by his word, accompanied Trailles to Gobseck. Trailles, outwardly courtly, was inwardly agitated; when they reached the Rue de Gres he grew nervous, glancing about with sweat on his forehead. At Gobseck’s, Derville vouched for him; Gobseck said he had no money except for his own clients’ business. Trailles sought to convince him by boasting of fashionable partners and foreign revenues, quipping that spendthrifts and usurers were mutually dependent. Gobseck responded coldly: Girard, Palma, Werbrust, Gigonnet—other creditors—were full of Trailles’s paper and offered it at heavy losses; he would not lend a stiver to a man who owed thirty thousand francs and had nothing.A cab arrived; Trailles left and returned shortly with a woman on his arm—one of Goriot’s daughters, the Countess. Derville had been watching unseen at the window; he saw the Countess enter Gobseck’s damp room with a look of distress hidden beneath proud features. She shuddered the instant she recognized Derville, and Derville felt pity for her despite her social brilliance and moral failings. Trailles had become her evil genius, ruling her by vanity and other strings.The Countess tremulously asked whether it was possible to obtain the value of her diamonds while retaining the right of repurchase, and handed over a jewel-case. Derville stepped forward and explained that this would be a sale with faculty of redemption—a formal transfer with a stipulated term allowing the previous owner to recover their title by paying a set sum. The Count breathed freer; the Count looked black, fearing Gobseck would undervalue the stones after their fall in price.Gobseck, impassive, took up his magnifying glass and inspected the jewels. Derville never forgot the look that came over the old man—an unnatural glitter in his eyes as he rose and went to the light, holding the diamonds close to his toothless mouth, as if to devour them. He handled bracelets, sprays, necklaces, and tiaras like a child or a doting miser, mumbling, letting the light draw out every fire. That flash of greed, the only time Gobseck’s heart seemed to open, shocked Derville and left a lasting impression.Earlier, when Trailles first argued his case, Gobseck had spoken with a mixture of irony and calculation. He mocked the idea of ruin for a man who seemed to have nothing, observed that the young man’s bills were being circulated at losses by other lenders, and made it plain he wouldn’t be fooled. Yet when Trailles returned with the Countess and her diamonds, Gobseck’s mood changed; he rose in triumph, stretching out his arms to Derville and calling the arrival “Life” itself, as if the security had saved him. He admitted he had been armed and wary, unwilling to trust the elegant scoundrel alone. But now, having the jewels, he felt exultant—his ecstasy frightful in its intensity.Gobseck then told Derville to stay; he was pleased at the prospect of outwitting his rivals, Werbrust and Gigonnet, and anticipated laughing at them that night. He sat again before his bureau, face reverting to the pale, impassive mask that made him resemble a statue. Yet the sight of the Countess—so beautiful, suffering, and controlled by Trailles—had barely registered in him beyond the value of her jewels. Derville reflected that Trailles had become the Countess’s tormentor: a young angel-faced man who, by charm and calculation, governed her life and misled her into degrading necessity.Mme. de Grandlieu interposed, lamenting how the Countess’s virtues had been subverted to destroy her—how Trailles had turned her devotion into a weapon and made her pay for her pleasures. Derville, however, felt no urge to weep for her; he shuddered at the sight of her murderer, the glittering youth whose angelic look masked moral ruin. In Gobseck’s room, they stood before the old man as if before a judge; Gobseck peered at them like an inquisitor measuring guilt and bargaining worth.The Countess’s plea and Gobseck’s examination revealed the essential truth Derville had learned in his dealings with the old man: Gobseck’s judgments were never sentimental—people were valued for what they could secure and what they promised. The bargain Gobseck struck with Derville earlier—interest, anonymity of bills, visits at dinner, unpaid counsel—underscored a system in which personal ties and youthful promise were commodified, and where financial life depended as much on shrewd calculation and social maneuver as on law or sentiment.That evening, Gobseck’s slight intoxication of triumph at possessing the diamonds contrasted with his usual calculating reserve. He had no tenderness for the Countess beyond the stones she offered; his joy at the prospect of outwitting other lenders, and at the glitter held to his hungry gaze, revealed the fusion of avarice and childish delight in him. For Derville, who had benefited from Gobseck’s money but never entirely from his heart, the image was emblematic: the usurer’s power came not only from capital but from a cold, almost scientific knowledge of men’s weaknesses—knowledge he wielded to acquire dominion, and to profit emotionally as well as financially from the commodities people brought him, whether practices, promises, or jewels.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“Fine stones! This set would have fetched three hundred thousand francs before the Revolution. What water! Genuine Asiatic diamonds from Golconda or Visapur. Do you know their worth? No one in Paris but Gobseck can appreciate them. In the Empire, such a set would have cost another two hundred thousand francs!”Gobseck’s eyes shone as he handled the case. He alternated between rapture and his usual hard, calculating precision. The Count wanted a loan of one hundred thousand francs for three years, offering the diamonds as security. I asked whether the jewels were the Countess’s personal property; she affirmed with pride. When Gobseck moved to draw up a deed, I intervened: as a married woman she lacked the legal right to alienate property without her husband’s consent, so any deed of purchase would be void on its face. Gobseck shifted tactics—possession being as good as title—and offered eighty thousand francs if the couple left the diamonds with him.The Count’s pride flared; the Countess seemed stupefied. Maxime, her lover, tried bluster; Gobseck, unmoved, produced a cheque for fifty thousand francs on the Bank of France and thirty thousand in protested drafts, and even two pistols to underline his implacable temper. Cornered, they accepted. Gobseck exulted over obtaining such valuable stones “tolerably cheaply”; his ferocious delight in possession chilled me.Soon after, a furious Count burst into Gobseck’s room accusing him of buying his family diamonds. Gobseck replied with his usual implacability: if Mme. la Comtesse could sign bills, she could engage in business; whether they were the Count’s diamonds could not be proven without bringing the wife into court and exposing her. He mocked the Count’s authority; our dispute boiled down to law: in equity perhaps, legally no. I advised the Count to come to terms with Gobseck—pay for the diamonds or agree to a redemption clause—since litigation would be ruinous and humiliating, and the buyer could claim good faith. With my help, the Count signed an acknowledgement of receipt of eighty-five thousand francs, including interest, in consideration of Gobseck’s promise to return the stones.A few days later the Count returned to me in agitation and, in a fit of fear and contrivance, asked me to draw papers for him to transfer his estate to Gobseck as a simulated sale, with Gobseck to hold it in trust and later restore it to the eldest son. He feared his son’s favoritism for his mother and distrusted the boy’s future loyalty. I warned him such a counter-deed risked disinheriting other children; he wept and agreed to provide for them in the instrument. I believed it a just, if desperate, precaution.The deed was signed but not delivered to me. Gobseck, dining at my house months later, hinted that the Count was dying and that he and I were the only ones trusted by him. I resolved to visit the Count, fearing what might happen to the counter-deed. At the Count’s lodging I found the Countess playing with her children, pale and anxious. She would not let me see her husband and feigned devotion so perfectly that some thought her penitent. She watched the death-chamber from an adjoining salon, a vigilant, embittered guardian of her children’s future, interrogating little Ernest about what he had heard. Her motives were mixed: maternal anxiety, fear of destitution, repentance for past wrongs, and a calculating will to recover the family fortune.Suspecting the Countess of seeking the counter-deed, I took precautions. I sued the Count for a sum ostensibly due to Gobseck so I could, on his death, attach the estate and secure the deed; I bribed a servant to tell me when the Count was gravely ill. My plan was to intervene quickly, threaten to affix seals, and secure the document before the Countess could act. Money, ever the motive, governed all these manoeuvres at the bedside.For two months the Count lay prostrate, living in a squalid disarray he seemed to prefer. He hated daylight, rejected nurses, and revelled in the discomfort about him. One morning he summoned Ernest and, in a moment of lucidity, entrusted the boy with a sealed packet addressed to me, begging him to carry it to the post and to keep the secret even from his mother. Ernest promised.When Ernest returned to his mother, she drew him to her knee and pressed him until he refused to repeat what his father had said. Furious, the dying Count confronted her. The scene was brutal: he accused her of filling his life with trouble and trying to corrupt their son; she begged for mercy and vowed to expiate her sins if the children could be spared. The Count, exhausted of affection, called her a bad wife and mother; she fainted. Priests administered the sacraments. At midnight he died.I and Gobseck arrived soon after. The house was in chaos. In the death-chamber and adjacent salon the Countess had been ransacking drawers and trunks; her search had been violent—mattress overturned, drawers emptied, papers and furniture strewn. A paper bearing the Count’s arms lay at her feet; she had, in panic, thrust some documents into the grate and was partially burning them when we entered. I snatched an addressed paper from the hearth: it was to me. Confronted, the Countess was near collapse, her gestures those of a hunted woman torn between shame and fear. Gobseck, cold and triumphant, reminded her—without mercy—that he was the legal owner of what her husband had sold. He asked if I would judge him; I could not deny that under the law he had rights. I left her beside her husband’s corpse while Gobseck declared himself trustee of the estate.From then on, Gobseck managed the property with the hard efficiency of a usurer turned landowner—leasing the mansion, spending summers on the estates, repairing mills, planting timber. He became, in all but name, lord of the manor, showing no softness. When I suggested he help young Ernest, he scoffed: adversity is the best teacher; let the youth learn to fend for himself. My attempts to move him to honor the Count’s intentions and the provisions for the children met the old man’s icy thrift.Time passed. Gobseck grew feebler but retained his iron will. He kept his rooms exactly as always and received clients even from his bed. During the liquidation after France recognized the Haitian Republic, his knowledge of claims and fortunes had made him influential; he devised agencies, took slices of large transactions, and accepted gifts—plates, snuff-boxes, game—without ever parting cash. He was as thin as ever, his avarice concentrated to a point.Then Gobseck’s death came. Summoned to his bedside by his old porter, I found him kneeling by the grate, muttering bequests and naming people—half delirious, half lucid. He died with a final enumeration of riches. At the hearth, amid cinders, I dug and found metal: coins, receipts, tucked papers—proof of the vast hoard he had amassed. I ordered the justice of the peace to seal everything at once.The rooms revealed the scale and eccentricity of his thrift: one chamber reeked of putrid pies, moldy fish, and shells amongst chests of tea and coffee; others were packed with furniture, silver-plate, vases, pictures, books, and curios—many likely presents or pledged goods. Jewel-cases bore ciphers and coats of arms; fine linen and weapons lay in confusion. Every item had been argued over, bartered, haggled for. Some bargains remained unsettled; dealers who had expected presents or returns balked at Gobseck’s stingy terms. The stench and clutter bespoke a miser’s final degradation.There was talk of Gobseck’s intended bequests: he had given an address for a supposed heiress, a woman called “La Torpille” in the Rue Vivienne; I foresaw the absurdity of pouring an immense fortune into such a character’s hands. But more immediate was the question of the Restaud estate: the Count’s counter-deed, the burned fragments, the tormented Countess, and young Ernest’s uncertain inheritance. Despite Gobseck’s harshness, Ernest would, in time, inherit a fortune sufficient to marry Mlle. Camille and to provide for his mother and siblings—assuming the legal knots could be untangled and the provisions be enforced.Thus closed the arc of transactions, betrayal, and legal scrimmage: Gobseck’s cold mastery of money had changed an aristocratic family’s fate, the Count’s plans had been thwarted by rage and sickness, the Countess’s frantic attempts to save her children had degenerated into crime, and a miser’s death revealed treasures amid squalor. The outcome promised both restitution and new trials, as the rightful heirs would have to navigate the tangled transfers and the old usurer’s secreted wealth.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Monsieur de Manerville, a respected Norman gentleman who married a wealthy Bordeaux heiress, became attached to her Gascon estate, the château de Lanstrac. He served as major of the Gate Guards until his death in 1813, having avoided the Revolution by going to Martinique in 1790. On his return he found Lanstrac well run by Mathias, a notary’s clerk open to new ideas. After Madame de Manerville died in 1810, the old man grew miserly and deprived his only son, Paul, of resources.Paul returned from college in 1810 and endured three years of strict paternal control that stifled his character. Physically brave, he lacked the will to oppose his father and thereby lost moral courage; his repressed feelings made him timid in society. Trapped by the old mansion, he failed to engage with peers and felt out of place at his father’s royalist gatherings among the remnants of nobility, where he remained bored and socially isolated.To escape monotony, Paul practiced fencing and riding—activities his father insisted a gentleman master—and read novels in his free time. His father’s death finally freed him: he inherited substantial estates and well-kept property but retained an aversion to Bordeaux and Lanstrac, scenes of constrained youth. Seeking a new life, Paul invested in consols and traveled across Europe, serving at several embassies. Years of indulgence and poor management depleted much of his fortune, and he resolved to return to Bordeaux to settle as a country gentleman. Conscious of rank, he felt compelled to marry well, believing his foreign experience had refined him for a successful match.Despite his extravagance, Paul had harmed no woman and kept a reputation for integrity; his household remained tasteful and well run. He confided to his friend de Marsay his wish to marry and settle. De Marsay warned him that marriage invites ridicule and responsibility, diminishing the pleasures of bachelorhood. He argued marriage suits those seeking money or heirs, but otherwise mortifies individuality: parental authority breeds children’s hatred, domestic life reduces freedom, and marriage often ends in compromise and anti-climax.Paul countered that he sought emotional companionship and affection, not social position, and was prepared for family duties. De Marsay remained skeptical, advising patience and doubting a young wife would share Paul’s ideals. Their exchange framed a clash between de Marsay’s cynical view of marriage as a trap and Paul’s earnest longing for domestic meaning; Paul, despite the warnings, remained resolute, willing to accept marriage’s risks for the prospect of fulfillment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite earlier conversations, the Comte de Manerville returned to Bordeaux in the winter of 1821 resolved to carry out his plans. Restoring and furnishing the family mansion cemented his reputation for elegance, and his Parisian manners quickly won him a prominent place in Bordeaux’s royalist society. An old marquise called him “the pink of fashion,” a tag used satirically by liberals and admiringly by royalists.Paul de Manerville embraced the title and thrived on attention. Charming and witty, respectful with women yet engaging, he balanced a certain foppery with genuine care for his appearance. Medium height, healthy complexion, and graceful movements added to his appeal. More inclined to receive happiness than to bestow it, Paul sought understanding and encouragement—qualities that made him well suited for marriage in his own view. Young and averse to mundane pursuits, he also recognized that provincial life demanded matrimony and looked to a household for variety. His inclination was intensified by a secret love for Mademoiselle Evangelista.Around the turn of the century an affluent Spaniard, Evangelista, had settled in Bordeaux and by fortune and connections entered noble salons. His Creole wife, from an illustrious Spanish family, maintained their place with extravagant tastes, indulging whims without heed to expense and supported by her devoted husband. After Evangelista’s death in 1813, Madame Evangelista—now a wealthy widow with a beautiful daughter—continued to live lavishly as her fortune dwindled.By Paul’s return, Natalie was a striking young woman, yet no proposals had been made, largely because her mother’s extravagance deterred suitors. Gossip exaggerated the situation: jealous rivals suggested that only a prince could afford to marry Natalie. Accustomed to luxury and adoration, she remained blissfully unaware of life beyond privilege.Six months after Paul’s arrival they met at a high-society event and recognized each other’s charm. Madame Evangelista perceived his interest and hoped he would become her son-in-law; Paul pictured Natalie as his future wife. He had known of the Evangelista fortune since childhood, which eased his approach. Warnings about their extravagance were dismissed by Paul with disdain; his Parisian experience let him navigate provincial gossip without being deterred. Madame began inviting him to formal dinners, and Paul grew increasingly drawn toward marriage.As he spent time with the Evangelistas he felt pride in their company. Society soon viewed him as Natalie’s suitor, and he relished the attention. Royalist circles suggested a match was prudent given Natalie’s beauty and her mother’s intelligence and connections. Paul frequently visited their home and found Madame herself captivating—at forty she retained a striking presence, and a reputation for virtue enhanced her allure. Their relationship contained mutual understanding: Madame saw Paul as a means to raise her social standing in Paris; Paul felt flattered and increased his visits.A year after his return Paul’s attentions made society conclude he was courting Natalie, though neither mother nor daughter pressed actively for marriage. Natalie’s charming reserve intrigued him; he valued her ability to engage without familiarity, which allowed him to imagine a future with her. He rationalized that marriage would bring happiness and suit his lifestyle, brushing aside concerns about potential difficulties and focusing on the benefits. He idealized the union as one that would secure his position and elevate his status, unaware of the complexities ahead.Natalie, sheltered and naive, was attracted to Paul’s charm but lacked experience of married life. Paul, enamored yet cautious, worried whether he had the strength to manage a relationship with someone who might not fully appreciate him.The narrative widens to note that marriage in French society is often governed by negotiations over marriage contracts—frequently ignored in literature but crucial in shaping couples’ lives. The impending discussions about the contract would ultimately influence Paul and Natalie’s future, foreshadowing the social and personal complexities to come.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At the beginning of winter 1822 Paul de Manerville formally requested Mademoiselle Natalie Evangelista’s hand through his great-aunt, the Baronne de Maulincour, who stayed in Médoc to assist him. After discussing the dowry with Madame Evangelista, the baroness reported the marriage was likely: Natalie’s dowry consisted of her patrimony and her mother would contribute nothing. The baroness advised Paul to consult their notary, Maître Mathias, to ensure the marriage contract protected his interests.Paul, anxious, visited Madame Evangelista to discuss terms. She proposed both families’ notaries meet, but privately confessed she owed her daughter a substantial sum from her late husband’s estate and could not pay. Terrified that Paul might withdraw and ruin Natalie’s prospects, she summoned her notary, Maître Solonet. Solonet, pragmatic and opportunistic, reassured her they could conceal the difficulty and negotiate a contract favorable to their side.That evening Mathias and Solonet met. Mathias, cautious and traditional, focused on safeguarding Paul; Solonet favored flexible, pragmatic solutions. While Paul and Natalie shared tender moments at dinner, the notaries debated the marriage’s financial stakes. Mathias warned that Natalie’s family had squandered wealth and marrying into it could jeopardize Paul. Madame Evangelista and Solonet proposed selling her house to pay debts and bolster Natalie’s dowry; Mathias doubted this, fearing it would still expose Paul to vulnerability.Arguments grew heated as each side pressed for terms that would secure its interests. Ultimately they reached a compromise: Madame Evangelista would create an entail on her property—thus securing her daughter’s future while protecting Paul’s position. This arrangement allowed the marriage to proceed without immediate financial threat to either party.As the evening closed both families felt relief—Paul hopeful, Madame Evangelista satisfied—but tensions lingered. The first day of negotiations revealed the entwined complexities of love, ambition, and money, foreshadowing further challenges ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Elie Magus, then in Bordeaux, answered Madame Evangelista’s summons expecting to discuss jewels for Comte Paul’s fiancée, Mademoiselle Natalie. Instead he was asked to appraise the mother-in-law’s property, including her diamonds. Aware of differences between Asian and Brazilian stones, Magus valued Madame Evangelista’s necklace and earrings at 250,000 francs and the “Discreto” at 100,000 francs, warning that selling them would mean a heavy loss.That conversation fueled staff rumors and society gossip about the marriage’s stability: whispers that Madame Evangelista was selling her house and that the union might be in jeopardy. Notaries denied the claims, but public opinion remained skeptical, sharpened by envy of the Evangelistas’ wealth and rank. Paul organizing a hunting party only intensified the talk. Madame de Gyas, eager to confirm the story, visited Natalie and her mother under a pretense of concern and relayed the circulating tales; the two women dismissed the gossip, recognizing its self-interested origins—especially the desperation of mothers pushing daughters into advantageous matches.To counter the rumors and display her wealth, Madame Evangelista planned a grand fête—the fête of the camellias—with lavish decorations and the finalization of the marriage contract. As the day approached she grew uneasy about the contract’s implications, suspecting her daughter’s fortune might be at risk.On the day of signing the mood was festive; guests arrived, Natalie radiant in bridal attire and Paul equally enamored, paying little heed to legal details. Madame Evangelista, however, focused on the contract, which spelled out financial arrangements and an entail. When the notaries explained that if Paul died first Natalie would inherit only a fraction of her expected fortune, the revelation struck her: she felt her daughter was being ruined.Though she had once considered manipulating the arrangement to other ends, the public nature of the wedding constrained her. Determined to secure her daughter’s happiness even at personal cost, Madame Evangelista’s emotions surged as the contract was finalized. She vowed to protect Natalie’s future and, nursing resentment toward Paul, resolved to reclaim and rebuild the family fortune—transforming from a careless spender into a shrewd strategist bent on influencing Paul and safeguarding Natalie, setting the stage for future conflict.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The fête had briefly distracted Paul from his anxieties, but they returned that night. He worried Madame Evangelista might have deceived him without Mathias's intervention. Still, he comforted himself that in two days Natalie would be his wife and their finances were settled, though he resolved to remain watchful.Unbeknownst to him, his future had shifted. The more astute woman in his life had become an adversary, considering withdrawing her interests from their shared future. He failed to perceive the cunning of his Creole mother-in-law—part European intellect, part tropical passion, part Eastern indifference—awakened by the prospect of losing her daughter. With only a day until the marriage, Madame Evangelista realized she could shape the couple’s life: Natalie trusted her implicitly, and a single conversation might alter their fate.The next morning, seated on Natalie’s bed, Madame Evangelista recalled their harmonious past and mourned the thought of her daughter bound to a man she might have to obey. Natalie dismissed the idea of obedience, expecting love to govern. Madame Evangelista warned that conjugal love can fade if not carefully cultivated and urged Natalie to establish authority at the start. She suggested her own presence could complicate the new household and proposed leaving to avoid conflict, advising Natalie to seem obedient while subtly asserting dominance.Their talk laid out marital complexities: Madame Evangelista insisted Natalie keep an active social life to hold Paul’s interest and cautioned against constant proximity, which breeds indifference. She urged Natalie to preserve her allure, maintain independence, and keep Paul entertained so their relationship would stay vibrant.Overwhelmed, Natalie sought guidance on managing her future life. Madame Evangelista promised they would remain in contact and that she would visit, but stressed that some separation was necessary for Natalie’s happiness and prospects.When Paul arrived, he tried to conceal his unease; Madame Evangelista noticed and offered to sell her jewelry to support the couple, framing it as a sacrifice for Natalie’s vanity and future. Paul, moved, accepted. Solonet, the notary, then brought news of potential buyers for Madame Evangelista’s property, which could provide a substantial financial boost; Natalie agreed to the sale, recognizing the need for prudence.Determined to secure her daughter’s prospects, Madame Evangelista resolved to step back from their Parisian life, supporting them from afar so Natalie and Paul could form their own dynamic. As the midnight wedding neared, its preparations carried a foreboding air and guests wondered at Madame Evangelista’s absence. After the ceremony she played the grieving mother, extracted promises from Paul to secure her influence, and sent the newlyweds to Paris. Alone with Paul, Natalie began to assert her authority, setting the stage for the complex dynamics of their marriage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Five years later, in November, Comte Paul de Manerville, cloaked and somber, arrived at the home of his old friend Monsieur Mathias in Bordeaux. The retired notary was absent; his housekeeper led Paul to the late Madame Mathias’s room, where he slept until evening. When Mathias returned, finding Paul there on the anniversary of his wife's death, he expressed concern and embarrassment over the public notices of Paul’s financial ruin. Mathias blamed Paul’s marriage to Natalie Evangelista for his downfall, though he was relieved to have preserved the titles to Paul’s estates even as the usufruct was lost.Paul appeared altered but calm. Over a meal, Mathias lamented the seizure of Paul’s properties. Paul then revealed he had left Natalie without her knowledge and intended to sail to Calcutta to rebuild his fortune. Mathias, shocked that a count would enter commerce, was told Paul no longer considered himself a count and planned to succeed under another name. Paul outlined enormous debts accumulated through lavish spending and gifts to Natalie; he admitted selling assets and borrowing heavily from usurers to sustain their lifestyle, leading to the present catastrophe.Despite Mathias’s doubts and skepticism about de Marsay—who was supposed to send funds—Paul insisted India was his only chance and vowed to return wealthy by forty. The next day Mathias received de Marsay’s bill of exchange confirming some funds would arrive, but Paul remained determined to leave. Mathias lamented losing his friend as Paul boarded the ship and waved goodbye.At sea, Paul was consumed by memories of his past and his love for Natalie, torn between regret and resolve. Struck by seasickness, he spent days in his cabin reflecting on his choices and the affection that bound him to his wife. When he recovered, he opened letters from Natalie and de Marsay.Natalie’s letter revealed anguish and a vow to support him: she pledged her jewels to pay his debts and promised to live at Lanstrac with her mother, economize, and make practical sacrifices to help him. Her words moved Paul, deepening his awareness of their bond while intensifying his doubt about their future.De Marsay’s letter, by contrast, detailed the full extent of Paul’s ruin and exposed the machinations of Paul’s mother-in-law, who had profited from his misfortune. It painted a grim picture of betrayal by those closest to him.Confronted with both love and treachery, Paul felt anger, helplessness, and remorse for his naiveté. Left to grapple with the consequences of his choices and the depth of his feelings for Natalie, he sailed away from France uncertain of what the future might bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Railroads promise to make many contemporary industries obsolete and will alter travel around Paris; the Scene’s elements will one day seem relics. The once-common "coucous" of 1830 were already fading by 1842, surviving only at occasional suburban spectacles. In 1820 many celebrated districts still lacked regular stage-coach service, and local short-route vehicles remained vital.The Touchards, father and son, held a near-monopoly on travel within roughly forty-five miles from their base in the rue du Faubourg-Saint-Denis, yet they faced lively competition from local coucous on shorter routes. Parisians’ appetite for the countryside let small, familiar enterprises thrive: proprietors—often innkeepers—knew the roads, undercut prices, bent rules, and earned local loyalty. These coucous offered speed and convenience that could challenge the Touchards’ more formal coaches until omnibuses later eclipsed them by carrying more passengers more efficiently.One lucrative contested line ran from Paris to Beaumont-sur-Oise, with multiple rivals in 1822 despite Touchard efforts to cut fares and improve equipment because of profitable stops like Saint-Denis and Saint-Brice. The Touchards even extended to Chambly while rivals reached Beauvais. By contrast the road to Isle-Adam, known for its quarries and picturesque end, was served by only two coaches; its terminus discouraged speculation, giving Pierrotin and his colleague a virtual monopoly on a route beloved by locals who often preferred them to the crowded Beaumont coaches.Pierrotin, in his forties, had taken over his father’s coucou after leaving the cavalry in 1815. He married an innkeeper’s daughter, enlarged the business, and combined intelligence with military precision. His dress—sturdy boots, bottle-green trousers, a blue embroidered blouse—matched his trade and hinted at the soldier’s respect for order. Yet his daily work inclined him to regard passengers as cargo.Sensing the industry’s future, Pierrotin tried to modernize. He ordered a larger coach from a respected maker to meet demand; meanwhile his fleet included an inherited coucou painted yellow with blue trim—built for six yet often overloaded—and a noisy four-wheeled coach that could hold seventeen, though officially limited to fourteen. The four-wheeler was so recognizable that villagers announced its approach. Pierrotin cleverly paid taxes only on the coucou, using a special permit for the larger coach to skirt regulations.On a Saturday morning in autumn 1822 Pierrotin stood outside the Lion d’Argent watching other coaches depart and fretting over the lack of passengers and parcels. He was optimistic about the new coach but burdened by a pressing debt of a thousand francs still due. Desperate, he considered asking Monsieur Moreau, steward at Presles and a man he respected, for a loan.A footman then arrived seeking a ride for his master, the Comte de Serizy, traveling incognito after countermanding his carriage for reasons unstated. Pierrotin seized the chance, hoping the fare would ease his debts. The footman’s insistence on secrecy and the count’s mysterious purpose heightened Pierrotin’s sense of responsibility: he resolved to ensure a smooth, discreet journey while still anxious about his finances and the fate of his new coach.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Monsieur Huguet de Serisy descends from the eminent President Huguet, ennobled under François I; the family arms are per pale or and sable, and their motto combines modesty with a play on “Serisy,” the estate’s name.The current count’s father served as president of parliament before the Revolution. The count himself became a councillor of State at twenty-two, earning a reputation for persuasive diplomatic memoranda. He remained at Serizy during the Revolution, protected by his father’s standing, and after his father’s death in 1794 he sought distraction from grief by joining the Council of Five Hundred. After 18th Brumaire he gained the First Consul’s favor, rejoined the Council of State, and was charged with reorganizing a chaotic department—work that helped form much of Napoleon’s administrative system.At forty, in 1806, he married the widow of a prominent Republican general, materially improving his fortune. By 1814 years of labor and ill health forced him to resign; though Napoleon regarded him with some mistrust, he retained rank and later earned Louis XVIII’s confidence, who appointed him a cabinet minister. During the Hundred Days he remained loyal to the Bourbons, staying at Serizy. After Napoleon’s final defeat he resumed roles as privy councillor and vice-president of the Council of State, influencing public affairs without seeking acclaim. His routine—early to rise and early to bed—reflected a life of relentless, quiet duty; he received many honors, including the grand cross of the Legion of Honor, yet his devotion seemed shaded by an unspoken sorrow.The count had long been passionately attached to his wife; despite strains their bond endured. Work served as refuge from heartache, and he treated her with respect and granted her considerable freedom. Though she acknowledged some of his virtues, her ingratitude often wounded him; only occasionally did she soothe his pain.A pressing business matter then occupied him: a farm called Presles was encroaching on his estate. Leased by a tenant, Léger, from a local burgher, Margueron, the lease was expiring and the property was to be sold. The count intended to buy it to end disputes and directed his notary and lawyer to prepare for quick negotiations.His steward, Moreau, had served faithfully for seventeen years; his father had saved the Serizy family during the Revolution, and the count had later spared Moreau from a death sentence in gratitude. Moreau asked to manage Presles and at first was diligent, but after securing the post he grew lax and corrupt, taking bribes and arranging shady dealings. As the count planned a visit to Presles, Moreau plotted to profit from the sale, having already cut a deal with Léger.Moreau’s conduct provoked the wrath of a neighboring retired officer, Monsieur de Reybert. Madame de Reybert brought the count detailed accusations of Moreau’s dishonesty and attempted fraud. Though at first skeptical of her motives, the count’s doubts increased; he ordered his notary to accompany Madame de Reybert to Presles. What began as a straightforward purchase thus turned into an investigation fraught with intrigue and the threat of betrayal by trusted servants.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Pierrotin left the Café de l’Echiquier he noticed a woman and her son at the Lion d’Argent searching for him. The woman, Madame Clapart, wore a faded black gown and an old shawl; anxious and dignified despite evident poverty, she was a devoted mother eager to entrust her son Oscar—travelling alone for the first time—to Pierrotin’s care. Oscar, eighteen, in a short-sleeved coat and patched trousers, remained under his mother’s influence and discomforted by her fussing about his gloves and journey to Monsieur Moreau.Pierrotin knew the family from runs for Moreau, who maintained a discreet connection with Madame Clapart. Once Madame Husson in better days, she had suffered misfortunes—her first husband’s suicide—and later married Clapart, a government clerk of meager means. The household was sustained sporadically by Moreau’s help; he had even secured a scholarship for Oscar. Still, the family’s circumstances were strained, and Clapart’s coldness compounded Oscar’s faults.Madame Clapart clung to past glories and centered her life on Oscar, blind to how her overprotectiveness and reminders about decorum fed his vanity and sense of entitlement. Unaware of his limitations, Oscar provoked Moreau’s concern; Moreau recommended the young man spend time with the steward as a test of character. Preparing to depart, Oscar found his mother’s attentions humiliating, especially before two well-dressed young men who mocked him. Their laughter deepened his yearning for independence and intensified his sense of inadequacy.As boarding began a young artist and his apprentice, Mistigris, arrived, their shabby, bohemian air and lively banter further highlighted Oscar’s self-consciousness. The arrival of the Comte de Serizy, traveling incognito as a bourgeois, shifted the coach’s social dynamic; his presence turned the mockery to curiosity. Pierrotin, anxious to oblige the count, rearranged seating and drew complaints from other passengers, adding to the tension.When the coach finally set off the compartment revealed a cross-section of social types and motives: proud mothers, insecure youths, discreet patrons, bohemian artists.
