Beneath the surface interactions Oscar’s struggle with identity and self-worth continued to simmer, setting the stage for the journey ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Those who travel in public conveyances often begin in silence, using the time to observe and settle in; the quiet usually breaks when the most talkative person starts a conversation. National types are sketched briefly: Englishmen are taciturn, Germans somber, Italians cautious, Spaniards rarely use public transport, Russians suffer from poor roads. In France, by contrast, the atmosphere is livelier and conversation flows, especially among the lower classes and bourgeois.Georges, a clever, lively young man, quickly sized up his fellow passengers, deemed himself superior, and resolved to amuse himself at their expense. As the coach descended from La Chapelle to Saint-Denis he toyed with identities, finally presenting himself as a disguised Russian prince or a soldier returned from the East. When Mistigris complained about dust, Georges made a joke and steered talk toward travels in the Levant. He claimed to have served under Ali, pacha of Janina, and even invented a scare about a plague that alarmed the count enough to have the driver stop. He also said he’d been a soldier in the 1813 campaign and at Waterloo, where he was promoted to captain.The count, intrigued, asked about decorations; Georges said he did not wear them when traveling. The count produced his own honors, and Georges professed admiration for Napoleon and spun further tales—of Egypt and a harem supposedly presented him by Ali Tebelen, though he dismissed harem life as uninteresting. At Saint-Denis the party disembarked at a tavern famed for its cheese-cakes. There the count, suspicious, discovered a portfolio revealing Georges to be Crottat’s second clerk. This revelation quickened the count’s doubts about Georges’s identity and motives.Despite this, Georges continued to impress the others—especially Pere Léger and Oscar—by paying for their treats and ordering drinks, cultivating the pose of an important man. Mistigris, a painter, introduced himself modestly. The count watched the group dynamics, noting Georges’s efforts to buy admiration and elevate his status.As the journey resumed, Georges spun more accounts of Eastern oddities: lax policing, peculiar farming practices, and heavy taxation under the pacha that left peasants with little beyond subsistence. He portrayed local farmers as chiefly bent on surviving rather than cultivating. The count and Mistigris discussed the implications of such a system and drew parallels to French conditions, while the count remained skeptical of Georges’s authenticity.Georges’s storytelling grew more elaborate, climaxing in a dramatic tale of love in Dalmatia: a beautiful Greek woman named Zena, a jealous pirate husband, a poisoning, and a narrow escape from execution. The narrative, full of peril and romance, captivated the travelers; Oscar was especially envious. Mistigris added his own anecdotes, bringing humor and variety. The count remained amused yet doubtful, questioning the plausibility of Georges’s exploits, while Georges maintained his bravado, insisting such adventures were common for a man of experience.Throughout, the coach’s mood warmed with laughter, banter, and drink. Georges’s confidence and charisma won admiration from many, even as the count stayed alert to contradictions. The journey became a rich weave of tales, camaraderie, and intermittent skepticism—passengers drawn together by shared narrative and the entertaining performance of identity.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Pierrotin’s coach descended into Saint-Brice around half past one, where he usually stopped to rest the horses. The innkeeper greeted them by name, and the party disembarked for a brief halt. Georges, boasting and ostentatious, twirled his cane and called for a good breakfast. Père Léger, the fat farmer, agreed to “break a crust.” Georges produced a straw case and lit a pale cigar on the threshold, offering cigars to Oscar and Schinner; Oscar, flattered then embarrassed, puffed awkwardly and was teased for not knowing how to smoke. Schinner demonstrated easily, Georges fumbled trying to imitate the gesture, and Mistigris joked at Oscar’s discomfort. Oscar watched Georges’s spending with thinly veiled resentment—counting up the bills Georges must be running through.Georges proposed a bottle of Bordeaux with Père Léger, and the innkeeper chimed in on local gossip. He’d heard François, conductor of Touchard’s coach, say Monsieur Margueron had been invited by the Comte de Serizy to dine at Presles that day. Père Léger laughed that they had their own plans to thwart such arrangements. They discussed Les Moulineaux, the farm: the innkeeper said he liked to see nobles fooled, imagined lending twenty thousand, and calculated rents and returns, approving Père Léger’s scheme to buy Les Moulineaux cheaply by using newly minted coins and buying it up before the count could. Père Léger spoke quietly of having done for “him,” taking advantage of royal favor and pictures on coin to make Moreau a partner and secure the estate. The innkeeper could see it was sound business—Les Moulineaux produced six thousand francs, a good return on investment, and even if the count pulled strings, the farm was worth the price.The count, listening, grew concerned about discretion. When Pierrotin came out to water the horses, the count drew him aside and reminded him of the bribe he’d promised—ten louis to keep his secret—and increased the incentive: if Pierrotin kept his name concealed, the count would give him a thousand francs the next day for a new coach. He instructed him not to go into the inn or speak of the matter and to stay with the horses. Pierrotin reassured him curtly and hurried his customers on, explaining he must deliver fish by three for a big dinner in Stors; he could not delay customers.Père Léger suggested harnessing the horse Pierrotin wanted to sell into the cabriolet so they could breakfast peacefully and overtake Pierrotin. To the count’s surprise, Pierrotin himself rebridled and set off; Schinner and Mistigris walked on. Georges carried the conversation with Père Léger about the hostess’s beauty and the landscape. He joked about having cigars left, and Oscar, insulted at being called “the little young man,” defended himself—saying he was not an aide-de-camp of Mina, had not been to the East, and that his family’s destined career would spare him such travel for now. He announced that his family intended him for diplomacy, which opened him to ridicule: the others laughed at the idea of his lineage and the pretension.They teased him about the “household” language he used—Oscar proudly said his mother ran their household—prompting more barbs. Georges signaled the others to manage Oscar for sport. They mocked his vocabulary, tutor, and social standing; Oscar insisted his tutor had been the Abbé Loraux, vicar of Saint-Sulpice, and that his family influence might help the abbé’s ecclesiastical rise. When asked if he knew Abbé Frayssinous, Oscar said the abbé was under obligations to his father, raising eyebrows. He then declared he was headed to the Château de Presles to visit the Comte de Serizy and that he was a comrade of the count’s son.This claim drew interest and incredulity. The count, who had been listening, seized the opportunity and asked Oscar advice: he wanted information about Monsieur de Serizy for a claim he intended to press with the American government. Oscar advised him not to go to the count but to his wife: the count was madly in love with her, and she secretly despised him. He described the count’s disfigurement by a skin disease and the elaborate treatments he submitted to—sulphur and steam baths, an iron box ordeal—painting a picture of a man ashamed and reduced by physical affliction; his wife, Oscar suggested, might well prefer others. Oscar, delighted with the attention, urged the count to seek influence instead from the Marquis d’Aiglemont, who might help by having formerly admired Madame de Serizy.The young man’s familiarity with the conversation and private details shocked and offended the others. Schinner scolded him: people don’t talk about their friends in public conveyances. Mistigris snorted and said he was deaf to tale-bearing. Georges advised knowing how to talk without saying anything in a public coach, a maxim he favoured. The count, however, interrupted sharply when the talk turned to the Comtesse de Serizy. He warned the young men to say nothing against her, claiming to be a friend of her brother, the Marquis de Ronquerolles, and vowed that disparaging the countess wouldn’t go unpunished. The painter agreed: no man should blague (make fun of) women. The count, asserting his authority, hinted at his influence—knowing ministers, preventing unworthy men from obtaining decorations—and even claiming acquaintances with the architect of Presles. He asked Pierrotin to stop at the next inn so he could step out a moment.Once clear of the others, the count had Pierrotin show him aside in the inn-yard and asked about the young man who’d been speaking: Pierrotin explained the boy was the son of a poor woman in the rue de la Cerisaie, Madame Husson, to whom he often carried fruit and game from Presles. Pierrotin had driven this youngster for the first time and thought he might be the prince of Maffliers; the boy had told him to leave him near Maffliers because he didn’t want to go on to Isle-Adam. The count was reassured. After the count rejoined the party, Père Léger and the others traded glances, feeling as if they had been caught in a lie; the three young men shared a guilty silence. Mistigris quipped about suffering for the sake of loose talk, and Georges told Oscar he’d never make an ambassador: in public conveyances, it was smarter to talk without saying anything.They rode on in a heavy silence into the Carreau woods, the count noting their quiet as if they were bound for the scaffold. Mistigris muttered a proverb; Georges observed the fine weather. Oscar, trying to assert himself, insisted he was Oscar Husson and would be famous in ten years; the others pressed him for his family’s designation, and the count corrected the pronunciation: Husson de la Cerisaie—Oscar’s modest boast was thereby dignified. The coach descended the hill toward the magnificent château of Presles.Before disembarking, the count made formal, somewhat grandiose farewell remarks, addressing each man: the colonel should make his peace with the King of France; Schinner’s fame was already assured; Oscar, the young diplomat, needed no protection because he possessed “the secrets of statesmen;” Père Léger was about to pluck the Comte de Serizy, and the count exhorted him to do it firmly. Then, asking Pierrotin to set him down and pick him up at the same spot the next day, he left the coach, leaving his companions bewildered and whispering guesses about his identity. Père Léger surmised he was the count who had leased Franconville; Schinner swore never to blague in a public coach again, blaming Mistigris for egging him on. Mistigris, unrepentant, shrugged and quoted misfortunes and proverbs.Georges warned Oscar that if that had indeed been the Comte de Serizy, he wouldn’t envy him, a remark that chilled Oscar, leading him to remember his mother’s warnings. Pierrotin called out as they reached a fine iron gate: they were at the Château de Presles. Some of the young men feigned familiarity, but Georges, not wanting his companions to know his real destination, said he would continue to Les Moulineaux. Père Léger then revealed, with triumph, that he was the farmer at Les Moulineaux, and Georges, forced to backtrack, admitted it was so. Oscar asked Pierrotin to leave his things at the steward’s; he intended to go straight to the château.Oscar plunged forward along a narrow path, uncertain which way to go. Père Léger called to him, correcting his route: if he wished to reach the château, he should use the little gate rather than the forest path. Guided thus, Oscar entered the grand courtyard. While Père Léger watched, Georges quietly slipped away upon realizing Père Léger indeed had possession of Les Moulineaux. The iron gates had been opened at Pierrotin’s request; he drove in with a swagger, proud to deposit the painter’s packages, Oscar’s bundles, and a small leather portmanteau, which he handed over to the concierge’s wife. Mistigris and the painter were to stay in the chateau itself, a discovery that stunned Oscar.The scene closed on Pierrotin’s contentment: he had obeyed the count’s injunctions, kept the secret for the present, and now looked forward to the promised reward. His face beamed with the sly satisfaction of a peasant who had calculated his profit—tomorrow he would receive a thousand francs and be able to buy the magnificent new coach he had long desired. The coach rolled on, the passengers dispersing into the estate, gossip and pretension settling back into their respective places, while small social ambitions and private bargains continued to move like unseen forces beneath the surface of polite conversation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Oscar, feeling awkward, hid behind trees in the courtyard and watched Monsieur Moreau emerge from the guard-room in a blue overcoat, carrying a riding whip. Moreau greeted Oscar warmly and introduced himself to Mistigris and his master, the two painters sent by architect Monsieur Grindot. He told the concierge to show them to their rooms, invited them to dinner at five, and mentioned hunting on the estate.As Moreau led Oscar through the park, he sent his son Jacques ahead to inform Madame Moreau of their arrival. Stern in appearance but kind in nature, Moreau impressed Oscar, who felt small beside him. He encouraged Oscar to learn to ride and hunt despite his inexperience. Oscar’s mother had allowed only a two-week stay because Madame Moreau was present, yet Moreau seemed intent on extending it.The steward’s house, built before the Revolution and connected to the chateau, stood in a large garden and contained a salon, dining room, and a modern boudoir furnished to Madame Moreau’s taste. The Moreaus lived comfortably on estate provisions with little expense. Madame Moreau, once a maid, kept an air of elegance and worked to be seen as the mistress of the chateau, often patronizing villagers.Tension with the Reyberts dated to a past slight when Madame de Reybert reminded Madame Moreau of her former status. The Moreaus had notice of the painters’ arrival, and Madame Moreau was eager to impress them, hoping the artist she hosted would raise her social standing.On the day the painters were due, Madame Moreau dressed elegantly, but the Paris coach brought only Mistigris’s packages, leaving her disappointed. The two artists eventually arrived; Madame Moreau welcomed them and professed a love of art. Mistigris and Bridau quickly recognized her pretentiousness and decided to humor her, engaging in conversation about art.Their talk was cut when a maid announced the count’s arrival, and Madame Moreau grew flustered. Oscar, feeling out of place, was brought in by his younger brother and chastised for his appearance. Mistigris cracked a joke about Oscar’s future, which only increased the awkwardness. The maid returned saying the count wanted dinner promptly, adding to the household’s bustle.The count had arrived unexpectedly and was distressed to learn Moreau was absent. He had overheard remarks suggesting Moreau’s disloyalty, prompting a confrontation. The count expressed disappointment that Moreau had discussed private matters, especially in front of Oscar. Moreau attempted to explain but faced the consequences of his indiscretions.Moreau returned home distraught over the loss of his position and the implications of Oscar’s careless words. Estelle, his wife, was furious and understood the gravity of their predicament. Moreau confronted Oscar, demanding an explanation; Oscar remained silent, overwhelmed by fear and shame. In anger, Moreau dragged him back to the count, insisting he apologize. The count dismissed Oscar’s behavior and doubted his potential. Humiliated, Moreau took Oscar home to contemplate the consequences and the uncertain future of their family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While the horses were being harnessed, Moreau wrote to Madame Clapart about his distress over Oscar's conduct: during a journey Oscar had revealed sensitive information about Madame de Serizy to a count traveling incognito, and the count had immediately ordered Oscar sent home. The incident cost Moreau his stewardship at Presles and threatened his family's prospects, a prospect that terrified him and his wife.At the Clapart home, Monsieur Clapart worried aloud about Oscar’s character, while Madame Clapart defended the boy’s nature and potential. Brochon then returned with Oscar and Moreau’s explanatory letter; on reading it Madame Clapart fainted, panic-stricken about her son’s future.The next day she found Oscar alone, visibly distressed, and reminded him of Moreau’s past support and how precarious their situation had become without the stewardship. She outlined the few remaining options and pressed the need for discretion, hard work, and prudence, but Oscar remained largely indifferent, much to her frustration.Insisting on greater discretion, she proposed appealing to Uncle Cardot as a last hope. Cardot, a self-made businessman who had amassed a fortune, lived modestly and generously supported his children, though he was unaware of the family’s plight. He greeted them warmly, praised Oscar’s academic promise, and, moved by Madame Clapart’s account, offered to fund Oscar’s legal education. He counseled integrity, discipline, and effort, convinced Oscar could succeed if he applied himself.Madame Clapart felt renewed hope despite ongoing financial strain. Moreau later visited, saying he had begun land speculation and could help place Oscar as a pupil in a law office. He reiterated that discipline and diligent study were essential to repair Oscar’s reputation and secure a future. Grateful, Madame Clapart urged Oscar to show his gratitude and heed Moreau’s admonitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Ten days later Monsieur Moreau took Oscar to Maître Desroches, a young solicitor in the rue de Béthisy who had recently set up in a spacious but inexpensive office. Desroches, twenty-six and raised in poverty, recognized Oscar’s situation; the youth’s gaunt look and curt manners seemed to intimidate him. “We work here day and night,” Desroches declared amid a mountain of papers, and he put his head clerk, Monsieur Godeschal, in charge of Oscar’s training so the boy would not lose time on the way to law school and would master the Code. Oscar was lodged in a small attic beside Godeschal’s room; Desroches openly aimed to make him a capable clerk.For two years Oscar lived at the office in a regimen of petty but exacting work. Under Godeschal’s strict oversight he kept a monk-like schedule: rising at five to prepare for the day, running errands, studying the Code under rigorous tests, returning to office tasks after classes, and subsisting on plain meals. His social life was minimal — monthly breakfasts with his uncle Cardot and Sundays with his mother — punctuated only by the occasional outing Moreau could arrange. Oscar’s efforts at elegance, however, met disapproval from his superiors.Godeschal, who counseled a modest wardrobe and frugal living — insisting a good clerk should not spend more than seven hundred francs a year — lived these principles and embodied honesty without pretension. When a colleague failed in his duties, Godeschal discreetly guided him to resign rather than suffer public dismissal, showing a practical, humane leadership.By twenty Oscar had become the third clerk, doing the second’s duties without pay but receiving room and board. His legal knowledge exceeded that of many licensed graduates, and he even began arguing cases in chambers. Yet he felt constrained by a life at odds with his desires: he envied the well-dressed young men he saw in the streets and yearned for freedom and pleasure, feelings he habitually suppressed. Godeschal, aware of these struggles, tried to shield him from temptation and sometimes covered the cost of outings to give him some enjoyment; Moreau supported him but could not connect as Godeschal did. Both men feared the youthful follies that might imperil his future, though they hoped to steer him aright.“How is he getting on?” Moreau asked. “Too much vanity,” Godeschal replied, noting Oscar’s taste for fine clothes and suggesting his talents might suit a barrister rather than a solicitor’s clerkship.In November 1825, shortly after Oscar’s promotion, a new clerk, Frédéric Marest, arrived. Well-to-do and aiming for the magistracy, Marest claimed he wanted hands-on legal practice; Godeschal announced his arrival, hoping he’d fit the office camaraderie. The clerks enjoyed practical jokes: Godeschal maintained a fictitious register of receptions for new clerks filled with absurd entries, a long-standing source of amusement. When Oscar asked for the register, Marest read it and laughed privately, then said he would consult a cousin who was a head clerk about the welcome he should give — a remark that made Godeschal suspect Marest’s inexperienced status.Despite the office’s lighthearted solidarity, Oscar’s inner conflict persisted as he navigated professional pressures and the expectations pressing on his formative years.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day at two o’clock Oscar recognized Georges Marest, now head-clerk to notary Hannequin, who entered the office. Georges greeted him, recalling their past mischief involving a peer of France that had led to Georges’ transfer. He invited Oscar and the other clerks to a gathering at the Rocher de Cancale and later to an evening with Madame la Marquise de las Florentinas y Cabirolos, promising a night of high society.Desroches, head of the office, interrupted, charging Oscar with obtaining a crucial legal document from the Palais and warning him to beware of Derville, who might obstruct the task. Oscar was keen to prove himself in this first assignment as second clerk.After Georges and Oscar left, Godeschal tried to learn more about the Marquise, but Frederic, keeping up a ruse, suggested she was a wealthy widow from Mexico. The clerks, excited by the promise of a grand breakfast and fashionable festivities, were eager to meet her.In reality the Marquise was Mademoiselle Agathe-Florentine Cabirolle, a successful danseuse at the Gaîté, supported by Père Cardot, a merchant who had taken her and her mother in. Cardot had invested in Florentine’s career; she became a celebrated star, living comfortably and preparing for a possible engagement at the Opera.With his newly acquired wealth, Georges actively pursued Florentine. The clerks, anxious to impress, dressed in their best. Oscar’s mother, Madame Clapart, voiced hopes for her son’s future and warned him about Georges, whom she blamed for Oscar’s earlier misfortune.On the day of the gathering Oscar felt excitement mixed with apprehension, resolved to enjoy it but mindful of his mother’s and Godeschal’s advice. After a lavish breakfast the clerks, by then intoxicated, went to Florentine’s salon and found a scene of opulence and fashionable guests.Introduced to Florentine, Oscar was charmed by her grace. He joined a game of vingt-et-un with her and the actress Fanny Beaupré and quickly lost the money he’d been given for the legal document. Florentine, noticing his distress, offered him a thousand francs, which he accepted. Yet his luck failed; he lost everything and fell into despair and drunken stupor.The party went on into the early morning; Florentine and her friends reveled while Oscar remained oblivious to the wider consequences, asleep in the boudoir when she retired, unaware of what his actions would bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning a loud noise woke clerk Oscar Husson. Hearing his uncle Cardot scold him for late-night revelry and the disorderly apartment, Oscar pretended to sleep, burying his face in the cushions. Florentine, a danseuse, laughed and revealed him as Cardot’s nephew and urged the uncle to help. Oscar had lost money gambling—money belonging to his employer, Monsieur Desroches—and, desperate and ashamed, begged Cardot for aid. Cardot reluctantly lent five hundred francs, warning it would be the last help.Mariette, concerned, sent a note and money; Godeschal, another clerk, hurried to obtain the necessary documents for Desroches. Angered, Desroches resolved to dismiss Oscar despite Godeschal’s defense. Godeschal recounted Oscar’s past mistakes and blamed the bad influence of Georges Marest. Desroches, half in jest, suggested Oscar might try law, thinking his vanity might suit the bar.At home, Clapart tormented his wife with reminders of their son’s failures; Madame Clapart defended Oscar. When Oscar returned he confronted his father, asserting independence, but his plea was ignored. Determined to take responsibility, Oscar faced the prospect of military service. Clapart believed conscription would instill discipline. Oscar drew a number and, despite his fears, hoped for a favorable outcome. Three days later, through the Comte de Serizy’s influence, Oscar was drafted into the cavalry.Overwhelmed by her son’s misfortunes, Madame Clapart turned to religion, seeing past sins as the cause and seeking redemption through piety. In the army Oscar applied himself, eventually rising to lieutenant-colonel.Years later a decorated Oscar stood with his mother outside the Lion d’Argent, waiting for a coach. Both were unrecognizable from their former selves, and though haunted by past mistakes, Oscar had achieved success. As they prepared to board they encountered Georges Marest, now a shadow of his former self, who failed to recognize him. Poorly dressed, Georges tried to preserve dignity, but Oscar reflected on how far they had diverged since youth.On the journey passengers exchanged stories of transformed lives; military service had reshaped Oscar and taught him hard lessons. Georges’s attempts to impress with past tales fell flat. Oscar, confident and composed, credited his military career for his stability, was invited to a wedding—signs of his integration into respectable society—and eventually married Georgette Pierrotin, the coach proprietor’s daughter. His earlier errors became instructive lessons; through connections, discipline, and changed character he earned respect and security in a world that had once seemed precarious.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ THE MIDDLE OF THE STORY ### — MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo a Polish Lady. De Balzac dedicates the book to a woman of contrasts—childlike in faith, aged by experience, inspiring through love and sorrow—whose inner poesy the author cannot match, though he attempts to weave her qualities into this tale.CHAPTER I. THE CHALETIn October 1829 Monsieur Simon Babylas Latournelle, a notary, walks from Havre to Ingouville with his wife, son Exupère, and their head clerk Jean Butscha to visit the Mignons. Latournelle quietly coaches Exupère in a small ruse: after paying polite respects to Mademoiselle Modeste Mignon, Exupère is to step aside and message Monsieur Dumay that “the young man has come,” a signal intended to stir romantic interest. Exupère is bound for Paris to study law, and Latournelle involves him in the plan. Madame Latournelle, vain and concerned with social standing, contrasts with her serious, odd husband, noted for his green goggles. Butscha, a hunchback who adores Modeste, completes the group.Ingouville, a picturesque suburb where merchants escape the city, contains the Chalet, the Mignons’ cottage with a garden and cherished hot-house. Dumay, the family’s cashier, holds a lease that irks the new proprietor, Monsieur Vilquin. Vilquin offers money to vacate, but Dumay, fiercely loyal, refuses—determined above all to protect Modeste from suitors.CHAPTER II. A PORTRAIT FROM LIFEThe Latournelles find Gobenheim, a pale, serious young banker, already visiting. Gobenheim’s attentions are more worldly than affectionate. The household is tense: Dumay readies himself to confront any suitor who might compromise Modeste’s honor. Blind Madame Mignon remains the dignified center of the family while Modeste, embroidering at her side, is the focus of unspoken anxieties. Dumay’s vigilance promises an evening meant to test Modeste’s feelings.CHAPTER III. PRELIMINARIESJean François Bernard Dumay, once a soldier, became the devoted cashier to the Mignons after their misfortunes. His fidelity to Colonel Mignon—exiled to Siberia—never falters. Dumay’s wife, bereft of children, treats Modeste as her own. The couple’s devotion shapes the household’s protective tone.CHAPTER IV. A SIMPLE STORYA business collapse and the Colonel’s exile reduced the family to the Chalet, where Dumay and Latournelle work to settle debts. Madame Mignon, blind and grief-stricken, weakens under sorrow. Modeste’s innocence and rising beauty worry Dumay, who embraces a strict guardianship to preserve her reputation amid their diminished circumstances.CHAPTER V. THE PROBLEM STILL UNSOLVEDModeste’s attractiveness fuels speculation and fear. The women of the household watch for signs of love, suspecting their charge may be susceptible to romance. Dumay vows no man will approach her without his knowledge. The domestic vigilance reflects anxiety about Modeste’s future in a world that may exploit her.CHAPTER VI. A MAIDEN’S FIRST ROMANCEModeste lives richly in imagination, shaped by books and the tragedy of her sister. She longs to be the muse of a great poet and escapes the provincial life through romantic reveries. Acting on this impulse, she writes to the poet Canalis, pouring out her heart in a letter that seeks connection and the promise of transformation.CHAPTER VII. A POET OF THE ANGELIC SCHOOLCanalis receives Modeste’s letter with a mix of amusement and condescension. He recognizes her innocence and fears being misconstrued. His reply, flattering yet cautious, warns against romanticizing the poet’s life. Disappointed but undeterred, Modeste resolves to press the correspondence further, determined to prove her ideals and to assert herself against the limits of her world. The exchange sets the stage for a dramatic exploration of her hopes, ambitions, and the social expectations that surround her.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################CHAPTER VIII. BLADE TO BLADETo Monsieur de Canalis:Monsieur, you are a great poet and an honest man. After your frankness to a young girl on the brink of despair, can you answer me truthfully? Would you have replied the same had you known Mademoiselle O. d’Este M. has six million francs and seeks a fool for a husband?Answer frankly. I am wiser than my years; nothing candid can harm you. If you speak openly, I will reply to your first letter.Having admired your talent, I must also praise your integrity. Your humble servant, O. d’Este M.Ernest de La Brière read this and paced the boulevards, conscience and curiosity at war. Parisians would call such a note flirtatious; a man of honor felt judged by Truth, Justice, and Honor. Truth accused him of hypocrisy; Justice defended the girl’s rebellion against materialism; Honor demanded sincerity. He delayed then wrote:Mademoiselle, you are not a D’Este. The name is a disguise. Do I owe truth to someone who lies about herself? Are you noble or of the people? Ethics do not change, but obligations vary with rank. A daughter of Este with six million may frankly confess a fondness for poetry; a girl of the people would be mocked. What class are you? Answer plainly, and I will reply to your question.Modeste answered:To Monsieur de Canalis, you grow sensible, my dear poet. My father is a count; our family includes a cardinal, and I am the last of our name. We are equal to the Canalis. You will excuse my refusing to send our arms. Answer me as truthfully as I have answered you. I await your response, to know if I may sign myself, Your servant, O. d’Este M.La Brière smiled at her mischief. Four years in Paris had taught him intrigue. He booked a seat to Havre, wrote that delay was due to duties, then obtained a note from the post-office director to ensure secrecy. He watched Francoise Cochet collect letters and followed her discreetly to Ingouville, where he glimpsed Modeste at the Chalet window. Struck by her beauty, he learned the estate belonged to Monsieur Vilquin, the richest merchant in Havre. The Vilquins were immensely wealthy; one daughter was married to M. Althor. Rumors hinted at high-society visitors and a possible match with a young duke.Having seen enough to dream, La Brière resumed the correspondence, though he regretted the reconnaissance. Parisian curiosity seldom rests. He wrote a letter shaped by conscience and passion; Modeste read it at her window on a fine summer day.Mademoiselle, without hypocrisy: had I known your wealth I would have acted differently. Men often confuse happiness with means; money becomes a religion. Faced with such a prize, a young man may pursue it for social gain and flatter, perhaps losing esteem for you and earning yours for me. Honesty compels me to say this.Your letter forced deep reflection—on you and on myself. You stirred many base thoughts, from which something generous emerged. I went to Havre, followed Francoise to Ingouville; you are as beautiful as a poet’s dream. I blush at such spying and stopped. You have altered my opinion: you are not merely a woman but a poetic Ideal. Exceptional souls may transcend the crowd’s laws. If your letter sprang from lofty reveries and the wish for friendship beyond ordinary relations, you are an exception.Accept my thanks for forcing me to sound my heart. You corrected my false idea that marriage should be for fortune. I swore to make my own fortune and not choose a wife from cupidity. I reproach myself for the curiosity you provoked. You are not worth six million; such a fortune could not be hidden in Havre. I now fix my rule: seek a partner made by God; do not marry a dunce. If I were rich and you poor, I would lay my heart and fortune at your feet; you seem loyal and rich in soul.Modeste felt many things: delight at touching a noble soul, joy that worth might still meet genius, and triumph at following her instinct. She altered her concealment, giving Francoise envelopes of her own so letters could be slipped back into the box, and resolved to meet the postman herself.Ernest de La Brière received another letter five days later that revealed more than any analysis.CHAPTER IX. THE POWER OF THE UNSEENTo Monsieur de Canalis:My friend, I call you that; you have delighted me. I would not change your image from your first letter—oh, may it not be the last! Who but a poet could so delicately excuse and understand a girl on the brink?I admit with joy I am not the Vilquin nor the withered d’Herouville; my ancestors are respectable and my precautions stand—I will remain veiled. As to my person and belongings, be easy: I am at least as beautiful as the girl whose light you saw, and not poverty-stricken. I have seen the humiliating comedy of heiresses hunted; make no attempt to find me. I am free yet watched—by myself and by resolute people who would not hesitate to stab a man who threatened my retreat. I do not say this to quicken your courage; I merely warn you.Now to your sermon. When I read your distrust, I thought the Muses may have led you astray. You are safe with me: I love poetry but do not affect hypocritical airs. Should I ever bid you come, I promise no old maid or poor wretch will appear. I regret that you came to Havre; you lowered the charm of the secret meeting I dreamed—God alone knew the treasure I reserved for a man noble enough to come. You spoiled it, but I forgive you; Paris breeds a man in every poet.Do you think me gullible? When your first letter reached me I did not toss it aside; my net was carefully cast by a fisherman expecting a miraculous catch. You have my approval in what you say about family. The man who pleases me will have my heart with my parents’ consent; I will neither grieve nor surprise them. I am guarded by devotion and disdain for calculating motives. I worship the ideal without being romantic; but I know social truths.For now we must be friends. Your person may be unknown, but your mind and heart please me; an infinity of thoughts in me needs a man of genius to share them. I do not want my poem wasted; I want it known to you as to God. A true comrade’s confidences are precious; accept these leaflets of a young girl’s thoughts. Later, if our souls match, some white-haired servant may guide you to whatever dwelling fate holds for us—but not now. Come to me when you are unhappy; I have balms for your ills. I am twenty but feel fifty; I have known through another the horrors and delights of love and have no illusions but a religion and beliefs. Whoever I marry—if I choose him—will find me faithful and constant. I will not follow my husband blindly; I will be the divinity of his hearth. But I must test and choose the man who will be life’s companion.Your letter was precious. Yes, it held necessary truths for family life. Act as an honest man, think as a poet, love as women love—that is what I long for. Adieu, my friend. I am poor at present; that is why I hide. I have read your verses in the Revue with delight now that I know your inner loftiness. Will it grieve you that a young girl prays for you, that you are her solitary thought? Send me the counterpart of these pages; your confidences—if full and true—will make me happy.O. d’Este M.La Brière, surprised at his own absorption, wondered if he was already in love. She thought him the great poet; could he sustain the deception? The charm of the unseen—the obscurity that stirs fancy—became an obsession. He answered with a studied letter where passion, pride, and caution mingled.Mademoiselle,—Is it loyal of you to enthrone yourself in a poor poet’s heart while reserving the right to abandon him if he disappoints? Your confidence leaves little of yourself but much charm. I offer humble remonstrances.Do you believe letters—brief, momentary expressions—can replace knowledge gained by daily life? Man is dual: an invisible life of heart and a material life. Letters may suffice for spirit, but cannot reconcile both. A solitary, educated, chaste homage consoles literary men, and I thank you as one equal might thank another.But what next? I am neither Byron nor a colossus. I might have vanity, discordant character, and ambition; I need a career. I have modest means and the external bearing of an ordinary man. I can be neither martyr nor hero. Your project is perilous: if you make yourself the handmaid of a man of genius and worship him till death, you may live forever in his glory; if you attach that ideal to me now, my modest gifts may disappoint you. If we continue, I might love you wildly—but if I pleased you, the tale would likely end in the common fashion: marriage, house, children. Therefore, adieu.CHAPTER X. THE MARRIAGE OF SOULSTo Monsieur de Canalis:My friend, your letter gives me both pain and pleasure; perhaps someday all will be pleasure. I want the soul’s answers you seek from God. Cannot the friendship of Mademoiselle de Gournay and Montaigne be revived in us? Remember Sismondi’s household or the Marquis de Pescaire’s; can two hearts vibrate in unison across distance? Man is harp, musician, and listener. Will you find me jealous like ordinary women? I know you go into the world; I know your beauties and wits. Yet I seek a flower that blooms on Alpine peaks—men of genius—and I wish to cultivate it. I am no commonplacid creature; I would not belong to a ninny or merchant who could ruin us. My father adores my wishes and will not oppose them. If I please my poet and he pleases me, we will build love high and secure.I am an eaglet; I want to be imprisoned by love as I am now by my father’s will. Listen: a young imagination locked in a tower longs to run in its neighbor’s park—your genius. Parents arrange marriages by appearances; a young girl is often denied choice. I want a long marriage of souls before any legal pledge. My mother followed instinct and beauty and prospered; I follow mine. I will not bind us prematurely to vulgar forms. If our correspondence reveals true sympathy, we may be spared common follies and reach graveside hand in hand. I await your orders, monseigneur, and am with all my heart,Your handmaiden, O. d’Este M.To Mademoiselle O. d’Este M.—You are a witch and I love you! Is that what you desire, most original girl? Perhaps you amuse provincial leisure with a folly to make a poet act rashly. If so, you have done wrong. Your letters have mischief. But I am no longer master of myself; my life depends on your answer. If a certainty of unbounded affection that disregards social conventions touches you—if you will let me seek you—say so. If your reply is favorable I change my life; I shall renounce the trivial pleasures we call happiness. For me, happiness is a fusion of feeling, a harmony of souls, an imprint of a noble ideal on daily life—a constancy of heart more precious than fidelity. We may call such devotion sacrifice, but is it not the dream of poets and maidens? I cannot speak more of myself—my past nor my filial affection, which you have altered. Say one word and I will love you until death. Either write no more or give me hope. Let me see you, or let me go. Must I bid you adieu? Will you permit me to sign myself,Your Friend?To Monsieur de Canalis,—What flattery! How quickly the grave man becomes Leander! To what should I attribute such change—my ink, your fancy, or the memory of the girl at the window? Your letter has given me elating pleasures beyond family affection. What are blood ties compared to those forged by mysterious sympathies? Bless you for the happiness. You explain social injustices: glory dazzles men; God gives love to women to soothe them. I have felt my mission and you confirm it.Sometimes I wake in inexpressible sweetness; after your feverish letter I felt heavenly freshness, destined to die for one I love. Your poem “The Maiden’s Song” paints these moments; I think you worthy to be me. Your letter lets me read within you: I have guessed your struggles, curiosity, and plans, but do not yet know you well enough. Shall we make a compact? Write your life briefly—tell me what you do in Paris day by day, as to an old friend—and then I will see you. This is a great step.This involves my life and my parents’—they must find a son in you. To what extent can superb spirits bend to family yokes and endure little miseries? You said Goethe and Byron were egoists; you erred. Great minds are slaves of ideas; their visible egoism often hides abnegation. A man who gives gifts to his epoch is not simply selfish. The poet’s life needs vast organization to bear pleasures and sorrows; consider Molière, whose private suffering darkens his comedy.I place you among those whose generosity borders on the divine. Had I found self-interest where I now find noble flowers, I would have been anguished. I once met disappointment before sixteen; what would have become of me had I learned at twenty that fame is a lie? I should have surrendered to my father and become resigned to a dull life. But I have you; I believe in you. That belief straightens my thoughts and fancies. Sometimes I wish I were already in the middle of the book we are beginning; I feel persistence, strength to love, constancy tempered by reason, a heroism for duty.If you could follow me to the exquisite retreat I fancy, if you knew my plans, you might not call me foolish. I wish to be an inexhaustible spring for the twenty years nature allows me to shine; to vary happiness, put intelligence into tenderness, and give piquancy to fidelity. I wish to be courageous for my friend, to abnegate for the nest like no bird can. Tell me, do you now think me to blame for my first letter? The mysterious wind of will drove me to you. In your letter I hold your cry: “God wills it.”Ah, you will call me a chatterbox; the people around say I am taciturn.O. d’Este M.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The renowned surgeon Desplein’s visit deeply moved Modeste; she admired his selflessness in relieving suffering. That evening in the salon guests praised the Mignon family for the hope he inspired, and conversation turned to Desplein’s genius. The medical community acknowledged it, but Canalis dismissed it as application, not invention. Modeste defended Desplein, arguing genius shouldn’t be judged narrowly and citing practical contributors like Parmentier and Jacquart. Canalis insisted genius requires invention, invoking figures such as Napoleon and Scott; the debate grew heated. Modeste scorned purely utilitarian views while others stressed utility’s role, and Canalis, wounded, revealed his vanity in attempting to dominate the discussion.Modeste’s flirtation with Canalis irritated La Briere, who left convinced he’d lost her; her bold challenge to her father surprised the adults and revealed growing independence. The next day Modeste’s behavior toward Canalis grew more provocative, intensifying La Briere’s jealousy. Canalis staged grand gestures as competition mounted, but Modeste remained skeptical of his motives, asserting her independence and resisting social expectations.As a grand hunt approached — heightened by the arrival of the Duc d’Herouville — Modeste’s feelings for La Briere deepened while her rapport with Canalis strained. Her beauty and fortune attracted attention, shifting the suitors’ dynamics. The hunt served as backdrop to unfolding drama, exposing true natures and the social intricacies she must navigate as a young woman of rank.Ultimately Modeste moves toward a more complex understanding of love and ambition, trying to balance desire with social reality. The conclusion suggests the sacrifices and compromises required to pursue happiness under society’s expectations.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyBEATRIXBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyNOTEBalzac, famed for detailed character studies and daily-life depictions, seldom portrayed real people. In “The Great Man of the Provinces in Paris” some characters recalled actual figures; in Beatrix he borrows recognizable traits: Mademoiselle des Touches (Camille Maupin) suggests George Sand, while Beatrix, Conti, and Claude Vignon evoke the Comtesse d’Agoult, Liszt, and critic Gustave Planche.The opening scene captures the customs of an old Breton family — a social state largely vanished — signaling a transition from past to modernity and standing as one of Balzac’s notable depictions in the “Comedy of Human Life.”I. A BRETON TOWN AND MANSIONIn Brittany some towns remain scarcely touched by nineteenth-century change because poor links to Paris keep them insulated. They regard new civilization as spectacle and preserve old customs. A traveler seeking human types would find remnants of earlier eras across provinces: Louis XV in Provence, Louis XIV in Pitou, and older traditions in Brittany. Many feudal towns declined, neglected by historians focused on dates, yet their memory and character persist.Guerande is one such town: a well-preserved feudal life contained within walls and moats, with intact battlements and gates. The mayor’s planting of poplars along the moat provoked criticism—the promenade had been public for a century. Houses remain unmodernized, streets resembling those of four centuries past and now quiet, letting a solitary traveler wander a deserted town.The atmosphere evokes ancient customs; even a gendarme seems out of place. The public square displays traditional Breton costumes, contrasting sharply with bourgeois and noble styles. Social divisions are rigid; revolutionary spirit failed to erase them. Guerande, fervently Catholic and self-contained, resists modern ideas.Geographically, Guerande overlooks famed salt-marshes, whose salt locals claim improves their butter and sardines. It connects to France by two roads: one from Savenay and a more frequented route from Vannes. Slow government and inhabitants’ acceptance of separation reinforce the town’s isolation, so Guerande endures as a feudal remnant.The town blends nature and history: salt-marshes and surrounding landscapes evoke tranquility. A carriage’s arrival is an event, drawing locals who barter. Guerande is noted for historical incidents like a 1365 treaty and for coastal importance.Near the church stands the du Guaisnic family mansion, symbolizing past grandeur. The family, tracing roots to the du Guesclins, is ancient and noble, embodying Brittany’s history. Though fortunes declined, they remain significant; the Baron du Guaisnic is a prominent local figure.The mansion’s grand archway bears a family crest. The courtyard reveals stables and a kitchen; the freestone house shows remnants of former glory. Inside, a grand hall with intricate woodwork and a massive fireplace survives. The dining room, hung with fourteenth-century tapestries, retains historical charm; upper stories hold simple family and guest rooms, servants sleeping above the kitchens.The half-acre garden has fruit trees and a sundial, tended by a devoted gardener. Venetian architectural touches and a spiral column hint at family heritage.The mansion’s enduring grandeur contrasts with modern, often temporary homes. The du Guaisnics, hosting friends for generations, embody old Brittany’s spirit; their legacy testifies to history and tradition’s endurance amid rapid change.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Early in May 1836 the Guenic household consisted of Baron and Baroness du Guenic, the baron’s elder sister Mademoiselle Zéphirine, and their only son Calyste, aged twenty-one. The baron, Gaudebert-Calyste-Charles, a veteran of the Vendée and Brittany insurrections who fought with Charette and La Rochejaquelein, had prudently sold his property to his sister before the war. After years in resistance he returned to Guérande in 1813, where he married Fanny O’Brien, an Irish noblewoman; Calyste was born the day Louis XVIII landed.Now seventy-three, the baron bore the marks of his turbulent life: a tall, wiry frame, deep wrinkles, sunken features, and large scarred hands that testified to a soldier’s past. Illiterate except for his prayer book, he still dressed in sturdy shoes, ribbed stockings, and a coat bearing the cross of Saint-Louis. His manner combined serenity and a somnolent gravity born of long service, and his conversations—like those of his household—turned to the exiled Bourbons, the Catholic faith, and the future of Brittany. Calyste was their sole hope.The baron rigorously trained Calyste in gentlemanly ways and, when the Duchesse de Berry attempted to restore her son, took him to join the cause—leaving wife and sister unaware. They returned after three months, unexpectedly, and the baron observed, “The barons did not all do their duty,” a remark that reflected both pride and the old commitment to duty.That evening the baron slept in his armchair while his wife read the newspaper. Fanny O’Brien, at forty-two, retained the delicate beauty and warmth of her Irish heritage: refined features, elegant dress, ringleted hair, and a tender, radiant manner that contrasted with her husband’s austerity. Nearby, Mademoiselle Zéphirine sat blind and knitting; despite her age she kept a proud, cheerful composure and had refused cataract surgery to avoid burdening her brother financially. She embraced her domestic role and shared a close bond with the baroness.As the sun set and the room warmed, silence deepened and Zéphirine remarked on the baroness’s distraction. Mariotte entered to light the lamp while Gasselin tended the horses and dogs—both long-time retainers whose lives were entwined with the family. Gasselin took pride in his gardening and caretaking; Mariotte matched his steadfastness. Their loyalty under Mademoiselle Zéphirine’s quiet management exemplified the household’s cohesion.The Guenic home functioned with unity and purpose: affectionate, duty-bound relations that turned daily routine into a sustaining, nurturing atmosphere amid the trials of their times.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When night fell, Gasselin entered the hall and respectfully asked his master if he needed anything further. The baron, roused, said he could go out or to bed after prayers unless Madame or his sister required him. The two ladies consented, and Gasselin knelt with the family. Mademoiselle du Guenic led the prayer. A knock followed; Gasselin opened for Monsieur le curé.Monsieur Grimont, a middle-aged priest in a cassock, entered with the easy civility of his office. His plump, calm visage gave him the air of a Dutch burgomaster or Breton peasant; his joviality suggested a clear conscience. Well regarded in Guérande, he nevertheless acknowledged the du Guenics’ feudal superiority. He inquired about Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel’s tardiness and noted the young chevalier’s presence at Les Touches that evening. Mariotte cautioned against speaking of the chevalier’s visits in front of Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, who loved town gossip.Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel arrived next, led by a page with a lantern. Thin and elderly, she dressed in the fashion of her youth and always carried keys and a silver snuff-box. Though of the highest Breton nobility, her family’s influence was waning: a sister’s marriage to a Kergarouet had annexed the name, and she ran her property with strict frugality. Her admired avarice drew no censure; nieces visited often, hoping to win her favor.Jacqueline de Pen-Hoel, a friend of Zéphirine du Guenic, intended to marry one niece to Calyste, the young chevalier, to bind the family to the du Guenics. Despite the du Guenics’ wealth, they kept a respectful distance, valuing pride and status. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel treated Calyste with a proprietary air, believing her plans granted her oversight. She held conservative views on morality and distrusted new influences, preferring old customs.She asked whether Monsieur du Halga would join them; the rector confirmed seeing him earlier. Mention of du Halga stirred expectation as the group prepared for an evening game; the rector fetched a basket and counters with a childlike eagerness.A firm knock announced Chevalier du Halga’s arrival. Frail and dressed for his health, he was once a competent naval officer. Now gentle and preoccupied with his little dog, he retained traces of former gallantry but rarely spoke of past exploits. Hardships suffered during the emigration had undermined his health, yet he remained respected in Guérande, having served as mayor. The rector’s cough during the prayer for the king quietly hinted at the political tensions still present in the town.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Mouche is a card game in which each player receives five cards and one card is turned up as trump. Players may play or abstain; abstaining costs only the stake. Winning a trick shares the pot; losing makes one mouche, obliging payment of the full stake and enlarging the pot for the next deal. Players may exchange cards from the remaining deck, but only in order. The trump card belongs to the dealer, who may exchange it for any card in hand. The strongest card is Mistigris, the knave of clubs.Despite simple rules, mouche proves engaging, exposing greed, cunning, and expression. At the Hôtel du Guénic players begin with twenty counters each, representing five sous, making the total stake five farthings—a notable sum for the group. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël and Mademoiselle Zéphirine treat the game seriously, likening it to a financial venture or stock speculation.A convention from September 1825 allows a player who loses ten sous to quit the game; the chevalier and the baron, however, subvert this by lending counters to losers to keep play going. The presence of Demoiselle de Kergarouët and her aunt raises the stakes further, as the young women, enamored of Calyste, care little for the small sums at risk.The nightly games are punctuated by talk of local events and public affairs. Players sometimes forget stakes, provoking accusations of cheating—particularly aimed at the rector. Evenings can end abruptly, with players departing empty-handed and grumbling. Attempts to introduce other games like whist and Boston have failed; the circle prefers mouche, which outlasts newer diversions.As the rector deals one night, the baroness inquires about the chevalier’s health; he jokes about migrating ailments. Conversation turns to the recent death of the Duc de Lenoncourt and reflections on mortality. Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël announces the Comtesse de Kergarouët’s marriage, prompting gossip about Mademoiselle des Touches, a woman of dubious reputation. The baroness and the rector exchange concerned looks—further muted when Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël holds Mistigris.After the game the chevalier escorts Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël home. She confides that Calyste seems infatuated with Mademoiselle des Touches, an actress. The baroness frets Calyste might be led astray. The rector tries to reassure her: Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoël intends to leave her fortune to the niece who marries Calyste, showing good intentions, but he warns that Mademoiselle des Touches is a dangerous influence, notorious for scandal.Distressed, the baroness laments the erosion of her close bond with Calyste and fears being supplanted. The rector promises to investigate Calyste’s involvement with the actress. The baroness accompanies him to the gate, anxiously awaiting Calyste’s return; instead she hears the rector’s footsteps fade into the night.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The distressed baroness returned home to find the whole town aware of her son Calyste's frequent visits to Mademoiselle des Touches, visits she had assumed private. She had tried to lure him earlier by taking up her needlework, but her maternal jealousy was in vain; Calyste spent more time there and came home later each night, once as late as midnight.As she stitched, the baroness wondered what held men so captive to Mademoiselle des Touches, what secret charm could make them forget family and duty. She longed to meet the woman and judge her character, fearing modern influences that might corrupt her innocent son. Calyste, of noble Breton and Irish lineage, had been carefully raised; she had entrusted his education to the rector of Guerande, believing him shielded from impure thoughts. Yet his schooling left him ignorant of contemporary literature and science, and he remained a fervent Catholic.The baroness had envisioned a modest, secure life for Calyste, hoping to arrange a suitable marriage with a wealthy heiress and aided by an expected inheritance. Those plans seemed threatened by Mademoiselle des Touches. When he finally returned at midnight she greeted him with relief, noting his fatigue and signs of emotional turmoil, which she attributed to excitement rather than deeper trouble. Still, bitterness grew as she realized his affections lay elsewhere and feared the consequences.The next day Calyste slept late, indulged by his mother. At dinner she noticed his attire and suspected he planned to dine again at Les Touches. Her sister voiced concern over his changed behavior, blaming Mademoiselle des Touches’s influence. The baroness feared the woman's morals and the impact on Calyste, who had begun reading varied books under her guidance.Calyste defended Mademoiselle des Touches: she had rejected his love and offered friendship. He insisted she was a talented artist deserving respect despite an unconventional lifestyle. The baroness remained unconvinced, picturing a loss of values and prospects. The baron dismissed his wife's worries as youthful alarm.Meanwhile Guerande buzzed with rumors that Mademoiselle des Touches was infatuated with Calyste and exerted a mysterious hold over him. The rector and other townsfolk disapproved of her lifestyle, seeing her as a threat to social norms.Mademoiselle Félicité des Touches, known as Camille Maupin, was a celebrated writer whose life and works provoked curiosity and scandal. Orphaned during the Revolution and raised by an aunt, she developed a strong intellect through extensive reading. Despite beauty and talent, she struggled with the limits society imposed on women.Félicité’s early tragedies shaped her into a formidable literary figure whose plays and novels challenged conventions. She rejected traditional marriage to preserve independence and made her salon a hub for artists and thinkers. Drawn to Calyste’s youthful innocence, she found their relationship complicated: it awakened private longings that conflicted with her public ambition, her fear of aging, and her determination to maintain beauty and autonomy. As their connection deepened, Félicité had to weigh love against ambition and independence, risking the carefully constructed life she had built.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few hundred yards from Guérande, Brittany’s soil gives way to salt marshes and sandy dunes. We descend into a bleached landscape of ponds and a narrow arm of the sea that separates the mainland from Croisic, a peninsula reached by treacherous quicksand beaches.At the road junction from Croisic to Guérande stands a country house set in a garden of wind-sculpted pines. Built of slaty stone and granite, its plain exterior—regular windows, high pointed roof with gables and dormers—faces the road on one side and the desert toward Croisic on the other. A brook runs from the park, crosses the road, and disappears into the sands.The house, Les Touches, harmonizes in gray with its surroundings. The estate yields income from the marshes and nearby farms—over ten thousand francs a year—and though the Revolution stripped feudal rights, local salt workers still call it “the château.” Félicité, its owner, restored Les Touches while preserving its desolate aspect, adding only brick gate columns to mark the entrance.The ground-floor plan is that of many century-old country houses, probably raised on castle ruins. Félicité turned the entrance hall into a billiard room that opens to a large salon and dining room; the kitchen communicates with the dining room via an office. Décor is simple and graceful: gray-painted walls, mahogany furniture covered in green silk. The dining room holds mahogany buffets and horsehair chairs, adorned with engravings by Audran.Upstairs, Félicité occupies a suite with a salon, a large bedroom, and two cabinets; another suite serves guests. The guest rooms are modest, but Félicité’s apartment reveals luxurious taste—Gobelin tapestries, heavy silk drapes, fine Louis XV furniture. Her study, in modern mahogany and filled with intimate objects, contrasts with the bedroom’s opulence and hints at her complex character.Outside, the salt marshes, dunes, and distant ocean create a melancholic yet elevating atmosphere. This stark beauty captivates Camille, who prefers Les Touches’ desolation to Guérande’s lushness. Calyste, too, feels drawn to the estate as refuge from Guérande’s confines; his attachment to Félicité, founded in admiration and a desire to love, persists despite her rejections.At Les Touches, Calyste encounters rich art and culture—nineteenth-century music and literature that awaken dormant sensibilities. Félicité, who nurtures his intellect, becomes a maternal figure, inspiring both admiration and affection. She now gives him music lessons and the house feels alive with creativity, a vivid contrast to rural dullness that profoundly affects him.One visit, Calyste hears Félicité playing the piano as he approaches; the music stops when he reaches the billiard room. He sits quietly and the improvisation resumes—haunting and deeply expressive. Entering, he finds her in tears, anxious over Claude Vignon’s absence. He offers to search for him, but Félicité gently asks him to stay, calling her tears a natural overflow. She reflects on the complexities of her feelings and her fraught relationship with Claude, acknowledging the age difference and that her earlier rejection of Calyste sprang from self-awareness and the inevitability of separation. Seeking truth and intimacy, she prepares to tell him her story over tea. Calyste, eager to support her, sits at her feet, unaware of the full depth of what she will reveal.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“I promised you this tale of the past, and here it is,” Camille begins. The woman who sent the letter is the Marquise de Rochefide. The old marquis, after marrying his eldest daughter to a Portuguese grandee, sought a noble match for his son to secure a peerage he never obtained. The Comtesse de Montcornet named a young lady in Orne: Mademoiselle Béatrix-Maximilienne-Rose de Casteran, youngest daughter of the Marquis de Casteran, who was eager to marry off daughters without dowries to preserve his fortune for his son. The Casterans were of the bluest blood. Béatrix, raised at the château de Casteran, was twenty when she married in 1828. She had originality, independence of thought, enthusiasm, and a passion for art — traits that, Camille warns, expose women to abyssal risks men can more safely navigate. Her grandmother approved the match to a lesser man; the Rochefides liked that the Casterans linked them to distinguished families and helped secure a peerage for their son in Charles X’s batch, later undone by the July Revolution.Early married life proved perilous. Rochefide misread Béatrix’s ignorance for coldness and, branding her among frigid women, reverted to his bachelor ways. Society said he was fortunate to have a wife content to shine and host a salon of distinguished minds. But Rochefide was no simpleton; his clever vanity bred jealousy and a petty tyranny when Béatrix appeared indifferent to his infidelities. Camille offers this analysis to explain Béatrix’s conduct. Béatrix admired Camille greatly, but admiration can curdle to jealousy. With a remarkable Parisian salon behind her, Camille watched Béatrix try to found her own, drawing superficial guests without the time to build true society. Camille at first thought Béatrix sought mere celebrity; yet Béatrix possessed a grand soul, regal pride, and the intellect to grasp topics from metaphysics to music.Béatrix was striking: slender and fair, with a long pointed face, a magnificent forehead, pale sea-green eyes often ringed darkly, sharp yet elegant features, an exquisite waist and noble shoulders. Her lines were severe; her complexion and coloring lacked the pliant charm of brunettes, and at times her face could seem harsh. Dressed well, however, she could appear nearly divine.Félicité showed Calyste a fine copy of a Mieris painting, and Camille noted that fair women have a precious diversity; brunettes, she joked, have a single charm. She warned Calyste not to fall for Béatrix; by the time he noticed, it would be too late.Béatrix had spent early marriage judging men and life. The revolutionary disturbances bored her at her husband’s country estate. Returning to Paris after the July Revolution, she expected a moral upheaval and took an intellectual interest in the new doctrines, but soon saw the regime offered no place for individual superiority and chose personal happiness over societal ideals.Then she met Gennaro Conti, a Neapolitan composer of brilliant surface gifts who would never reach the first rank. Conti’s singing and manner captivated Béatrix, and she fell madly in love, taking him from Camille. Conti posed as an inspired artist but was duplicitous and self-serving beneath the mask. Camille, devoted to Conti’s talents yet clear-eyed about his nature, warned Béatrix of the gulf she was entering. Blinded by passion, Béatrix was undeterred; Conti rejoiced, and Camille even lent him money for their journey to Italy. Béatrix left a letter for her husband and departed, remaining abroad two years and writing enchanting letters to Camille, expressing adoration and seeing Camille as the sole woman who understood her.At that moment Claude Vignon enters the scene, causing a hush. He is imposing despite a haggard look: a high forehead, a firm, sarcastic mouth. Vignon is complex — capable of deep thought but often paralyzed by it, prone to criticism that destroys rather than builds, his intellect sharp yet indifferent to worldly matters. His dry, brusque remarks ruffle those present.“Which way did you come?” asks Mademoiselle des Touches, surprised.“By the door,” Claude replies dryly.Sensing tension, he asks, “Am I in the way?” Calyste, provoked, attempts to speak but is cut by Claude’s sardonic asides about sentiment. Calyste leaves angry at Claude’s indelicacy and pitying Félicité. Returning home, his mother delights at his arrival; he takes out Béatrix’s letter, which threatens to disturb family peace.In the letter Béatrix describes Italy: the fatigue of constant enjoyment, the strains of life with Conti, a felt loss of social equality and the sacrifices made for love. She contrasts her constrained position with Camille’s freedom, longs for solitude, admires Italy’s beauty, and laments financial struggles that will likely bring them back to Paris. Camille’s account shows Béatrix’s mix of exaltation and disillusionment.Folding the letter, Calyste is sad. His mother senses his melancholy and questions him. He confesses that Félicité opened his eyes to the insufficiency of his education and revealed the possibility of a nobler life. His aunt suggests a safe match with a good Breton girl; Calyste rejects this, yearning instead for the freedom and passion he associates with love. His mother, understanding, embraces him and encourages the pursuit of happiness while trying to temper his dreams with maternal wisdom.After dinner Calyste returns to Les Touches, where Claude and Félicité remain at table. Claude, fond of fine dining, enjoys Félicité’s cooking. Calyste presents Béatrix’s letter; Claude muses on the fortune of Parisian women to have men of genius to adore. Félicité defends Béatrix, insisting her grand nature will endure.The evening’s witty banter between Claude and Félicité captivates Calyste. Claude speculates about Béatrix and Conti’s return, stirring emotions in Camille. Calyste, lost in thought, pictures Béatrix as Félicité described her and feels deep sympathy. At home he explains his late hour; his mother reassures him, emphasizing love and happiness and urging him to seek what is noble if he must love. The scene closes on a tender family dynamic of encouragement, grounding, and the young man’s yearning for passion and purpose.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################What young man, full of restrained emotion, would not be eager to see Madame de Rochefide land at Croisic? Calyste surprised his parents by leaving early, moved by an unfamiliar vigor as he hurried along the walls of Les Touches to avoid being seen, feeling excitement and a touch of shame at the thought that Felicite or Vignon might notice.At the shore he hid in a travelers' shelter and watched two boats arrive from Croisic, loaded with luggage suggesting distinguished passengers. One boat carried a young woman in a straw bonnet, a maid, and a footman. Calyste was captivated by a chest stamped "MME. LA MARQUISE DE ROCHEFIDE," and felt an immediate, inexplicable attraction, ready to transfer the affection he once held for Camille to this new figure.While the luggage was unloaded he overheard the maid and footman speaking of waiting for someone from Les Touches and learned that the marquise had already been collected. Agitated and longing, he hurried toward Guerande, avoiding the servants of Les Touches.Consumed by thoughts of the marquise, he contrasted this fresh ardor with his earlier feeling for Camille. Returning home late, he found a letter from Mademoiselle des Touches inviting him to celebrate the marquise's arrival; it mentioned the Casteran family and reassured his mother of the woman’s noble lineage.

Augustine Guillaume's cousin's tale is worth mentioning. Élodie-Rose, the widow of Maelien, lived a very miserable life. She was born in the same city, Paris, in the same year as Augustine, and grew up happily alongside her, until she got married. She married very early at 16, to a skinny man exactly twice her age with the same hair color and height,  and moved to Albi with him immediately afterward. The last time Augustine and Élodie-Rose saw each other was at Élodie-Rose’s wedding. Élodie-Rose became single again very shortly after and had to continue her life in a town far from her family. She endured a life full of hardship and pain. The six feet tall lady aged very quickly—her dark black hair turned gray, she developed stage 4 glioblastoma, the worst type of brain cancer, and her beautiful hazel eyes grew sad. She lived in Albi for two decades, most of it alone,  and died there a lonely death.


At the gathering Calyste arrived a tumult of feeling. In Camille's suite he was introduced to Gennaro Conti, a man with a Byron-like presence. The marquise, reclining on a divan, surpassed his imagination: more beautiful than he had pictured, hair braided with bluebells and elegantly dressed. His affection mingled with hope and fear.At dinner he was assigned to escort her; his heart raced and he stumbled for words, daunted by her beauty. She noticed and asked if he were unwell; he confessed he had never seen anyone so beautiful except his mother. Mademoiselle des Touches, aware of his feelings, counseled restraint and warned the marquise could not return such fondness.Conversation shifted to Camille's travels, and Calyste perceived a growing distance: the marquise seemed absorbed by Conti, whose talent made Calyste feel insignificant. When Conti and Camille performed, their music stirred him to tears; the marquise turned away. Overwhelmed by admiration and despair, he slipped away to a nearby sofa, lost in thought and the painful sense of his own inadequacy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################X. DRAMACalyste returned to the house where Claude Vignon was waiting in the bedroom. Claude, who sensed more than he revealed, told Calyste that the situation was not what it seemed: he believed Camille loved Calyste. Calyste reacted with bewilderment and asked about Claude himself. Claude confessed a deep weariness with life and a bitterness at recent events; he had not wandered the marshes for pleasure but because he felt wounded when he thought Calyste favored Camille. He planned to leave Les Touches the next day, perhaps for Paris with Conti, and predicted that once he was gone passions here would become tumultuous. He warned Calyste that the game before him was perilous: he faced two extraordinary women—Beatrix, relentless; Camille, noble—and Calyste’s excessive feeling for one might keep him from using the other with prudence. Claude, disgusted with women yet clear-eyed, advised Calyste to be careful: between those reefs he might be wrecked.Calyste, stunned to learn that Camille loved him while he loved Beatrix, felt crushed. He replayed Felicite’s (Mademoiselle des Touches’) earlier rejection, Claude’s return, and Beatrix’s laughter in the salon. If Camille loved him, how could he continue to adore Beatrix? He could see no solution; silence settled over him.Later, at midnight, he heard Claude confront Camille (called Mademoiselle des Touches or Felicite). Claude accused her of coming to Paris last year desperately in love with Calyste and using him as a screen, bringing Claude to Les Touches to hide her secret adoration. He called her scheme grand and ignoble, said she had misjudged him as simple when he had divined her, and declared that she had never really been loved and likely never would be after refusing chance. Camille asked softly why love fled her; Claude answered that she was not lovable in the ordinary way—her mind too deep, her charm abstract and overpowering, repelling strong men. She might please youth like Calyste briefly, but such strength fatigued others. Camille protested, asking if she were an anomaly; Claude suggested possibly. He said he would leave to spare her the shame of his continuing presence as a screen. He insisted they could part without the pain of betrayed hope because they had no mutual happiness to mourn. Love for Camille, he said, was for her an ideal, a sort of spiritual kingdom she mistook for the usual needs of humanity; she had misled him. He would return to Paris to his misery.Calyste then revealed himself. Claude told him plainly that Camille loved him, not for herself but for the exquisite pain of longing—a passion he sarcastically observed to be, in some sense, masculine. Camille’s tears and agitation showed the truth. Claude accused her of driving Calyste toward Madame de Rochefide, saying Camille had dug her own grave and that if she had confided in him the suffering might have been avoided.
