Camille, moved, took Calyste’s head, kissed his hair, and begged him to forget what he had heard; “I count for nothing in all this,” she urged. She rose before them abruptly and made a declaration: in conceiving love without hope she felt it brought one nearest God; she commanded him not to love her, yet vowed, “I will love you as no woman will!” Claude knelt and kissed her hand. She dismissed Calyste to spare his mother concern.Calyste returned to Guerande distraught, glancing at Beatrix’s window on the way. That night he told his mother, tearful and abrupt, that Camille loved him but that he no longer loved her. The next day he stationed a watcher on the road toward Saint-Nazaire and rode out with two horses. A carriage arrived with two ladies and two gentlemen; among them were Beatrix and Camille. Also appearing were Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel, the Vicomtesse de Kergarouet, and Charlotte. Camille invited the new ladies to ride in her carriage; the viscountess, delighted at meeting Camille Maupin, accepted despite her elder sister's misgivings. Charlotte recognized Calyste and greeted him, but was painfully aware of her plainness beside the Parisian women.In the crowd Beatrix, elegant in straw and muslin, leaned on a parasol; Camille took Calyste’s arm. The viscountess and her companions conversed with airs of local superiority and curiosity. When the viscountess learned Madame de Rochefide accompanied the party, she expressed pity for the marquise’s misfortunes; Camille explained the separation from Conti was by mutual consent, prompting the old lady’s sympathy. Charlotte retreated embarrassed. Calyste kissed Camille’s hand, leaving a tear, and Beatrix, wiping anger from her eyes, looked away. Camille’s calm and graceful manner affected Calyste strongly; she had soothed his agitation despite the earlier explosive revelations.Back at home, the visitors dined amid lively games. The viscountess attempted to flatter literature; the household played mouche; Calyste’s momentary gaiety made everyone assume the worst was over and that marriage prospects with Charlotte might succeed. Yet Calyste remained inwardly troubled, comparing the accomplished Parisians to his childhood acquaintances. Only his mother, Fanny, perceived his unease and urged him to offer the viscountess a ride in Camille’s carriage; Calyste ran back to Les Touches to arrange it.XI. FEMALE DIPLOMACYCalyste arrived as Camille and Beatrix left the salon after dinner. Camille teasingly accused him of abandoning the viscountess and her daughter for them. When Beatrix commented that the viscountess’s niece might suit Calyste, Camille immediately demurred: “The marriage will never take place.” Calyste, mortified by the marquise’s indifference toward the Breton girl, stayed silent. Camille, seeing his discomfort and her own flushed face, yielded him her hand briefly and then sat at the piano to play melancholy variations.Beatrix watched him closely. Later, in private, Camille took Calyste into her chamber and delivered hard but candid counsel. She told him that if he wanted to win Beatrix, he must seem to love Camille still; otherwise Beatrix would be lost. Loving and making oneself beloved were different skills, and Calyste’s candor would not serve him. She proposed a deliberate deception: he should promise blind obedience, spend every day with her until four, avoid private encounters with Beatrix, and allow her to use her art to direct Beatrix’s feelings. Camille trusted her ability to fashion the marquise’s heart; in a week Beatrix would love him, she boldheartedly claimed. Calyste, naive and desperate, agreed and embraced Camille, who wept but did not stop him.He left gaily, convinced of success, and returned to the card table where Charlotte believed victory assured. The household celebrated modestly. After Calyste departed Les Touches that evening, Camille, alone and distraught, confessed to Beatrix the depth of her feelings: she was forty and in love; she had mourned youth and feared being loved only out of pity. She admitted that she might even fling herself into the sea if Calyste asked it—an expression of the despair she felt. Camille, foreseeing how Beatrix’s coldness could secure Calyste for her, pleaded with the marquise’s generosity and warned that she, too, feared losing him. Yet she had a secret means to keep him true and begged Beatrix’s help. Beatrix, piqued and curious, left with mixed feelings; Camille sank into tears and smoked opium-laced tobacco, brooding on the young lover’s head and the pain of her situation.Next morning Calyste returned before midday; Camille, pacing by the window and watching the sea, told him to stay to dinner but to show Beatrix a look of regret when she passed—a signal to conceal his devotion. Throughout the next six days Camille used all her conversational art and small deceptions—the feints and false magnanimities usual in a duel of hearts—aiming to conceal her love while revealing enough to inflame Beatrix’s pride and curiosity. Her comments, sometimes treacherous, often wounded Beatrix, who took offense at the perceived distrust. Yet Beatrix could not help being secretly amused to find such a great writer caught in pettiness and resolved to demonstrate where Camille’s greatness ended.Camille’s tactics included assigning Calyste to study, keeping him in her company, and hinting at the marquise’s supposed indifference to his visits. She reported to Calyste that Beatrix had written only once to Conti that week, implying a thaw. Calyste lived for the daily moments before Beatrix and read late into the night, bringing home armfuls of books. His mother, alarmed, implored him to leave Les Touches, but he refused. He nearly broke his promise and resolved to approach Beatrix privately in the garden one morning to read her eyes.On that morning, after Camille and Beatrix returned from the marshes, Calyste met Beatrix on a garden path. He could not restrain his trembling and asked if he had displeased her. She answered faintly that he had neither pleased nor displeased her. When he implored whether he was indifferent to her, she asked if they should not be indifferent given the “binding attachment” each had elsewhere. Calyste, stunned, declared that he had once loved Camille but loved her no longer. Beatrix, hurt yet composed, noted that Calyste and Camille spent every morning together and suggested that Camille did not prefer a cigar to his company. Camille then appeared and rebuked Calyste: was this what he had promised? She took his arm and drew him away. The scene displayed Camille’s great sacrifice: she forced Calyste to reciprocity of appearances, shocking and grand, an abnegation that could lift to angelic heights or plunge to hell.At breakfast Beatrix determined to stifle any rising love and behaved with gentle indifference toward Calyste, trying the course Camille had advised. Felicite proposed excursions, but Beatrix refused, saying her position was delicate and she would not jeopardize her happiness because Conti was coming to fetch her. Calyste took this news with pallor, left Les Touches depressed and near madness at the thought of losing Beatrix, and spent his time pondering a decisive explanation to win her.Throughout these scenes the interplay of strategy, pride, and genuine feeling is constant. Claude, who plans to leave, remains the cynical observer who recognizes motives and predicts calamities. Camille, inwardly torn and capable of self-effacement, chooses to guide events through subtle deception, convinced she can shape Beatrix’s heart and preserve her own claim to Calyste. Beatrix, proud and resolute, answers with politeness that may hide deeper sentiment but insists on preserving her autonomy and attachments. Calyste, immature and ardent, is trapped between youthful passion and artful manipulation, a pawn in the hands of stronger wills whose consequences he cannot yet foresee.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Calyste returned home he shut himself in his room until dinner and continued writing. His mother, alarmed by his seclusion, found him amid crumpled papers composing a letter to Beatrix, stirred by a new distrust of Camille after the marquise’s recent demeanor.Calyste’s first love letter poured out intense feeling. He told Beatrix his love had swelled since meeting her, surpassing any dream. Though he claimed there was nothing new to say about her beauty, he insisted on a profound connection, comparing himself to an insect drawn to her light and acknowledging his youth and naivety. Feeling unworthy, he wondered why his ardor failed to warm her, seeing her disdain overshadow his worship. He contrasted a former attachment to Felicite with his present truth: Beatrix.He admitted she had unintentionally shaken his happiness yet expected no return of affection. Recalling Camille’s lesson that true love must grow and endure, he believed that if Beatrix loved him they could rise above the fears that had gripped Camille. He implored kindness and recognition, likening his love to a martyr’s sacrifice, asking simply to accompany her, be treated as a friend, and receive an answer. Much of the letter repeated these themes. His mother, reading it, was moved to tears—alarmed by the rawness of feeling in a love letter she had never seen from him.Torn about sending it, Calyste consulted the Chevalier du Halga on delivering the note discreetly. The chevalier suggested slipping it to Beatrix’s maid, but Calyste chose to give it directly. He hurried to Les Touches, found Beatrix and Camille together, and impulsively handed the letter to Beatrix, who accepted it without hesitation.After a restless night, Calyste awaited her reply and read it in the garden. Beatrix acknowledged his noble feelings but reminded him he was still a child and affirmed her commitment to Camille. She doubted her own suitability as a partner and warned that accepting his love would be an insult to Camille. Candid about her flaws and jealous tendencies, she said she could not provide the happiness Camille offered and would likely bring Calyste unhappiness, urging him to seek a young girl worthy of his devotion.Moved, the baroness approached Calyste to probe his feelings and read Beatrix’s reply aloud—admiring Beatrix’s nobility yet worrying about her son’s future. She urged him to consider marrying Charlotte de Kergarouet to secure the estate and their prospects, but Calyste remained steadfast for Beatrix and dismissed his mother’s counsel.He withdrew to write again, reaffirming his unwavering love and even fantasizing about escaping with Beatrix, convinced their love could defy society’s constraints. Troubled by this escalation, the baroness sought the Chevalier du Halga’s view as she walked through Guerande; amused by the infatuation, he offered lighthearted remarks.Returning home, the baroness found another letter revealing the depth of Calyste’s obsession. Alarmed, she prayed for his well-being. When he came back she disclosed she had read his letters; he reassured her of his love. They planned a boat trip to Croisic the next day, hoping to see Beatrix. The letters had shown a surprising maturity and clarity in Calyste but also laid bare the intensity of his emotion and the possible consequences of his infatuation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of the greatest pleasures for small minds is deceiving a greater soul, and Beatrix felt herself inferior to Camille Maupin in both talent and passion. While Calyste rushed to Les Touches, thrilled by first love, Beatrix enjoyed being the object of his affection even though she did not intend to reciprocate. She prided herself on repressing any feeling, imagining a noble sacrifice like Camille’s; the vanities of Frenchwomen flattered her, and she felt righteous resisting Calyste’s allure.The two women lounged in the salon, surrounded by flowers and a gentle breeze, their inner unrest at odds with the serene outdoors. Camille, aware of the upheaval she had caused, guarded her secrets fiercely, seeing Beatrix as a cunning rival. She sought solace in nature, finding meaning beyond personal pain; Calyste had become almost a guardian angel, lifting her to higher reflection. Yet Beatrix’s triumphant manner unsettled her.When Calyste entered with a letter, Camille watched their interaction and sensed an unspoken understanding: Beatrix now regarded Calyste as hers, while he looked guilty and transparent. He announced plans for an excursion and Camille, anxious, urged preparations—suspecting conspiracy between them, which left her discomposed.After Calyste left to arrange the boat, Camille’s fears deepened. On his return, neither woman met his eye; he was puzzled and later returned home heavy with despair, a stark contrast to his earlier joy—his mother noticed but he dismissed her concern.Beatrix, meanwhile, found herself stirred by Calyste’s letter in ways she had not expected. Doubts about what Calyste and Camille might be scheming together bred jealousy and anger; she paced her room in turmoil.At dinner the atmosphere was tense. Camille, sensing confrontation, tried to avoid Calyste’s presence. The meal passed with veiled insults and a battle of glances, leading to a direct clash afterward. Beatrix accused Camille of trickery and cruelty; Camille countered by exposing Beatrix’s motives and character. The exchange escalated, Beatrix denouncing what she saw as Camille’s lack of traditional femininity and compassion.Camille then disclosed her deep love for Calyste. Beatrix replied that she could not accept a relationship that would burden him—she would not be his mistress if she could not be his wife. That declaration struck Camille and intensified their rivalry. Camille declared their friendship over and threatened to write to Conti, Beatrix’s former lover; Beatrix refused to yield.The night left both women reflective, each confronting desires and consequences. Their conflict did not resolve so much as settle into a precarious balance as they navigated their feelings for Calyste and the societal expectations that constrained them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It was late August; the sky was clear and the sea shimmered. Paludiers in white harvested salt in the marshes as the baron and baroness admired the scene from the jetty. The baron wanted one last look at the marshes, prompting a paludier’s wry joke about the Guenic family’s mortality. At that moment a party from Les Touches arrived: the marquise at the head, followed by Calyste and Camille. The baroness felt a strong aversion to Beatrix, who, though tastefully dressed, seemed heartless to her.Calyste introduced the visitors; the baron noted Camille’s substantial ownership of the marshes, and a paludier praised her character. The groups separated, the baron remarking on Mademoiselle des Touches’ youthful appearance while the baroness fretted over Calyste’s attachment to Beatrix. They then boarded a boat for Croisic, where the marquise maintained a cool demeanor. Camille chastised Calyste for disobedience; he was torn between anger and love for Beatrix. The passage was tense and silent, and at the port barefoot women unloaded salt.The Croisic peninsula offered strange, wind-sculpted granite rocks and a hollow basin used for bathing; a lone box bush clung to a distant promontory, a small marvel of resilience. Calyste led Beatrix to a viewpoint while Camille sought solitude among the rocks, reflecting on her life and the nature of her feelings for Calyste. Calyste and Beatrix discussed the ocean, then turned to their emotions.Beatrix confessed an inner desolation and insisted she could never be with Calyste despite loving him; she feared abandoning her present life and the pain of unfulfilled love. Calyste, desperate to prove his devotion, offered to sacrifice everything, but Beatrix rejected the idea of sacrifice and reaffirmed her commitment to her situation. At the rock where the box bush grew, Calyste’s passion overwhelmed him, and in a moment of physical intimacy Beatrix slipped and fell. He rushed to save her; amid the confusion their feelings intensified. Gasselin and Camille arrived to help, and the hurt Beatrix was carried to a farmhouse for care.Calyste stayed by her side, alternating despair and hope as the doctor assured them she would recover. Beatrix, moved by his devotion, began to soften. Playing the invalid, she enjoyed his adoration—an unusual power over him that contrasted with her past experiences—while keeping a careful distance and reminding him of her obligation to another man. Despite that resistance, she found herself drawn to Calyste’s steadfast affection, disclosed past struggles, and accepted his vow of exclusive love. Their bond deepened and Beatrix began to see him as a possible source of happiness.They prepared to return to the rock where she had fallen; Beatrix acknowledged the joy he brought but reiterated her duty to another. Calyste, full of hope, believed love could overcome her past. Back at Les Touches, however, they encountered Conti, Beatrix’s former lover, and the moment shattered—revealing the tangled complexities of love, loyalty, and uncertain consequences.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The marquise's inner turmoil was evident when Conti arrived unexpectedly and took Beatrix's arm, forcing her to abandon Calyste, who felt humiliated and conflicted. Though convinced of her love, he was struck by her visible distress and fell silent.Calyste turned to Camille, who revealed the tangled power dynamics: Beatrix could not refuse Conti because of lingering ties from their past. Camille warned Calyste that Conti was manipulative and would use insinuations to regain control.Conti’s return after a failed opera seemed opportunistic. He feigned indifference to Beatrix's new attachment while subtly undermining her confidence. At dinner his jovial manner masked intent; veiled comments about women's loyalty wounded Beatrix and Camille. Afterwards Beatrix told Camille she felt helpless against Conti and even blamed Camille for his premature return, their conversation exposing deep rivalry. Beatrix insisted she would always hold a place in Calyste's heart; Camille retorted about the fleeting nature of Beatrix's reign.Meanwhile Conti and Calyste had a tense exchange. Conti cast himself as the injured party, claiming to have lost interest and urging Calyste to pursue Beatrix, suggesting her pride would spur revenge. Unaware of Conti’s designs, Calyste confessed details of his time with Beatrix, inflaming Conti’s jealousy.That evening Conti staged a salon performance to provoke Beatrix and assert dominance. Entranced by Conti's singing, Calyste approached Beatrix with newfound confidence, not perceiving a trap. The next morning Beatrix left with Conti, and Calyste was devastated. Camille informed him; he sank into grief, clinging to his belief in Beatrix despite Camille's attempts to reveal her true nature.Weeks passed and Calyste’s sorrow deepened. He wandered aimlessly, haunted by memories, while his family mistook his melancholy for a passing phase. His health and social withdrawal alarmed them. They sought to distract him with a marriage to Charlotte, but his heart remained with Beatrix. He confessed his love, rejected Charlotte’s advances, and begged his family to let Beatrix go.Doctors could not diagnose his decline. The baron, fearing for the family legacy, pressured him to marry; under strain Calyste promised but remained tethered to Beatrix. After the baron’s death Calyste slid further into despair. A chance meeting with Camille offered a flicker of hope: she proposed helping him find Beatrix in Paris. Renewed, Calyste agreed, and they set out for the city, hoping reunion might restore him.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Felicite’s love had set the stage for a prosperous future for Calyste. She wrote to the Duchesse de Grandlieu about him, sold her house for 2,500,000 francs, bought another for 700,000, and planned to use one million to recover the du Guenic estates, settling the remainder on Sabine de Grandlieu. Aware that the duke and duchess intended advantageous matches for their daughters—only the youngest, Sabine, was marriageable—Felicite aimed to redirect Calyste’s affections from Beatrix to Sabine.During the trip to Paris, Mademoiselle des Touches confided in the baroness, who prepared a new house for Sabine and Calyste. They were welcomed at the hôtel de Grandlieu, where the baroness received due respect. Mademoiselle des Touches encouraged Calyste to explore Paris while she sought news of Beatrix abroad. The city’s liveliness eased Calyste’s obsession; noticing a resemblance between Beatrix and the engaging Sabine, he found himself drawn to the latter. By the winter of 1837 he seemed refreshed and listened to his mother’s marriage proposal for Sabine, though he hid any indifference.On the day the marriage contract was to be signed, Calyste startled everyone by refusing the benefits offered by Mademoiselle des Touches. Sabine arrived with a letter from Camille Maupin, which Calyste read alone. In it Camille professed her love for him, declared her intention to enter a convent, urged him to accept her gifts for his future welfare, and asked him to remember her kindly while fulfilling his duties as husband and father—placing duty above passion.After reading the letter, Calyste rejoined the others and the contract was signed. The wedding at Saint Thomas d’Aquin followed the next week; Calyste and Sabine left for Brittany amid tears and congratulations. The duchess and Clotilde worried for Sabine, aware that Calyste had not married wholly of his own will. They understood marriage as complex, needing true compatibility: a greater risk for women than men. The Baronne du Guenic, with only one son, took responsibility for looking after Sabine.On the journey the newlyweds felt awkward, each aware of their first moments together. Calyste suggested they be like friends or siblings, which unsettled Sabine and left her pensive. Conversation turned to the past; Calyste reluctantly recounted his former love for Beatrix. Sabine was hurt that their union had resulted from his rejection of another woman, but she found some comfort in his promise to forget Beatrix. Gradually their relationship began to thaw.In letters to her mother Sabine described Brittany, her longing for home, and the challenges of adapting. She acknowledged Calyste’s sorrow and her own difficulty in loving him fully, yet despite counsel to preserve her dignity she became devoted to him, experiencing both happiness and vulnerability. As chatelaine she felt fulfilled: adored by Calyste’s family and tenants, she embraced Breton traditions and the work of reclaiming their estates.A visit to Mademoiselle des Touches in a convent yielded blunt advice: be capricious when necessary and never allow Calyste to see Beatrix again, lest old passions revive. Sabine felt conflicted—unable to feign caprice or entirely control her feelings. Her letters trace the delicate navigation of love and marriage: devotion mixed with insecurity, the struggle to reconcile affection with a husband’s past, and the balance between social expectation and personal identity. The narrative shows how careful management of emotion and circumstance is required for such unions to endure.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Guerande, July 1838.To Madame la Duchesse de Grandlieu:Ah, my dear mother! After three months, I have learned what jealousy is. My heart is torn between love and hatred; I feel betrayed and unloved. How fortunate I am to have you to confide in!It takes so little to set a wife’s curiosity ablaze: “Here’s a key to your palace; go everywhere, but avoid Les Touches!” Mademoiselle des Touches has sown bitterness in me. Why forbid Les Touches? What happiness depends on avoiding a single house in Brittany? Why should I fear?“My dear mother-in-law said, ‘Les Touches belongs to you.’ ‘If Calyste had never set foot in Les Touches!’ exclaimed Aunt Zephirine. ‘He would not be my husband,’ I replied. ‘Then you know what happened there?’ she asked slyly. ‘It is a place of perdition!’ declared Mademoiselle de Pen-Hoel. ‘Mademoiselle des Touches committed many sins there.’ ‘They saved her soul and enriched a convent,’ said the Chevalier du Halga. ‘Would you like to visit Les Touches?’ my mother-in-law asked. ‘No, no!’ I hastily replied.”That little scene felt like a diabolical drama. Such involuntary stabs force anyone to question the stability of their happiness. Calyste never suggested we visit that hermitage—now his property—but his silence made me ask him about it. “Let’s go there,” he said, and so I let chance catch me.Les Touches is enchanting and profoundly artistic—poisonous flowers can be charming; the devil has flowers too. I delighted in that place of horror and watched Calyste for signs that his passion for Mademoiselle des Touches might still live. I dared to mention Madame de Rochefide—“la Rocheperfide”—and he said nothing; when I pressed, he begged me to stop reviving that passion. The plea itself inflamed me. Still, when I spoke to him about forgiving misrecognitions tied to that place, he blushed and I embraced him. Yet I left Les Touches and vowed never to return.My hatred of Madame de Rochefide grows as proof of my love for Calyste. That woman haunts my dreams. The postulant of the Visitation was right—Les Touches is fatal. Calyste has revived past emotions there, stronger than our joy. If Madame de Rochefide is in Paris, I shall stay in Brittany. Poor Mademoiselle des Touches might regret her role when she knew how our odious rival haunted me. But I am ashamed—sometimes I wish to flee Guerande and the sands of Croisic.August 25th: I am determined to live in the ruins of the old chateau. Calyste, worried by my restlessness, consents. Either he suspects nothing or he knows and loves me not. I tremble at finding certainty, so like a child I cover my eyes to avoid the explosion.Oh, mother! I am not loved as I love. Calyste is charming—what man wouldn’t be amiable when adored by a pure, loving girl? Yet suspicion intrudes. By September I oscillate between despair and clinging joy, fretting over whether his past has returned to claim him. Sister Clotilde’s melancholy wisdom recurs to me: a woman faithful to the dead fears no rival. Poor me: I fear I can never win Calyste back from himself.A thousand tendernesses, dear mother. If my terrors are not delusions, Camille Maupin has sold me her fortune dearly. My affectionate respects to papa.These letters reveal the private breach between husband and wife: Sabine imagined a love marriage; Calyste accepted a conventional match. Their honeymoon joys misaligned with expectations.In Brittany, the hotel du Guenic was restored and furnished under the famed architect Grindot, supervised by Clotilde and the Duc and Duchesse de Grandlieu. The young couple planned to return to Paris in December 1838. Sabine took pleasure in settling rue de Bourbon—both to preside over a great household and to read her family’s judgment of her marriage.Calyste, indifferent, allowed Clotilde and the duchess to guide his social life. He earned his standing by name, fortune, and alliance. Sabine, celebrated in Paris as one of its most charming women, found diversions and duties of the season enlivening. Her pregnancy and the birth of a son in October 1839 confirmed a neutral, socially respectable union; Sabine mistakenly nursed the child herself, believing a mother should be devoted wholly to her child.By August 1840 Sabine was nearly done nursing. Calyste, in two years in Paris, had shed the innocence of his early experience in passion. He mixed with Duc Georges de Maufrigneuse, Vicomte Savinien de Portenduere, and others, learning the differences between Parisian and provincial life. Wealth brings idle hours; Paris provides amusements. Contact with young husbands who abandoned noble women for cigars, whist, and clubs gradually eroded domestic virtues. A proud wife may tolerate such dissipation as proof of her success.One October evening, to escape the cries of their newly weaned child, Calyste went to the Variétés, urged by Sabine. He took a stall near the avant-scène. During the interlude he saw Madame de Rochefide in a proscenium box—Beatrix in Paris! The sight struck him like an arrow. He had sometimes even substituted Beatrix for his wife in thought; Beatrix embodied light, life, the Unknown; Sabine embodied duty and the expected.Calyste’s first impulse was to leave. Yet the proscenium door was ajar and he approached. Beatrix sat between Canalis and Raoul Nathan. In three years her appearance had changed—gaunt, faded, eyes hollow—but Parisian artifice and contrivance made her instead more poetically attractive to Calyste. She greeted him with a mix of affected grief and coquettish nostalgia—complaining at his marriage and teasing him about “such a little fool.” She revived old intimacy, pressing his hand and turning tears to performative gestures. She spoke of living modestly in a rue de Chartres house “suitable to my means,” filling her head with literature to distract herself.Calyste left with the perfume of Beatrix still in his nostrils; the rest of the evening she darted sidelong looks that tore at his soul. He returned home haunted: his apartments’ splendor reminded him of the mediocrity Beatrix had mocked, and he resented his wealth because it could not belong to her. He delighted in the possession of a night to relive emotions and feared the way Sabine could read him like a book. A woman who adores can detect every quiver, every sadness; she studies his face for signs of love or its absence.The next morning Calyste learned Sabine watched for signs of convulsions in their son and was joyous to hear the child was better. Yet he avoided breakfast, preferring a walk to his bachelor café. Love puzzles: a man raised with sacred morals may still be drawn to a fallen, dangerous woman while his virtuous wife waits at home; the reasons may be buried vanity—men sometimes seek to assert superiority by conquering what is beneath them—or the thrill of triumph in winning a repugnant or repellent object. Calyste was drawn to Beatrix for reasons neither philosophy nor morals fully explain.He decided, consumed by the thought of seeing her again, to go to rue de Chartres. The house was familiar to him though he had never been there; the old footman asked his name. Calyste waited, gazing into a garden marked by rain-streaked stucco.Beatrix, who had left fortune to her husband when she fled and relied on small sums from her mother and friends, lived in a modest yet tasteful house formerly owned by a lord, with servants and frugal comfort. Calyste entered richly furnished rooms—garnet velvet, celadon vases, a silver cup ascribed to Benvenuto Cellini—elegant relics of a past life. A little table held jeweled trinkets and a work-in-progress, a dagger handle used as a paper-cutter: symbol of critique; watercolors showed the bedrooms of her wandering life.She appeared in studied attire, a gown revealing her bosom, hair in a lace cap, smiling as though expecting a lover. The perfume she wore recalled Les Touches; the scent, her dress, the glint in her eyes made Calyste dizzy. She bade him sit; she said she treated him as a friend and would be no one’s honest mistress, yet she spoke of the impossibility of belonging to any man on earth because of what she had once expected of Calyste. Three hours passed with her urging him to resolve his conjugal faith; she affected to see Sabine as a stiff, dry, brown woman—good, dutiful, untempting—hardly the ideal for a man like Calyste.Calyste, pressed, rose to leave; she teased him to dine with her. He had the impulse to write Sabine a note, and when he began “My dear Sabine—” Beatrix snapped, “‘My dear’? Can you say your wife is still dear?” She accused him of cowardice and deceit and forced him to write that he dined out; she would send the note by her messenger. He returned home at two in the morning. Sabine, who had waited until half-past twelve for him, had gone to bed exhausted. She slept, distressed by the terse note, yet made the charitable assumption that love explains everything.The next morning the child was better. Sabine, smiling, came with the baby to present him before breakfast; Calyste charmed her, overplaying affection. In the salon she caught his face in a mirror and saw weariness. She asked, “What did you do yesterday?” He said Savinien had kept him to dinner and then they went to the club to play whist. Sabine lectured on noblesse and industry, then gently reproached the coldness of his first note to her; he claimed club directors were dandies. Small suspicions accumulated: she remembered the paper she had thrown into her letter-box—perhaps it bore a coronet.Calyste, planning to conceal his doings, rushed to Savinien and then to rue de Chartres to see how Beatrix had passed the night. He found her after her bath, fresh and lively, breakfasting on eggs with wit and candor. He stayed until late; his horse returned sweating such that Sabine, watching from a window, noticed.Meeting him on the staircase she said, “Abd-el-Kader is nearly foundered. You said you’d be gone a moment. I waited three hours.” Calyste, improving in dissimulation, tried to present the surprise of a present: a dressing-table being finished for her, with contributions from his mother and aunt. Sabine clasped him, faint with joy. Then, like a viper’s bite, she recoiled—she had smelled a perfume on his cravat: the scent of another woman’s note-paper. She had kissed where the rival’s kisses had been. Panic overcame her: “Fetch the doctor and my nurse!” she cried. Calyste fled to call them.Sabine, seized by a chill of reaction, lay shivering, crying, “I am dying!” Her maid fetched Madame de Portenduere. In feverish haste she wrote to her mother—a letter of thanks for the dressing-table, an attempt at ordinary gratitude—but her mind raced: “My God! my God! my God!” She feared betrayal after three years and twenty-two years of age; her teeth chattered, her face turned greenish. “I don’t know yet [the rival], but Calyste has told me two lies. Do not pity me. Oh! that letter of yesterday!” She seized the thrown-away paper from her letter-box: a coronet—could it belong to a marquise? She begged Ursula to find out if Madame de Rochefide had returned to Paris.Ursula, stunned, went to the duchess, pledging secrecy. The duchess, who had seen such scenes, advised deceit for Sabine’s sake: “Friendship gives you an experience of a woman my age. I know how Sabine loves her husband; she might become insane.” “Or lose her beauty,” Ursula added. “Let us go to her.”Fortuitously, they arrived before the accoucheur Dommanget and Calyste. The duchess told Sabine that Beatrix was not in Paris; as for Calyste’s night, she declared he had lost a great deal of money at cards and didn’t even know how to pay for the dressing-table. She scoffed at the coronet on the paper—“Everybody writes on coroneted paper now”—and burned the fatal sheet.When Calyste and Dommanget arrived, the duchess informed them: “Sabine’s life is at stake; you have betrayed her for Madame de Rochefide.” Calyste blushed like a schoolboy. The duchess, with the physician, invented a lie to steer the diagnosis: Calyste had lost thirty thousand francs at cards. Dommanget, quick and self-interested, used the lie to explain Sabine’s fainting as reactionary hysterical symptoms due to overexertion from nursing and anxiety. The physician prescribed measures; Calyste sat at the foot of the bed, trying to look tender. Sabine, hearing the explanation, retorted that her husband could lose any sum without being lectured. Calyste embraced her and called her an angel.Two days later, thought safe, Sabine improved. Calyste visited Madame de Rochefide, making a merit of his infamy.“Beatrix,” he said, “you owe me happiness. I have sacrificed my poor little wife to you; she discovered everything. The fatal paper bore your name and coronet; I didn’t notice—the ‘B’ was erased. Your perfume betrayed me. Sabine nearly died; her milk went to her head; erysipelas set in; she may bear marks for life.”Beatrix’s face froze. “So much the better,” she said; “perhaps it will whiten her for you.” Her icy cruelty continued in a series of sarcasms. There is no greater blunder than to speak of one’s virtuous wife to one’s mistress—unless it is to tell one’s wife of the mistress. Calyste had neither the Parisian training to lie to his wife nor the tact to handle his mistress. He begged Beatrix for pardon; she forced him to beg for two hours. She answered from an elevated bitterness: “To speak to me of your wife the day after my fall! Why did you not tell me she is a pearl of virtue?” She declared her love for his soul and then scorned Calyste’s looks.This speech was part of Beatrix’s system: with each passion she became another being, advancing into profligacy. She had judged herself by the mirror, counting wrinkles and crow’s-feet; she knew her resources and used them. Reduced by Conti’s desertion and grateful for scraps from friends, she aimed at a terrible notoriety—vengeance through fame. To prevail over a splendid young woman, she used the arts of courtesans: contempt mixed with rare affection, comparisons meant to mortify, and a cultivated disdain to stimulate conquest. She resolved to make Calyste think himself ugly and unwelcome—thus rekindling his desire to conquer. She contrasted her poetic, comfortable home with the hotel du Guenic’s Faubourg Saint-Germain luxury, painting the latter stupid and empty.Thus, the scene of reconciliation—where she made him swear hatred for his wife and denounce her as playing comedy—took shape in a bower of flowers and rare plants, amid trifles and sumptuous yet carefully economical luxury. Fallen into a mortifying position by Conti’s desertion, Beatrix was determined to gain, at least, the triumphs of notoriety. The misfortune of a rich and beautiful wife like Sabine was to be her pedestal.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Sabine returns to ordinary life after nursing her first child, she appears refreshed and charming, but finds her husband Calyste changed. Instead of the security she expected, she feels anxiety. A letter from her mother hints at Calyste’s discontent and the presence of another woman, Beatrix, confirming Sabine’s worst fears.The vague note offers no clear statement of his feelings. In despair Sabine writes a comment on the letter and leaves it where Calyste will see it. He finds the remark but ignores it, throwing the paper into the fire. Humiliated and convinced she has displeased an idealized husband, Sabine’s distress deepens.Beatrix’s influence becomes visible when a perfume on a later letter reveals her hand. Sabine, recognizing it, realizes the extent of the rival’s hold. In a fleeting, febrile moment she dances with her child, clinging to motherhood’s innocence, but weeps alone when Calyste departs to read Beatrix’s message. Her attempts to regain his affection—decorating the house with flowers, improving her cooking and appearance—meet only indifference, intensifying jealousy and self-doubt.Despite her suffering, Sabine’s love persists. She strives to embody the devoted wife but finds these virtues ineffective. After another failure to move Calyste, she collapses into despair and tears off a beautiful dress in frustration. Over time grief undermines her health; the duchess, her mother, notices and tries to distract her. Sabine remains silent, fearing interference in her marriage, but ultimately confides in her sister Athenais, speaking of anguish and asking that her child be cared for should she die.In a vulnerable moment Sabine warns Athenais of love’s dangers and counsels caution in marriage, lamenting that straightforwardness and passion—her virtues—have become obstacles to happiness. She feels driven toward the duplicity she believes necessary to retain Calyste’s affection.Alarmed, the duchess consults Abbe Brossette and reveals a plan to break Calyste’s attachment to Beatrix and restore her daughter’s happiness. The abbe warns against blending spiritual authority with worldly intrigue but recognizes Sabine’s plight. He urges consideration of moral implications and suggests pursuing a more honorable solution. Nevertheless, the duchess, determined to spare Sabine further suffering, resolves to act for her daughter’s sake, even if it means engaging in morally questionable measures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A storm was brewing for Monsieur de Rochefide, who enjoyed Parisian life as fully as he had with Beatrix and presumed such a satisfactory existence nearly impossible to disrupt. To see how society treats men and women differently in similar situations, we follow Rochefide’s life after Beatrix left—a tale that might encourage women to stay home and defend their dignity, as Sabine du Guenic did.After Beatrix’s departure, Arthur de Rochefide, now an only child after his sister’s death, became sole owner of the hôtel de Rochefide and inherited a large fortune. His income reached about a thousand francs a day, ample for his tastes. While Beatrix had fulfilled maternal duties, Rochefide enjoyed his wealth without extravagance. His vanity, aided by good looks and a jaunty military tone, let him dismiss women and appropriate others’ wit; acquaintances found him witty enough. As a husband he was pitied; society blamed Beatrix for leaving such a “good” man. Seeking distinction, he joined clubs and championed fashionable causes. His main indulgence was racehorses, run by an English groom, costing him a sizable monthly sum. Despite wealth, he lacked taste and intellect, relying largely on inherited fortune, and embraced bachelor freedoms, claiming he was “born with a caul,” destined for luck.Rochefide’s indifference to domestic life was clear: he rarely dined or slept at home. After several affairs he married Madame Schontz, known in the circles of Fanny Beaupré and others. Madame Schontz, originally La Petite-Aurélie and daughter of Colonel Schiltz, emerged from a difficult upbringing—orphaned, schooled at Saint-Denis, and launched into precarious social worlds where many women ended in poverty or early death. Aurelie’s tumultuous relationships eventually brought her to Rochefide at Valentino’s; captivated by her charm, he married her two years after Beatrix left, a choice society did not condemn.Their relationship unfolded in four phases. First, courtship: he provided an allowance and a furnished apartment; she revealed noble charm and talents, though ambition surfaced as she sought greater comfort. Second, she moved to a better apartment; Rochefide lavished gifts while she ran their household efficiently, impressing him with budgeting and superior dinners and rebuffing wealthier suitors. Third, she became a mother figure to Rochefide’s son, overseeing education and finances and advising investments that yielded gains she did not claim. Fourth, she established herself socially, curating guests and preserving reputation while amassing a modest fortune through savings, investments, and selling jewelry—framing expenses as necessary for maintaining their lifestyle.Through these phases Madame Schontz demonstrated tactical superiority, turning the relationship into mutual benefit while skillfully navigating their social world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Such conduct implied a plan, and Madame Schontz had one. Jealous of Madame du Bruel for two years, she was determined to marry in both church and civil ceremonies — which meant finding a second Arthur. Rochefide’s secret rival remained unknown; Bixiou suspected Léon de Lora, the painter thought Bixiou himself was favored, and others named Victor de Vernisset or Stidmann. None truly understood Madame Schontz, whose single focus was securing a good marriage.The only man of dubious reputation in her salon was Couture, a stock-gambler fallen from grace but still loyal. When he introduced Fabien du Ronceret, a provincial, Madame Schontz took interest. Fabien, son of a chief justice, had left a judgeship in Alençon for Paris with a small income and an appetite for exploiting modern social mores. He grasped the era’s new currency — pride, vanity, and notoriety — and aimed to make a name for himself by being talked about, cultivating attention through ostentation.His Paris debut was conspicuous: a lavish apartment and a greenhouse intended as spectacle. He sought influential associations and entry into Madame Schontz’s circle. After failing through established acquaintances, he turned to Couture, who recognized his potential and recommended him. Madame Schontz quickly assessed Fabien as suitable for her ambitions, flattered him, and led him to suppose he was the object of her affection. Eager to impress, Fabien presented himself in his finest attire, bewitched by her charm.A month before a significant meeting Madame Schontz disclosed her identity and aims; Fabien failed to grasp her intentions. Realizing his misunderstanding, she took him for a carriage ride, declaring she wanted to marry a man of ambition who could appreciate her character. Despite her wealth and encouragement to rise, Fabien hesitated at marriage; she then turned to Couture, who promptly proposed upon learning of her fortune.Meanwhile the Duchesse de Grandlieu sought Comte Maxime de Trailles’s help with a delicate family matter. Maxime, a seasoned political operator, agreed but demanded conditions, notably that the duchess accept his future wife into her circle. His plan involved removing a rival, requiring the duchess to follow his instructions. He acknowledged emotional entanglements — especially Calyste’s deep infatuation with Béatrix — and the difficulty of manipulating such feelings. His understanding of love and manipulation revealed the moral ambiguity of his scheme and the lengths people would go to secure desires at others’ expense.The next day Rochefide told Madame Schontz of Maxime’s impending visit. She readied her house and appearance: Maxime’s reputation as a powerful figure in Parisian society preceded him, and his presence promised both privilege and challenge as she navigated her ambitions and relationships.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Maxime de Trailles summoned Finot to arrange a breakfast at the Café Anglais with Couture and Lousteau to discuss Madame Schontz. Maxime lingered on Tortoni’s portico under the pretense of dealing with du Tillet while waiting for the Comte de la Palferine. When La Palferine arrived, Maxime questioned him about his debts; the Comte admitted his straits with wry good humor, and Maxime—admiring his spirit—offered to help him woo a woman, promising a loan of twenty thousand francs for the campaign.Maxime’s target was a well-bred marquise; he urged La Palferine to court her sincerely and they agreed to meet later at Madame Schontz’s. He also visited the duchess, who worried over her daughter Sabine’s distress at Calyste’s absence, and asked the duchess to keep his plans secret while providing the funds, assuring her the campaign would not be extravagant.Madame Schontz prepared a dinner, inviting notable guests including Claude Vignon and La Palferine; she dressed to impress and insisted she was an artist, not a courtesan, expressing a desire to make a respectable marriage. At dinner Maxime praised her success with Rochefide, noting she had cost him less than other conquests. He conferred privately with La Palferine, handing over the promised funds and detailing how to make a striking, unforgettable impression on the marquise. Maxime and Madame Schontz plotted to manipulate Rochefide’s affections, and Maxime vowed to secure a position for Couture in return for his cooperation.Meanwhile Calyste, feeling neglected, confronted Beatrix, who was torn between genuine feeling for him and an increasing attraction to La Palferine. Their relationship became strained as Beatrix wrestled with guilt and desire. Maxime intensified his scheming, arranging encounters between Beatrix and La Palferine that deepened her entanglement; Calyste’s desperate attempts to reclaim her failed, culminating in a public humiliation for Beatrix at the opera.Beatrix’s attachment to Calyste waned as she sank into La Palferine’s orbit. Maxime, observing the shifting loyalties and turmoil, counseled Calyste to reconsider his tactics. The narrative examines love, betrayal, and ambition—how manipulation and desire produce disillusionment and force characters to question past choices and future paths.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY ########### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################If the French dislike traveling, the English embrace it; both have reasons. Other countries may offer fine scenery and comfort, but France’s particular charms—wit, conversation, and an implicit social understanding—are hard to find elsewhere. Many Frenchmen therefore take pleasure in recognizing their homeland’s ways even abroad, a comfort reminiscent of Paris.In 1836, during the Sardinian Court’s stay in Genoa, two notable Parisians found themselves lodged in a palazzo, a remnant of Genoese nobility. Genoa’s evenings, especially after rain, have a serene atmosphere suited to friends and Italian fare. At a dinner given by the French Consul-General were Mademoiselle des Touches, known as Camille Maupin, the painter Léon de Lora, and the critic Claude Vignon.Camille had gone to Florence on business and brought Léon to see Italy. Their stop in Genoa was a courtesy to the Consul-General, who wished to honor her. Despite her fame she was wary of formal interactions with the ambassador and preferred to enjoy the evening unpressured. Conversation flowed, lively but light on literature, until the Consul-General decided to tell a personal story.The Consul-General, thirty-four, bore a striking resemblance to Lord Byron: a dreamy expression that attracted attention. He had married Onorina Pedrotti, the only child of a wealthy banker, after some reluctance; the union had been shaped by the ambassador’s influence and by the Consul’s own private struggles. Onorina, the graceful Genoese wife, lent an air of harmony to family life, yet Camille perceived a persistent melancholy in him.Debate at the table turned to women’s morality in cases of infidelity; the three women judged harshly while the men urged nuance. To change the mood, the Consul-General recounted a pivotal youth.At twenty-two, his uncle, the Abbe Loraux, secured him a post as secretary to Comte Octave, a prominent statesman. The uncle’s influence promised a bright future for the naive young man, sheltered from life’s complexities. Serving the Count, he observed a man outwardly composed and devout but inwardly sorrowful. Their relationship deepened: the Count treated him with a warmth that blurred master and mentor, revealing intellect, duty, and a private sadness beneath public success.The Count’s tenderness—his love of flowers, a fragile echo of his own faded beauty—contrasted with a life consumed by duty and a search for elusive happiness. Outward piety and industry masked an unquiet interior; this tension between public expectation and private despair shaped both the Count and the Consul’s understanding of human complexity.The Consul-General’s story, rich in feeling and detail, held his audience and left them pondering the hidden struggles beneath seemingly perfect lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“On a certain Monday,” he resumed, “M. le Président de Grandville and M. de Serizy (then Vice‑President of the Council of State) met at Comte Octave’s house. They formed a committee of three with me as secretary. The Count had secured my appointment as Auditor to the Council of State. All the necessary documents for their inquiry lay in the library; MM. de Grandville and de Serizy relied on the Count for the preliminary examination. To avoid carrying all the papers to M. de Serizy, they agreed to meet first in the Rue Payenne. The Tuileries valued this work, and it led to my appointment as Master of Appeals that year.“Though the Comtes de Grandville and de Serizy, who rarely dined out, were still discussing late into the evening, a servant interrupted: ‘MM. the Cures of Saint‑Paul and of the White Friars have been waiting in the drawing‑room for two hours.’“It was nine o’clock.“‘Well, gentlemen, you find yourselves compelled to dine with priests,’ said Comte Octave. ‘I do not know whether Grandville can overcome his horror of a priest’s gown—’“‘It depends on the priest.’“‘One of them is my uncle, and the other is the Abbe Gaudron,’ I said. ‘Do not be alarmed; the Abbe Fontanon is no longer second priest at Saint‑Paul.’“‘Well, let us dine,’ replied the President de Grandville. ‘A bigot frightens me, but there is no one so cheerful as a truly pious man.’“We entered the drawing‑room. The dinner was delightful. My uncle’s wit was refined and elegant, typical of those who conceal thoughts under a black robe. The Abbe Gaudron, as M. de Grandville observed, was more like a Saint Peter than a Saint Paul: a peasant full of faith, whose ignorance of worldly matters enlivened the talk with guileless amazement. They began discussing a social issue—adultery. My uncle pointed out the contradiction between civil and religious law that legislators had left after the Revolution.“‘In the eyes of the Church,’ he said, ‘adultery is a crime; in your tribunals, it is a misdemeanor. Adultery drives to the police court in a carriage instead of standing at the bar. Napoleon’s Council of State, touched with tenderness towards erring women, was quite inefficient. Should they not have harmonized civil and religious law and sent the guilty wife to a convent, as of old?’“‘To a convent!’ said M. de Serizy. ‘They must first have created convents; monasteries were being turned into barracks. Besides, think of what you say, M. l’Abbe—give to God what society would have none of?’“‘Oh!’ said Comte de Grandville, ‘you do not know France. They had to leave the husband free to take proceedings; well, there are not ten cases of adultery brought up in a year.’“‘M. l’Abbe preaches for his own saint,’ said Comte Octave; ‘in the East, woman was merely a luxury, regarded as a chattel; no virtues were demanded of her but obedience and beauty. By exalting the soul above the body, modern Europe—daughter of Christ—invented indissoluble marriage and made it a sacrament.’“‘Ah! the Church saw the difficulties,’ exclaimed M. de Grandville.“‘This institution has given rise to a new world,’ the Count continued with a smile. ‘But its practices will never match a climate where women marry at seven and are old at twenty‑five. The Catholic Church overlooked half the globe. Let us discuss Europe only.“‘Is woman our superior or inferior? That is the real question. If woman is inferior, placing her on such a high level as the Church does required severe punishments for adultery. Formerly, cloister or death summed up legislation. But practice has since modified the law. The throne served as a hotbed for adultery, and the growth of this inviting crime marks the decline of Catholic dogmas. Nowadays, where the Church demands only sincere repentance from the erring wife, society is satisfied with a brand instead of execution. The law still condemns the guilty, but it no longer terrifies. In short, there are two moral standards: that of the world and that of the Code. Where the Code is weak, the world is audacious and satirical. Few judges would not gladly have committed the faults they condemn. The world punishes a blunder after encouraging hypocrisy. The entire marriage law seems to require reconstruction. The French law would be perfect if it excluded daughters from inheriting.’“‘We three know the question thoroughly,’ said Comte de Grandville with a laugh. ‘I have a wife I cannot live with. Serizy has a wife who will not live with him. As for you, Octave, yours ran away from you. So we represent every case of the conjugal conscience, and if divorce is ever brought in again, we shall form the committee.’“Octave’s fork dropped, breaking his glass and plate. He turned pale and shot a thunderous glare at M. de Grandville, hinting at my presence.“‘Forgive me, my dear fellow. I did not see Maurice,’ the President continued. ‘Serizy and I, after witnessing your marriage, became your accomplices; I did not think I was committing an indiscretion in front of these venerable priests.’“M. de Serizy changed the subject, recounting his efforts to please his wife without success. The old man concluded that human sympathies and antipathies cannot be regulated; social law is most perfect when closest to natural law. Nature does not consider the affinities of souls; her aim is the propagation of the species. Thus, the Code, in its current form, wisely leaves latitude to chance. The incapacity of daughters to inherit while male heirs exist is an excellent provision, whether to prevent degeneration or to make households happier by abolishing scandalous unions.“‘But then,’ he exclaimed, lifting his hand in disgust, ‘how are we to perfect legislation in a country that insists on bringing together seven or eight hundred legislators! After all, if I am sacrificed,’ he added, ‘I have a child to succeed me.’“‘Setting aside the religious question,’ my uncle remarked, ‘Nature only owes us life; society owes us happiness. Are you a father?’ he asked.“‘And I—do I have any children?’ said Comte Octave in a hollow voice, making such an impression that the topic of wives and marriage was dropped.“When coffee was served, the two Counts and the two priests quietly left, noticing that poor Octave had fallen into a melancholy fit, oblivious to their departure. My patron sat in an armchair by the fire, crushed.“‘You now know the secret of my life,’ he said to me when we were alone. ‘After three years of marriage, I came home one evening to find a letter from the Countess announcing her flight. The letter was dignified; women often retain some virtues even when committing such a sin. The story now is that my wife went abroad on a ship that was wrecked; she is presumed dead. I have lived alone for seven years! Enough for tonight, Maurice. We will discuss my situation when I am ready to speak of it. When suffering from a chronic disease, it takes time to adjust to the idea of improvement. That improvement often seems merely another aspect of the complaint.’“I went to bed greatly agitated; the mystery seemed more obscure than ever. I anticipated a strange drama, realizing that the events leading the Countess to leave such a noble and loving man must have been extraordinary. M. de Grandville’s remark had illuminated the caverns I had been navigating, and though the light was dim, I could perceive their vastness. I could imagine the Count’s suffering without grasping its depths. His sallow face, parched temples, overwhelming studies, and moments of absentmindedness stood out vividly during the twilight of mental questioning before sleep.“Oh! how I loved my poor master! He seemed sublime. I read a poem of melancholy, seeing perpetual activity in the heart I had accused of being torpid. Must not supreme grief eventually lead to stagnation? Had this judge, with so much power, ever sought revenge? Was he feeding on her long agony? Is it not remarkable to harbor anger for ten years in Paris? What had Octave done since this great misfortune—when the separation of husband and wife has become a social question?“We allowed a few days to pass, for great sorrows have their own diffidence; but eventually, one evening, the Count said gravely:“‘Stay.’############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“This, as nearly as may be, is his story.My father had a ward, Honorine, rich and lovely, who was sixteen when I returned from college to live in our old house. Raised by my mother, she was awakening to life with an innocent, poetic view of religion and happiness, seeing the future as a perpetual fête. We lived like siblings until I asked her by the garden basin if she would marry me. We waited eighteen months at my mother’s insistence; when I was twenty-six and Honorine nineteen, we married. Out of respect for my parents we kept the old house unchanged; I introduced her to society more as duty than pleasure.I later learned how treacherous such marriages can be: a husband becomes teacher and moralist, and love may wither under authority. I trusted her innocence too much. I reproached myself like an anatomist seeking a disease’s cause. After my parents’ deaths, eighteen months later, Honorine left me—she wrote a farewell letter. The shock stunned me; for a month I was numb, then I resolved to live on memory. I loved Honorine and have never ceased to worship her; since her departure I have subsisted on the recollection of pleasures she perhaps never appreciated.Do not make a hero of me; I sought no diversion. I was too young or too in love and never met another like her. After struggling to forget, I almost fell into infidelity, but the memory of Honorine restrained me. My life became a relentless agony of fears, joy, and despair; I felt no anger toward her, only a compassionate, paternal sorrow. I realized I had idealized her and perhaps exhausted her with my uncritical passion. Too young to understand the unwritten laws of a woman’s heart, I blamed myself and absolved her. Love held me captive.Society mocks a husband who cannot keep his wife; I had to remain silent. Serizy is happy—able to see and protect his wife—while I have nothing but a love that starves. I loathe prostitution and remain faithful under a kind of spell. Without religion, I might have killed myself; instead I threw myself into work, feverish and sleepless.The interior drama of my life interests no one; one can only vaguely perceive another’s sorrow. I calm despair by contemplating a miniature of Honorine, kissing her brow, breathing her perfume. I suffer fits of collapse and wild hilarity, and terrors like a criminal meeting the police. The public believes I am absorbed in official duties—Council of State, Chamber, Courts, Politics—but seven hours of nightly work suffice for those. Honorine is my true occupation. To recover my wife, to guard her without her knowing, to satisfy small pleasures she allows, to be always near her like a sylph unseen—that is my life. For seven years I have never gone to bed without checking her night-lamp and the shadow on her curtains.Honorine left my house with only the dress she wore. Eighteen months later she had been deserted by her lover, who recoiled at poverty. She had a private income of sixty thousand francs a year. In November 1820, I secured the services of a skilled accoucheur disguised as a humble suburban apothecary and induced a parish priest to act as charity when she needed help. To hide her, save her from discovery, and find a devoted housekeeper who would be my confidante—this was a task worthy of Figaro. Locating where my wife had taken refuge was not difficult for me.After three months of despair, I devoted myself to Honorine with God as my witness. Love needs daily nourishment; should I not protect this child whose guilt sprang from my imprudence against further ruin? At seven months her infant died—mercifully for both of us. For nine months she lay between life and death, deserted when she most needed a man’s support; but my arm was extended over her head. Honorine was cared for as she would have been at home. When she recovered and asked who had assisted her, she was told it was the Sisters of Charity, the Maternity Society, and the parish priest.Honorine’s pride, almost a vice, has at times become obstinacy in misfortune. She determined to earn her living. For five years I lodged her in the Rue Saint-Maur, in a charming little house where she makes artificial flowers. She believes she sells her creations to a shop, earning twenty francs a day and living as if on fifteen thousand. She adores flowers, pays a gardener handsomely—an expense I secretly meet. I promised the gardener a market-garden with a house near the porter’s lodge in the Rue Saint-Maur, held in the name of a law-clerk; the smallest indiscretion would ruin him. Honorine has her little house, a garden, and a splendid hothouse for a rent of five hundred francs a year. She lives under the name of Madame Gobain, her housekeeper, an old woman of impeccable discretion who has won Honorine’s affection. For three years Honorine has been happy, believing she owes her comfort solely to her own toil.Yes, I have tried to reconcile with her. Once, believing there was a chance, I wrote her a letter—rewriting it twenty times. I will not describe my agonies. It was dark and foggy; I went to meet Madame Gobain. Honorine, recognizing my handwriting, threw the letter into the fire without reading it, declaring she would leave the next day. It felt like a dagger in my heart. I had just succeeded in deceiving my wife once—sending an Indian shawl presented as a gift from an actress, which I had wrapped myself for a night. My life may be summed up: I love, and I wait.Madame Gobain is my faithful spy. I visit her every evening for reports of Honorine’s day; a single exclamation might reveal the secrets of a soul that remains willfully deaf and mute where I am concerned. Honorine is pious—she attends church and prays—but she has never confessed or taken Communion, anticipating what a priest would say. She will not return to me; the horror I inspire overwhelms her, though I have never harmed her and have always been kind. Even if I was hasty in teaching her, is that a reason for her implacable determination? Her silence is absolute: she has never told Madame Gobain who she is; the housekeeper cannot advocate for me. The others know nothing; they live in awe of the Prefect of Police and respect the power of a Minister. Thus it is impossible for me to penetrate her heart. I have no means of action; any violence would ruin me forever.How can I argue against reasons I do not know? Should I write and have my letter copied and delivered, risking a third removal, which cost me fifty thousand francs previously? The last purchase of a house had been made in the name of my second secretary. The poor man, unaware of my vigilance, was caught opening a private box; I intimidated him into selling the house and dismissed him. If I did not feel my noblest faculties satisfied by this divine fatherhood—if I did not drink delight in every pore—I might think myself a monomaniac. Sometimes at night I hear the jingling bells of madness and dread the violent transitions from feeble hope to complete despair. Recently I thought of the tragic ends of Lovelace and Clarissa Harlowe; if Honorine were the mother of my child, she must return under her husband’s roof.I have such faith in a happy future that ten months ago I bought one of the finest houses in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré. If I win her back I will not let her see the room from which she fled; I will place my idol in a new temple where life will seem entirely new. That house is being transformed into a marvel of elegance and taste. I was inspired by an anecdote of a poet’s extravagant purchase for an actress; great passions share such impulses. I love like a poet—like an emperor!From the vehemence of my speech you might imagine a burning physical passion, but it is nothing compared to my worship for her soul, mind, and heart. By retrospection I now see the graces of Honorine’s mind and heart I overlooked in happiness. Day by day I appreciate my loss, discovering gifts in that capricious and proud young creature who has grown strong under poverty and the cowardly shock of desertion. That heavenly blossom is fading in solitude and hiding. The law we discussed cannot compel love—seizing my wife by force would be triumph over a corpse. Religion has little hold on her; she craves its poetry but does not heed its commands. I have exhausted mercy, kindness, and love; I am at my wit’s end. Only one chance remains: the cunning and patience of bird-catchers who finally trap the wariest birds.When M. de Grandville’s indiscretion revealed my secret life to you, I regarded it as fate’s decree. Have you affection for me to show romantic devotion? I replied with a question about the danger: my first secretary had tried to open my deed box; my second might fall in love with my wife. Can anyone put his hand in a brazier without burning it? The Count answered that he would send me well-gloved. It would not be his secretary who lodged in the Rue Saint-Maur but his distant cousin, Baron de l’Hostal, a lawyer high in office—an appearance of respectability.Shortly after, Madame de Courteville and her daughter came calling; the Count had family ties on his mother’s side. A young woman of twenty might be suitable as a mistress for a man like the Count, but he did not propose that match. Instead, three weeks later, I moved into the gardener’s cottage, repaired and furnished by Paris workmen and money. I was as much in love as the Count could desire for security. Would a young man of twenty-five play this part faithfully for his friend’s cause? I confess I relied on my uncle’s advice; the Count had authorized me to confide in him if needed. I engaged the garden, devoted myself to horticulture, and worked frantically—obsessing over varieties of flowers as part of my disguise. The Count dictated the tragi-comedy enacted in the Rue Saint-Maur. Each night, after Honorine went to bed, Octave, Madame Gobain, and I held council; Octave lived only for that hour.For two months I worked in the garden without ever seeing the little house where my fair neighbor lived; I had not even inquired if I had a neighbor. Her garden front was divided from mine by a paling, her cypress trees already four feet high. One morning Gobain warned me that a neighbor intended to build a wall between the gardens. My curiosity burned to see the Countess—my wish almost extinguished my budding affection for Amelie de Courteville, whom I had briefly admired earlier. Building a wall would close off fresh air from Honorine’s garden and turn her plot into a narrow alley. The cottage, once a summer villa, imitated a trellis with flowers up to the second floor—a charming, fragile place. My wall would cut away three-quarters of it.‘My good Gobain,’ the Countess asked, ‘what sort of man is this florist?’Gobain replied that he disliked women, was the nephew of a Paris cure, and perhaps a little cracked. A harmless lunatic is the only man no woman ever distrusts in matters of sentiment—so the Count had chosen a wise disguise for me.When Gobain informed the Countess that the florist wished to be left in peace, she replied she would speak to him herself. The next day as I walked the path I saw the curtain parted and a face peering out. Gobain announced me as the eccentric neighbor. I had dressed as a countryman—gray flannel trousers, wooden shoes, shabby coat, peaked cap, hands soiled with mold—to strengthen the part. The Countess did not shrink.It was early May and the air was pure; seeing Honorine, I understood Octave’s passion. Her pallor was of a particular white, with pink flushes at emotion. Sunlight through the acacia gave her a pale gold halo. Her brown eyes were tender and lively, framed by long lashes; a glance could enchant. Light-brown hair framed a high brow; her mouth was voluptuous; her head bore a stamp of nobility. Slight but not thin, she embodied mignonne—pliant, ready to be petted, yet proud and imposing. Her walk had a proud carriage, and even humble people made way. Gay and tender, haughty and commanding, she combined contradictions and remained childlike. She could be as implacable as an angel if hurt.The violet perfume she wore made sense of the Count’s adoration: she inspired devotion that demanded chivalrous sacrifice. Many women study their expressions; in her it flowed from a nature that went straight to the heart. If I sketch her it is because her soul and exquisite heart are central to this tale, and you would blame me had I not done so.I was about to forget my ridiculous role as half-crazy rustic when I asked her, with assumed gravity, if she loved flowers. She replied she made artificial flowers—after growing them she imitates them, as a mother paints her children—an occupation that proves she was poor. I pretended to be a gardener with an interest in blue dahlias and blue roses; I argued that blue is the color of superior souls. I proposed open railings to unite our gardens. She, who loved solitude and independence, allowed me the right to enter her garden anytime and urged I be treated as a neighbor with whom she would keep good terms. With a leap I cleared the paling.For a fortnight I seemed indifferent; by late May on a lovely evening we walked on opposite sides of the paling and exchanged civil words. I leaped the fence and joined her. She led me into the house—a charming sanctuary of eighteenth-century taste: frescoed dining-room, tasteful staircase, faded but elegant drawing-room, a bedroom, dressing-room, and workroom used as a library. The kitchen lay below. Everything harmonized with the woman I had come to love.Exquisite simplicity reigned. Her little house was like a box of 18th-century art; but occasionally a bitter smile hinted at deeper sorrow. In days we reached intimacy born of proximity and her conviction of my indifference to women. A look would have spoiled everything; I kept my feelings masked. Honorine saw me as an old friend and treated me with pity rather than coquetry. She soon allowed me into her pretty workshop, full of books and curiosities—a boudoir where elegance softened the vulgarity of the tools of her trade. She had poetized manufacture.Perhaps making artificial flowers allows a woman to remain herself: she can chat, laugh, sing and still work. Honorine had an artist’s sense in arranging petals and shades; white china saucers held colors; a cabinet with drawers held molds; a Japanese bowl held the paste; a Venetian glass held the living model she strove to imitate. She pursued difficult tasks—tiny corollas, wildflowers—moving like an accomplished pianist. Her fingers seemed fairy-like in their deft economy of action.I could not tire of admiring her; she displayed the genius of a painter in bold attempts, copying faded flowers and yellowed leaves to create poetic crowns suitable to women’s moods. She explained a fanciful botany of feelings—flowers for bacchantes, for bigots, pensive flowers for bored women. She employed me to stamp leaves, cut material, and prepare wires. My affected desire for occupation made me useful. We talked as we worked; when idle I read to her to keep my role. My gloomy, misanthropic persona amused and matched her antipathy to love; she was content with a companion on her desert island.I spared you the shame I felt as I feigned wounds in my heart to excite her compassion. Her renunciation of love, her dread of happiness, she proclaimed with vehemence. These happy days convinced me that a woman’s friendship can be superior to her love. She resisted any trap of sentiment; mulish obstinacy is common among women, and she was stubborn.One evening, playing the cynic, I argued that women, once they have inspired passion, hold men sacred forever, while women forget. She rebuked my generalization and denied being like those who give themselves to successive loves. She declared herself a nun, seventy-two years old in spirit, not a woman. Her disgust for life made her invulnerable. She often spoke of the angel Gabriel singing in her heart; if rich she would work still to prevent herself from soaring on “many-tinted wings.” She found some solace in her flowers but sometimes they failed to occupy her. She felt some great event impending; her life seemed about to change.After three months of this diplomatic struggle between my assumed melancholy and her proud indifference, I told the Count I could not coax her out of herself; the shell must be broken. The Count left me free to act.One Saturday in July, she gleefully showed me five gold pieces she had earned for her flowers and caps. It was late, a moonlit evening. The room smelled of perfumes; the Countess jingled the coins that Octave’s accomplices had provided as part of the ruse. She said she earned her living by amusing herself and felt transports of pride every Saturday. I suggested the image of a pretty child running among the flowers calling her “mother,” and her reaction was immediate and extreme: she suffered an attack—what she called “a cramp of the heart.” Madame Gobain tended her; I walked the path weeping and doubting whether I should abandon the bird-catcher’s role I had assumed. Gobain returned saying I cried like a child, and the Countess, roused by the danger of misinterpretation, wore a wrapper and came down to me.‘You are not the cause of this attack,’ she said, and when I asked about her troubles she declared her heart a poem laid before God. I threatened, lightly, that I could know all her secrets by tomorrow. She defiantly challenged me to be curious and told me to reveal what I discovered and the means by which I discovered it, for the preservation of the small happiness she so dearly bought depended on my discretion.‘I defy you,’ she said; she wanted me to find out who she really was. I warned that if I learned anything carelessly, others would be informed, and then—she would fly “on wings” to the New World. I interrupted that Paris is a place where those who must work can hide. She persisted: find out who I am. I promised to tell her what I might discover the next day and pleaded she not hate me. She replied coldly, almost imperiously, that only two words would suffice to destroy her peaceful fraud and she must be allowed to keep her carefully arranged retreat.
