After a few more words, she dismissed me abruptly.Thus the Count’s scheme had reached a crucial point: I was to be the Count’s instrument, the gardener-spy and companion, someone who could win Honorine’s confidence without revealing his identity. The house’s delicate balance—Honorine’s love of flowers, her pride, her vow of solitude, and the secret charity that sustained her—would be the stage for whether pity, cunning, or patience could redeem what force and authority could not.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“The battle is to-morrow, then,” I said with a smile, trying to seem indifferent. As I walked down the avenue the phrase kept ringing in my ears.Octave shared Honorine’s agitation. The Count and I walked together until two in the morning, pacing like generals before a decisive engagement, each aware victory depended on seizing a single moment. We lay awake in different moods—one hopeful, the other dreading reunion. For both Octave and Honorine, the real drama lay not in events but in feeling, in that spiritual world where lofty souls live.The next evening I arrived at ten and entered Honorine’s charming white-and-blue bedroom—the sanctuary of this wounded dove. The Countess looked poised to speak but fell silent at my respectful manner.“Madame la Comtesse,” I said with quiet gravity.She sank back, wrapped in grief—an attitude one might wish to paint.“You are,” I continued, “the wife of a noble and respected man, honored by the world and nobler still in his conduct toward you than public opinion knows. You and he are two lofty natures. Where do you suppose you are living?”“In my own house,” she answered, astonished.“In Count Octave’s,” I corrected. “You have been deceived. M. Lenormand, the usher, is only a front for your husband. The seclusion you enjoy, the money you earn, the protection around every detail—all come from him. Your disappearance has been plausibly explained; he even hopes you escaped the wreck of the Cécile. He hides you as carefully as you hide yourself. He obeys you through a magnanimity that has endured seven years.”The Countess trembled, defiant. Her eyes, dry and fierce, eventually yielded to tears—tears of helplessness, not tenderness. She had believed herself free; marriage weighed on her like a prison.“I will go!” she cried. “He forces me to it; I will go where no one will come after me.”“What—kill yourself?” I asked. “You must have powerful reasons to refuse going back to Comte Octave.”“Certainly I have!” she answered.“Then tell me; tell my uncle. You will find two devoted advisers. Though my uncle is a priest, he’s no pedant in a drawing-room. We will listen and try to find a solution. If you are the dupe of some misunderstanding we may clear it. Your soul is pure, and if you have done wrong, the fault is expiated. In me you have a sincere friend. If you wish to evade the Count’s tyranny I will find you the means; he shall never find you.”“Oh! there is always a convent!” she said.“Yes, but the Count, as a Minister of State, could secure your rejection from many convents. Yet I will save you from him—if you prove you cannot and ought not to return to him. Do not fear to fall from his power into mine,” I added, seeing her suspicion. “You shall have peace, solitude, and independence. I would never see you without your consent.”“And how? By what means?” she asked.“That is my secret. Prove to me this is the only life you can lead, preferable to that of Comtesse Octave—rich, admired, in one of the finest houses in Paris, beloved by her husband, a happy mother—and I will decide in your favor.”“But will there never be a man who understands me?”“No. That is why I appeal to religion to decide between us. The Cure of the White Friars is a saint of seventy-five. My uncle is no Grand Inquisitor but a gentle man.”“Nay, monsieur, the convent is my last hope. There is none but God who can understand me. No man, not even Saint Augustine, could enter the scruples of my conscience, which are to me like circles of Dante’s hell. Another man, however unworthy, has had all my love. For me there can never be two loves. It is complete when it comes; eighteen months were to me like eighteen years; I poured out my being. The cup of happiness is broken; nothing can refill it. I had but one heart. My husband had the girl; a worthless lover had the woman—there is nothing left! I will not be a prostitute. I could imagine surrendering to another man’s love, but to Octave’s?—No, never.”“Ah! you love him,” I said.“I esteem, respect, venerate him; he has never hurt me; he is kind, tender; but I can never love him. Let us speak no more. I will write my notions to you. I am suffocated. All I see now—the things I believed I earned by labor—remind me of what I wish to forget. I must flee as I fled from my home.”“Where will you go?” I asked. “Can a woman exist unprotected? At thirty, in all your beauty, rich in powers you don’t suspect, are you ready to live in the wilderness where I could hide you? Be at ease. The Count, who for nine years has not allowed himself to be seen here, will never go there without your permission. You have his devotion of nine years as a guarantee for your tranquillity. Discuss the future in confidence with my uncle and me. He wields moral weight equal to a minister. A priest with white hair, who has borne the hearts of kings in his hands, will understand you; before your flowers he will be as tender as they are.”I left Honorine at midnight; she seemed calm but depressed, harboring a secret purpose I could not divine. I found the Count a few paces away in the Rue Saint-Maur; drawn by irresistible attraction, he had left our agreed spot.“What a night my poor child will go through!” he exclaimed after I told him. “Suppose I were to go to her! What if she saw me suddenly?”“At this moment she might throw herself out of the window,” I answered. “She is one of those Lucretias who could not survive the violence, even into Octave’s arms.”“You are young,” he said; “you do not know that in a soul tossed by such choices the will is like a lake lashed by a tempest; the wind changes and the waves drive to one shore then the other. Tonight, the chances are as great she might rush into my arms as that she would throw herself out the window.”“And you would accept the equal chances?” I asked.“Well, come,” he said, “I have a dose of opium at home Desplein prepared to send me to sleep without risk!”Next day at noon Gobain brought me a letter informing me the Countess had gone to bed at six, exhausted, and after taking a chemist’s draught had fallen asleep.“This is her letter, of which I kept a copy—for you,” said the Consul, addressing Camille. “Literature cannot equal such language in real pathos; truth is its own terror. Here is the letter written by her anguish:”She wrote to me, Maurice, that she knew her conscience condemned her. Conscience is God’s interpreter to man. If she did not reconcile with Octave she would be damned; civil law condemned her to obey; if her husband did not reject her, the world would deem her pure. That was the sublime thing about marriage; society ratifies the husband’s forgiveness but forgets that forgiveness must be accepted. Legally, religiously, and socially she ought to return. It would be cruel to refuse him happiness, to deprive him of children, to blot his name. Her sufferings, repugnance, feelings—she admitted selfishness—ought to be sacrificed to family. She should be a mother; a child’s caresses would wipe many tears; she should be admired, wealthy, the queen of parties. God, the law, society were in accord.But she declared she would not live false to herself. She would become “white and innocent again” only in death. Not obstinacy, she said, but a nature that could not renew the passion once consummated. “A young bride is like a plucked flower; a guilty wife is a flower trodden underfoot,” she wrote. She could not love Octave. Every time her eyes met his she would see her sin reflected in them; his generosity would measure her crime; neither esteem nor mutual sacrifice could survive. She feared she would by coldness kill him or provoke his suspicion; she could not bear to be treated as a chattel, to once again kneel while he stood. She clung to the memory of a child conceived in rapture and nursed for seven months; other children would draw tears from her in their milk. “I have a child’s memory that returns at death. So you see, not one situation in that beautiful life to which the world and my husband wish to recall me but hides a snare or a precipice.” Religion offered solace but she lacked the energy to face that particular suffering. She had chosen between this hell, where God did not forbid her blessing Him, and the hell awaiting her under Count Octave’s roof.She closed by asking me to tell her where to find the solitude, peace, and silence I had promised.After copying the letter I went to the Rue Payenne. Anxiety had conquered the opium’s power. Octave paced his garden like a madman.“Answer that!” I said, giving him Honorine’s letter. “Try to reassure modesty with experience. It is harder than seducing ignorance.”“She is mine!” cried the Count, joy lighting his face as he read. He waved me away; ecstatic and tortured alike, he would act. I went on to receive Madame de Courteville and Amelie, who were dining with the Count. Seeing Mademoiselle de Courteville beside him, I felt that love has three aspects and that women who inspire perfect love are rare. Comparing Amelie with Honorine, the erring wife seemed more attractive: fidelity had not been a duty to her but inevitable; the sinner to be reinstated appeared sublime and demanded special generosity; whereas Amelie’s faithful steadiness promised peaceful motherhood, the poetry of the commonplace. Such comparisons are fatal and wrong at the threshold of marriage. Life teaches that marriage excludes passion and that family cannot be founded on tempests of love. Having dreamed of impossible passion and tasted its torment, I saw modest reality. Pity me: at twenty-five I did not trust myself; yet I took a manful resolution.I returned to the Count to announce his relations’ arrival and saw him grow young in the reflected light of hope.“What ails you, Maurice?” he asked, struck by my changed look.“Monsieur le Comte—”“No longer Octave? You, to whom I shall owe my life, my happiness—”“My dear Octave, if you succeed in bringing the Countess back, I have studied her well,” I said—(he looked at me as Othello might at Iago)—“she must never see me again; she must never know that Maurice was your secretary. Never mention my name to her, or all will be undone... You got me an appointment as Maître des Requêtes—well, get me instead some diplomatic post abroad, a consulship, and do not think of my marrying Amelie—Oh! do not be uneasy,” I added as he straightened, “I will play my part to the end.”“Poor boy!” he said, taking my hand and pressing it while holding back tears.“You gave me the gloves,” I laughed, “but I have not put them on; that is all.”We agreed on that evening’s part at Honorine’s, and I returned to her house. It was August; a hot, stormy day had left the air heavy with scent. The Countess sat beneath an arbor in a loose white muslin dress with blue bows, hair in loose waves, feet on a small stool. She did not rise but gestured me to sit.“Now, is not life deadlocked for me?” she asked.“Life as you have made it,” I said. “But not the life I propose; if you choose you may be very happy...”“How?” she asked, her whole person a question.“Your letter is in the Count’s hands.”She started like a frightened deer, paced away, then came to the drawing-room where I joined her after letting the shock settle.“You—a friend? Say rather a traitor! A spy sent by my husband.”Women’s instinct is as sharp as men’s perspicacity.“You wanted an answer to your letter, did you not? There was but one man in the world who could write it. You must read his reply; if after that you still find life a deadlock, the spy will prove a friend; I will place you in a convent impervious to the Count’s power. But first consider the other side. There is a law, divine and human, which even hatred obeys: do not condemn without hearing the defense. Till now you have judged like a child with ears stopped. The devotion of seven years has claims. You must read the answer your husband will send. I forwarded your letter through my uncle and asked him what he would reply if his wife wrote thus. You are not compromised. He will bring the Count’s answer. In the presence of that saint and me, out of respect for your dignity, you must read it, or you will be no better than a wilful child. Make this sacrifice to the world, to the law, and to God.”Seeing no attack on her resolve, she consented. Months of effort had led to this point. The Count had pinned all hopes on this instant, and he had reached it.In all my life I remember nothing more formidable than my uncle’s entrance into that little Pompadour drawing-room at ten. His fine head, silver hair set against black, and divinely calm face had a magical effect on the Countess; she seemed soothed by that virtue.“Monsieur the Cure of the White Friars,” said old Gobain.“Are you come, uncle, with a message of happiness and peace?” I asked.“Happiness and peace are always found in obedience to the Church,” replied my uncle, handing Honorine the following letter:“MY DEAR HONORINE,—“If you had but trusted me and read the letter I wrote five years ago, you would have spared five years of useless privations. In it I proposed an arrangement that would have relieved your fears and made domestic life possible. I have much to reproach myself with; in seven years of sorrow I have discovered my errors. I misunderstood marriage. I failed to scent the danger; an angel was in the house and the Lord punished me for my audacity.“You cannot lash yourself without striking me. Have mercy on me, my dear Honorine. I have so appreciated your susceptibilities that I would not bring you back to the Rue Payenne, where I could not bear to see you. I am decorating another house in the Faubourg Saint-Honoré, to which I would, in hope, conduct not a wife secured by ignorance and law, but a sister who will allow me to press on her brow such a kiss as a father. Will you bereave me of the right I have won from your despair—that of watching over you? Women have a mother’s heart; you never knew any mother but mine, who would have brought you back. But have you never guessed I had for you a mother’s heart, both of my mother and of your own? My affection is neither mean nor grasping; it will not let annoyances wrinkle your brow. What can you think of the companion of your childhood if you believe him capable of accepting trembling kisses and living between delight and anxiety? Do not fear passion; I would not want you back until sure of my strength to leave you free.“Your solitary pride exaggerates difficulties. You may, if you will, live under the life of a brother or father without suffering or joy; you will find neither mockery nor indifference. The warmth of your life will be equable and genial, without tempests. If later you desire pleasures or amusements, expand their circle at will. The tenderness of a mother knows neither contempt nor pity. In me, admiration shall hide every sentiment that might offend you.“Thus, living side by side, we may be magnanimous. In you, the kindness of a sister and the thoughtfulness of a friend will satisfy him who wishes to be your companion; and you may measure his tenderness by the care with which he will conceal it. Neither of us will be jealous of the past; we will look only forward.“You will be at home in the new house as in the Rue Saint-Maur: unapproachable, alone, occupied as you please, living by your own law; but with legitimate protection and the fortune for good works. When you long for absolution ask for it; it will not be forced by the Church or Law; it will wait on your pride. My wife might fear what you do; but not my friend and sister. To see you happy is enough for me; I have proved this for seven years. The guarantee is in the flowers you made, carefully preserved, watered by my tears. If this compact does not suit you, I begged the saintly man who takes charge of this letter not to speak in my behalf. I will not owe your return to ecclesiastical terrors or legal bidding. I will not take the quiet happiness I ask from anyone but you. If you persist in condemning me to lonely life, my will yields to yours. You shall be troubled no more than you have been. I will rid you of the meddling fellow who has annoyed you...”Honorine folded the letter and placed it at her bosom, looking at my uncle. “Thank you,” she said. “I will avail myself of Monsieur le Comte’s permission to remain here—”“Ah!” I exclaimed, involuntarily betraying my feeling.My cry made my uncle look at me uneasily and drew from the Countess a mischievous glance revealing her motive. Honorine had wanted to test whether I was an actor or a spy; I had the melancholy satisfaction of deceiving her—the cry I could not control came from the heart, which women read so well.“Ah, Maurice,” she said, “you know how to love.” The light in my eyes replied. The Count found me useful to the last.Honorine took out the letter again. My uncle signed to me, and I rose.“Let us leave the Countess,” he said.“You are going already, Maurice?” she asked without looking at me.She rose, still reading, followed us to the door. On the threshold she took my hand, pressed it affectionately, and said, “We shall meet again...”“No,” I replied, wringing her hand so that she cried out. “You love your husband. I leave tomorrow.”I rushed away, leaving my uncle, who explained my agitation to her as a touch of madness, a theory he really believed.Six days later I set out with an appointment as vice-consul in Spain, in a large commercial town where I could rise. After I had established myself there I received this letter from the Count:“MY DEAR MAURICE,—“If I were happy I should not write, but I have entered a new life of suffering. I have grown young again in desire, with a diplomatist’s prudence, moderating passion. When you left, I had not yet been admitted to the pavilion in the Rue Saint-Maur, though a letter promised permission—the mild melancholy of a woman who feared agitation. After waiting more than a month, I called and asked Gobain if I could be received. I sat near the lodge, head buried in my hands, for almost an hour.“‘“Madame had to dress,” said Gobain to hide her hesitation, flattering my hopes.“‘At last we were overcome by a trembling like a speaker’s nerves, speaking sacred phrases. “You see, Honorine,” I said, “the ice is broken; I am so tremulous with happiness that you must forgive incoherency.” “There is no crime in being in love with your wife,” she said with a forced smile.“‘“Do me the favor,” I said, “no longer work as you do. I have heard you have been living on savings; you have sixty thousand francs a year of your own; if you cannot give back your heart, at least do not abandon your fortune to me.”“‘“I have long known your kindness,” she answered.“‘“Though you prefer to remain here,” I said, “still do not toil.”“‘I gave her three certificates worth twelve thousand francs each; she took them, opened them languidly, and giving me only a look as reward understood I offered not money but freedom.“‘“I am conquered,” she said, holding out her hand, which I kissed. “Come and see me as often as you like.”’He had forced himself to receive me. Next day she seemed brighter; two months of habit brought out her true character. It was a delicious springtime of love that gave him ineffable bliss; she was no longer afraid; she studied him. When he proposed she go to England to resume her rank and life in their new residence she was seized with alarm.“‘“Why not live always as we are?” she said.“He submitted. He asked himself if she were making an experiment. On his way from his house to the Rue Payenne he said, as a young man, “This evening she will yield.” All his force was blown away by a smile and a command from those proud calm eyes. He felt how necessary her consent was and how impossible to wring it out. Could she guess the storms that distracted him?“At last he painted his case in a letter and begged her answer. She made none and seemed sad; he pretended not to have written. She read his heart and forgave him. Three days ago she received him in her blue-and-white room, bright with flowers. She was in a dress that made her bewitching; her hair framed her face. She was like a bride of long past days. His joy chilled; her face was terribly grave, fires beneath ice.“‘“Octave,” she said, “I will return as your wife when you wish. But this submission has dangers. I can be resigned—”“‘He made a movement.“‘“Yes,” she went on, “I understand: resignation offends you; you want what I cannot give—Love. Religion and pity led me to renounce solitude; you are here!” There was a pause.“‘“At first you asked no more. Now you demand your wife. Well, here I give you Honorine as she is without deceiving you as to what she will be. What shall I be? A mother? I hope it eagerly. Try to change me; you have my consent; but if I should die do not curse my memory or call obstinacy what I should call the worship of the Ideal.”“‘She sat calm as you used to admire and watched me turn pale. Her words took effect; she took my hands and said:“‘“Octave, I do love you, but not as you wish. I love your soul... Yet I love you enough to die in your service without regret. It will be my expiation.”“‘She knelt and added sadly: “And perhaps I shall not die!”“‘For two months I have struggled with myself. What shall I do? My heart is too full; I seek a friend and cry, “What shall I do?”’”I did not answer this letter. Two months later the newspapers announced the Comtesse Octave had returned on an English vessel, restored to her family after adventures contrived with plausible invention.When I moved to Genoa I received formal notice of the birth of a son to the Count and Countess. I held that letter two hours on the terrace bench. Two months later, urged by Octave, by M. de Grandville and M. de Serizy, and broken by my uncle’s death, I agreed to marry.Six months after the Revolution of July I received a letter concluding the story:“MONSIEUR MAURICE,—I am dying though I am a mother—perhaps because I am a mother. I have played my part as a wife well; I have deceived my husband. I have had happiness as genuine as the tears of actresses on stage. I am dying for society, for family, for marriage, as early Christians died for God! I do not know of what I am dying; I try to find out; I am not perverse; I was bent on explaining my malady to you—who brought the heavenly physician, your uncle, at whose word I surrendered. He was my director; I nursed him in his last illness; he showed me the way to heaven, bidding me persevere in duty.“‘I have done my duty. I do not blame those who forget; I admire them as strong natures; but I have the malady of memory! I have not been able twice to feel that love which identifies a woman with a man. To the last moment, as you know, I cried in the confessional and to my husband, “Have mercy!” But there was no mercy. I am dying with stupendous courage. No courtesan was ever gayer than I. My poor Octave is happy; I let his love feed on illusions. I throw all my powers into this masquerade; the actress is applauded and smothered in flowers; but the invisible rival—my dead child—comes every day to seek its prey. I am rent and I smile. I smile on two children, but the elder, the dead one, will triumph. I told you so before. The dead child calls me.“‘The intimacy of marriage without love degrades my soul every hour. I can never weep or dream but when alone. Social exigencies and care of my child leave me no moment to renew myself as I could in solitude. The constant need for watchfulness startles my heart. I have not the sharp vigilance of others; it is not the lip of one I love that drinks my tears; my burning eyes are cooled with water and not tender lips. My soul acts a part and perhaps that is why I am dying. The doctors, who discovered my secret, allow me to die of some plausible complaint. Octave believes I adore him, so I fear lest he should follow me. I now beg you to be my little Count’s guardian. You will find a codicil with my wish; do not produce it except if needed. Do not mourn for me; I have been dead this many a day if Saint Bernard was right that where there is no love there is no life.’”“And the Countess died,” said the Consul, putting away the letters and locking the pocket-book.“Is the Count still living?” asked the Ambassador; since July he had vanished from politics.“Do you remember, Monsieur de Lora,” asked the Consul-General, “seeing me go to the steamboat with—”“A white-haired man? an old man?” said the painter.“An old man of forty-five, going in search of health and amusement in Southern Italy. That man was my poor friend and patron, passing through Genoa to take leave of me and place his will in my hands. He appointed me his son’s guardian. I had no need to tell him of Honorine’s wishes.”“Does he suspect himself of murder?” asked Mademoiselle des Touches to the Baron de l’Hostal.“He suspects the truth,” replied the Consul, “and that is what is killing him. I stayed on board the steam packet till it departed; a small boat brought me back. We sat for some time taking leave—for ever, I fear. God only knows how much we love the confidant of our love when she who inspired it is no more.“‘That man,’ said Octave, ‘holds a charm and wears an aureole.’ The Count went to the prow and looked down at the Mediterranean. It was fine; moved, he spoke his last words: ‘Ought we not, in the interests of human nature, to inquire what irresistible power leads us to sacrifice an exquisite creature to the most fugitive of pleasures, and in spite of reason? In my conscience I heard cries. Honorine was not alone in her anguish. And yet I would have it!... I am consumed by remorse. In the Rue Payenne I was dying of joys not had; now I shall die in Italy of joys I have had... Wherein lay the discord between two equally noble natures, I dare assert?’”A profound silence reigned on the terrace.Then the Consul, turning to the two women, asked, “Was she virtuous?”Mademoiselle des Touches rose, took his arm, and said, “Are not men wrong too when they take a young girl to wife while cherishing at heart some angelic image, comparing us to unknown rivals and finding us wanting?”“Mademoiselle, you would be right if marriage were based on passion; and that was the mistake of those two, who will soon be no more. Marriage with mutual heart-deep love would be Paradise.”Mademoiselle des Touches turned and Claude Vignon whispered: “A bit of a coxcomb is M. de l’Hostal.”“No,” she replied; “he has not yet guessed that Honorine would have loved him.—Oh!” she cried, seeing the Consul’s wife approaching, “his wife was listening! Unhappy man!”At eleven by all clocks the guests went home along the seashore.“Still, that is not life,” said Mademoiselle des Touches. “That woman was rare, perhaps the most extraordinary exception in intellect—a pearl! Life is various, pain and pleasure alternating. Dante’s Paradise is found only in the soul; to ask it of life is a luxury against which nature protests. For such souls the six feet of a cell and the kneeling chair suffice.”“You are right,” said Leon de Lora; “but good-for-nothing as I am, I cannot help admiring a woman who could live by a studio, under a painter’s roof, never coming down, never seeing the world.”“Such a thing has been known—for a few months,” said Claude Vignon with irony.“Comtesse Honorine is not unique,” replied the Ambassador. “A man, a politician, a bitter writer, was the object of such a passion; the pistol that killed him hit him and made the woman live like a nun.”“Then there are yet some great souls in this age!” said Camille Maupin, and she stood for a few minutes pensively on the quay’s balustrade.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ####################################################################### MIDDLE OF THE STORY ############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################“HULLO! There is that old Box-coat again!” cried the lawyer’s clerk Simonnin, leaning from a window and tossing a breadcrumb at a stranger crossing Maître Derville’s courtyard.“Come, Simonnin, don’t play tricks on people, or I will turn you out of doors. However poor a client may be, he is still a man!” admonished the head clerk, bent over a bill of costs.Simonnin, a typical thirteen- or fourteen-year-old messenger, was known for impudence. He and the other clerks, including Godeschal, mocked the shabby visitor as “old Box-coat”; Godeschal suggested a prank, asserting that M. Derville received clients only at odd hours.The office buzzed with laughter. Cluttered and dim, it smelled of food and musty papers; the clerks accepted the disorder as part of their chaotic routine.When the old man entered, weary and out of place, he politely asked for M. Derville but was dismissed. Boucard, the head clerk, brusquely told him to return at one in the morning, leaving him bewildered.The clerks resumed their jeering, spinning extravagant guesses about the man’s past—nobleman or porter—to explain his shabby dress. When he insisted he was Colonel Chabert, reported dead at Eylau, they treated the claim as a joke and laughed.Amid the mockery they argued about evening theatre plans, their lively, youthful spirit cutting across monotonous work. At last Boucard broke the chatter, ordered them to finish their tasks, and refocused the room—its rowdy irreverence stark against the old man’s somber, unsettling presence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At about one in the morning Colonel Chabert knocked at the door of Maître Derville, a prominent Paris attorney. The porter said Derville had not returned, but the head clerk—busily arranging papers for the next day's cases—explained that Derville preferred to work at night and that the Colonel was only the third person to seek him so late.When Derville arrived he was struck by the man's appearance: thin, pale, and with scars on his head suggesting past trauma. Introducing himself, the stranger claimed to be the Colonel presumed killed at Eylau, prompting Derville and the clerk to suspect madness. The Colonel, however, insisted on telling his story.Derville, accustomed to varied clients, urged brevity. Chabert recounted his service at Eylau: he had been gravely wounded and buried among the corpses. Miraculously he survived because his horse’s body had shielded him; he described awakening in a grave, struggling free, and being rescued by a woman who took him to a hospital. After months of recovery he found he had lost everything—especially proof of his identity. Without documents, his attempts to reclaim his life met disbelief and ridicule. He wandered as a beggar, often treated as mad, and spent two years confined in a mental institution. Upon release he discovered his wife, the Countess Ferraud, had remarried and borne children.Derville, sensing the gravity of the claims, was skeptical but willing to help. Chabert said he sought to reclaim his rights and fortune, which he believed had been unjustly taken, and lamented that no lawyer had taken him seriously. Moved, Derville offered to obtain necessary documents from Germany, gave him a small sum to subsist on, and provided a letter to a notary for further assistance.Overwhelmed with gratitude, Chabert felt hope for the first time in years. He voiced a longing for vengeance against his wife and revealed his deep turmoil, declaring that if he failed he would rather die than live in obscurity. Derville acknowledged the case’s complexity but promised to treat it seriously. As he left, Chabert experienced a renewed sense of purpose—the encounter was a turning point that gave him a glimmer of hope.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About three months after the interview, in Derville’s room, the notary Crottat—tasked with advancing half-pay to Derville’s eccentric client—raised a serious point: he wanted the six hundred francs the old soldier had received refunded.“Are you amusing yourself with pensioning the old army?” Crottat laughed; he was a young man who had recently taken over the office after his chief fled in bankruptcy.“Thank you for reminding me of that affair,” Derville replied. “My philanthropy will not extend beyond twenty-five louis; I fear I have already been duped by my patriotism.”Noticing papers on his desk, Derville opened a letter stamped from various post offices; it was written in German. He ordered his head clerk Boucard to translate. The Berlin notary informed Derville that the requested documents would arrive shortly and were legally stamped. Witnesses to the recorded facts still lived in Eylau, Prussia, and the woman who saved Comte Chabert was alive in Heilsberg.“This looks like business,” Derville said after the translation. He then questioned Crottat about Chabert’s estate and the rogue Roguin.“Let’s call him the unfortunate Roguin,” Crottat interjected with a laugh.“Wasn’t it that ill-used man who absconded with eight hundred thousand francs of his clients’ money?” Derville continued. “I believe he handled Chabert’s estate.”“Yes,” Crottat confirmed. “I copied the papers when I was third clerk. Rose Chapotel, wife of Hyacinthe, called Chabert, Count of the Empire, married without settlement, so they held all property in common. The personalty was about six hundred thousand francs. Before his marriage, Colonel Chabert made a will leaving one-quarter to the hospitals and the State to take the other quarter. The will was contested, leading to a forced sale, and the widow received the portion bequeathed to the treasury.”“So Comte Chabert’s personal fortune was no more than three hundred thousand francs?” Derville asked.“Exactly!” Crottat replied. “You lawyers can be quite clear-headed despite accusations of false practices.”Derville went to find the Colonel. Chabert was lodging in the Faubourg Saint-Marceau with an old quartermaster named Vergniaud; Derville had to walk because his coachman refused the unpaved street. Stone gate-posts bore the sign “Vergniaud, dairyman.” The yard and house were dismal and on the point of collapse.The house was ill-built, windows mismatched, floor of trodden earth. Derville noted the squalor but also the Colonel’s calm demeanor when he emerged smoking a pipe, still showing military coolness.“Why did you not write to me?” Chabert asked, directing Derville to avoid the manure.Derville entered a room with only a chair and a bed of straw; damp walls, a grim atmosphere. Yet Chabert seemed content.“Here, we are not distinguished for luxury,” Chabert replied. “But I have done no one wrong, and I sleep in peace.”Derville hesitated about the money he had advanced, instead asking why Chabert had not moved to Paris to live better.“The good folks here have taken me in and fed me for a year,” Chabert explained. “I cannot leave them now that I have a little money.”Derville told him the papers from Heilsberg were expected and that the woman who saved him was alive. Chabert, frustrated at the thought of money he did not possess, threw down his pipe.Derville explained the legal complexities: proving Chabert’s identity would require lengthy, costly proceedings. He warned that the case could be prolonged by the bigamy issue with Comtesse Ferraud, who had remarried. Opponents would demand inquiries and formalities that might take years and drain resources.“You will face many challenges,” Derville cautioned. “Your opponents will demand inquiries that could take years.”Overwhelmed by the prospect of a drawn-out lawsuit, Chabert expressed despair. Derville reassured him the outcome could be favorable, proposing a friendly suit to annul both the death certificate and the subsequent marriage. He offered to send a power of attorney for Chabert to sign and to provide financial help.Chabert, feeling hopeless, agreed to trust Derville completely. As Derville left, he met Louis Vergniaud, who asked for a loan to improve the Colonel’s living conditions. Derville agreed but insisted Chabert should be the one to help his friends. He then planned to visit Comtesse Ferraud, assessing her position and the leverage he might gain.Monsieur le Comte Ferraud, a former Councillor’s son, had returned to France after the Revolution and married Madame Chabert, who had turned her share of her husband’s fortune into a substantial income. Their marriage was socially accepted; Ferraud was politically ambitious but had begun to regret his union.Derville, reflecting on the situation, recognized the Countess’s precarious position. He arrived at the Hotel Ferraud and found her surrounded by luxury—a stark contrast to Chabert’s squalor.“Good-morning, Monsieur Derville,” she greeted him, but he quickly shifted to serious matters.“I have come to speak with you on a very serious matter,” he stated, revealing that Colonel Chabert was alive.The Countess laughed dismissively until Derville’s serious tone silenced her. He warned her of the danger she faced if Chabert’s identity were proven, hinting at the legal ramifications. She insisted her second marriage was secure because of her children, but Derville countered that the law might not favor her if Chabert proved his identity and that Ferraud might not support her if his name and fortune were at risk.Derville’s words struck a nerve; the Countess began to consider the implications. He proposed a compromise, sensing her vulnerability. “Does he still love me?” she asked, contemplating how to use Chabert’s affection to her advantage.Derville left her with the suggestion to consider a compromise, confident he had gained the upper hand.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A week after Derville's visits, Colonel Chabert and his wife, Madame la Comtesse Ferraud, set out from opposite ends of Paris to meet at their lawyer's office. Thanks to Derville's support, Chabert arrived in a dignified blue suit with the Legion of Honor ribbon, regaining the soldierly bearing that had been lost and transforming from a shabby figure into one reflecting his past nobility.When Chabert entered Derville's office, Madame Ferraud arrived shortly after, elegantly dressed to appear youthful. The office remained the same; clerks made jesting remarks about Chabert's transformation. Aware of the couple's tension, Derville separated them to discuss terms. He presented a memorandum: Chabert would formally acknowledge his identity in exchange for the financial support his wife would provide. The Countess was startled by the obligation and reluctant to see the matter go to court.When Chabert returned he confronted her about their past, exposing the pain of their separation. She feigned surprise and tried to leave, but Derville urged her to stay. A heated exchange followed; despite Derville's warnings against rash action, Chabert pressed for justice. The Countess then led him to her carriage and took him to her country house in Groslay. During the ride she revealed emotional turmoil: she admitted affection for another man, Comte Ferraud, and wanted to preserve her dignity without public scandal. Chabert, though hurt, offered a measure of understanding, recognizing the social consequences she feared.At the country house she treated him kindly, attempting to erase past pain. They reminisced, and Chabert found temporary solace in her presence even as resentment and betrayal lingered. Meanwhile the Countess worked discreetly to secure her position and eliminate Chabert's claim. Her unease grew; she wrote to Delbecq to manipulate the situation further. When Chabert discovered this, he felt betrayed and resolved to confront her.Ultimately he decided to sacrifice his identity for the sake of the Countess and their children, accepting anonymity to avoid scandal. Her continued manipulations, however, revealed the depth of her betrayal; he expressed his disdain and chose to live without reclaiming his former name. Chabert then vanished from society. Months later Derville found him in a courtroom, reduced to a vagabond. Despite his circumstances, Chabert retained dignity and scorn for society's indifference. Derville sought to help, but Chabert, contemptuous of material concerns, rejected assistance.Chabert's story remains one of loss and resilience, illustrating the complexities of love, betrayal, and the struggle for identity in a world that often overlooks the individual.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In June 1840, Godeschal, an attorney, accompanied Derville to Ris. Near Bicêtre they saw an old pauper under an elm drying his handkerchief in the sun. In a shabby wrapper-coat he had a striking look: Godeschal compared him to grotesque German carvings, while Derville recognized him as Comte Chabert, husband of the pious Comtesse Ferraud, who had likely put him in the workhouse because he reminded her of their past.Curious, Godeschal urged Derville to tell Chabert’s story. Two days later they returned and found the old man drawing in the sand. When addressed, he insisted he was no longer Chabert but “Hyacinthe,” only a number in the institution; he even envied a condemned man for not being married. Godeschal gave him money for snuff; Chabert accepted eagerly, greeted them as “brave troopers,” waved his stick and cried “Vive Napoleon!” Another pauper noted Chabert’s mix of philosophy and childishness and recalled how he had impressed a Prussian officer with his military recollections.Derville reflected on Chabert’s tragic arc—from foundling to soldier under Napoleon to a workhouse inmate—and on the disillusionment faced by priest, doctor, and lawyer, each witnessing humanity’s darker side. He painted the lawyer’s view as particularly grim: betrayal, greed, moral decay, fathers abandoned by daughters, mothers exploiting children. Godeschal admitted he’d seen similar horrors and longed to escape Parisian corruption for a quiet country life with his wife.An addendum lists characters from Balzac’s Human Comedy and their appearances in other stories, noting figures like Napoleon and Comtesse Ferraud.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Bianchon, a physician known for theoretical physiology, first trained in surgery under the celebrated French surgeon Desplein. Desplein’s genius lay in diagnostic and operative skill, but it did not translate into a lasting school of followers. His atheism, reinforced by dissecting bodies without perceiving any soul, was central to his outlook.Despite his brilliance, Desplein’s life was full of contradictions used by critics to diminish him. Eccentricities—alternating between lavish and indifferent dress—and a temperament fluctuating from kindness to harshness produced mixed judgments. He understood human nature well but often displayed contempt for humanity after seeing its follies up close.Among his students, Horace Bianchon stood out for enduring poverty while studying medicine. Noted for integrity, loyalty, and cheerfulness amid hardship, he gained Desplein’s mentorship, which introduced him to practice and Parisian society. Once Bianchon told Desplein about a destitute water-carrier with a debilitating illness; moved, Desplein rushed to help, revealing a compassionate side beneath his stern exterior. This sympathy for the less fortunate, especially fellow Auvergnats, recurred in his actions.Bianchon later saw Desplein enter a church—odd given the surgeon’s avowed atheism. Curious, he followed and observed him attending mass. At a later dinner Desplein dismissed the mass as a farce, leaving Bianchon puzzled. When questioned months later, Desplein gave a matter-of-fact explanation, but Bianchon resolved to investigate. A year later he found Desplein at the same mass; the sacristan said Desplein had attended it regularly for twenty years and had even founded it.The discovery deepened the mystery of Desplein’s beliefs: a man who professed atheism yet engaged in religious ritual. The episodes reveal a multifaceted character shaped by skill, skepticism, compassion, and paradox.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Some time passed before Dr. Bianchon, despite their friendship, found an opening to discuss a pivotal episode in Desplein’s life. Their meetings rarely allowed private talk, but seven years later, after the Revolution of 1830, Bianchon saw Desplein entering Saint-Sulpice and knelt beside him. They attended mass together.“Can you explain your sudden piety?” Bianchon asked as they left. “You, who do not believe in God, at mass? You owe me an explanation.”“I am like many devout people—outwardly religious but inwardly skeptical,” Desplein replied, then criticized various political figures. Bianchon pressed: “That doesn't answer my question. Why did you found this mass?”Desplein answered simply: he was nearing the end of his life and could tell its beginnings. Standing in the Rue des Quatre-Vents, he pointed to a building where he had lived for two desperate years. He recalled hunger, poverty, isolation, and an irritable disposition that made friendships difficult. He had no money for books or medical training and struggled alone to rise from destitution.He spoke of meager meals and tireless work without sympathy or support. Those born to wealth could not imagine his plight; they asked why he had incurred debt as if it were a choice. Early hardships shaped his view of humanity: witnessing selfishness and envy, he came to expect mediocrity’s attempts to undermine those who strove for excellence.A turning point came when, at the depth of his poverty, he considered enlisting. He had hoped for a box of linen from a relative, but when the porter demanded too much to release it, despair set in. Then his neighbor, Bourgeat—a humble water-carrier—appeared and changed his fate.Bourgeat, though poor himself, paid Desplein’s debts and secured new lodgings. He devoted his savings to Desplein’s future, understood his mission, and supported him selflessly. Desplein described Bourgeat as a nurturing, almost maternal figure who took pride in his success yet sought no recognition. When Desplein became a house surgeon, leaving Bourgeat pained him; Bourgeat was proud nonetheless.Later, Desplein repaid him by buying him a horse and a barrel, fulfilling a long-held dream. Bourgeat was angry that Desplein had spent his money on him, yet delighted; he never claimed credit for Desplein’s rise, though Desplein felt he owed him everything.When Bourgeat fell ill, Desplein tended him devotedly, but Bourgeat died in his arms, leaving him all he had. He had strong faith, and Desplein honored his wishes by having masses said for him after his death. Desplein explained that the mass he founded was a tribute to Bourgeat’s faith; he hoped, if there is a paradise, that Bourgeat had found peace there. Desplein said he would give anything for the faith Bourgeat possessed.Bianchon, who had witnessed Desplein’s last days, could not say definitively whether the great surgeon died an atheist. He wondered whether Bourgeat might have opened the gates of Heaven for Desplein, just as he had opened the doors to the earthly temple they had shared.PARIS, January 1836.ADDENDUMThe following characters appear in other stories of the Human Comedy: Bianchon, Horace; Father Goriot; Cesar Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Bachelor's Establishment; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan's Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Muse of the Department; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty; The Country Parson.In addition, M. Bianchon narrated: Another Study of Woman and La Grande Bretèche.Desplein appears in: Cousin Pons; Lost Illusions; The Thirteen; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Bachelor's Establishment; The Seamy Side of History; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan's Life; Honorine.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Hashes mark the middle of the story, centered between hash lines.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################In 1828, at one in the morning, Dr. Horace Bianchon and Baron de Rastignac left a house in the Rue du Faubourg Saint-Honoré and walked to the boulevard because they had no carriage. Their conversation turned to the Marquise d’Espard. Bianchon distrusted her character; Rastignac, captivated by her beauty and rank, believed she could raise his social standing and help pay his debts.Bianchon warned that pursuing the Marquise would be a mistake, likening her to a “one-eyed horse” and arguing that cosmetics cannot conceal the truth of age and temperament. As a physician, he scorned fashionable women as often lacking genuine feeling and acting from selfish motives; the Marquise, he said, was a polished facade hiding an inauthentic nature. Rastignac dismissed these moral cautions, insisting a woman of fashion could be a powerful ally for an ambitious man, opening doors and providing influence.Still, Bianchon agreed to try to help by introducing Rastignac to his uncle, Jean-Jules Popinot, a judge who might intervene in the Marquise’s affairs—though Bianchon doubted Popinot’s willingness given the judge’s inflexible reputation.The next morning Bianchon set out for Popinot’s residence in a poor, dilapidated quarter of Paris, a building that hinted at former grandeur now decayed. Popinot himself was unkempt and unambitious; his career had stalled and he remained a supernumerary judge despite ability. His life was defined by a conscientious commitment to justice—kindness, a talent for eliciting confessions without force, and a blend of compassion and firmness—coupled with frustration at the system’s injustices. As Bianchon approached the door, he reflected on how hard it would be to persuade such an uncompromising man.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Twelve years before this narrative, in the famine of 1816, Popinot was named President of the Commission Extraordinary to distribute food to the poor in his neighborhood. A prominent lawyer and criminal judge, he had spent five years observing legal outcomes without grasping their causes; his encounters with the destitute fostered a deep compassion that gradually transformed him into a protector of the poor, likened to a modern Saint Vincent de Paul.The change was incremental: one charitable act led to another, and Popinot became a central figure in local benevolence. He worked to prevent crime, secure employment, and aid the vulnerable while keeping a largely private life unknown to most Parisians. The gratitude of those he helped was often unspoken—too burdened to voice thanks.By the end of his second year of charity, Popinot had converted a storeroom into a simple parlor for the needy, furnished with wooden benches and basic chairs. He kept meticulous records of donations and requests so he could allocate resources fairly; his aide Lavienne managed pawn tickets and visited areas hardest hit by famine. From dawn until late morning the parlor swelled with women, children, and the destitute. The crowd’s presence made heating unnecessary, and even criminals respected his household. Popinot devoted mornings to the poor, afternoons to legal practice, and evenings to his judicial duties.His judicial acumen was sharpened by close observation: he could discern hidden virtues in the impoverished and detect subtle signs of wrongdoing. His modest inherited fortune, supplemented by his late wife’s wealth, and his restrained judge’s salary required frugality, further stretched by his charitable outlays. Despite his contributions, he remained notable among judges of the Court of the Seine for not having the Legion of Honor.When the President of the Second Chamber commissioned Popinot to examine the Marquis d’Espard at the Marquise’s request for a Commission in Lunacy, his routine was interrupted. The Rue du Fouarre, alive with the needy early on and deserted by nine, was where his nephew Bianchon went to find him. Bianchon expected a contrast between Popinot’s rough domesticity and the Marquise d’Espard’s courtly expectations.He found the parlor a tableau of hardship: worn faces, anxious lines, and quiet resilience. Popinot, in a threadbare nightcap and dressing gown, was absorbed in his work; Lavienne kept the flow of petitions orderly. Bianchon brought an urgent matter, but Popinot, even while consulting, paused to help a woman who needed funds to sustain her grocery during her husband’s imprisonment for debt—providing aid and recording the transaction in his ledger.By midmorning Bianchon learned Popinot had attended to more than two hundred people that day, with others waiting. Sent off on an urgent medical errand, he returned to find the parlor emptied—the last of the needy had been helped.Popinot’s home reflected his divided life: dust, scattered papers, his late wife’s lingering belongings, and tokens of gratitude from those he aided. As they prepared for breakfast, Bianchon urged Popinot to dress appropriately for the Marquise’s meeting. Popinot hesitated, citing rules against magistrates accepting hospitality from parties in cases they oversee, but Bianchon argued it would be revealing—and kinder—to meet her at her home. Persuaded, Popinot agreed to visit, noting the appointment as he prepared to consider the Marquis d’Espard’s mental state and its implications for his family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next evening at nine, Bianchon climbed his uncle’s dusty staircase and found him bent over a complicated judgment. Because the tailor had not sent the coat Lavienne ordered, Popinot wore his old stained coat; Bianchon straightened his cravat and buttoned the coat, but the judge’s habitual movements soon revealed a gap between his waistcoat and trousers. Bianchon noted the absurdity just as his uncle entered the Marquise d’Espard’s room.A brief account of Madame d’Espard is needed to follow the interview. For seven years she had been a Parisian fashion-figure. After separating from her husband in 1815, the Marquise—about thirty-three and the mother of two boys, fifteen and thirteen—managed to remain in vogue, a feat in a society that prized youth. She kept a youthful appearance through elaborate hair, cold baths, and strict eating; emotionally she was detached, admitting neither love nor hatred. She parted from M. d’Espard with satisfaction when he took the children, whom she regarded as obstacles to her ambitions.With an income of twenty-six thousand francs a year, she initially lived more quietly after the separation, but by 1820 re-emerged into society, hosting gatherings and replacing other prominent women in Paris. She cultivated ties with powerful figures—duchesses, countesses—which increased her political clout. Her drawing-room became a hub for political discussion; she was discreet when it served her, adroit at navigating social complexities, and admired by ambitious young men. Yet she was also vain and indiscreet in ways that served her power: she treated relationships as means to influence rather than for genuine feeling and managed interactions to avoid personal compromise.Rastignac, a young man seeking alliance with her, saw her as a useful but cunning power. The Marquise’s household displayed wealth and status; when Popinot arrived he noted the opulence. She received him with feigned weakness; the Chevalier d’Espard stood silently observing.Popinot, outwardly simple but perceptive, quickly assessed the situation and questioned the Marquise about her husband’s mental state and their separation. She offered a crafted story: a change in the husband’s temperament had produced estrangement. Popinot’s skepticism led him to probe deeper—about the children’s welfare and the practicalities of her life. He observed inconsistencies: her lavish living clashed with claims of financial hardship, suggesting she might seek control over her husband’s affairs for self-serving reasons. As he pressed, the Marquise tried to deflect, but Popinot’s persistence forced her to face the implications; he warned that the court would scrutinize motives and transparency, and her carefully constructed façade risked collapse.Afterward Bianchon and Rastignac discussed Popinot’s character and the case’s potential for scandal; Bianchon worried about the Marquise’s influence. Popinot resolved to investigate further, planning to summon Madame Jeanrenaud, the woman linked to M. d’Espard.The next day Madame Jeanrenaud came to Popinot’s office. Stout and unrefined, she contrasted sharply with the Marquise. She denied wrongdoing and expressed simple concern for M. d’Espard’s well-being. Her honest, straightforward manner intrigued Popinot. As he began to piece together M. d’Espard’s financial dealings, he realized the situation was more complex than it first appeared, with implications for both husband and wife; unraveling the truth would require careful examination of all involved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################People who have matured know how trivial incidents can shape major events: Popinot’s minor illness delayed a crucial meeting. The judge, feeling unwell, stayed home instead of visiting the Marquis d’Espard, costing a day whose loss echoed larger historic delays.Before following the lawyer and his clerk to the Marquis’s residence, consider this father’s home life, which his wife’s petition branded as mad. In old Paris some buildings still show a taste for decoration and permanence. The house on Rue de la Montagne-Sainte-Genevieve where M. d’Espard lived was one such stone structure: once grand, now weathered and damaged by time and revolutions. Formerly housing dignitaries, it had become multi-tenant, its interior degraded by a printing establishment.M. d’Espard took the ground floor, likely for his children’s sake, since the garden was unusually large for the area. He secured the rooms at a reasonable rent given the building’s disrepair and the depressed market. The house, dating from the reigns of Henry III–Louis XIII, still bore traces of former glory, including a perron and the garden.The Marquis furnished his rooms in rich browns, creating a peaceful atmosphere with carefully chosen pieces and a harmonious scheme. Few visitors were admitted to the family’s private life, which remained largely secluded. The household comprised the Marquis, his two sons, and a small staff, including a long-serving cook and a man-servant, both contributing to the orderly quiet of the house.M. d’Espard avoided socializing with other tenants to shield his children from outside influences. That choice, however, bred gossip and suspicion: neighbors labeled him mad and his servants untrustworthy. Tenants believed the Marquis was financially ruined, a view reinforced by his extreme frugality and habit of paying cash, avoiding credit.The family was noble by birth, embodying ideals becoming rare in France and retaining a deep sense of dignity that clashed with egalitarian sentiments among neighbors. M. d’Espard, about fifty, had a distinguished, somewhat cold appearance—his slightly crooked nose and prominent forehead lending an eccentric air that fueled rumors. Though elegant in manner, he was frugal, wearing the same coat for years. His sons, Comte Clément de Négrepelisse and Vicomte Camille d’Espard, were handsome and graceful; the elder, nearly sixteen, favored more fashionable attire. The Marquis concentrated on giving them a rounded education—mathematics, Eastern languages, and history—eschewing what he considered unproductive studies.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Popinot planned to question M. d’Espard fell on a Thursday holiday. About nine, before their father rose, the two brothers were in the garden; the younger begged to visit the shooting-gallery and pressed Clément to support the request. The Viscount often took advantage of Clément’s weakness and delighted in wrestling with him. Their noisy quarrel roused their father, who watched amused from the window. Two neighbors remarked that the Marquis seemed mad to make his children fight. Noticing them, the Marquis called the boys; they leapt into his room and, after a moment’s hesitation, Clément obtained permission from Camille.Through the house people whispered about the Marquis’ supposed new madness. When Popinot arrived near noon with his clerk, the portress conducted him up to the third floor, remarking that M. d’Espard had made his children fight that morning and had laughed when the younger bit the elder until he bled; she added that the Marquis rarely showed himself. The landing’s muddy floor and grimy banisters lent some credibility to her gossip and made Popinot, though impartial, receptive to the tale.They entered an outer office where an old man, probably the office clerk, sat amid shelves of published numbers; a grating separated a private room, and a cash-desk showed the enterprise’s commercial aspect. A shopman opened the inner room to reveal the Marquis, a venerable, simply dressed old man wearing the Cross of Saint-Louis, seated at a desk. Popinot explained they wished to speak on private business. After formalities the Marquis asked their purpose. Popinot identified himself as a judge on the Inferior Court of Appeal for the Department of the Seine, charged with examining M. d’Espard regarding a petition for a Commission in Lunacy filed by the Marquise d’Espard. The old man withdrew; the clerk sat to take notes. The Marquis reddened and trembled, then steadied himself.“How is it,” he asked, “that I have had no notice of such a petition?” Popinot explained that notice is unnecessary because those deemed subject to such a commission are presumed to lack reason; the Court must verify the petitioner’s claims. The Marquis accepted this and asked what he must do. Popinot urged him to answer fully and confidentially, especially concerning delicate accusations Madame d’Espard might make. The Marquis feared that his explanations could blame his wife. Popinot promised discretion and the customary courtesy should the inquiry reveal a misunderstanding.Then Popinot confronted the major allegation: that the Marquis had dissipated his fortune for the benefit of Madame Jeanrenaud, widow of a bargemaster, or her son Colonel Jeanrenaud, for whom he allegedly procured an appointment and exhausted influence with the King. The Marquis flushed; tears came, but pride returned quickly. He said he was placed in a strange dilemma: revealing his motives would mean disclosing secret wounds and trusting the family’s honor to strangers. Popinot assured him of confidentiality. Freed from his usual reserve, the Marquis spoke with clarity and passion.After his marriage his wife had incurred heavy expenses that forced him to borrow. He could not afford a steward; most of his title-deeds had been taken to Paris by his father, who feared scrutiny. Their family name was Negrepelisse; the title d’Espard had been acquired through marriage in the time of Henri IV. An ancestor had lost the town of Negrepelisse during the religious wars and been ruined by property destruction. Though a later ancestor secured favor with Louis XIV and fortune, a stain of blood and disgrace remained, and the Marquis was determined to erase it.He discovered that his grandfather had obtained the lands of Negrepelisse by means tied to the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes: a Protestant merchant was arrested and hanged, and the land passed to his ancestor by iniquitous means. The merchant’s family had the rightful claim; the victim’s name was Jeanrenaud. The Marquis could not bear the disgrace and resolved to find the heirs. Eventually he located M. Jeanrenaud, reduced to poverty and serving as a lieutenant under Napoleon. Believing the heir’s rights incontestable and their lack of means to sue, he offered to pay them the lands’ value at the time they were taken—eleven hundred thousand francs—payable at his convenience and without interest.To effect restitution he lived on little for a long time. When he proposed to Madame d’Espard that they move to the country to live on half her income, she treated him as mad; her coldness convinced him she would have condoned his ancestors’ crimes. Terrified by her attitude, he resolved to leave her in control of her fortune after settling their debts, and to take charge of educating their children himself, determined to raise them as gentlemen. He invested prudently, repaid the debt sooner than expected, and had recently completed the payment of the eleven hundred thousand francs, making restitution without harming his children's prospects. These were the reasons for payments to Madame Jeanrenaud and her son.Popinot asked whether Madame d’Espard knew his motives for retiring from public life. “Yes, monsieur,” the Marquis replied. Popinot, visibly moved, excused his clerk. He then inquired about other allegations, such as that the Marquis was engaged in a business unbecoming his rank. The Marquis declined to discuss it there and summoned Comte de Nouvion, an old family friend, explaining that he used the rooms as offices for the publication of the Picturesque History of China by a reputable firm, not as his private residence.He showed Popinot his ground-floor apartment—a place of simple, touching harmony that struck the judge. The pleasant weather and garden convinced Popinot that a madman would hardly produce such beauty. The Marquis said he intended to remain until his younger son finished his studies, then travel with the children to European capitals to further their education and languages. He explained his China project: Comte de Nouvion, returned to France without fortune, was his partner; the Abbé Grozier, Keeper of the Books at the Arsenal, had been his tutor and taught him about China. Though enamored of China’s history, he remained a French gentleman. Financially, they had two thousand five hundred subscribers—twelve hundred in France—the book would cost about three hundred francs, and the Comte would earn six to seven thousand francs a year from it; the hundred thousand francs he hoped to make would cover his children’s education and pleasures. Twelve years of retirement to educate his children had cost him his memory at Court and political prospects, but he believed he had purified his family’s past and secured a glorious future for his children—a noble, if secret, task.At that moment horses clattered in the courtyard. The two boys entered, robust and sunburnt, dressed for riding. They greeted their father and the lawyer. Popinot needed no further evidence of the Marquis’ bond with his sons. The boys reported shooting success and that they had ridden in the Bois, where they had seen their mother. “Why didn’t you speak to her?” the Marquis asked. “I think I noticed, father, that she doesn’t want us to speak to her in public,” Clément whispered. A cloud passed over the Marquis’ brow; Popinot felt deep sympathy at the scene of fatherly devotion. “Justice may look in here at any time,” the Marquis said. “If anyone is mad, it can only be the children, who are a little crazy about their father, and the father who is very crazy about his children—but that kind of madness rings true.”Madame Jeanrenaud then entered, disregarding protests from the man-servant. “I take no roundabout ways!” she exclaimed. Bowing to those present, she announced she had come too late because the criminal judge was there. She declared that she and her son were of one mind to return everything, for their honor was at stake; they preferred to give back everything rather than cause trouble. “They must be as stupid as pans without handles to call you a lunatic,” she added. The boys clung to their father, alarmed.“Silence, madame,” Popinot interposed. The Marquis sent his children to the garden. Popinot addressed Madame Jeanrenaud: the payments made by the Marquis were legally due, though prompted by an expansive sense of honesty. If every possessor of confiscated goods were compelled to make restitution every century and a half, few legitimate owners would remain in France. The law allowed M. d’Espard to dispose of his income without accounting for it. A Commission in Lunacy is appropriate only when actions lack reason; here, the remittances were driven by the highest motives. She could keep the money without remorse and let the world misinterpret a noble action. Popinot declared he would rather see the Marquis crowned than subjected to a Commission. Madame Jeanrenaud applauded his defense, calling it judge-like and warmly praising his words.The Marquis extended his hand to Popinot, who pressed it gently; a private understanding passed between the humane judge and the noble, sublime man. “Monsieur le Marquis,” Popinot added, “from the first words of this inquiry, I regarded my clerk as unnecessary.” Leading M. d’Espard to the window, he said it was time to return home and that Madame la Marquise had likely acted under an influence the Marquis should counteract. Popinot left, the scene lingering with him; he even thought the rooms would suit him if the Marquis left them.The next morning, having written his report the night before, Popinot went to the Palais de Justice to pursue prompt justice. As he prepared to put on his gown, the usher said the President wished to see him privately. “Good morning, my dear Popinot,” the President greeted. “I’ve been waiting.” Popinot asked if something was wrong. The President called it a silly trifle: the Keeper of the Seals, with whom he had dined, had suggested it would be best if Popinot did not sit on the case. Despite Popinot’s protest that he had left Madame d’Espard’s house before tea, the President cited propriety—“Caesar’s wife must not be suspected”—and the need to avoid any appearance of conflict that might expose the Bench to journalistic malice.It was to be a matter of propriety, not discipline. Popinot was to signal his retirement from the case as a personal favor; in recompense, the President would secure the Cross of the Legion of Honor for him, long deserved. M. Camusot, a newly appointed judge from a provincial court, had been named in Popinot’s place. Popinot noted with ironic amusement Camusot’s pale ambition. He bowed and withdrew, choosing not to contest the implication.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ################################################################ MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ### MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers and DagnyPIERRE GRASSOUBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDedicationTo The Lieutenant-Colonel of Artillery, Periollas, As a Testimony of the Affectionate Esteem of the Author,De BalzacPIERRE GRASSOUHave you felt unease and sadness in the crowded galleries since the revolution of 1830? The true Salon has vanished, overwhelmed by a multitude of artists. Once the Salon displayed only the finest works, honoring a chosen few and provoking passionate public debate; criticism helped make names like Delacroix and Ingres. Now the excitement has faded: the public is numbed by sheer volume, exhibitions close quickly, and individual works lose impact. Before 1817 only a select number of paintings were shown; now galleries overflow, diluting quality and attention.The Salon’s essence is lost in this chaos. Instead of refined selection we face a tumultuous bazaar where great artists are overshadowed. Decamps’ notable works, for example, would have stood out more among fewer high-quality pieces than they do amid mediocrity. Ironically, despite more artists, talk of unrecognized genius has increased. When the Academy’s selection was strict, true talent was acknowledged; without that judgment, art suffers and the Salon risks perishing.Among the swelling catalogue of names stands Pierre Grassou, called “Fougeres” to acquaintances. In 1832 he lived in a cramped rue de Navarin apartment with a studio overlooking Montmartre. Modest and tidy, his studio reflected his careful nature and limited means. As a genre painter he avoided the burdens of historical subjects, concentrating on easel painting.One December morning Grassou, preparing to work, was visited by Elie Magus, a picture dealer. Magus bought Grassou’s paintings cheaply and proposed a business arrangement: Grassou would paint portraits of the bourgeois Vervelle family, eager to enter the art world. Their daughter had a substantial dowry, and Magus hinted that marriage could benefit Grassou financially.Grassou, living simply and solitary, was startled by talk of marriage. The idea of painting three family portraits, however, intrigued him as a chance to establish himself as a portraitist. Magus left to find the Vervelles, leaving Grassou to consider the opportunity’s potential.To understand Grassou’s situation we must look back. He studied under various masters—Servin and Schinner among them—but never fully grasped their techniques. His education was marked by a lack of recognition; he was often mocked for mediocrity. Gentle and diligent, he nevertheless failed to stand out among flashier peers.His first Salon attempt, a village-wedding scene in 1819, was rejected. Rather than despair, he sought Schinner’s critique, who bluntly advised him to give up painting. Still determined, Grassou refined the rejected piece and sold it to Magus for a meager sum. That sale began a long, difficult career characterized by perseverance against discouragement.As Grassou navigated his art life he remained committed to his craft, embodying the struggles and hopes of many contemporaries. His story reflects broader themes of ambition, recognition, and the harsh realities of the art world, where circumstance often overshadows talent.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For several days Pierre Fougeres lingered near Elie Magus's shop hoping his painting would attract notice, but it was ignored. After a week it disappeared; when Fougeres casually asked, Elie said he had sold it and would buy more if Fougeres produced Flemish interiors, anatomy lessons, and landscapes. Overjoyed, Fougeres felt validated and missed the ironic tone a more cynical artist might have noticed.He met his friend Joseph Bridau but, consumed with painting, could not enjoy the evening. Back home he sketched furiously, bought supplies, hired models, and devoted himself to his craft. After two months he produced four paintings. Schinner and Bridau dismissed them as derivative and suggested he try literature; undeterred, Fougeres retouched the canvases and sold them to Elie for twenty-five francs apiece, barely breaking even. He noticed a strange mist over his paintings in the shop but dismissed it.Over seven years he honed his skills into competent, though unremarkable, work. Elie sold the paintings, and Fougeres earned a modest living. At the 1829 Exhibition, with friends' help, he entered a powerful piece—a young man in prison inspired by Gerard Douw—which drew significant attention, even from Charles X. Madame purchased the painting and commissioned more.Fougeres’s success came largely from his talent for imitation rather than innovation. Despite criticism, his modesty and perseverance won him supporters and a reputation for dedication. With his earnings he furnished a studio and completed commissions punctually, a rarity among artists. By thirty-seven he had produced around two hundred paintings, achieving a style that appealed to the bourgeoisie. Friends often preferred his character to his art and regretted his career choice, yet he still offered sound advice and maintained a keen appreciation for beauty.After the July Revolution he continued submitting work to the Salon, lived frugally, and managed finances with his notary Cardot. He dreamed of a comfortable life as an artist, aspiring to join the Institute and gain recognition.One day the wealthy Vervelle family visited his studio for portraits. The father, once a bottle-dealer, admired Fougeres’ Legion of Honor cross. The family liked his work, and Pierre was charmed by their daughter Virginie. During sittings they grew familiar; the father boasted of their collection and stability, and Cardot praised Fougeres' character and prudence. The Vervelles began to see him as a suitable match, and Pierre's feelings for Virginie deepened, intertwining personal hopes with professional ambitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day Fougeres began painting Mademoiselle Virginie, he had effectively become part of the Vervelle family. They were attracted to his clean studio, which had become a second home. Near the end of the sitting Joseph Bridau burst in, disheveled; he scanned the room, approached Grassou, and, struggling with his coat, remarked that wood was dear and begged five hundred francs to meet creditors. Grassou agreed to write a note while Bridau criticized his style, urging him to accept nature’s imperfections.As Bridau left, Madame Vervelle feared he had spoiled Virginie’s portrait, but Grassou reassured her he had improved it. Intrigued by the artist’s genius, the Vervelles invited him to their country house the next Sunday to charm him with hospitality and their paintings.
