Flattered, Grassou went to Ville d’Avray, where they received him warmly: admired grounds, a lavish dinner, and praise treating him like a celebrated artist. They then showed their picture gallery, eager for his opinion on a collection they believed rivaled the king’s.Examining the paintings, Grassou was shocked to recognize many as his own works long ago sold to the dealer Elie Magus. Vervelle boasted the collection was worth three hundred thousand francs; Grassou, aware of the irony, whispered that he had sold those pictures for far less. Vervelle promised to double his daughter’s dowry if Grassou could prove his claim. The revelation boosted Grassou’s standing; when he later completed their portraits he gifted them, cementing their favor.Today Pierre Grassou is regarded as a competent portrait painter, earning around twenty thousand francs a year. He lives comfortably with his wife, who brought a six-thousand-franc dowry, paints bourgeois family portraits for five hundred francs each, and even received the Legion of Honor for conduct during unrest. Yet he harbors insecurities, convinced other artists mock him and critics ignore him. He still strives for Academy recognition and secretly replaces weaker works in the Ville d’Avray collection with those of celebrated painters. His mediocrity, softened by kindness and generosity, makes him a complex and sympathetic figure.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ######################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########### MIDDLE OF STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers; translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley. Dedication: To Maria.In certain provincial towns some houses give off a cloistered melancholy; silence seems to freeze life, broken only by a solitary figure at a window. Such an air hung over a house in Saumur, at the end of a steep, little-used street with pebble pavement and three-century-old buildings. These wooden houses, prized by antiquarians, display great carved oaken beams, slate-covered façades, worn window-sills with neglected flower-pots, and heavy studded doors bearing traces of history—Protestant marks or forgotten bourgeois insignia. Here the history of France appears recorded in wood and stone.Beside a dilapidated dwelling stands a country gentleman’s mansion; its stone arch faintly shows armorial bearings, worn by revolutions since 1789. The ground floors are not modern shops but plain dark rooms where goods lie exposed; a girl knitting will call her parents to serve customers. The local climate governs commerce: merchants, wine-growers, and coopers watch the weather anxiously, fearing frost or rain that could ruin them. On Saturdays vineyards call their owners away, streets fall silent, and neighbors observe one another’s doings.In this old town the most melancholy mansion belonged to Monsieur Grandet. To understand the house, one must know the man. Grandet began life as a master cooper in 1789 and married the daughter of a rich wood merchant. With his savings and his wife’s dowry he bought prime vineyard land during the sale of church property and rose in reputation as protector of local nobility and supplier to Republican armies. Through shrewd investments in vineyards and estates, and by 1806 serving as mayor, he steadily increased his fortune. That year he also inherited three fortunes, adding vast vineyards, farms, and an abbey to his holdings and making him one of the region’s wealthiest men.Townspeople speculated about the extent of his riches; he had a knack for timing sales to secure maximum profit, and his financial cunning made him both admired and feared. By 1816 his fortune was estimated at nearly four million francs, and many believed he kept as much again in cash. Former ridicule of him faded as his acts took on the weight of community law. Frugality governed his life; he spent only for necessities.Grandet’s manner was plain and unpretentious. He spoke little and stuttered in prolonged conversation—an appearance of awkwardness that concealed calculation in business. He never committed to anything without consulting his wife, whom he kept in a dependent, subservient state. Physically stout, with a round face and a crafty look, he dressed simply and practically, signaling thrift.Only a handful had access to his house, among them a young magistrate, C. de Bonfons, who hoped to win the hand of Grandet’s daughter, Eugénie. The town’s social life revolved around competition for Eugénie, chiefly between the powerful Cruchot family, who sought the marriage for their nephew, and the des Grassins, who wanted her for their son Adolphe. In early 1811 the Cruchots gained advantage: when the estate of Froidfond came up for sale they negotiated its purchase for Grandet, who paid in gold—an ostentatious display that confirmed his wealth and influence throughout Saumur.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################It is now clear what is meant by “the house of Monsieur Grandet,” a cold, silent dwelling above the town, sheltered by the ruined ramparts. The entrance, with two pillars and an arch of tufa—the soft white stone that lasts barely two centuries—bears irregular weathered holes that give the façade a jail-like look. Above the arch is a crumbling bas-relief of the four seasons, topped by a plinth where plants, even a small cherry tree, have taken root.The door is solid oak, brown and split, fastened with iron bolts. A small rusted grating in the middle panel lets curious visitors peer into a dark, slimy vault leading to a garden enclosed by thick damp walls, remnants of the ramparts that border neighboring plots.The principal ground-floor room is a large hall serving as antechamber, salon, office, boudoir, and dining room. It is where the barber cuts Monsieur Grandet’s hair and where farmers and officials conduct business. Entirely wooden, with gray panels and exposed beams, the room has two street-facing windows, a white stone chimney topped by an old brass clock and a greenish mirror, and antique chairs in faded tapestry. In each corner stand closets or buffets with dirty shelves. Between the windows sits an old card table topped with a barometer and two pastel portraits of Madame Grandet’s ancestors. Red gros de Tours curtains—a rare luxury—contradict Monsieur Grandet’s frugality. A straw chair by the window lets Madame Grandet watch passersby; a work table and Eugenie’s armchair occupy the rest.For fifteen years Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet’s lives have revolved around constant industry; fires are lit only on specified dates, and they rely on a foot-warmer for cold months. La Grande Nanon, the family servant, has tended the household thirty-five years, paid only sixty francs a year yet managing to invest in an annuity. Unattractive but welcomed into the family, she cooked, washed, and ran domestic affairs with tireless loyalty. Grandet’s rare acts of kindness—giving her an old watch, for example—were treated as monumental favors.In 1811, during a difficult grape harvest, Grandet’s treatment of Nanon blended dependence and control: he relied on her labor while keeping strict authority, and Nanon, grateful for employment and the meager kindnesses she received, remained devoted. Her gratitude and submission reveal a simple but complex bond shaped by power and dependence.Nanon’s domain contrasted sharply with the standards of other servants in Saumur. Her kitchen was a model of cleanliness and order; she worked late and slept in a small closet-like space, while the upper floors of the house remained bare and unadorned compared with the hall’s modest comforts.On a November evening in 1819, Nanon lit the fire for the first time that season, coinciding with Eugenie’s birthday. The family prepared for a gathering as local notables arrived to pay respects. Grandet maintained the tradition of giving Eugenie a gold coin; Madame Grandet presented a new dress. Grandet urged Eugenie to save for her future marriage, underscoring the household’s thrift and fixed habits.Guests, including the des Grassins family, soon arrived. Madame des Grassins, lively and pushy, and her son Adolphe presented gifts that provoked jealousy among other visitors. Compliments and attentions masked rivalry and calculation; beneath the laughter and light games, attendees subtly vied for a share of Grandet’s wealth. Grandet watched with pride and possessiveness over his daughter, manipulating relationships for his benefit. The evening blended familial warmth with cold calculations of social status—until a loud knock at the door signaled an unexpected interruption that would change the night.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Nanon took a candle and opened the door; Monsieur Grandet followed. His wife called anxiously, the players exchanged uneasy looks, and Monsieur des Grassins suggested they all go, sensing trouble. A young man arrived with a porter carrying trunks. Grandet brusquely told his wife to return to the game and stepped out to speak with the newcomer.The players sat but did not resume. Madame des Grassins asked if the traveler was from Saumur; her husband said he was from Paris. The notary checked the time, confirming the diligence was punctual. Abbé Cruchot inquired if the gentleman was young; des Grassins answered yes, with heavy luggage. Eugénie noticed Nanon’s absence; the president mused the newcomer might be a relative. Madame Grandet gently urged the group to continue, aware of her husband’s irritation. Adolphe hinted discreetly that the young man might be a cousin, but his mother silenced him.Grandet returned alone; the young traveler entered and received polite bows from the men and ceremonious curtsies from the women. Madame Grandet offered him warmth, but Grandet, absorbed in a letter, dismissed her concern. The stranger, Charles Grandet, thanked Eugénie for her hospitality but claimed he was not tired.Charles, adjusting his eyeglass, surveyed the room and its occupants, noting the loto game. At twenty-two, handsome and confident, he aimed to impress provincial relatives with Parisian polish. Sent by his father to stay with his uncle in Saumur, he intended to display fashionable dress and urban ease. He arrived with luxurious clothing and accessories—a hunting costume, several waistcoats, a gold toilet set—and despite his father’s instructions to travel modestly, he had taken a private coupé to flaunt his means.His elegance sharply contrasted with the provincial guests. The Cruchots, shabby and unkempt, were taken aback; locals reacted with both admiration and disdain at his nonchalance and fashion. Eugénie was captivated, longing for the world he represented; enchanted by his appearance and the delicate items he carried, she felt a strong desire to connect with him.The loto ended when Nanon returned to ask for sheets for Charles’s bed; Madame Grandet followed her. Guests, sensing the evening’s close, began to leave. Eugénie, seized by a new impulse, excused herself to help prepare Charles’s room, suggesting small refinements—a warming pan, a wax candle instead of tallow—using her birthday as an excuse to persuade Nanon.While Eugénie fussed, Madame des Grassins engaged Charles, subtly vying for his attention and hinting at local opportunities. Abbé Cruchot made sly remarks that exposed underlying tensions and ambitions. Grandet, who had read a letter from his brother revealing impending bankruptcy and pleading for his son’s protection, masked his emotions as he processed the implications. When the guests departed, Grandet offered his nephew a modest room, downplaying his wealth. The evening closed with speculation: Charles’s presence might unsettle Saumur. The Cruchots and des Grassins recognized a common rival in him and resolved to unite against any attachment between Eugénie and her cousin, scheming to manipulate the situation to their advantage.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When the four relatives were left alone, Monsieur Grandet told his nephew Charles it was too late to discuss the matters that had brought him and set the household schedule: breakfast at eight, a light lunch at noon, dinner at five. He professed poverty despite appearances, insisting he had earned his money by hard work. He told Nanon to bring candles for Charles; Charles at first said he needed nothing and bade them goodnight, but accepted a candle from Nanon so worn it resembled tallow.Grandet led him through the house, avoiding the cold stairwell. As they climbed, Charles’s expectations dwindled; the staircase was shabby, and he wondered why his father had sent him to such a place. On the landing he noticed the doors, especially one walled up that led to Grandet’s office — the secretive room where Grandet did his business and was said to hoard his wealth. Across from the office Eugenie, Grandet’s daughter, sat in her room. After a brief goodnight she and her mother retired. Grandet showed Charles to his attic room, warning him to call Nanon if he needed anything because the dog could be dangerous. Nanon returned with a warming pan, which Grandet dismissed with a grumble as he left.Charles surveyed the shabby decor and questioned his father’s intentions in sending him there; he felt out of place and uncertain of his purpose. Meanwhile Eugenie rose early and began her morning routine with a new sense of beauty and longing. Dressing, she admired her reflection but still felt inadequate beside her cousin Charles. The garden and courtyard that had once seemed dull now appeared full of charm; her heart swelled with unfamiliar emotions toward Charles and she longed to impress him.Eugenie hurried to Nanon, asking her to prepare cream for Charles’s breakfast. Nanon reminded her they needed Grandet’s permission for provisions. When Grandet entered, he and Nanon engaged in a rare quarrel over supplies, revealing his miserly nature. Eventually, reluctantly, he agreed to give some flour and butter for a galette.After breakfast Grandet took Eugenie for a walk to the fields, where they met Cruchot, the notary. Grandet spoke of his land and the recent sale of poplar trees, showing his calculating side. Cruchot mentioned gossip that Charles might be a suitor for Eugenie. Grandet reacted vehemently, saying he would rather throw his daughter into the Loire than marry her to her cousin. The declaration shocked Eugenie, dashing her hopes.Back at the house, Grandet received news of his brother’s suicide — a development that would have dire consequences for Charles. At breakfast the atmosphere was heavy with unspoken grief and tension. Eugenie, feeling deep empathy, began to cry; Grandet remained indifferent. Eugenie and her mother shared a quiet understanding: she wanted to do something kind for Charles and, despite risking Grandet’s wrath, she and Nanon conspired to prepare him a better breakfast. As they worked, her affection for Charles deepened and she felt an urgent need to show him kindness amid the family’s troubles.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After two hours' work Eugenie produced a simple breakfast that broke with the family's usual habit of a standing midday meal of bread, fruit, and wine. She set the table and placed an armchair for her cousin Charles, anxious about her father’s reaction; Madame Grandet promised to take responsibility if he returned.Charles arrived dressed with Parisian flair, cheerful despite a difficult journey. He said he seldom ate before midday but was glad for something to eat and joked about wanting a partridge. As they sat, his charm captivated Eugenie; she admired his elegance and felt a flutter of affection. Their conversation highlighted the contrast between his life of luxury and her modest upbringing. When Nanon brought eggs, Charles expressed delight and praised Eugenie, making her blush.He showed Eugenie his ring and their fingers briefly touched, deepening the spark between them. The mood shifted when Nanon brought coffee: Charles called the coffee outdated, and the women exchanged anxious glances, fearing Monsieur Grandet’s return.When Grandet entered he surveyed the scene with suspicion, noting the breakfast and especially the sugar, and asked where it came from. Charles, unaware of the household dynamics, asked for sugar for his coffee, but Grandet rebuffed him, suggesting more milk instead. Eugenie, quietly defiant, returned the sugar to the table.Grandet’s demeanor hardened as he spoke to Charles, then revealed devastating news: Charles’s father had died by suicide, leaving him bankrupt and without means. The announcement shattered Charles; he broke down in tears. Eugenie and her mother felt deep compassion, while Grandet remained detached and immediately focused on the financial consequences. He explained bankruptcy to Eugenie, stressing the dishonor it brought; she struggled to grasp the harsh realities and sympathized with her cousin’s plight.Grandet declared that Charles must go to the West Indies to seek fortune. Eugenie expressed a desire to help; Grandet reluctantly agreed to fund the journey, and for a moment there was a warm connection between father and daughter. The house, however, remained heavy with grief as Charles’s cries echoed.Eugenie and her mother found Charles mourning in his room. Moved by his pain, Eugenie urged him to preserve his honor, though he was overwhelmed with sorrow. As they left him to grieve, Eugenie recognized the depth of her feelings for her cousin and resolved to share his mourning—her life irrevocably changed by the entwined loss and love.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################About four o’clock a sharp knock at the door startled Madame Grandet.Grandet burst in rubbing his hands. “Wife,” he announced, “I’ve trapped them all! Our wine is sold! The Dutch and the Belgians have gone. I pretended to do nothing in the market-place, and the Belgian came to me in despair. The owners of the good vineyards are holding back their vintages, but I didn’t stop them. I made a deal for my vintage at two hundred francs a puncheon, half down. He paid me in gold; here are six louis for you. In three months, wines will have fallen.”His calm words, laced with sarcastic triumph, electrified Saumur, where news of his sale increased panic. Eugenie, excited, asked whether he had a thousand puncheons this year. “Yes, little one.” “Then that makes two hundred thousand pieces of twenty sous each?” “Yes, Mademoiselle Grandet.” “Then, father, you can easily help Charles.”At that, Grandet’s temper flared; his nephew, until now scarcely remembered, provoked sudden hostility. He scolded Eugenie for presuming to spend or advise him, asserted his duty to his nephew would be done his way, and threatened to send her to the Abbaye des Noyers with Nanon if she meddled. When Madame Grandet reported Charles had not come down, Grandet asked what he was doing. “He is weeping for his father,” Eugenie answered. Grandet’s face showed momentary bewilderment; then he retreated to his secret room to consider investing the proceeds in public funds. His thinning forest had brought six hundred thousand francs; with this sale his total reached nine hundred thousand. The lure of twenty percent tempted him; he calculated instead that he might get eight percent and in two years withdraw fifteen hundred thousand in gold.Nanon’s knock summoned him to dinner. He accepted a plan for eight percent, murmuring that hunger would drive his nephew from grief. At table he learned Charles refused to eat; he commented curtly, and Nanon agreed, “So much saved.” Madame Grandet suggested mourning. Grandet mocked the expense: “What will you invent next to spend money on? Mourning is in the heart, not in clothes,” and told her to buy her mourning out of her six louis, a hat-band being enough for him. Eugenie sewed in silence while he ran through calculations. The town buzzed with the sale; Nanon’s spinning was the only other sound in the house. “Nothing should be wasted,” Grandet repeated, lost in arithmetic and plans.That night Grandet, haunted by the present and dismissive of any future life, showed how money shaped his laws, politics, and morals. He bargained with conscience and religion, ordering Madame Grandet off to her prayers and finally growling, “The devil take your good God!” He visited Charles’s dark room to berate him gently in his own miserly fashion. “Well, nephew, you are in trouble. Yes, weep, that’s natural...I am a good uncle to you. Come, take courage! Will you have a little glass of wine?” He noticed a wax candle and scoffed at the theft of so small an item. Madame Grandet and Eugenie, listening from the landing, fled in fear.Alone afterward, Madame Grandet prayed for him and worried about the threatened punishment for Eugenie should she interfere. Eugenie, meanwhile, lay restless, her concern for Charles swelling into a personal disturbance. She rose in the early hours, found him asleep in an armchair exhausted by grief, and admired his face now smoothed by sorrow. He woke and apologized; she counseled him to go to bed and fled, ashamed and secretly happy. Her innocent boldness marked a turning point: she worried over what he would think of her visit, hoping he would believe she loved him.The next morning she and her mother waited anxiously for Grandet. When he arrived he seemed distracted but affectionate, kissing Eugenie and taking his seat at breakfast as if he had forgotten his harsh threats. “What has become of my nephew? The lad gives no trouble,” he remarked; Nanon answered that he was asleep. “So much the better; he won’t want a wax candle,” Grandet mocked. This unexpected softness surprised Madame Grandet, but it was only surface. Grandet, a poor sleeper, had spent the night calculating schemes to enlarge his fortune and dominate others, equating imposition with power.That day he contrived a way to restore his brother’s honor without disbursing a franc: to save Charles by manipulating public opinion and the Parisians, a sport of prudence for him. In his absence, Eugenie found a tender pleasure in caring for her cousin. She prepared his breakfast with loving, self-forgetting joy. She asked whether he would eat downstairs or in his room; when he expressed shame at being hungry, she resolved to bring breakfast to his room to avoid annoying her father.Her anticipation and care lit her day. She set the table, listened for his footsteps, and went to join him and her mother. The warmth and central attention they offered contrasted with the indifference Charles had known in Paris; he felt their kindness deeply. Eugenie’s innocence and genuine affection drew him closer; they shared an unspoken understanding. The household, unaware of this budding intimacy, continued its routines: Grandet, returning, found the women at their work. Later he invited the Cruchots to dinner, a further surprise.As the day unfolded the atmosphere shifted: behind Grandet’s calculations and the household’s routines, love and family obligations began to complicate the prosiness of wealth. Eugenie’s tenderness and Charles’s gratitude hinted at changes that money alone could not govern, and at the complex burdens that would shape their lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At this moment, Saumur was more excited about the dinner Grandet gave the Cruchots than it had been the night before at the sale of his vintage, an act the wine-growers had felt betrayed by. Grandet, who thought himself superior to those he deceived, paid little heed to their opinion.The des Grassins, learning of Guillaume Grandet's failure and death, came that evening to offer sympathy and to find out why Grandet had invited the Cruchots. At five o’clock Monsieur C. de Bonfons and his uncle, the notary, arrived in their Sunday best. The dinner was sombre: Grandet grave, Charles silent, Eugenie mute, Madame Grandet speaking little—a proper meal of condolence. Afterwards Charles asked to retire to write a letter.When the women left, Grandet displayed the cunning that earned him the nickname “the old dog.” He used a practiced stutter—an old tactical affectation—to control the conversation and frustrate his guests while steering it. He began haltingly about bankruptcy. The president of the commercial court explained that a respected man facing insolvency could have a receiver appointed, distinguishing liquidation from outright failure, and that sometimes liquidation could be managed without the courts: if the merchant did not file his schedule and no creditor appeared, the family or heirs could liquidate the business.The notary urged that this would save Grandet’s name. Grandet, mindful of the family reputation and his nephew’s future, weighed the advantage. He professed ignorance of Parisian tricks and emphasized his focus on vineyards and local business, but wanted to be sure he grasped the implications. The president explained that debts could be bought at a discount and, if the titles to all debts were acquired, the brother or heirs would owe nothing. Grandet asked how such negotiation worked; the president offered to go to Paris to deal with creditors and suggested Grandet could provide some assets to facilitate the process.Before Grandet committed, the des Grassins arrived, offering condolences. The notary declared he intended to liquidate his brother’s debts honorably; the banker applauded the sentiment. Grandet, cautious, said he would not bind himself beyond his means. Madame des Grassins suggested she might go to Paris, competing with the president’s offer. Favoring the banker, Grandet pulled him aside to discuss a separate investment in government bonds and asked him to say nothing of these plans to the Cruchots—discretion to avoid annoyance.Des Grassins agreed to leave the next day and meet again for final instructions. After they left, Grandet said he needed time to deliberate. As word spread of his supposed generosity, the town began to admire him again, forgiving the sale of the vintage; their enthusiasm reflected how quickly people could forget past grievances.Once the house was closed, Grandet ordered Nanon to keep silent about his plans. He was preparing a secret trip to sell gold in Angers while the market was favorable. He took precautions to avoid attention, ensuring no one would suspect his departure or its true purpose. In the early hours, as he and Nanon prepared to leave, Eugenie—worried about Charles—thought she heard a cry and considered checking on him but was distracted by seeing her father and Nanon carrying a heavy keg. Grandet told Nanon to tell his wife he had gone to the country and hurried off in the carriage, determined to reach Angers before morning.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################“My father has gone,” thought Eugenie, listening from the top of the stairs. Silence wrapped the house; the carriage’s rumble faded. A cry from her cousin’s room pierced the stillness. A thin vein of light through the door illuminated the old staircase.“He suffers!” she exclaimed, rushing up. Charles slept in an armchair, head drooping, pen fallen from his hand. His labored breathing alarmed her; she entered the room.“He must be very tired,” she thought, noticing sealed letters on the table addressed to tradesmen. “He’s settling his affairs to leave France at once.” Her gaze fell on two open letters; the words “My dear Annette” struck her like a blow. Her heart raced; she felt rooted to the spot.“His dear Annette! He loves! He is loved! No hope! What does he say to her?” Curiosity warred with shame. For the first time she felt an inner moral struggle. Passion won. She gently adjusted his head, kissed his hair, and steeled herself to read. The intensity of his feelings moved her; the joys of first love seemed more precious.My dear Annette,—Nothing could have separated us but the great misfortune that has now overwhelmed me. My father has killed himself; his fortune and mine are lost. I am orphaned at an age when I am still a child, yet I must rise as a man from this abyss. If I wish to leave France an honest man, I have not a hundred francs to try my fate in the Indies or America. I cannot remain in Paris, where the disdain shown to a ruined man would kill me. Your love cannot draw me back. Alas! I have no money to go to you for a last kiss to give me strength for my forlorn enterprise.Eugenie wiped her eyes and read on, thinking, “Poor Charles! I did well to read the letter. I have gold; I will give it to him.”I have never thought of the miseries of poverty. I have not even a sou for an outfit. If I have nothing, I shall go to Nantes and ship as a common sailor, starting anew like others who have begun without a sou and returned wealthy. I have faced my future coolly. It seems more horrible for me than for others because I have been so petted by my parents and blessed by your love. Such happiness could not last. Nevertheless, I feel more courage than a careless young man is supposed to feel. Dear Anna, if to keep me in Paris you were to sacrifice your luxury, we would still not have enough for my extravagant living. I would never accept such sacrifices. We must part now and forever—“He gives her up! Blessed Virgin! What happiness!” Eugenie quivered. Charles stirred; terror pricked her, but he did not wake, and she continued.When shall I return? I do not know. The climate of the West Indies ages a European, especially one who works hard. In ten years, your daughter will be eighteen; she will be your companion, your spy. Society will be cruel, and your daughter perhaps more so. Keep in your soul the memory of our happiness, and be faithful, if you can, to the memory of your poor friend. I cannot exact such faithfulness, for I must conform to my new life; I must think of marriage, which becomes a necessity. I have found a cousin here in Saumur whose face, manners, and heart would please you—Eugenie noted the abrupt end. She excused him: a girl might not perceive the coldness in his words. Young girls, raised in purity, see all as love when passion begins; they view their lover through their own feelings. Eugenie’s heart echoed “My dear Annette, my loved one” as the sweetest music.Charles, though mourning his father, was more struck by the loss of fortune than by genuine feeling. His indulgent upbringing had sheltered him from hard calculations; yet Parisian society had shaped him into a calculating youth who measured actions by self-interest. Annette had taught him worldly prudence; he had learned to think of his future more practically than romantically. He retained some inexperience’s beauty, but seeds of selfishness lay dormant, ready to grow.Most young girls succumb to tender promises; Eugenie, despite innocence, was enchanted by Charles’s demeanor, unaware of the coldness beneath. A chance encounter with his last heartfelt words stirred her. She laid the letter down, filled with love, and watched him sleep, vowing to love him always. Then she glanced at the other letter.My dear Alphonse,—When you receive this letter, I shall be without friends. While I doubt the world’s friendship, I have never doubted yours. Please settle my affairs and get as much as you can from my possessions. I intend to go to the Indies. I have written to those I owe money, and you will find a list of their names. I do not wish to keep anything except perhaps a few baubles for my journey. I will send you a power of attorney for the sales. Keep Briton for yourself; I would rather give him to you than see him sold. Farry, Breilmann, & Co. built me a comfortable carriage, which they have not yet delivered; persuade them to keep it without payment. If they refuse, do what you can to avoid dishonor in my present circumstances. I owe the British Islander six louis, which I lost at cards; don’t fail to pay him—“Dear cousin!” whispered Eugenie, running back to her room with a candle. A thrill of pleasure coursed through her as she opened an old oak cabinet and found a red velvet purse with gold tassels, a relic from her grandmother. She weighed it and counted her hidden treasures: twenty portugaises, five genovines, three gold quadruples, a hundred Dutch ducats, and a few rare rupees. About five thousand eight hundred francs—nearly six thousand—glittered at her feet.Filled with joy, she thought only of her cousin and ran upstairs without hesitation. The secret misery of Charles made her forget propriety. Standing at the threshold, candle and purse in hand, she found him awake, speechless with surprise. She placed the candle on the table.“My cousin, I must beg pardon for a wrong I have done you; but God will pardon me if you will help me wipe it out.”“What is it?” he asked, rubbing his eyes.“I have read those letters.”Charles blushed. “How did it happen?”“I do not know. I am tempted not to regret reading them; they have shown me your heart, your soul, and your plans.”“My dear cousin—”“Hush! Not so loud; we must not wake others. Here,” she said, opening her purse, “are the savings of a poor girl who wants nothing. Charles, accept them! This morning I was ignorant of the value of money; you have taught me. It is just a means. A cousin is almost a brother; you can surely borrow from your sister.”Her confidence met a stunned silence. “Oh! you will not refuse?” she implored, kneeling. “I will never rise until you have taken that gold! My cousin, I implore you, answer me! Let me know if you respect me, if you are generous—”As she spoke, tears fell from Charles onto her hands. Eugenie sprang to the purse and poured its contents onto the table. “Ah! yes, yes, you consent?” she cried, weeping with joy. “Fear nothing, my cousin; you will be rich. This gold will bring you happiness; someday you shall return it to me—are we not partners?”Charles finally spoke. “Yes, Eugenie; my soul would be small indeed if I did not accept. And yet—gift for gift, confidence for confidence.”“What do you mean?” she asked, alarmed.“Listen, dear cousin; I have here—” He pointed to a square leather-covered box. “This is something as precious to me as life itself. My mother would sell the gold she lavished on this dressing-case, but I cannot. Eugenie, you shall be its guardian. Never did friend commit anything more sacred to another. Let me show it to you.”He opened the box, revealing a finely crafted dressing-case and, in a hidden drawer, two portraits by Madame Mirbel, set with pearls. “This is my mother, and here is my father, your aunt and uncle. Eugenie, keep my treasure safely. If I die and your fortune is lost, this gold and these pearls will repay you. You alone are worthy to keep them. But destroy them at last, so they may pass into no other hands.”Eugenie was silent for a moment, then, with the same beseeching tone she had used, he urged, “Ah, yes, say yes! You consent?”She looked at him with love—her first look of womanhood. He took her hand and kissed it. “Angel of purity! Between us, money is nothing. Feeling, sentiment, must be all henceforth.”“You are like your mother—was her voice as soft as yours?”“Oh! much softer—”“Yes, for you,” she murmured, lowering her eyelids. “Come, Charles, go to bed; I wish it; you must be tired. Good-night.” She disengaged her hand gently; he lit the way to her room. At the threshold he sighed, “Ah! why am I ruined?”“What matter? My father is rich; I think so,” she replied.“Poor child!” said Charles. “If that were true, he would not have let my father die; he would not let you live this way.”“But he owns Froidfond.”“What is Froidfond worth?”“I don’t know; but he has Noyers.”“Nothing but a poor farm!”“He has vineyards and fields.”“Mere nothing,” Charles said disdainfully. “If your father had only twenty-four thousand francs a year, do you think you would live in this cold, barren room?” He glanced at the old cabinet. “Ah! there you will keep my treasures.”“Go and sleep,” she urged, preventing him from entering her disordered room.They exchanged good-nights with smiles and slept, sharing the same dream; from that moment Charles began to wear roses with his mourning.The next day, before breakfast, Madame Grandet found Eugenie in the garden with Charles. He was still sad. “My father will not be home until dinner,” Eugenie said, noting her mother’s anxious look. The connection between Eugenie and Charles showed in their shared thoughts and Eugenie’s sweet voice. Charles spent the morning in the hall; the three women respected his sadness and attended to their tasks. Tradesmen came to discuss neglected affairs; Grandet’s household required tending. Nanon prepared produce, and the day passed in the hum of work and worries.Around five, Grandet returned from Angers, having made fourteen thousand francs from an exchange on his gold. He had left Cornoiller at Angers to look after the horses, which were nearly foundered.“I am hungry,” he said upon entering.Nanon called, “Haven’t you eaten since yesterday?”“Nothing,” he replied.Nanon brought in soup as Des Grassins arrived to take Grandet’s orders. Grandet had not even noticed his nephew. “Go on eating, Grandet,” said the banker. “Do you know what gold is worth in Angers? They have come from Nantes after it? I shall send some of ours.”“Don’t send any,” Grandet replied. “They have enough. We are such old friends, I ought to save you from a loss of time.”“But gold is worth thirteen francs fifty centimes.”“Say was worth—”“Where have they got any?”“I went to Angers last night,” Grandet said quietly.Des Grassins was astonished; a whispered conversation began between them, often glancing at Charles. The banker seemed to learn of Grandet’s plans to invest a sum that would yield him a hundred thousand francs a year. “Monsieur Grandet,” Des Grassins said to Charles, “I am going to Paris; if you have any commissions—”“None, monsieur, I thank you,” Charles replied.“Thank him better than that, nephew. Monsieur is going to settle the affairs of the house of Guillaume Grandet.”“Is there any hope?” Charles asked eagerly.“What!” exclaimed his uncle with feigned pride. “Are you not my nephew? Your honor is ours. Is not your name Grandet?”Charles rose, kissed his uncle, turned pale, and left the room. Eugenie looked at her father with admiration.“Well, good-bye, Des Grassins; it is all in your hands. Decoy those people as best you can; lead them by the nose.”The two men shook hands, and Grandet returned to his armchair, asking Nanon for some black-currant ratafia. Too excited to sit still, he got up, looked at a portrait, and began to sing, doing what Nanon called his dancing steps.“Dans les gardes francaisesJ’avais un good papa.”Nanon, Madame Grandet, and Eugenie exchanged silent glances. Grandet’s joviality frightened them when it reached its peak. The evening passed quickly; Grandet chose to bed early, expecting everyone else to follow. Madame Grandet lived according to her husband’s will. During the two hours of digestion, Grandet, more jovial than ever, shared his peculiar sayings, which revealed his mindset: “You have no sooner put your lips to a glass than it is empty! Such is life. You can’t have and hold. Gold won’t circulate and stay in your purse. If it were not for that, life would be too fine.” He was cheerful and, in his own way, generous—offering Nanon some ratafia, which she gladly accepted.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning the family breakfasted together in an atmosphere of private intimacy. Grief had brought Madame Grandet, Eugenie, and Charles closer; even Nanon felt sympathy. The old man, absorbed in his stinginess and planning to send Charles away to avoid further expense, remained indifferent to the young people. He occupied himself with farm tasks, leaving Madame Grandet to watch over Eugenie and Charles as their relationship quietly deepened.For Eugenie, love grew out of their shared secret. Their bond took on the simplicity and tenderness of childhood affection: soft glances, gentle words, and small consolations. Eugenie delighted in comforting Charles, easing his pain with the sweetness of new love; Charles, hardened by worldly passions, found in her a pure, sincere attachment and valued the modest, uncomplicated life they shared.While Grandet worked in the fields after breakfast, Charles savored being with Eugenie and her mother, charmed by their humble virtues. He had once thought such morals belonged to Germany alone; now he saw them as real and alluring. Eugenie became his ideal; she surrendered to their love without foreseeing the looming separation.Three days after the des Grassins left, Grandet took Charles to the civil courts so he could renounce his claim to his father’s estate—a legal act that felt like a painful betrayal. Charles also signed powers of attorney, obtained a passport, and sold his belongings to prepare for departure. Grandet admired Charles’s new appearance in mourning and regarded him as a man ready to seek his fortune.When Charles gathered valuables to sell, Grandet offered to buy them, valuing them at nearly a thousand francs; Charles gratefully accepted. He gave his mother’s thimble to Madame Grandet, who was deeply moved. Grandet then insisted on paying for Charles’s passage to the Indies, further binding them by generosity that paradoxically cost him little.As Charles prepared to leave, Eugenie confessed her sorrow; he acknowledged his obligations and his coming-of-age. The family wore mourning for a Requiem Mass for Guillaume Grandet, and Charles received letters from Paris about his affairs, which he shared with Eugenie. In the garden he explained his affairs were in order, that his effects would be sent to Nantes, and he urged Eugenie not to tie her future to his uncertain fate. Still, their love held firm and they kissed passionately.On the day of departure Grandet took an unexpected personal interest, packing Charles’s belongings for Nantes. The family escorted him to the diligence; Grandet offered restrained encouragement and Charles expressed gratitude. After he left, the house felt empty. Eugenie, determined to preserve his memory, kept his room unchanged and conspired with Nanon. Her beauty shifted—becoming more radiant and dignified—as she followed his voyage on a map and confided her constant love only to Nanon, who, though herself resigned, deeply empathized with her mistress’s longing.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Two months passed, and the once-monotonous life of Eugénie and her mother became consumed by the secret that bound them: Charles lived constantly in their thoughts; Eugénie even studied her aunt’s portrait for his features. One Sunday Madame Grandet discovered the truth about the gold Eugénie had given Charles, and panic followed.“What will you say to your father on New Year’s Day when he asks to see your gold?” Madame Grandet cried. They spent a morning in dread, missed high Mass, and feared the coming confrontation. Madame Grandet, unprepared for winter and chilled by her husband’s impending wrath, fretted over their future and suggested consulting Monsieur des Grassins in Paris, but Eugénie insisted it was too late.On January 1, 1820, a cold morning, Madame asked for a fire in her room to avoid a confrontation. Grandet, in a surprisingly good mood, agreed and revealed he had received a large sum from investments. As he prepared breakfast he boasted about his financial success, unaware of their anxiety. Their polite New Year wishes only deepened the unease.After breakfast Grandet demanded to see Eugénie’s gold and ordered her to return it. Cornered, she said she no longer had it. His anger rose; he accused her of deceit and pressed to know what she had done with the money. Eugénie defended herself and asserted her independence. Grandet, furious, threatened confinement to her room on bread and water until she obeyed. Heartbroken, she fled to her mother, who pleaded for her, but Grandet remained obstinate, insisting on his authority. Eugénie resolved to stand firm in her love for Charles. Nanon, the servant, feared for her and vowed support.That evening Grandet dined alone for the first time in years; guests were surprised by Madame and Eugénie’s absence. Later Nanon secretly brought Eugénie a hare pâté, deepening their bond as they faced Grandet’s harshness together in quiet resistance.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For months the old wine-grower visited his ailing wife constantly while never mentioning their daughter Eugénie, who remained confined at home by his orders. Madame Grandet’s health worsened, and her husband grew harsher and more unyielding, even erring in business. Townspeople learned Eugénie was restricted to her room and given only bread and water, with Nanon secretly bringing extra food. The community condemned Grandet, and Eugénie’s gentle presence at Mass drew sympathy and curiosity.Eugénie’s principal grief was her mother’s decline; she often blamed herself. Madame Grandet tried to comfort and urged reconciliation, begging her husband to restore their daughter to the family, but he remained indifferent as his wife’s condition deteriorated.In spring Madame Grandet confided in the Cruchots, who were appalled by Eugénie’s treatment and discussed the legal consequences of her confinement. Eugénie overheard but, fearing scandal, begged them to remain silent and insisted on obeying her father to preserve the family’s honor.One day, as Grandet walked in the garden torn between stubbornness and a wish to embrace his daughter, the notary Cruchot visited and warned him that his wife’s failing health could have legal consequences: if she died, Eugénie’s inheritance might force the sale of property, jeopardizing the father’s control over his wealth. Pressed by this, Grandet began to reconsider his severity.Returning home determined to reconcile, he found Eugénie and her mother admiring a gold dressing-case given to her by Charles. Seeing the gold, Grandet became possessive; a confrontation followed in which Eugénie pleaded for the case’s return to its rightful owner and, in despair, threatened self-harm. Shocked, Grandet relented, allowed her to keep the dressing-case, scattered coins on the bed, and promised kindness. Despite this fleeting tenderness, Madame Grandet’s health failed, and she died peacefully in October 1822, exhorting Eugénie to seek happiness in heaven and expressing deep concern for her future in a selfish world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The day after her father’s death Eugenie felt a renewed attachment to her childhood home, where she had endured much suffering, including her mother’s death; familiar objects moved her to tears. Her father’s sudden tenderness—taking her to breakfast and watching her with an almost kind gaze—made the household suspect his mind was failing. But at the mourning dinner he revealed his plan.“Maitre” Cruchot explained Grandet did not want to divide property or pay taxes on supposed funds; to avoid this he needed Eugenie to renounce her rights so he could retain control. Confused, Eugenie asked for the deed to sign. Grandet, visibly moved, instead proposed she trust him and accept a monthly allowance of a hundred francs. She agreed; he clasped her hand and declared they were quits. The next morning she signed away her inheritance. A year later he had not paid the promised money; when she reminded him he awkwardly offered jewels.Over the next two years Grandet trained Eugenie in running the household and managing finances, instilling his frugality. By the third year she became mistress of the house, and their monotonous life continued for five years. Eugenie’s sadness was well known though she never spoke of it. Her only company was the Cruchots and their circle, who played whist with her father.In 1827 Grandet, now eighty-two, suffered a stroke. As he declined Eugenie devoted herself to his care, feeling increasingly isolated. His death was uneventful; to the end he remained fixated on hidden wealth. When the priest came for last rites, Grandet’s avarice surfaced in his final words, urging Eugenie to take care of his riches.After his death Eugenie was left alone with only Nanon, her loyal servant and confidante. Maitre Cruchot then informed her she had inherited seventeen million francs. Despite this fortune, her thoughts centered on finding her cousin Charles. In the empty house she and Nanon lamented their isolation while news of her wealth spread through the province.Eugenie’s first act was to secure an annuity for Nanon, who soon married Antoine Cornoiller and became Eugenie’s housekeeper. Cornoiller managed the estate; life for the farmers continued unchanged. At thirty Eugenie had never known joy; her childhood had been shadowed by her mother’s suffering and an unfulfilled love for Charles, who had left her with only memories. Her mourning delayed any thought of marriage. The Cruchots surrounded her with flattering attention; Monsieur de Bonfons sought to ingratiate himself. Madame des Grassins tried to promote a match with the Marquis de Froidfond, and Eugenie’s indifference was misread as interest, fueling speculation—all while Charles had not contacted her in seven years.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################While events unfolded in Saumur, Charles prospered in the Indies. His early ventures yielded six thousand dollars, and he quickly learned that human trafficking was the most profitable trade. He went to Africa, bought enslaved people, and combined that traffic with other lucrative goods. Ambition drove him: he aimed to return to Paris wealthy and regain social standing.Experience hardened Charles. He grew skeptical, lost a moral compass, and came to believe what one society condemned might be praised in another. He became ruthless—engaging in exploitative trades, customs fraud, and buying pirate-captured goods cheaply to resell. Though he once carried memories of Eugenie, successive escapades estranged him from his past; he came to view his family with contempt and regarded Eugenie as little more than a creditor.To conceal his identity while pursuing wealth, he used the alias “Shepherd.” His efforts paid off: by 1827 he returned to Bordeaux aboard the Marie Caroline with gold-dust worth nearly two million francs, planning further profit at the Paris mint.On the ship he met Monsieur d’Aubrion, a gentleman connected to the royal court, returning with his family. The d’Aubrions, formerly prominent, were now financially strained. Madame d’Aubrion, eager to secure her family’s future, sought a wealthy husband for her plain daughter. She saw Charles as a suitable son-in-law and promised to help him obtain a noble title and a comfortable income through marriage. The match would elevate both families and allow them to live comfortably in Paris.Intoxicated by ambition, Charles entertained the marriage to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion as a route to social prominence. He consulted Annette, who encouraged him, recognizing the advantage for her friend of an influential, affluent husband. Now more confident and assertive after his Indies career, Charles felt ready to embrace this new life.Des Grassins, aware of Charles’s return and impending marriage, approached him about settling his father’s debts. Newly wealthy, Charles dismissed Des Grassins’s concerns, asserting that his father’s financial failures were no longer his problem. He was preoccupied with his new identity as the Comte d’Aubrion and believed wealth would insulate him from his family’s past.In August, Eugenie sat on the bench where they had once pledged their love and received a trembling letter from Charles. It recounted his success and announced his engagement to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion—marriage as a pragmatic step to status. He declared love in marriage a delusion and closed the door on their youthful dreams. Devastated but resigned, Eugenie recalled her mother’s warning about suffering and resolved to live a life of prayer and acceptance.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eugenie returned from the garden cautiously, avoiding the corridor haunted by memories of her cousin. The day was pivotal: the parish priest, connected to the Cruchots and allied with Monsieur de Bonfons, had been urged to speak to her about marriage. Consumed by unhappiness, Eugenie confessed a desire to withdraw from the world and live only for God. The priest warned against such a drastic step, reminding her of duties to society and the poor.Madame des Grassins arrived, driven by vengeance and despair, to reveal that Charles Grandet had returned from the Indies and was engaged to Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. Eugenie masked her shock. Madame des Grassins produced a letter from her husband outlining Charles’s situation, including his father’s bankruptcy and the marriage’s consequences. Contempt and sorrow filled Eugenie; she returned the letter unread.Tormented, Eugenie asked the priest whether remaining a virgin after marriage would be a sin, exposing her inner conflict. She then secluded herself in her father’s secret room, skipped dinner, and avoided guests. Later, curiosity charged the evening when she unexpectedly asked Monsieur de Bonfons to stay—an act taken as a pledge of marriage.Privately, Eugenie told the president she could give only friendship and asked him to settle her uncle’s debts in exchange for her hand. The eager president accepted. After he left, Eugenie wept over the weight of her choice.The president hurried to Paris, met creditors, and arranged payment of all debts with interest. He carried a letter from Eugenie to Charles explaining her decision and its conditions. Charles, at first excited, was shocked when he learned of Eugenie’s fortune and the implications. Madame d’Aubrion, unaware of the president’s interventions, remained confident of the marriage.The president returned to Saumur and announced his marriage to Eugenie. Though wealthy, she maintained a simple life devoted to charity. Her husband entered politics but died soon after being elected deputy, cutting short his ambitions. Widowed, Eugenie lived piously, dedicating her fortune to the community and rejecting worldly desires. Rumors of a new marriage with the Marquis de Froidfond circulated, but she remained independent, supported by loyal servants Nanon and Cornoiller.Eugenie’s story reveals the tension between wealth and personal fulfillment, showing her struggle to meet social expectations while remaining true to her values and becoming revered in her community.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyPIERRETTEBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:Dear Child,—You, the joy of the household, whose pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of Wierzschovnia, how can I dedicate to _you_ a story full of melancholy? Yet should not sorrows be spoken of to a young girl like you, since the day may come when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them? It is difficult to find a subject worthy of your eyes, so I have no choice; perhaps you will feel fortunate when you read this story sent to you by your old friend, De Balzac.PIERRETTEI. THE LORRAINSOn an October morning in 1827 a sixteen-year-old boy in modest clothes stood in a small square of Lower Provins, noting mills at work and the simple bourgeois houses framed by linden trees. He recognized the house he sought: a recently restored white stone dwelling with gray blinds and yellow iron balconies, its neatness reflecting the pride of a retired petty shopkeeper.As the sun lit a calico curtain in a garret window, the boy began a Breton ditty for newlyweds, a song that stirred memories of Brittany and life’s bittersweet turns. A girl, Pierrette, signaled from the window; he revealed a yellow broom-flower. He was Brigaut, her childhood companion, and he spoke of settling near her. Pierrette, alarmed, urged him to flee when a woman below—a grotesque old maid, notorious for sharp observations and a tendency to read scandal into harmless scenes—appeared at a window.Overwhelmed by Brigaut’s visit, Pierrette reflected on her past. She sat in a shabby armchair, frail and thoughtful, recalling childhood joys now tinged by illness and uncertainty. Her mother, born a Demoiselle Auffray, had a tangled family history: a father who married twice and left a complicated inheritance. Her mother later married Major Lorrain, a Breton officer who died in battle, leaving the family poor. The Lorrains, Pierrette’s grandparents, endured hardships and could not care for her.Ultimately Pierrette was sent to live with her uncle and aunt, the Rogrons, in Provins. Unaware of all the intricacies of her lineage and the full consequences of her parents’ fortunes and losses, she found her fate increasingly shaped by these relatives. The Rogrons’ control sets the stage for the unfolding drama of her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Père Rogron, an innkeeper in Provins, was a short, fat man with a ruddy face and a taste for indulgence. He and his equally unattractive, selfish wife lived brutishly, eking out a modest living from their land and the sale of their inn. After selling it they moved into a dilapidated house inherited from old Auffray, Pierrette’s grandmother. Their two children, Sylvie and Jérôme-Denis, were neglected and ill-bred, their features coarsened and voices harsh. Rogron sent them to Paris to work in shops, subscribing to a doctrine of tough love and minimal support.Sylvie became an apprentice on rue Saint-Denis, eventually gaining independence and rising to forewoman; Jérôme followed a similar path and became a head-clerk. They met only occasionally, content with modest outings, and pooled their savings to buy a retail shop called the “Family Sister.” Though they met some success, fierce competition and their own severity made them notorious for harshness and lack of empathy.Sylvie, not conventionally attractive but shrewd, ran the business effectively; Jérôme lacked the intellect for broader trade concerns. Their lives revolved around the shop, leaving them oblivious to the wider world. Their stinginess and poor treatment of employees produced a high turnover of clerks. Sylvie cultivated a double persona: a pleasing saleswoman to customers and a sour spinster in private.While mourning their father and weighing their future, the siblings received a letter from the Lorrains about their cousin Pierrette’s inheritance. They considered selling the business and returning to Provins for a comfortable life but, seeing Pierrette as a potential burden, decided against taking her in to preserve their wealth for themselves and descendants. Sylvie meant to refuse in writing but was sidetracked by business; they left for Provins four years before Brigaut’s arrival—an absence and choices as consequential for Pierrette as their earlier Paris life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When a petty shopkeeper returns from Paris to his provincial town, he brings back a few ideas that soon fade as provincial life reasserts itself. The shift from shopkeeper to bourgeois feels like an illness: Paris’s vibrancy gives way to local stillness. With new wealth, the Rogrons, a brother and sister, indulge long-held desires—travel, politics, hunting—and remodel their old house to emulate Provins’s wealthier families.Their renovations signal these ambitions: the contractor consults them on every detail to match local elite taste. Their first year is consumed by talk of the house and efforts to enter the town’s social fabric, seeking acceptance among established families—the Julliards, Guepins, and Guenées—who dominate society.Madame Tiphaine, the town belle, maneuvers socially to elevate her husband to deputy, hosting gatherings that cement her influence. The Rogrons, by contrast, struggle. Sylvie studies card games to fit in; Rogron stays awkward and silent. Their attempts reveal a lack of refinement and social understanding.Despite lavish dinners intended to display wealth, invitations dry up and ridicule spreads. Sylvie’s faith that money guarantees acceptance collapses; she turns to frugality, abandons extravagant entertainments, and the siblings sink into ennui, nostalgic for their business days and resentful of their isolation. Community engagement yields little satisfaction, and bitterness toward the social elite grows.Hoping to regain standing, Sylvie invites their cousin Pierrette Lorrain to live with them, planning to use her as a bridge to the upper class. The scheme backfires when townspeople learn of Pierrette’s neglect in their household, deepening ostracism. Bitter and sidelined, the Rogrons begin aligning with the lower bourgeoisie, aiming to build a new social base.Colonel Gouraud and lawyer Vinet, both marginalized figures, spot opportunity in the Rogrons’ discontent and intend to exploit their wealth and social naiveté for political ends. Pierrette’s arrival catalyzes the Rogrons’ ambitions and frustrations, but rivalries and entrenched hierarchies show that wealth alone won’t secure acceptance or influence. Their experience illustrates the challenges of mobility in provincial life, where status and subtle codes often outweigh money.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After her grandparents were hospitalized, Pierrette, ashamed of living on charity, felt relieved when wealthy relatives were found. Brigaut, a childhood friend and son of her mother’s friend, offered sixty francs for her trip to Paris; she accepted without thanks, a sign of their close bond. Brigaut often visited to console her and ran after the coach in tears when she left. Arriving penniless in Paris, the conductor paid for her hotel and treated her like a package; four days later a kinder conductor took her to Mademoiselle Rogron’s, where her cousins received her with astonishment.She came with a small bundle of clothes, charmingly dressed in Breton attire. Sylvie and Rogron were initially taken aback and questioned her, but Pierrette, shy and overwhelmed, said little. Sylvie and the maid Adèle showed her a modest room that seemed luxurious to Pierrette, and Sylvie immediately fretted over her lack of clothing, resolving to buy a new wardrobe.In Brittany Pierrette had known affection and freedom; with the Rogrons she found the opposite. Warmth was replaced by coldness and criticism, leaving her confused and lost. Exploring the house and garden the next morning, she was soon reprimanded by Rogron for dirty shoes. Her cousins’ concern for possessions over her well‑being, and their constant fault‑finding, contrasted sharply with the love she’d known. Pierrette’s innocence made their disdain more painful, and she began to suppress her natural joy, becoming passive for fear of their scorn.The Rogrons fixated on her clothing, treating her as a burden rather than kin; their harshness stifled her spirit. When she accidentally tore a dress while playing, she was devastated, expecting stern rebukes. Life in Provins grew harder: Sylvie forbade visits to friends, extinguishing remaining pleasures. Their obsession with appearances and conditional affection confined Pierrette to a cycle of fear and repression, their care rooted in self‑interest rather than love.The arrival of Vinet and the colonel intensified this. They encouraged Sylvie’s social ambitions and opposition to the local elite, deepening Pierrette’s isolation. As her cousins pursued schemes and status, she was increasingly confined, transforming from a lively child into a shadow of her former self, struggling to endure her harsh new life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One evening, at the start of a new phase with her wealthy cousins, Pierrette went to say goodnight and Sylvie turned away coldly, making her cry. When asked, Pierrette said her rich cousin was not as kind as her poor grandmother. Sylvie dismissed this, noting the grandmother had taken money while the cousin would leave her some; Pierrette wanted affection, not wealth.Sylvie then accused Pierrette of mischief—breaking a watch and ignoring orders—and Pierrette left ashamed. Rogron wanted punishment, Madame Vinet suggested sending her to school, and the colonel thought teaching might keep her silent. Vinet proposed hiring a schoolmaster, calling Pierrette a rough diamond needing polishing.A writing master was appointed and his lessons brought chaos: ink stains, scattered materials, and blame placed on Pierrette. Harsh words followed—she must earn her keep. Rogron’s severity and Sylvie’s indifference intensified Pierrette’s isolation. Her curiosity and spirit were stifled; she began to feel ill, complaining of pains her cousins dismissed.Only Adèle, the servant, showed kindness, secretly warming Pierrette’s bed. Sylvie believed in hardening children; Pierrette’s innocent affection was seen as manipulation. The colonel and Vinet supported the Rogrons’ harshness, attributing Pierrette’s behavior to a stubborn Breton nature. Vinet and Rogron invested in a local newspaper, boosting their social standing while Pierrette’s situation worsened.She thought of escaping to her grandparents in Brittany, but two events prevented it: a death and Rogron’s appointment as her guardian. Vinet hinted at Pierrette’s possible inheritance; Rogron secured from her grandmother a promise of eight thousand francs upon the old woman's death. These developments weighed on Pierrette, especially as preparations for her first communion changed household dynamics.Sylvie insisted Pierrette be instructed by the zealous priest Monsieur Habert, who profoundly influenced her. Pierrette found solace in faith and abandoned escape plans, embracing the Church’s teachings. Sylvie, noticing the change, grew curious about the priest’s methods and adopted a new piety herself.Monsieur Habert’s sister, Mademoiselle Habert, befriended Sylvie and offered to take Pierrette into her school, but the Rogrons refused. The colonel and Vinet feared the priest might press for a convent education.As political and matrimonial intrigues unfolded, Vinet introduced the socially useful but impoverished Madame and Mademoiselle de Chargeboeuf. Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, beautiful and charming, drew Rogron’s attention; Sylvie began to see Pierrette as an obstacle to her own prospects. She made Pierrette learn domestic skills and effectively turned her into a servant. Losing Adèle’s protection, Pierrette accepted the role, treated with less regard than the house staff, scolded for trifles, and given no praise. Over the years her health deteriorated—severe internal pains, stunted growth—and the once-vibrant child became a shadow, enduring in silence while her cousins remained oblivious.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Before recounting the domestic drama Jacques Brigaut's arrival would incite in the Rogron family, it is necessary to explain how he came to Provins, since he plays a significant yet passive role in the events.Brigaut fled the Rogron house, shaken by Pierrette’s distressing change. He scarcely recognized her; her once vibrant spirit had given way to illness and sorrow. From a high point in the Upper town he looked mournfully at Pierrette’s home, consumed by questions about her well-being and the depth of his feelings. Their bond, rare between children of opposite sexes, was profound. Brigaut had resolved to improve his prospects for her, learning a trade and moving to Paris with dreams of success to support her. But after a fortnight in the city he returned to Provins, quickly finding work as a journeyman cabinetmaker so he could stay near Pierrette.He buried his feelings while trying to learn more about the Rogrons from Madame Frappier, who disclosed their dubious acquisition of Pierrette’s grandfather’s property. He often saw Pierrette at the market, where her frailty distressed him. Sundays at church brought them close, though she signaled him to remain hidden, sensing danger. Determined to discover the cause of her pallor and weakness, he feared for her health.Pierrette, meanwhile, fretted about the changes since Brigaut’s arrival but was unaware of the internal strife among the Rogrons, particularly between Sylvie and Mademoiselle Habert (Céleste), both angling for marriage. Sylvie, forty-two, felt her age as a threat and dreaded dying alone, while Céleste, ten years younger and encouraged by her brother the priest, actively sought a husband. A recent political shift emboldened local Liberals and intensified competition for social standing in Provins.Sylvie’s anxiety led her to consult a doctor about the risks of marrying at her age; his discouraging prognosis heightened her fear. Céleste, seeing an opening, urged Sylvie to delay, hoping to remove a rival. Sylvie’s unease deepened into jealousy when she noticed the colonel’s increasing attention to Pierrette. Vinet, a lawyer with designs on Bathilde, saw Sylvie’s vulnerability as an opportunity: he plotted to separate the colonel from Pierrette and steer the Rogron fortune toward Bathilde.Vinet persuaded the colonel to court Pierrette, painting a tempting picture of happiness while secretly maneuvering to benefit Bathilde. The colonel, who displayed a mixture of affection and reserve toward Pierrette, began seeking her out more, which inflamed Sylvie’s fears.Sylvie’s suspicions sharpened when she overheard Brigaut serenading Pierrette and concluded the colonel was involved with the girl. Her jealousy clouded her judgment and made her increasingly paranoid about losing the colonel. On a tense morning she accused Pierrette of harboring a secret admirer, mocked her innocence, and demanded answers. Pierrette, frightened and bewildered, remained silent, only intensifying Sylvie’s wrath. Sylvie then threatened Pierrette with severe consequences if she did not confess.As Pierrette endured the oppressive Rogron household, her health continued to decline. Her innocence sheltered her from some realities, but emotional strain and heavy labor eroded her strength. Brigaut’s presence offered brief comfort and hope, yet Sylvie’s relentless scrutiny and hostility deepened Pierrette’s isolation.Amid this turmoil, love, jealousy, and ambition became entangled, setting the stage for a tragic sequence that would affect everyone involved; Pierrette’s innocence and vulnerability made her an easy target in a household rife with envy and desire.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For a month Rogron had stopped delivering the Constitutionnel to Gouraud, who came for it himself and lingered to gossip and walk with Sylvie. Eager to see the colonel, Sylvie dressed in her finest. When Gouraud arrived, Rogron excused himself, leaving Sylvie alone with the colonel.Noting her solemnity, Gouraud asked after her cousin’s health; she said the cousin was unwell. The colonel was uneasy: Vinet’s influence over the Rogrons made him feel marginalised. A half-pay officer, he feared being sidelined by Vinet’s control of their political plans, and by Rogron’s growing infatuation with Bathilde de Chargeboeuf, which threatened his prospects. Gouraud even suspected Vinet might be manoeuvring to push him toward Pierrette as a ploy benefiting Vinet and Bathilde.Alone with Sylvie, he detected her unease and, when she tentatively raised Pierrette, he dismissed any affection for the girl, saying he preferred a more mature partner. Relieved, Sylvie concealed her intentions and hinted that her brother had once considered Pierrette a match. Sensing her interest, Gouraud declared he would marry Sylvie, delighting Rogron. Sylvie began planning their home but remained wary of Pierrette’s interactions with the colonel.As dinner neared, Sylvie scolded Pierrette for a stain and, despite harshness, ordered her to dress nicely—suspecting collusion with Gouraud. The Chargeboeuf ladies arrived; Bathilde was spectacularly dressed. Under Vinet’s guidance she viewed a marriage to Rogron as a stepping stone to power, treating him as a means while Vinet urged her to exploit beauty and connections for independence and influence.The evening grew tense: Bathilde’s condescension and Vinet’s remarks sharpened rivalry. At cards, Sylvie tried to manipulate the game; Pierrette’s innocent comments provoked Sylvie, and when Pierrette left in tears Sylvie resolved to make her pay. The night ended with Sylvie feeling betrayed and reflecting on men’s duplicity and her precarious position.The next day Pierrette suffered a painful fall; Sylvie blamed disobedience and showed no sympathy. Rogron suggested caring for Pierrette, but Sylvie dismissed it, revealing her coldness and the widening rift between the cousins.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The week ended in relentless torment for Pierrette under Sylvie’s growing cruelty. She kept silent about Brigaut’s arrival to avoid further anger, but his memory sustained her; she longed for him and feared for his safety. Their connection felt vital, and even a glimpse seemed a lifeline.Determined to communicate, Brigaut arranged to exchange letters with Pierrette. One morning he slipped a note to her while pretending to buy fruit. The exchange unleashed deep feelings in both. Pierrette returned home with Brigaut’s heartfelt letter, in which he expressed longing, a wish to escape their oppressive circumstances, and asked her to lower a string from her window so they could pass notes nightly. Moved, Pierrette resolved to reply despite the difficulty of obtaining writing materials without waking Sylvie, pouring out her love and describing the suffering inflicted by her relatives.At midnight she sent her letter down to Brigaut, who awaited it eagerly. Their correspondence continued—full of hope and plans—despite Sylvie’s latent jealousy. Observing Pierrette’s newfound joy, Sylvie grew watchful, suspecting she had seen someone.Under Sylvie’s scrutiny Pierrette’s health declined and one evening she fainted, drawing concern from the colonel and others. Sylvie dismissed the illness as a ruse, but Pierrette’s condition worsened. In desperation Pierrette cried for help, prompting a violent confrontation as Sylvie tried to seize her letters. At the height of Sylvie’s fury Pierrette’s grandmother, Madame Lorrain, arrived, witnessed the struggle, and rushed to her granddaughter’s aid.Her arrival brought hope. Confronted, Sylvie seemed confused and frightened. The grandmother, determined to protect Pierrette, sought medical help, fearing for her life. Meanwhile Brigaut wrote urgent letters to Madame Lorrain, pleading that she come rescue Pierrette. Upon receiving his appeals, the grandmother immediately traveled to Provins.She found Pierrette distressed and traumatized, bearing physical wounds—an injured hand from Sylvie’s violent attempts to control her. Filled with anguish, the grandmother vowed to secure justice and ensure Pierrette’s safety. As Pierrette recovered, her bond with her grandmother deepened, and the promise of medical care and a better life with her grandmother and Brigaut offered a glimmer of hope. The struggle for freedom and love continued, with Pierrette determined to reclaim her life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At nine o’clock that morning Monsieur Martener told Monsieur Tiphaine about the mistreatment Pierrette had suffered at the Rogrons’ hands. Tiphaine brought in Auffray, a notary and Pierrette’s relative. Longstanding enmities between the Vinet and Tiphaine factions—stoked by damaging truths about Madame Tiphaine’s family—framed the dispute and made the incident a political lever.Seeing a chance to weaken the Rogrons, influential royalist opponents, Tiphaine consulted the public prosecutor. He, Auffray, and Martener arranged to petition the courts to appoint Auffray Pierrette’s guardian, so a Family Council, supported by medical testimony, could remove her from the Rogrons’ control.By midday the town buzzed: neighbors had heard Pierrette’s cries and rumor spread. Doctors Bianchon and Brigaut examined her and confirmed abuse. Vinet, the Rogrons’ lawyer, warned of criminal exposure. Sylvie, at first evasive, later admitted jealousy and the truth, realizing the gravity of their situation.Vinet, exploiting their fear, proposed a marriage announcement to quiet the scandal. Under pressure the Rogrons signed a marriage contract that served their political interests. Vinet then filed a petition accusing Brigaut and the widow Lorrain of abducting Pierrette, trying to recast his clients as victims.The hearing was postponed, heightening public interest. The Tiphaines rallied support for Pierrette, who, unaware of the turmoil, was cared for by her grandmother and Brigaut. The doctors, appalled by her condition, unanimously condemned the Rogrons’ treatment.When the court reconvened, Auffray presented the physicians’ report to justify Pierrette’s protection. Vinet countered, but the judge ordered Pierrette kept from the Rogrons until the Family Council could meet—a notable public rebuke. The Council of relatives and allies voted to remove Rogron as guardian and appoint Auffray. Despite Vinet’s defense, the evidence prompted a civil suit for neglect and abuse.As the legal battle proceeded, Pierrette’s health deteriorated.
