Martener and the Auffray family grew fond of her; her grandmother’s care and Brigaut’s visits offered comfort. In March 1828 a critical operation was performed, but recovery was unlikely. As she declined, Pierrette expressed a wish to forgive her cousins. She died soon after; her funeral filled the town while the Rogrons, fearing outrage, stayed away.Brigaut, grief-stricken, made her coffin. The Rogrons sought legal advantage, but community support for Pierrette stymied them. Vinet faced backlash as the truth spread. Brigaut left Provins for a military life. The Rogrons and Vinet, however, largely retained political standing; many townspeople came to believe the Rogrons had acted nobly. Only a few—Martener, Brigaut, and like-minded friends—remembered the reality of Pierrette’s suffering. The account closes on a reflection about justice, accountability, and the lasting impact of her life and death.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Dedication: To David, Sculptor—Balzac expresses gratitude for the permanence of sculpture compared with the precariousness of other fame.THE VICAR OF TOURSIn the autumn of 1826, Abbé Birotteau was caught in a rainstorm returning from a friend's house and hurried across the deserted Cloister behind Saint‑Gatien cathedral. Short, corpulent, about sixty and subject to gout, he hated wet shoes, yet he was in high spirits after winning at cards with Madame de Listomère and nourished by the hope of obtaining a long‑desired canonry at the cathedral. That ambition absorbed him so completely he neglected his umbrella.The Cloister had once housed Chapter dignitaries but, after ecclesiastical confiscations, became the narrow Rue de la Psalette. On its left stood a single house, long occupied by priests and owned by a devout, discreet old maid, Mademoiselle Gamard, who preserved the property through the Restoration by taking priests as boarders. Gamard could take only two lodgers; for twelve years she had sheltered Abbé Troubert and the late Abbé Chapeloud. When Chapeloud died, Birotteau—who had long admired the apartment’s furnishings during visits—succeeded him.Chapeloud had been a close friend, and his death left Birotteau both bereaved and relieved: he inherited Chapeloud’s tasteful library and furniture, which had long fed his envy. To Birotteau, becoming Mademoiselle Gamard’s boarder and securing the canonry represented the comforts and status he craved. Installing himself in the rooms he had long coveted, he enjoyed the attentions and comforts he had imagined; but gratitude soon yielded to renewed obsession with the canonry.One rainy evening he returned to find the house door locked and had to ring repeatedly; his servant Marianne explained Gamard assumed he was already in. He noticed other small omissions—no slippers waiting, no fire—and felt slighted. These petty neglects pricked his vanity and suggested intentional disrespect. Innocent and lacking worldly tact, he could not see that these lapses were deliberate. Where Chapeloud had handled Gamard’s temperament with worldly shrewdness, Birotteau’s simplicity left him blind to a quietly mounting hostility.Mademoiselle Gamard’s discontent had been building during Birotteau’s eighteen months of tenancy. She prized social recognition and dreamed of cultivating a salon, ambitions frustrated by his indifference and by his resumption of former social habits—spending evenings with friends who provided the engagement she sought. As he distanced himself from her expectations, she felt her plans slipping away and began to nurse a quiet vengeance. Her coolness—locked doors, omitted attentions—was the first manifestation of this spite.Birotteau, absorbed in his hopes for preferment, confronted only symptoms, not causes. He reflected on possible offenses and convinced himself he must somehow have displeased her, unable to perceive the strategic nature of her omissions. The contrast between the two priests is telling: Chapeloud had navigated Gamard’s vanity with tact; Birotteau’s guilelessness made him an easy target for petty reprisals.Thus small domestic grievances escalated into a deeper conflict: unnoticed slights, unacknowledged ambitions, and provincial social maneuvering combine to estrange landlord and lodger. These episodes of neglect and mutual misunderstanding serve as a prologue to a fuller exploration of pride, naiveté, and the social intrigues that will follow.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next morning Birotteau woke preoccupied with thoughts of a possible canonry, momentarily forgetting the ominous signs of the night. He rang for Marianne to light a fire; she did not appear, and before he could ring again Abbé Troubert entered after knocking. This monthly visit was expected; Troubert noticed the absent fire, called for Marianne, then inquired about Birotteau’s health and news of the canonry. Unaware that Troubert resented Madame de Listomère for not inviting him to her home, Birotteau freely described his efforts to secure the position.The two abbés contrasted sharply. Troubert was tall, thin, serious, his wrinkled face suggesting sarcasm and contempt; Birotteau was plump with a round, cheerful face. Troubert’s calm exterior masked a dullness some attributed to his subservience to Mademoiselle Gamard; Birotteau’s jovial nature made him well liked. Neither seemed likely to rise above his station—Troubert through restrained ambition and a cultivated saintliness, Birotteau through amiability. At fifty, Troubert had smoothed his ambitions by adopting a pious life, and his failing health hinted he might soon attain vicar-general. Birotteau’s robust health promised endurance. Abbé Chapeloud, a respected diocesan, quietly opposed Troubert’s elevation, yet Troubert treated him respectfully despite Chapeloud’s skepticism.Troubert asked to borrow the Register of Bishops, which Birotteau gladly lent. Carrying large books downstairs, they met Mademoiselle Gamard’s sharp disapproval. She scolded Birotteau for cluttering her dining room; he felt the sting of her ill temper, a jarring contrast to his usual cheerfulness.Breakfast was tense. Mademoiselle Gamard complained of being disturbed the previous night; Birotteau, weighed by her displeasure, struggled to engage. His efforts at small talk about the coffee and weather met disdain. Her bitter demeanor reflected the resentment of an unmarried woman who, feeling unfulfilled and rejected, harbored self-loathing and social frustration. Her sharp features, abrupt movements, and ungracious manner mirrored that inner turmoil and the stigma she bore for never marrying.Feeling oppressed, Birotteau left the dining room and wandered the garden, only to find Gamard and Troubert watching him, which intensified his discomfort. Distracted, he took refuge in church duties and temporarily forgot his troubles. Returning home, he discovered he had missed dinner: Mademoiselle Gamard had set the clock ahead to avoid waiting, a petty manipulation that undermined his comfort while preserving her propriety.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Eight days after the events began, Abbé Birotteau discovered that Mademoiselle Gamard’s hostility, evident since the candlestick-and-clock incident, was a six-month plot against him. Previously oblivious, he now felt suffocated by her malice. Gamard, savoring revenge, executed her plan while the priest, absorbed by minor worries, confided in no one.His life became torment. Anxiety over confronting Gamard worsened his health, prompting him to seek Abbé Troubert’s help. Embarrassed to complain, he described Gamard’s provocations; Troubert listened with restrained amusement and advised understanding her, attributing her behavior to health issues.Desperate, Birotteau hoped a temporary stay at Madame de Listomère’s country house would ease tensions, not realizing Gamard could exploit his absence. After ten days, Monsieur Caron, Gamard’s lawyer, reported she was alarmed by his prolonged leave and wanted clarity to avoid disputes. Back among friends, Birotteau recounted his troubles; speculation followed. Madame de Listomère suggested Troubert coveted Birotteau’s apartment. An old landowner warned this was only the start of long troubles. Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix brought news that the vicar-general was ill and Troubert might be appointed, potentially giving him power over appointments.Madame de Listomère urged Birotteau to consider the canonry rather than endure Gamard. Friends proposed letting Gamard and Troubert have their way if it served his advancement. But the struggle intensified. Caron returned asking Birotteau to sign a document relinquishing his residence; urged by friends, he signed.Birotteau’s despair deepened when he learned he had signed away possessions Gamard claimed as compensation for board differences. Confronted, she coldly cited the agreement. Devastated, he fled to Madame de Listomère and found his belongings packed. Mademoiselle Salomon, fearing for his sanity, took him back to the Alouette, where he confided in friends.Monsieur de Bourbonne examined the deed, found it exploitative, and urged legal action. The group debated suing to recover property versus compromising to avoid scandal; they resolved to support Birotteau, though Bourbonne warned many would desert him if the fight grew bitter. Madame de Listomère’s invitation had already sparked gossip and divided the town; Gamard, feeling slighted, worked to undermine his reputation.Friends began gathering evidence for a lawsuit, complicated by political divisions and public suspicion of the clergy. An expert appraisal revealed the true value of Birotteau’s goods, indicating Gamard’s claims were fraudulent. As legal proceedings opened, town gossip intensified the conflict. Stakes were high and the outcome uncertain as Birotteau faced the formidable alliance of Gamard and Troubert, determined to preserve their power.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A few days after the quarrel between Birotteau and the old maid began, Baron de Listomere—expecting promotion to corvette captain—received a letter warning of possible retirement. Alarmed, he rushed to Paris; the minister of the Navy dismissed his fears, but subsequent inquiries produced a document confirming the retirement, awaiting the director’s signature.Desperate, the baron turned to his deputy uncle, who learned the minister believed the baron had sided with Radicals in Tours and had offended the influential Abbe Troubert, the vicar-general. His uncle urged reconciliation with Troubert, stressing that good relations with the clergy were vital to the baron’s career. Recognizing the danger, the baron resolved to avoid further conflict.Three days later he told his aunt the family’s position was precarious because of their support for Birotteau. They consulted Monsieur de Bourbonne, who advised a graceful retreat from the dispute: appease Troubert while distancing themselves from Birotteau, now a political liability.The baroness confronted Birotteau and asked him to withdraw his lawsuit against Mademoiselle Gamard and leave her house. Distressed, Birotteau agreed, feeling himself a burden; he asked for the return of Chapeloud’s portrait, which the baroness promised to negotiate for.Mademoiselle Gamard soon fell ill; gossip said Birotteau’s suit had driven her to it. The baroness sought out Troubert, and their meeting became a duel of polite insinuations. She presented Birotteau’s relinquishment; Troubert feigned indifference but was privately pleased. He later returned with Gamard’s refusal to surrender the portrait, which the baroness accepted to keep the peace.Gamard died and left her fortune to Troubert, intensifying the Listomeres’ plight. At her funeral Troubert delivered a grand eulogy while Birotteau, guilt-ridden, mourned alone. Five months later Troubert became Bishop of Troyes; Madame de Listomere died, leaving an annuity to Birotteau, but Troubert’s influence continued to threaten the baron’s career and to contest Birotteau’s legacy.Ultimately Birotteau was exiled to a distant parish, ill and grieving. Troubert’s ascent—marked by cunning and manipulation—reflects broader struggles within society and the Church, and the moral costs of personal ambition.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy, etc.Dear Nodier, this book uncovers actions hidden behind domestic doors, where divine intervention often replaces human justice. Its moral lessons focus on families and motherhood, and on the consequences of weakening paternal authority—the traditional tribunal for domestic wrongs. A mother, however loving, cannot stand in for patriarchal power. The story defends the indissoluble marriage bond and warns against unchecked selfishness in a society that exalts wealth and success while the law proves impotent. I hope such a society will return to Catholicism for moral guidance through a different education.In “Scenes from Military Life” I will portray noble self-sacrifice and also expose war’s corrupting influence on those who live at home as if on a battlefield.You have observed our times keenly; I entrust this tale to your name, which may shield it from criticism.Your sincere admirer,De Balzac  Paris, November 1842.THE TWO BROTHERSCHAPTER IIn 1792 the townspeople of Issoudun disliked Dr. Rouget; gossip said he made his beautiful wife unhappy, though the household’s true nature remained hidden. His wife, a demoiselle Descoings, had been frail in youth. They had a son, Jean-Jacques, and, a decade later, a daughter, Agathe.Rouget believed men must display strength, a conviction that caused much female suffering. The Descoings family—wealthy wool merchants turned investors in forfeited estates—were rich and miserly. Descoings, Rouget’s brother-in-law, had left Issoudun for Paris, failed as a grocer, and been driven by love for a beautiful widow. By 1792 he seemed prosperous; Rouget, expecting his wife’s death, sent Agathe to Paris to stay with the Descoings, hoping she would secure their favor and a share of the inheritance.Agathe, the most beautiful girl in Issoudun and like her mother in looks, provoked a quarrel between Rouget and his friend Lousteau, who then left town. Madame Rouget, learning her daughter had gone, predicted she would never see Agathe again—a prophecy that proved true. Her health declined further, worsened by her son’s neglect; Jean-Jacques, like his father, offered little comfort.Agathe’s arrival in Paris coincided with Descoings’ imprisonment after imprudent political remarks about the famine. His wife’s attempts to save him failed; he was executed alongside the poet André Chénier, an event that made local grocers avoid politics for years. Descoings’ death affected the community more immediately than Chénier’s, though history would later view the poet’s loss as greater.Afterward Agathe married Bridau, a ministry secretary, with Rouget’s approval; Rouget had hoped to profit from any Descoings inheritance. Bridau, captivated by Agathe’s beauty, married without suspecting manipulation. He supported Napoleon, earned a promotion in 1804, worked relentlessly, and neglected his health. In 1808 he died from overwork just as another promotion was imminent—an honor the Emperor had intended but was too late to confer.Following Bridau’s death, Agathe received no letters from Issoudun. Her mother died shortly before the birth of her second son; her father’s death coincided with the Emperor’s coronation, a distraction that kept Bridau away. Jean-Jacques had not contacted her since she left home, and she gradually accepted the family’s indifference.Madame Hochon, Agathe’s godmother, warned that Agathe would inherit nothing unless she granted power of attorney to her brother. Agathe hesitated to burden him; Bridau either didn’t notice or didn’t object. The arrangement left them financially fragile. Bridau had invested prudently to secure a modest income; after his death Agathe and Madame Descoings pooled resources and lived on a joint income of twelve thousand francs—a practical solution that masked underlying strains.By 1809 Madame Descoings, sixty-five but still charming, enjoyed theater and fine food and secretly gambled on lotteries. She also harbored a secret about her son Bixiou, a major in the army, who had a son educated alongside Agathe’s children. Devoted to her children and refusing to remarry, Agathe believed it her duty to be both mother and head of household. But her limited intelligence and blind trust in Madame Descoings proved disastrous: the latter’s lottery debts accumulated until she confessed. Agathe, without reproach, took charge—selling possessions and liquidating investments to settle the debts—a decisive turning point in their lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One of the bleakest parts of Paris was the stretch of rue Mazarin between rue Guenegard and rue de Seine, shaded by the high gray walls of the college and library given by Cardinal Mazarin. There, on the third floor of a damp, cold house, lived a poor widow, Madame Bridau (Agathe). Opposite were the Institute buildings, where aspiring artists, the rapins, worked amid the turmoil of examinations; from her windows Agathe could see their cages and the students’ tumult. To brighten her room she defied police regulations and set boxes of earth at the sill to make little gardens.The house was shallow, reached by a winding staircase to three rooms: a dining room, a small salon, a bedroom, plus a kitchen and two single rooms. Agathe had chosen the flat because it was cheap—four hundred francs a year—near her sons’ school and in a neighborhood she knew. The interior matched the building’s dreariness: yellowed wallpaper, unglazed tiles, and only essential furniture. The salon held an Aubusson carpet given to her and a mahogany sofa bearing a pastel portrait of her late husband, Bridau, which captured his noble essence; a vivid Napoleon hung above a card table.After Bridau’s death Agathe’s life narrowed. She filled the room with two large birdcages—canaries and Java sparrows—a hobby that left her space littered with millet, chickweed, and cat hair, signs of grief and neglected personal care. She preserved everything belonging to her husband, even wrapping his last pen with a note. Seized by sorrow, she seemed to lose her sense of womanhood and devoted herself entirely to her children.Agathe lived for her sons and worried ceaselessly over their finances. The Emperor provided three hundred francs a year for each child, and scholarships supplemented this. Her aunt, Madame Descoings, on the second floor, assigned three thousand francs from her annuity to help Agathe; the Descoings themselves now lived modestly on twelve hundred francs a year, employed a woman for morning chores, and shared dinners with the two widows. Madame Descoings doted on Philippe and Joseph, indulging them with treats and small sums. Distrustful of the future, Agathe scrimped and saved—she set aside three thousand francs from her pension—hoping to secure some inheritance for her boys. Thus both widows had passed from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty, each for different reasons.The house, the students’ uproar, and her husband’s portrait formed the setting that would shape her youngest son, Joseph Bridau, into a notable painter of the modern French school. Philippe, the elder, resembled his mother in appearance but had a bold demeanor often mistaken for courage; Agathe hoped his looks promised similar moral qualities. At fifteen his charm reinforced her hopes. Joseph, three years younger, took after his father in less favorable ways: untidy, often scolded, and plainly less favored by his mother.Joseph’s artistic bent was sparked by a visit to the Tuileries, where a caricature on an Institute wall captivated him and made him want to draw. He began sneaking into the Institute courtyard, enduring teasing from older students until his persistence won their respect. He later visited the sculptor Chaudet’s atelier; Chaudet recognized and encouraged his talent. Agathe feared art would mean hardship and tried to dissuade him, but Chaudet defended the vocation as a divine calling, insisting true talent must be followed.Meanwhile Philippe, enchanted by military life, secretly petitioned the Emperor to enlist. He was soon sent to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr and rose rapidly, becoming a captain by nineteen. Joseph, after his scholarship was withdrawn, tried to earn a living by art, encouraged by Madame Descoings. Despite Agathe’s concerns about his health and prospects, Joseph thrived among fellow students and mentors.As politics shifted, Philippe returned home a decorated officer while Joseph faced an uncertain career. Agathe’s preference for Philippe became more apparent as she admired his military success; yet Joseph remained devoted to his mother. Despite their divergent paths, both brothers pursued their callings, with Agathe’s constant love and anxiety marking every step of their futures.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In 1816 Joseph won his mother’s consent to convert the adjoining garret into an atelier, partly funded by Madame Descoings. While the two widows treated painting as a trade, Joseph embraced it passionately, installing a skylight and chocolate-colored walls and continuing his studies under Gros and Schinner.Political unrest simmered in France: conspiracies and riots surrounded the Chamber of Deputies. Philippe, implicated in plots, was arrested and released for lack of evidence; his military pay was cut, and Agathe feared for him. Friends urged the widows to send Philippe abroad for safety; she resisted the idea of him serving a foreign power, but he ultimately joined General Lallemand in the United States to help found the Champ d’Asile — an enterprise that failed. Agathe equipped him with ten thousand francs, and by late 1817 had adjusted to reduced means. Joseph lived frugally to support the family while his talent developed.In 1818 Roguin, the notary, absconded with funds intended for Madame Descoings. Soon after, Philippe sent a bill for a thousand francs, having lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. Agathe, distraught, blamed herself for urging his departure. Despite her own difficulties, Madame Descoings continued to support the family. Desperate, Joseph turned to Gerard, who arranged for him to paint copies of a portrait of Louis XVIII; Joseph worked tirelessly, selling panels to cover Philippe’s debt.When Philippe returned from America, Agathe welcomed him warmly, not seeing how time abroad had hardened him. He became self-centered, indulged in drinking and gambling, and adopted a swaggering arrogance. Agathe, blinded by maternal love, excused his faults as consequences of circumstance. The family’s finances deteriorated; friends discussed selling possessions to help him.Philippe’s conduct alarmed acquaintances: he rejected serving the Bourbons, which hampered employment prospects, yet lived as if supported by others. He often disappeared for days, maintained a lavish lifestyle, favored cafes and gambling halls, and grew distant from family duties. He sought to impress Mariette, a young dancer; Giroudeau encouraged him and proposed journalism as a way to exploit his experiences. Philippe grew hostile to the Liberal party, blaming it for his misfortunes and nursing a desire for revenge.A potential job as cashier at a newspaper brought temporary relief and prompted Agathe and Madame Descoings to secure his position, overlooking prior sacrifices. The role did little to reform him; his arrogance continued, and Joseph felt increasingly overshadowed and unappreciated. Ultimately Philippe’s return and altered behavior strained relationships, revealing the consequences of choices made in pursuit of dreams, the limits of maternal devotion, and the fragile balance between personal ambition and family duty.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Three months later the colonel, who ate enough for four, still criticized the food while the widows—reluctant to press him—spent lavishly on meals although he had not paid a penny. His mother and Madame Descoings hesitated to remind him of his promise; a year passed without any of the “tigers with five claws” Leon Gozlan joked about finding their way from Philippe’s pocket into the household purse. The colonel assuaged his conscience by seldom dining at home. “Well, he is happy,” his mother remarked, “he has a place.”Mariette, urged on by a feuilleton edited by Vernou, debuted at the Porte-Saint-Martin and triumphed alongside the famous Begrand. A wealthy general, enchanted with an actress and acquainted with Philippe and Giroudeau, became her impresario and arranged her first appearance—an example of the solidarity among those captivated by folly.Bixiou soon told his grandmother and Agathe that Philippe, the cashier, was in love with Mariette, the celebrated ballet-dancer. This appalled the widows; Agathe’s piety made her see all stage women as morally corrupt, and both believed such women lived extravagantly. “Do you think my brother will spend on Mariette? Such women ruin rich men,” Joseph protested. Bixiou reassured them that Mariette might be taken from Philippe by higher-ranking admirers; he’d heard an ambassador was infatuated with her.Philippe, partly from financial motive, proposed marriage; Mariette declined, either suspecting his intent or valuing her freedom, and anticipated an engagement at the Grand Opera. For the rest of the year Philippe visited his mother only twice a month, spending his days at the office, the theatre, or with Mariette, enjoying pleasures and mingling with Tullia and other entertainers.The carnival lasted eighteen months but grew stormy. After her Opera debut in January 1821 Mariette attracted a distinguished duke. Philippe, driven by passion, began dipping into the funds he managed, taking eleven hundred francs by May. Mariette left for London to seek patrons while the temporary opera house was prepared. Philippe, despite her infidelities, remained deeply in love; to her he was merely a stepping stone.Forced to relinquish Mariette, Philippe returned to his mother’s apartment, unable to imagine life without the recent pleasures. Suicide crossed his mind—not because of the deficit alone but because he could not bear life without Mariette’s company. He entered Joseph’s studio where Joseph was copying a picture for a dealer and explaining his earnings. Pale and subdued, Philippe asked how long it would take to paint his portrait, saying he wanted to leave his mother a likeness. When Joseph asked if he was leaving again, Philippe replied, “I am going never to return,” and, pressed, confessed he had taken money from the funds and could not live with the dishonor.Joseph tried to reassure him that repayment would save his honor, but Philippe, despairing, fled downstairs, leaving Joseph anxious. Joseph informed Madame Descoings, who was horrified. Agathe was devastated, crying at the thought of Philippe failing in honor. Madame Descoings urged hope, attributing his ruin to a bad woman; Agathe lived in terror for her son.That day Philippe calmly submitted his accounts, inventing a story that he’d retained the money for safekeeping. He then went to a gambling house—winning at first, then losing everything. Returning home, he found concern and affection from his family, which briefly comforted him. “Promise not to kill yourself, and all shall be forgotten,” his mother pleaded. Philippe, realizing the threat had worked, feigned gratitude and affection, and they shared dinner; he felt momentarily relieved.The next day Agathe appeared older and more anxious, aware the family’s finances were precarious. Madame Descoings suggested consulting Desroches to manage the affair and preserve honor. Desroches, in his diplomatic way, advised Agathe to sign a transfer of funds and petition for Philippe’s reinstatement in the army, assuring them Philippe would soon regain his rank and easing some fear. Yet Agathe’s faith was shaken and she lived in constant dread of his downfall.Philippe returned to a life of leisure—gambling, drinking, appearing disheveled and indifferent to the family’s plight. His love for Mariette had hardened him; he treated women with contempt. Joseph, concentrating on painting, gained recognition and Madame Descoings’s support, who pinned her hopes on his future. As Madame Descoings pursued lottery dreams, she remained oblivious to growing tensions. Joseph, noticing discrepancies in his finances, began to suspect Philippe and confided in Madame Descoings. They planned to catch him. When Joseph discovered that Philippe had indeed taken money from him, he felt betrayed; despite anger, he still struggled with confronting his brother. Madame Descoings urged sympathy for Philippe’s struggles, but Joseph remained wary of his influence on the family’s future.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Despite Philippe’s careful approach to nightly gambling, he was sometimes “cleaned out.” Needing his usual ten francs, he began stealing from his family—pilfering from his brother and whatever Madame Descoings or Agathe left about. After a distressing dream in which Philippe robbed her, Agathe realized the depth of his decline and begged him to ask for money instead of stealing. Within two weeks her savings were gone and she took worsted work for a meager twenty sous a day. The strain of her hidden anguish showed in her appearance; she spent sleepless nights waiting for Philippe, watching his drunkenness and the mood swings that followed losses at the tables.When Madame Descoings suggested Philippe get new clothes, he retorted that his mother had no money and that he had mortgaged his pension. He admitted borrowing from Giroudeau, calling it a “debt of honor.” Agathe defended him, thinking he was out of his element, while Joseph criticized his neglect of appearance and duty. Philippe sank further into debauchery and gambling, losing the good looks and steadiness he once had.One day Philippe asked Joseph for money to pay a debt to the tobacconist; Joseph refused. Philippe then schemed to find where Madame Descoings kept her savings, guessing she would be out buying Christmas treats. He broke into her room and discovered twenty napoleons hidden in the mattress. He took the money, intending to gamble it.At the gambling house Philippe won substantial sums over several sessions and, intoxicated by success, amassed over one hundred fifty thousand francs by the end of the night. Pressed by greed, he continued gambling and lost everything. The next morning he woke in despair, having ruined his family.Agathe and Madame Descoings waited for Philippe to return for Christmas dinner; when he failed to show they worried. Madame had pinned hopes on a lottery stake, but upon checking her savings she found the money gone and fainted. The concierge confirmed Philippe had been in the apartment that day. Agathe returned pale and shaken; the family confronted Philippe. He dismissed their accusations, claiming he had taken what was owed him. A physical altercation erupted between Joseph and Philippe, and Madame Descoings suffered a stroke amid the chaos.Afterward Joseph offered his mother money, but she refused, clinging to lottery hope; when the winning numbers were announced it was clear she had missed her chance, and she died shortly after. Philippe, recovering from illness, was told by Agathe that their finances had collapsed and urged to rejoin the army. Indifferent and feeling abandoned, he accepted the money she reluctantly offered, left to seek the company of those he called true friends. Agathe, heartbroken, prayed for her son and resigned herself to the loss of the child she once loved.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By February 1822, Madame Bridau had moved into the attic above the kitchen of her former apartment, recently occupied by her son Philippe. Seeing his mother’s reduced circumstances, Joseph resolved to make her comfortable: he rearranged the garret, added tasteful decor and a double door, and took Agathe to dine daily at a table-d’hôte that cost him nearly a hundred francs a month. Alarmed by the expense, Agathe, with her confessor’s help, accepted a seven-hundred-franc-a-year post at a lottery office that demanded long hours.Agathe adapted to the routine, preparing Joseph’s breakfast and managing his household despite her lack of art knowledge. She mended his clothes, kept the studio tidy, and aided his work; their bond deepened through small acts of tenderness. They discussed Philippe, whose reckless life worried Agathe, but Joseph dismissed her fears, convinced Philippe was prospering in New York.One afternoon Joseph visited Philippe, who worked at a newspaper. Philippe invited Joseph to dine, but Joseph declined to prioritize his mother. When he later told Agathe about Philippe, she felt renewed hope. She decorated Joseph’s studio and prepared a special breakfast for Philippe’s portrait sitting, then hid during the session, overwhelmed as she watched the two sons together.After posing, Philippe invited them to dinner, which made Joseph uneasy because of Philippe’s apparent wealth; Agathe eagerly accepted. The lavish meal heightened Joseph’s suspicions about Philippe’s finances. At the next sitting, Agathe learned more about Philippe’s social circle—journalists and actresses—and heard his boastful ambitions, though he remained evasive about money.Joseph had to postpone another sitting because of a pressing commission. When Philippe visited the studio he mistakenly took a valuable copy of a Rubens, believing it the original, and sold it for a fraction of its worth. Discovering the theft devastated Joseph; Agathe was heartbroken, feeling she had lost her son to vice.Two months later an old officer told Agathe of Philippe’s dire condition: ill and needing money for care. Working at the lottery office, she had little to spare and refused to help the son she had banished from her heart. Joseph agreed, insisting Philippe should go to the hospital. Some time after, Agathe encountered Philippe on the street—destitute and recently discharged—and, despite knowing the money would likely be squandered, gave him what she could, then despaired over his future.Agathe later learned Philippe had been involved in a conspiracy that led to his arrest; she fainted at the news. Joseph consulted Desroches, who urged them to secure the family inheritance and gather evidence of undue influence over Philippe. Desroches planned to visit Issoudun at the holidays to help. Desperate, Agathe had written to her godmother but received a discouraging reply about her brother’s unwillingness to assist. Agathe and Joseph set off for Issoudun, leaving Philippe to face the consequences, and Agathe reflected on the estrangement she would confront.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Issoudun, one of France’s oldest cities, is noted by Caesar and Rigord for its population and commerce. Recent excavations by M. Armand Peremet uncovered a fifth-century basilica, likely unique in France, built from stones of a Roman temple to Isis. The town’s name—derived from “Isis” with “dun” indicating a religious and military settlement—points to ancient Celtic origins.In the Middle Ages Issoudun was a center of power: Richard Coeur-de-lion built a tower above the basilica as part of its fortifications, and the town later served as a stronghold for Routiers and Cottereaux mercenaries sent by Henry II against his son Richard. Yet its prominence has faded. After the 1830 insurrection vine-growers resisted government intervention, burned tax offices, and asserted autonomy—demonstrating the force of a community of about eight thousand vine-growers.In the fourteenth century Issoudun housed sixteen to seventeen thousand people, prospering on the wool trade and producing blankets and gloves; by Louis XIV’s time it enjoyed some elegance. Today the population is about twelve thousand, including vine-growers from nearby suburbs. The local economy, especially wine, suffers from outdated practices: vine-growers oppose modernization and produce poor-quality wine that undermines potential prosperity.This decline stems from a pervasive spirit of stagnation. When a proposal for a new road linking Paris and Toulouse through Issoudun arose, local leaders opposed it, fearing higher food prices—an example of a provincial tendency to favor tradition over progress.Issoudun’s social fabric has also deteriorated. The disappearance of the nobility after past upheavals removed a source of competition and dynamism. The bourgeoisie, content with routine social forms, have grown complacent, contributing to stagnant governance and community life. Administration is dominated by intertwined local figures whose familial ties produce ineffectual leadership and weak accountability. The clergy exert little influence, further diminishing civic vitality.Against this background the town’s youth, feeling trapped, formed the “Knights of Idleness,” led by Maxence Gilet. Max—rumored illegitimate son of a local official—was charismatic and adventurous, raised in poverty, who enlisted and distinguished himself in the military. Returning home, he clashed with the bourgeoisie; after a violent confrontation with royalist officers he became a local hero. Under his leadership the Knights staged pranks and elaborate hoaxes—disruptive yet creative antics that brought excitement to an otherwise stagnant community.Their secret meeting place was the local tavern run by Père and La Cognette, who, for self-interest, sheltered the group without fully knowing their deeds. Issoudun’s ancient grandeur and archaeological significance thus contrast sharply with its modern decline; the Knights of Idleness embody the youth’s frustration and thirst for life in a town that has lost much of its former vibrancy.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Joseph and Madame Bridau traveled from Orléans to Issoudun, the Knights of Idleness staged a prank. An old Spaniard, a former prisoner of war, left his empty cart at the foot of the Issoudun tower one morning. Maxence, arriving at a Knights' rendezvous, suggested, “Here’s Père Fario’s cart. Let’s get it up on the embankment first, and then decide.” The tower of Issoudun, built by Richard Coeur-de-Lion atop a basilica, a Roman temple, and a Celtic dun, stands on a historically rich mound. In an hour the cart was disassembled and hoisted piece by piece to the embankment, like artillery over the Grand Saint-Bernard; once reassembled, the Knights, hungry and thirsty, returned to Mère Cognette’s to laugh at the Spaniard’s reaction when he came the next morning.They did not prank nightly; their activities varied, and personal ties sometimes kept members away. They gathered regularly for daytime hunts, autumn vintages, and winter skating. Among the twenty youths some were especially close to Max, now their idol. François Hochon and Baruch Borniche, grandsons of Madame Hochon, treated Max as a cousin, relying on him for money and adventures their grandfather denied them.That night, after twelve to fifteen bottles of wine, François and Baruch sat beside Max at a dim table. Baruch warned, “You are threatened in your stronghold.”“What do you mean?” Max asked.“My grandmother received a letter from Madame Bridau, her goddaughter, saying she and her son are coming here. She’s preparing two rooms for them.”Max downed his glass. At thirty-four, his soldierly looks—well-defined face, clear skin, expressive eyes, slightly curly black hair, and a winning smile—made him appear a destined general. Yet the news unsettled him.From the other end of the table someone called, “Madame Hochon’s goddaughter is Rouget’s sister. If she’s coming with her son she’ll secure the property when he dies—and goodbye to your harvest!”Max frowned. “What’s that to me?” he repeated, and when François suggested Rouget might revoke a will, Max snapped: “I’ve silenced those who meddle with you, François. Is this how you repay me? By using a contemptuous nickname for a woman I care about?”Max had never before spoken so openly about Flore Brazier, Rouget’s servant-mistress, whom society disparaged. His friends had learned to tread carefully around the subject. Encouraged by Max, François fell silent; Goddet reminded them of their freedom to speak in the Order. Max took charge of the conversation.“I’ll tell you what you’re thinking,” he said. “You believe Flore, the Rabouilleuse, has been providing for me since I returned. That I throw away three hundred francs a month and treat you to dinners because of her money? Yes—Flore aims for Rouget’s property.”“You think I plan to marry Flore when Rouget dies, and that these Parisians threaten my future?” he continued.“That’s it,” François affirmed.“Don’t worry,” Max reassured them. “If I need help to deal with these Parisians, will you assist me—within the limits of our fooleries, of course. I’m not asking you to kill or poison them. If the Bridaus succeed and Flore has nothing but what she stands in, I’d be satisfied. I love her enough to prefer her to Mademoiselle Fichet, if she would have me.”Mademoiselle Fichet, the richest heiress in Issoudun, was courted by Goddet. The Knights, moved by Max’s frankness, rose in unison: “You’re a fine fellow, Max!” “We’ll stand by you!” “A fig for the Bridaus!” “Here’s to the beautiful Flore!” Their cheers reflected the relaxed morality of the Order; Max’s influence secured their loyalty. He raised his glass of claret.“As a mark of ill-will I wish you all a mistress as fine as Flore! As for this influx of relatives, I feel no immediate unease; we’ll see what the future holds—”“Don’t forget Fario’s cart!”“That’s safe enough!” Goddet replied.“I’ll handle that,” Max assured them. “Be in the market early, and let me know when the old fellow comes for his cart.”At half-past three the Knights slipped away quietly in their soft shoes. Max returned to the place Saint-Jean, concealing his worries; his time in Cabrera had taught him deep dissimulation. The forty thousand francs a year from Rouget’s property anchored his affection for Flore. Despite outward confidence, the news of legitimate heirs unnerved him. Rouget’s savings, amassed over seventeen years, remained in the doctor’s name; even if Flore could claim a will, those funds could be secured elsewhere.“That fool of a girl never mentioned her sister and nephews in all these seven years!” Max muttered turning into the rue l’Avenier. “Seven hundred and fifty thousand francs with various notaries can’t be liquidated quickly without everyone knowing in this gossiping town! The priority is to get rid of these relatives; once they’re gone, we can secure the property. I need to think this through.”Exhausted, Max entered Père Rouget’s house with a pass-key and went to bed, telling himself, “Tomorrow, my thoughts will be clear.”Now to recount how Flore Brazier became known as the “Rabouilleuse” and quasi-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget’s household. As Doctor Rouget aged, he saw his son’s ineptitude and tried to impose a routine, which only readied Jean-Jacques to submit to the first authority that came along. One day in September 1799, returning from his rounds, the doctor found a captivating little girl by a brook, emerging from the water in a tattered petticoat and makeshift hat. The child, Flore, was catching crabs for her uncle Brazier.“Where do you come from?” he asked.“I belong in Vatan,” she replied. “I’m catching crabs for my uncle.”“Has your uncle permission to fish here?” the doctor inquired.“Hey! Aren’t we all under a Republic that is one and indivisible?” the uncle called from the brook.Struck by the child’s looks, the doctor offered to take her in, promising a better life. The uncle agreed to leave Flore with the doctor, who would pay wages. Flore’s life changed: she was well-fed, clothed, and given gifts to encourage study. Yet her peasant upbringing left her with a distaste for books; the doctor eventually stopped her schooling.Townspeople speculated about the nature of the doctor’s attention, their gossip coloring perceptions of impropriety. For five years the doctor enjoyed educating Flore without the complications often accompanying such arrangements. Born in 1787, she grew amid late-18th-century upheaval when traditional values were shaken; by 1802 the Church was barely reestablishing itself and social norms had shifted. Flore was envied by local girls for her privileged life despite the moral implications; raised in self-interest rather than strict virtue, she viewed her circumstances pragmatically.As Rouget neared death, his notary urged him to make provisions for Flore. His cynical response—refusing to leave her anything—reflected resentment and a desire to punish what he saw as an unreciprocated attachment. That refusal underscored his bitterness, as if he believed her beauty would suffice for her future, and set the stage for the inheritance struggle that now threatened Max’s position and Flore’s hopes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Jean-Jacques Rouget did not mourn his father’s death, though Flore Brazier did. The old doctor had made his son miserable, especially after Jean-Jacques came of age in 1791, but he had also provided Flore with the material comforts valued by country folk. After the funeral, when Fanchette asked Flore what would become of her now that Monsieur was dead, Jean-Jacques suddenly showed animation for the first time.At seventeen, Flore still had the beauty that had attracted the doctor, though she was growing plump like other countrywomen. Jean-Jacques hesitated to speak; the memory of his father’s death and benefactions weighed on him, and Flore left him puzzled by his silence.When his father died Jean-Jacques was thirty-seven: timid, submissive, and childlike. His timidity came from a torpor of the faculties that a strong influence might have roused. He was passionate but lacked confidence, awkward and unattractive, and preferred solitude where he felt at ease. The doctor, too late, recognized how his son’s temperament had suffered. He wanted to marry Jean-Jacques but feared losing control of his property, so he sought a daughter-in-law and tried to instill avarice in his son to give him some sense of financial instinct.Despite his father’s efforts, Jean-Jacques was secretly in love with Flore, the only woman whose education and presence he could comfortably observe. He took pride in the instruction his father gave her, thinking it would make her easier to win. Flore, unaware of his feelings, waited for him to declare himself, but he remained silent.Three weeks of uncertainty passed. One night Flore awoke to find Jean-Jacques crouched outside her door and took this as proof of his love; the next day she found him less repellent. Their conversations continued haltingly: Jean-Jacques asked if she wanted to return to the fields, and Flore, curious about his intentions, assured him of her honesty. These exchanges revealed his growing affection even as he struggled to express it.After ten months Jean-Jacques brightened and became attentive to his appearance, much to Flore’s satisfaction. The townspeople noticed and speculated. Fanchette, the cook who had disapproved of Flore’s place in the household, eventually left; Flore had anticipated this and learned to cook and manage the house, surpassing the former cook and taking pride in keeping things neat.Over the next nine years Flore gained complete control over Jean-Jacques. She treated him with a blend of familiarity and respect and gradually became the dominant figure; he, childlike and dependent, relied on her for everything, and she indulged his whims.By 1815 Flore was at the height of her beauty, a buxom housewife; in 1816 she fell in love with Maxence Gilet, a penniless officer. Her new passion changed their household. Feeling neglected, she confronted Jean-Jacques about his wealth and the empty rooms in the house and suggested inviting Max to stay. Fearing to lose her, Jean-Jacques reluctantly agreed.With Max in the home, Jean-Jacques found the companionship and conversation he had lacked. Flore, meanwhile, shirked household duties—claiming they spoiled her hands—hired a new cook, adopted a more elegant appearance to impress Max, bought him a horse, and took on a servant for him. The household shifted: Flore and Max became the central figures while Jean-Jacques receded into the background, increasingly dependent on them.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun to claim her threatened inheritance, Jean-Jacques Rouget had become semi-vegetative. After Max’s financial aid, Flore raised Rouget’s standard of living, feeding him from Vedie’s kitchen. Despite better food, he weakened physically and, utterly submissive, his devotion to Flore was his sole motive; he seemed older than his fifty-seven years.Max’s apartment displayed his devotion: provincial comforts, colored tiles, elegant wallpapers and mahogany furniture. A rush matting on the stairs muffled footsteps so Max could come home late without waking anyone, Rouget included, who remained ignorant of Max’s nocturnal activities.One morning Flore, in a cotton gown, found Max asleep and noticed his late return. He told her a serious matter: Rouget’s family was coming to Issoudun, likely to evict them. Flore vowed confrontation; Max urged caution, asking for coffee and suggesting they plan later. Anxious, Flore began to make coffee but was interrupted by Baruch, who announced that Fario was searching for his cart.Max joined the crowd. Fario, a small peculiar man, claimed his cart had been stolen. Max led him to the tower embankment where the cart was inexplicably found at the top. Amid the crowd’s laughter, Max pretended to assist, theatrically tossing the cart down to Fario’s dismay.The incident became Issoudun gossip: Fario’s anger and Max’s antics drew attention. Max grasped the implications and felt the need to manage the fallout, especially with the Bridaus’ arrival.François informed Max that the Bridaus would arrive that night. Max decided they should not appear hostile; he proposed letting the Hochons drive them out instead. Meanwhile Flore, unable to contain her anger, punished Rouget by withdrawing her affection, leaving him confused and distressed.Flore’s harshness struck Rouget hard; he depended on her emotionally. He asked what he had done, while Vedie urged him to placate Flore. Flore’s fury sprang from learning Rouget’s sister and nephew were coming, whom she saw as threats.Rouget, bewildered, denied inviting them and blamed Madame Hochon for the visit. Max arrived and argued Rouget should welcome his kin, since public perception mattered. Flore, mollified, prepared breakfast. Rouget thanked Max, who counseled him to keep his will while being polite to his sister.After breakfast Max and Flore discussed finances and plans to secure Rouget’s wealth while handling the incoming family. Flore admired Max’s cleverness; they plotted so the Hochons would help deal with the Bridaus.The Hochon household, opposite Rouget’s, was similarly modest. Monsieur Hochon, a retired tax collector, was famed for extreme frugality. His wife, Madame Hochon, resolved to properly receive her goddaughter Agathe and prepared the house despite her husband’s reluctance, borrowing furniture and praying for Rouget’s awareness. Her granddaughter Adolphine secretly hoped to win Joseph Bridau’s affection.News of the Bridaus’ arrival split Issoudun: the cautious older bourgeoisie offered measured support, while the Knights of Idleness were vehemently loyal to Max. The Fario incident and the town’s divided sentiment underscored how fragile reputations and power were in Issoudun, and how a few calculated actions could shape the looming confrontation over inheritance and influence.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Agathe and Joseph arrived at the Messageries-Royales in Issoudun at three. Though tired, Madame Bridau felt revived by familiar sights and memories of youth. News spread quickly; Madame Hochon greeted Agathe warmly, treating her like a daughter after years of loss and monotony had made her cling to that affection.Agathe met the uninviting Monsieur Hochon and the rest of the family: the young men Baruch and François, and shy little Adolphine, who noticed Joseph. The miserly Hochon noted Joseph’s recent hospital stay and apparent hunger; the youths were intrigued by his looks. “This is my son, the painter; my good Joseph,” Agathe said with maternal pride. Joseph bluntly remarked on resembling his father, which Madame Hochon read as affection for Agathe. Baruch hurried off to spread the news, focusing on Joseph’s striking appearance.Townspeople soon gossiped, likening Joseph to a brigand and airing disdain for artists. Their curiosity about the Parisians grew; many planned to visit the Hochons that evening.Settled in modest attic rooms, Joseph contrasted his Parisian artistic life with the drab provincial existence. Dinner revealed the Hochons’ frugality: plain bouilli, hard eggs, and meager sides. Accustomed to better fare, Joseph found the meal disappointing but tried to adapt. Agathe learned her childhood home had been sold to the Borniches, and Madame Hochon braced for many visitors that night. Joseph observed the family’s strict ways and limits of hospitality.After dinner the women gathered in the salon. Agathe and Madame Hochon exchanged life stories: Agathe described her struggles and Madame Hochon offered hard-won wisdom about enduring trials. Their conversation was repeatedly interrupted by a stream of callers eager to meet the Parisians. Joseph remained detached, studying visitors with a critical eye that made them uneasy. Later, as the house quieted, Agathe and Madame Hochon resumed their heartfelt exchange, reflecting on burdens they carried.Elsewhere in town, the Knights of Idleness convened to plot mischief against the Parisians. Max, their leader, proposed a prank involving rats to sabotage the grain supply of a disliked merchant, Fario. The gang welcomed the scheme and sought a way to drive the Bridaus away without harming the Hochons.The next morning at breakfast Joseph glimpsed his brother Jean-Jacques Rouget, unrecognizable and disheveled, accompanied by Flore Brazier, a striking, conspicuous woman. Joseph’s artistic eye admired Flore’s beauty; Agathe feared her influence over Jean-Jacques. The family debated how to approach him; Joseph suggested he visit first to assess matters.During breakfast a letter arrived from Jean-Jacques inviting Joseph to dine with him. The Hochons received it with mixed feelings, suspecting Flore’s involvement and worrying over motives. Gritte later reported the Rouget household’s ostentation and Flore’s extravagant display; the Hochons realized they must dress suitably. Monsieur Hochon advised Joseph to be observant and to speak frankly with Jean-Jacques about his nephews, stressing the need to understand the situations with Max and Flore. With tensions simmering and rival schemes in motion, the stage was set for a complex family reunion promising confrontation and revelations.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At four o’clock Joseph crossed from the Rouget to the Hochon house, where Kouski announced him. The table was set in the large hall; Joseph greeted his uncle Rouget, Flore, and Maxence Gilet. He bantered lightly, admiring Flore and the house, and noted Max’s good looks, suspecting a triangular household.Baruch and Francois arrived and Flore suggested a walk before dinner. While she prepared, Joseph eagerly examined the house’s paintings, which Rouget said had come from the Descoings, bought during the Revolution. Joseph was ecstatic; Max showed him more upstairs. Flore urged Rouget to give the paintings to Joseph, arguing Rouget didn’t care for them.Rouget offered them to Joseph—without frames—and Flore proposed paying Joseph four thousand francs to make copies. Overwhelmed, Joseph accepted. The news of this arrangement spread through Issoudun and enhanced Flore’s standing. Dinner that night was splendid, with local dignitaries. Joseph, somewhat tipsy, went home at eleven; Rouget, having drunk heavily, was carried to bed. Max and Flore, alone, agreed it was better to befriend the Bridaus than antagonize them and planned to send the pictures to Joseph the next day.The next morning the paintings were propped against Rouget’s wall. Agathe, Joseph’s mother, visited Rouget to thank him, prompted by old Hochon, who feared the Bridaus’ influence. Hochon warned Agathe that Joseph had been fooled and urged her to cultivate Flore to gain access to Rouget. Agathe was warmly received; Rouget was ill from the previous night. Flore feigned servitude and Agathe hinted Rouget should marry her, but Flore insisted Rouget would remember her in his will.In the following days the Knights of Idleness—local troublemakers—released rats and mice into Fario’s grain store, causing havoc. The Bridaus’ hold on Rouget seemed secure: Flore claimed Rouget was too weak for walks and needed a carriage; they bought a shabby vehicle that drew town attention. Rouget signed a power of attorney granting Max authority over his investments, while Flore handled local transactions. Fario returned to find his store devastated and suspected Max’s involvement; he began gathering evidence against the Knights.Agathe believed Rouget had not made a will and intended to ask him directly, while Hochon urged her to stay longer and use religious influence to counter Max and Flore—advice Agathe reluctantly considered, uneasy about manipulating faith. Progress for the Bridaus was slow; Hochon stressed persistence.Meanwhile Joseph, sensing the paintings’ real value, sent them to Desroches in Paris for appraisal, convinced they were worth far more than the 4,000-franc copying job. Agathe warned him to keep their worth quiet, but word leaked. Searches for old artworks spread through Issoudun. Max regretted aspects of their scheme, now fearful of the Church’s influence over Rouget if the paintings’ value became public.Joseph received letters from Schinner and Desroches; Desroches advised keeping his mother in Issoudun, warned about Max, and suggested Rouget might already have a will—so the Bridaus’ inheritance depended on a larger struggle between Flore and the Church. Disturbed, Joseph decided to return to Paris. He said goodbye to Rouget, who seemed altered by drink and flattery. Max and Flore exchanged glances, interpreting Joseph’s departure as part of a bigger plan. Irritated by talk about the paintings’ worth and the manipulations around him, Joseph resolved to return the paintings to Rouget later and to rely on his own talent.The Hochon family observed Joseph’s agitation and speculated about Max and Flore’s intentions. Hochon concluded that they had known the paintings’ value all along and had manipulated Joseph; Agathe defended her son’s decision to give them up, but Hochon warned that returning the paintings might jeopardize their chance at any inheritance.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Between midnight and one, the Knights of Idleness fed the town’s dogs. Their expedition lasted until three, and after supper at Cognette’s, Max, turning from rue l’Avenier into Grande rue at half-past four, was stabbed by Fario, who lurked in a recess, fled toward Vilatte, and wiped the knife on his handkerchief. Fario then washed the handkerchief in the Rivière forcée and returned to his lodgings at Saint-Paterne, where his new watchman later found him in bed.Max fell, crying out and alarming Lousteau-Prangin and young Goddet, who ran up the street calling for help. The townspeople, used to false alarms, stayed in bed. When they reached Max he had fainted. He recognized Fario but, on regaining consciousness and seeing his wound was not fatal, decided to exploit the incident, saying, “I think I recognized that cursed painter!”Lousteau-Prangin hurried to his father, the judge. Max was carried home by Cognette, Goddet, and others. Goddet, suspicious of Max’s words, asked Gritte if Joseph Bridau was in bed; she said Joseph had left at half-past four, restless all night. The revelation shocked the group, and they entered Rouget’s house to find Max in a bloody shirt.Joseph had spent the night anxious about how the townspeople would see him, fearing they might think he had robbed his uncle. He longed to flee to Paris and confront Max; at dawn he walked to the poplar avenue toward Tivoli to steady his nerves.Max plotted revenge. After Goddet examined the wound and found it serious but not fatal, news of Max’s condition spread, drawing a crowd to the place Saint-Jean. Max whispered to Flore that they could use the incident to rid themselves of the Parisians: pretend he was dying and have Joseph arrested. Flore, feigning tears, spread the story that Joseph had attacked Max out of jealousy over paintings. The crowd, incited by her, demanded Joseph’s capture; the mob grew hostile.Monsieur Hochon, warned by Gritte, locked himself in, fearing for his safety and wealth. He learned Joseph had left early; Madame Hochon, anxious for their daughter Agathe, hoped she would sleep through the shock. Agathe, however, woke, learned of the accusations, and fainted at the sight of the gendarmes. Monsieur Hochon urged the magistrates to act rapidly. The lieutenant reported a large crowd demanding justice.Joseph was spotted returning home and was nearly seized by the mob. Gendarmes intervened and, despite his protests of innocence, took him to the Palais for his safety as the crowd threatened violence. At the Palais Joseph calmly insisted on his innocence and provided an alibi, recounting his morning’s movements. The magistrates were somewhat swayed by his composure and explanation, but kept him in custody while they verified his claims. Joseph wrote to his mother, reassuring her he would soon be free.Max, recovering, remembered a crucial detail: the attacker had worn list shoes, while Joseph had left in boots. He wrote to the magistrate urging Joseph’s release. As the investigation progressed, witnesses confirmed Joseph’s alibi and he was freed. He returned home to his mother’s warm relief; the ordeal strengthened their bond and Joseph felt grateful for the support.The townspeople celebrated the departure of the Parisians, seeing it as a victory. Max and Flore were pleased at having rid themselves of what they viewed as disruptive outsiders. Joseph and Agathe returned to Paris; Joseph’s spirits improved despite the turmoil, while Agathe fretted about her son Philippe, whose trial loomed. To escape stress, Joseph went to the Comte de Sérizy’s chateau to work.Philippe, punished for actions during the unrest, was sentenced to five years’ surveillance and exiled to Autun. Desroches intervened, securing a change of residence to Issoudun and urging Philippe to reclaim his inheritance from Uncle Rouget. Motivated to restore the family’s fortunes and confront Max and the Rabouilleuse, Philippe prepared to return to Issoudun and, after a brief dinner, departed to face the challenge.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################On All-Souls’ Day, November 2, Philippe Bridau registered at the Issoudun police commissary and was advised to stay on rue l’Avenier. News spread that a banished officer linked to the painter falsely accused in the affair had arrived, unsettling the town. Maxence Gilet, recently recovered, had converted all of Père Rouget’s mortgages into cash, alarming many. Monsieur Hochon consulted notary Monsieur Héron, who observed that Rouget’s heirs should thank him for preventing Gilet from profiting from Rouget’s income. Long resentful of Gilet and afraid of losing his property, Hochon resolved to protect the Bridaus.Gilet and Flore Brazier welcomed Philippe, confident they could manipulate Rouget to secure his funds. The following day Philippe presented himself to his uncle in tattered clothes. His disordered appearance revealed recent hardship; he made plain he would not beg from Rouget despite past grievances. Flore urged that he should not remain on a meager salary while his uncle had wealth, but Philippe refused charity, his blunt pride making a strong impression on both her and Gilet. Gilet detected a latent threat though he avoided direct confrontation.Philippe then visited the Hochons, meeting curiosity and fear—Madame Hochon in particular linked him to past tragedies. He spoke candidly about living on limited means, winning some household members with charm and honesty. Discreetly asking Monsieur Hochon for guidance on Issoudun, Philippe requested secrecy; Hochon supplied names of officers hostile to Gilet—Mignonnet and Carpentier. Befriending them, Philippe gained respect by revealing knowledge of the recent conspiracy against the Bourbons, and through strategic behavior integrated into the local military circle while concealing his true aims.He dined regularly with Mignonnet and Carpentier, cultivated a respectable image among townspeople, took fencing lessons and practiced shooting, maintaining a façade of propriety. His disdain for Gilet was clear, yet Gilet remained cautious.Philippe’s inquiries led him to Fario, who supplied information about the Knights of Idleness and their schemes. Philippe began to suspect that Gilet and Flore planned to secure Rouget’s income and flee. To counter this, he insisted Rouget take a walk with him—an effort to loosen Flore’s influence—and later met Hochon to reveal that Gilet sought a power of attorney to access Rouget’s funds; they agreed to monitor Rouget’s movements.Meanwhile Flore and Gilet intensified plotting: Gilet proposed they leave Issoudun for Vatan to secure the money and marry; Flore, fearful of Philippe’s influence, reluctantly agreed. Tensions rose as Philippe’s resolve to protect his family and restore their honor hardened, setting the stage for a dramatic town showdown.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Philippe walked arm in arm with his uncle Rouget along the boulevard Baron, he thought about the looming confrontation between Colonel Bridau and Captain Maxence Gilet—one the town hoped would settle the struggle over Rouget’s fortune. Monsieur Hochon watched with interest as Philippe outlined his ruthless plan to regain control of Flore, the Rabouilleuse.Philippe told Rouget bluntly that Flore loved Maxence and wanted Rouget out of the way so she could marry him. Though Rouget still loved her, Philippe insisted he could make her obedient again as she once had been—by killing Maxence if Rouget would only refuse to sign the power of attorney they demanded before December third. That document, Philippe warned, would let them sell Rouget’s assets and flee to Paris. Rouget recoiled at the idea of Philippe taking Maxence’s place with Flore, saying it would break his heart; Philippe answered that fear and force were the only ways to handle women.They ran into Mignonnet and Carpentier, whom Philippe instructed to arrange the banquet seating so he could force a public confrontation with Gilet. They agreed to help but warned of Gilet’s skill as a duelist. Philippe reassured his uncle that after a proper mourning, he and Flore would soon be together.Back home Rouget found Kouski in tears: Flore had left with Vedie. Rouget’s frantic search failed. Gilet, meanwhile, sensed growing hostility for his conduct and felt the pressure to act before the duel. Captain Potel pressed him to face Bridau, but Gilet remained focused on securing Rouget’s fortune first. Maxence plotted to transfer Rouget’s assets to Flore to keep them from Philippe, believing the power of attorney would let him control events.Philippe, aware of these moves, prepared to counter them. He suspected Gilet would try a reconciliation with Flore and wanted to prevent it. He planned to use Baruch to secure the power of attorney on Rouget’s behalf while distracting or keeping Gilet at bay, determined to protect his uncle’s interests and return Flore to Rouget.When Rouget returned he began writing a letter threatening to revoke his will in Flore’s favor unless she came back; Philippe encouraged this, thinking it would force her return. Town gossip swelled about the impending duel and its stakes—the future of Rouget’s fortune and the contending ambitions of Philippe and Gilet.When Flore did return, Philippe asserted control, warning her of the consequences if she did not comply. Shaken, she understood how precarious her position was; Rouget, emotionally fragile, clung to her with hope. By morning the whole town speculated on the duel. Philippe, confident in his arrangements for the banquet confrontation, believed the outcome would decide Rouget’s fate and his own.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Around four in the afternoon the next day, officers of the old army gathered at the place du Marché in Issoudun, waiting for Colonel Philippe Bridau to attend a coronation banquet. Conversation turned to Max’s recent dismissal from old Rouget’s house; only Potel and Renard defended him. An old sub-lieutenant argued Max should not have been dismissed without a fight. When Max arrived he was coldly received, a sharp contrast to Philippe’s warm welcome, which stiffened Max’s resolve to confront him.At dinner Philippe toasted the Grand Army and was echoed by eleven soldiers, including Benjamin and Kouski. Mignonnet toasted those who had tried to restore the Emperor’s son, and Max, whose reputation lent weight to his words, rose with an ironic wish to meet again next year. Tension mounted as Philippe and Max exchanged barbs: Philippe kept calm, Max struggled to control himself. The meal ended tensely; the duel was set for the next morning with sabres, Goddet, a former army surgeon, attending.That night Monsieur and Madame Hochon urged Philippe to pray before the duel; Madame Hochon offered a relic, which he declined, though she vowed to pray for him. The next morning both men appeared composed, but Max felt intimidated by Philippe’s superior stance. When the duel began Max attacked but was quickly disarmed; Philippe paused to let him recover his weapon, increasing the strain. When they resumed they exchanged fierce blows; Philippe was wounded but dealt a fatal stroke. Max died, his ambitions and the Order of Idleness ended, much to the town’s relief.Flore Brazier, devastated, fell ill with fever and inflammation. Philippe, recovering from his own wounds, wrote letters outlining plans to secure his inheritance and manage Flore’s situation, noting her illness prevented her from fleeing and intending to use her to his advantage.By February 1823, urged by others and influenced by Philippe’s suggestions that marriage would secure her future, Flore began to consider marriage. Philippe manipulated events, presenting himself as supportive while arranging to benefit from the match; Flore, recovering, was persuaded to accept a marriage he orchestrated. The contract granted her a substantial dower and a life annuity. Agathe, Philippe’s mother, delighted in her son’s apparent reform and the town’s approval, but the family lawyer Desroches warned Agathe that Philippe’s cunning would likely prevent her and Joseph from inheriting Rouget’s property.Once married, Philippe asserted control over Flore and the household, demanding a power of attorney from Rouget and planning to use Flore to manage his uncle’s affairs while keeping her under close watch. Ambitious beyond Issoudun, he sought to reestablish himself in the army and secure a future in Paris. In a letter to the minister of war he professed loyalty to the new regime, enclosed local endorsements praising his conduct, and was ultimately reinstated as lieutenant-colonel.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lieutenant-Colonel Bridau (Philippe) returned to Paris with his aunt and the ailing Rouget, whom he took to the Treasury to transfer his uncle’s income to himself. The old man and the Rabouilleuse were soon swept into extravagant Parisian life; Rouget overindulged after a lavish supper attended by the ballet dancer Lolotte and died, the cause said to be indigestion.Madame Rouget prospered in Paris, though Philippe had Mariette watch her to prevent serious lapses. In October 1823 Philippe went to Issoudun to settle his uncle’s estate and quickly amassed a fortune of 1,600,000 francs, excluding valuable paintings held by Monsieur Hochon. He entrusted the money to Mongenod and Sons for investment.Philippe married, legally securing his wife’s fortune should she die childless. He bought her a furnished apartment and secretly acquired a grand hôtel on rue de Clichy, lavishing money on renovations and furnishings. The rise of Charles X benefited the Duc de Chaulieu’s family; because Philippe provided information about past conspiracies he was appointed lieutenant-colonel in the Duc de Maufrigneuse’s regiment and, through court connections, was ennobled as Comte de Brambourg.Ambitious and insatiable, Philippe sought further honors—an aide-de-camp post with the Dauphin and ultimately a general’s rank—distancing himself from old friends who might threaten his standing. He planned to marry Mademoiselle Amélie de Soulanges and acquire military distinctions to bolster his ascent.Meanwhile Joseph Bridau struggled as an artist, supported by his mother Agathe, who ran a lottery office. Despite talent, Joseph faced poverty and criticism while Philippe’s success overshadowed him. Agathe, deceived by appearances, came to accept Philippe’s rise as justification for his neglect.Desperate, Agathe wrote to Philippe asking for help for Joseph. His cold reply devastated her and hastened her decline. Near death, she sought consolation from her confessor and learned painfully of the contrast between her sons; realizing she had misplaced her affections deepened her sorrow, and she died soon after. Joseph buried her alone, heartbroken; Philippe was indifferent and later repelled by Joseph’s scathing letter.Joseph found solace among friends. Later he received a letter from Flore, Philippe’s wife, pleading for help in destitution. Joseph and Bixiou visited, arranged care, and uncovered Philippe’s neglect. They and friends plotted to expose his abandonment and secure justice for Flore. Philippe continued to rise socially until his fortunes collapsed during the July Revolution of 1830.Ultimately Joseph inherited Philippe’s estate and achieved success in art, becoming Comte de Brambourg; he remained humble, often reflecting on his family’s tragedy and the lessons of their tumultuous lives.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY CONTINUES HERE ########################################################################## MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Madame la Duchesse de Castries.CHAPTER IThe commercial traveller, a modern figure, signals the shift from material enterprise to intellectual force: his trade links local, often isolated power with universal influence, equalizing products and spreading metropolitan ideas into the provinces. He brings the capital’s light to the countryside, a self-taught scholar and actor who knows the world superficially, a bridge between village and metropolis. Skilled at mimicry and rapid judgment, he must read habits and solvency quickly.Travel taught him the haunts of vice and virtue across France; he persuades with polished speech, seizes opportunities, and outmaneuvers rivals. Abroad such agents often show truer diplomacy than ambassadors, negotiating commercial treaties in taverns rather than salons. The commercial traveller personifies civilization’s spirit, importing Parisian innovations into provincial life by sheer persuasiveness.One such traveller is Gaudissart: an acrobat in commerce, extracting wealth from the provinces with a torrent of phrases. His olive-green overcoat and striped shirt mark his trade. Persuasive, disarming, adept at courting attention, he made his fortune in hats, enchanting vendors and winning social favor. Jovial and festive but businesslike when needed, he adapts his diplomacy to each audience.Until 1830 Gaudissart stuck to hats; the upheaval that year turned ideas into commodities. Writers and thinkers began selling thought and speculators followed. Gaudissart seized the change, taking a commission on life-insurance policies and quickly learning the business. His employers praised him; newspapers sought him to sell subscriptions. Confident in his talents, he agreed to market both life insurance and periodicals.CHAPTER IIAfter a week steeped in Saint-Simonism and financial talk, Gaudissart prepared to travel in mid-April. Two hat houses tempted him back with good commissions, but he set off, joking with Jenny, a florist, about refurnishing her room from his travels.
