She doubted such promises, prompting playful banter about his prospects.Gaudissart assured her he would earn much more, aiming for a substantial income from insurance and subscriptions and measuring himself against successful figures in politics and commerce. The next morning he left for the towns assigned, stopped in Blois, and wrote to Jenny boasting of selling life insurance and, especially, the profitable "Children’s Journal."CHAPTER IIIArriving in Vouvray, Gaudissart expected his persuasive gifts to prevail but met a provincial mindset resistant to new ideas. The Tourangians were witty, satirical, and hostile to Saint-Simonism. At the Soleil d’Or inn he encountered Monsieur Vernier, skeptical of commercial travellers and aware of Gaudissart’s reputation. Vernier decided to humiliate him and sent Gaudissart to Margaritis, a local reputed lunatic.Margaritis, obsessed with his wine and newspapers, began a conversation that quickly turned absurd. Despite Gaudissart’s efforts to explain life insurance, the man’s eccentricities produced nonsensical replies. Gaudissart at first kept his composure, trying to make sense of the madman’s chatter and to sell his product.CHAPTER IVSeated at home with wine, Margaritis spoke delusions while two women listened amused and Vernier watched, intent on making a spectacle. The lunatic’s erratic behavior—his obsession with wine, newspapers, and peculiar views—thwarted Gaudissart’s reasoning. The talk devolved into misunderstandings and incoherent exchanges. Gaudissart persisted in his pitch, but the unpredictability of the encounter and the onlookers’ mockery made success impossible.CHAPTER VAfter prolonged banter, the innkeeper revealed Margaritis was indeed mad and Vernier had engineered the encounter. Enraged at the insult, Gaudissart demanded satisfaction. The townspeople enjoyed the scene; Vernier’s refusal to apologize escalated toward a duel until the innkeeper proposed mediation over breakfast. Ultimately they patched up the quarrel—recognizing its absurdity—and Gaudissart left Vouvray resolved to return to Paris and his usual work, reminded that commerce needs not only eloquence but also discernment about when persuasion is futile.######################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################To Monsieur le Comte Ferdinand de Gramont.MY DEAR FERDINAND,--If these lines endure in the world of literature, they will be a small token of gratitude for your efforts. You, the Hozier, the Cherin, the King-at-Arms of these Studies of Life, have contributed to the noble lineage of the “Human Comedy” with your heraldic designs. Your work, a complete manual of French Heraldry, reflects profound knowledge of the feudal spirit and the elegance of learned symbolism, which has greatly influenced my own.Your old friend,  DE BALZAC.On the skirts of Le Berry lies Sancerre, a town that charms travelers. Perched on a hill above the Loire flats, it benefits from fertile alluvium left by occasional floods, though some areas are ruined by sand. Its distance from the river allows the nearby port of Saint-Thibault to prosper, shipping wine and other goods.By the time this story begins, suspension bridges at Cosne and Saint-Thibault have replaced the ferries, signaling progress since the upheaval of 1830, when the House of Orleans invested in improvements.Sancerre’s steep, narrow streets, framed by the Great Ramparts, form its principal thoroughfares. The region’s chief industry is wine, producing vintages comparable to Burgundy and supplying Parisian wine shops. Below the town, villages like Fontenoy and Saint-Satur recall Neuchâtel’s picturesque vineyards.Though once of military consequence, Sancerre has declined as trade shifts to the Loire’s right bank. Its population, about 3,500, is eclipsed by Cosne’s 6,000, marking a reversal of fortune over fifty years. Still, the town retains historic charm, fine views, pure air, and a civic community that is friendly yet watchful—predominantly Calvinist but not rigorously Puritan.Sancerre boasts notable natives, including Horace Bianchon, a luminary in modern medicine, and Etienne Lousteau, a successful journalist. Politically, the municipality is dominated by a few large landowners who manipulate elections. A local plot to break their hold—motivated by vanity—collapsed through internal jealousy.A proposed remedy was to unite behind one champion for the next election, specifically one of the two Sancerre representatives in Paris. This was a progressive provincial idea, since parochial dignitaries have proliferated since 1830, making true statesmen rarer in the lower chamber. Though the scheme’s origins were dubious, it sprang from the borough’s Superior Woman, whose personal interests and history are key to understanding her ambitions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Around 1836 Sancerre was home to a notable figure known as the Superior Woman, long overlooked but now attracting local attention. At the same time two Sancerrois prospered in Paris: Etienne Lousteau, a writer, and Bianchon, a physician and professor. The influence of George Sand produced a fashion called “Sandism,” a sentimental tendency that brought many previously hidden superior women into public view.The Superior Woman of Sancerre lived at La Baudraye, a town-and-country house near Saint-Satur belonging to a diminished branch of a once-illustrious family. The story begins with a sheriff named Milaud, a former Calvinist who converted at the Revocation of the Edict of Nantes and was rewarded under Louis XIV; later measures against heretics brought misfortune. Under Louis XV a Milaud de la Baudraye advanced, securing a military commission for his son, who died in battle leaving a child later favored by Louis XVI.In 1800 an ex–farmer-general, returning from exile, bought La Baudraye out of vanity. His only son, Jean Athanase Polydore Milaud de la Baudraye, was frail—blamed on his father’s excesses—and raised devotedly by his noble mother until her death brought his father back to France. On his father’s death Polydore inherited the fief, some money, and a collection of autographs. He lived frugally in Sancerre’s healthy air, expanding vineyards from 1802 to 1815, but hesitated to press Parisian debts during the Restoration. After Napoleon’s fall he attempted to monetize his father’s collection, failed, and returned home.In 1823, at forty-three, Polydore sought a wife to secure an heir. He chose Dinah Piedefer, a beautiful, educated, intelligent woman from a once-Calvinist family fallen into poverty. Educated in a reputable school, Dinah hoped to rise socially and converted to Catholicism to attract a suitable husband. The Archbishop of Bourges, seeing her promise, tried to arrange a match, but families were wary of her beauty and intellect. When Polydore arrived in Bourges, he intervened—seeking the Cardinal’s support to recover debts—but his bluntness offended the Minister. Nevertheless he married Dinah, provoking local speculation about his motives.Polydore’s Paris financial dealings were fraught. He pressed debtors including the Duc de Navarreins until a government agent proposed a bargain: if Polydore forgave the Duc’s debts, he could obtain a tax-collector post. He accepted, briefly became a tax collector, then returned to Paris, secured lucrative appointments, and rose to Royal Commissioner. By 1827 he bought the estate of Anzy, raising his regional standing.Dinah transformed La Baudraye into a cultivated, pleasant residence—gardens, improved interiors, refined company—while keeping her husband’s financial maneuverings private. Her beauty, intellect, correspondence with Paris, and demand for conversation set her apart from provincial women, who resented and isolated her even as local men admired her. Madame Piedefer reveled in her daughter’s success, and Polydore in having a Superior Woman as a wife. Their story illustrates how societal perceptions can turn exceptional qualities into sources of both admiration and resentment, ultimately isolating those who exceed local norms.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1825 Dinah de la Baudraye faced accusations of socializing only with men and became a target of criticism. Trying to defend her reputation, she once made a rash remark to a false friend about her isolation—“I would rather have my bowl empty than with anything in it!”—which only backfired. Childless after five years of marriage, she invited further ridicule.Monsieur de la Baudraye was a frail man with thin legs who sometimes wore false calves and favored peculiar dress. Gentle and seldom contradicting Dinah, he let her dominate conversation. Dinah, admired for beauty and intelligence, drew local gentlemen from nearby towns. She relished the attention and grew adept at delivering prepared speeches based on wide reading across varied subjects.Her charm created a protective aura that shielded her from much censure; Monsieur often excused himself from her long expositions, preferring walks or business. As she established herself, she championed the romantic arts, collected antiques, and decorated their home with Renaissance treasures. Her husband supported these interests but concentrated chiefly on his land.Over the years she filled their house with a notable collection that impressed visitors and supplied material for her talks on art and history. She tried to form a literary circle in Sancerre, but it degenerated into social gatherings lacking literary focus—an evident disappointment for Dinah, who had hoped to elevate local culture. The circle became a venue for games and casual chat, much to her chagrin. Admirers, including local magistrates and a priest, were often diverted from intellectual pursuits; one young man who yawned during a lecture fell from her favor, demonstrating her strict expectations.When Dinah turned twenty in 1826 suitors still courted her. She remained devoted to her role as wife, sheltered by her mother and by the visible presence of admirers that kept slander at bay. Monsieur used his marriage’s leverage to secure favors and legal victories, while local women speculated about the relationship. In reality a slow, monotonous domestic drama played out at La Baudraye—one that might have remained private if not for the era’s appetite for exposing human relationships—and shaped Dinah’s seemingly virtuous early married life.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################A young woman whose academic successes sprang from pride was unlikely to halt her promising trajectory. Monsieur de la Baudraye, though seemingly frail, proved an unexpected match for Mademoiselle Dinah Piedefer. She nevertheless wondered why he married a seventeen-year-old and what she might gain.At first he surprised her: he let her use five acres for pleasure and financed house improvements so she could buy furniture and redecorate in varied styles. Dinah struggled to reconcile his reputation for stinginess with this apparent generosity, but the illusion dissolved after his second trip to Paris. When she asked for supplies she learned he strictly allotted a monthly allowance and paid her mother a yearly sum, stressing caution because their winegrower income was uncertain.Dinah’s attempts to provoke jealousy produced only indifference; he stayed steady and unyielding. Frustration grew as she felt trapped in La Baudraye and Sancerre, longing for a more ambitious life. She briefly believed he might share such ambitions when he bought the estate of Anzy and applied for a patent of entail; for a time she hoped for a brighter future. But their short reconciliation only revealed deeper contrasts: Dinah’s energy and potential clashed with Monsieur de la Baudraye’s lack of ambition and rigid routine.Over six years Dinah became increasingly provincial, losing individuality and spirit. Paris and the provinces diverged: Paris offered varied social life and opportunities, while the provinces confined women to a single, often uninspiring identity. Despite initial resolve, Dinah succumbed to mediocrity as scarce stimulating company and rural monotony dulled her aspirations.Her beauty and charm, once celebrated, faded as she adapted to local expectations. Her wardrobe shrank; she began making her own clothes and lost the elegance she once maintained. The provincial atmosphere stifled her creativity and intellect, and she adopted surrounding mannerisms, diminishing her distinctiveness.A pivotal meeting with her former school friend Anna, now a sophisticated Parisian, left Dinah feeling inadequate and despondent. In anguish she turned to poetry; her "Paquita la Sevillane" drew provincial attention and revealed her talent but provoked anxiety about exposure. Despite literary effort, Dinah remained trapped, oscillating between dreams of fame and the suffocating routine that bred lasting resentment.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################After the Revolution of 1830, George Sand’s fame inspired many French towns to celebrate local female talent, sparking a wave of aspiring women writers. Monsieur de Clagny capitalized on this trend by publishing a collection by "Jan Diaz," a poet who had died young. Two hundred copies were printed; many sold across the departments where the poet had lived and died, though some sent to Paris were lost in editorial offices. It later emerged that Jan Diaz was a pseudonym for a woman: Madame de la Baudraye—Dinah—who quickly became celebrated and even cast by some provincials as a rival to George Sand. Local praise, often polarized, elevated work that might otherwise have been overlooked.Dinah had long felt unrecognized and sought fame to fill unmet ambitions. The late Abbe Duret had warned her about her husband’s character, but she resisted. Over time she discovered him incapable of love and, after denying it at first, recognized his deep resentment and vengefulness: the Abbe’s warning that offending him would bring retaliation proved accurate. As Dinah’s reputation grew, her domestic life felt increasingly constraining; public sympathy for her plight deepened her despair. Following a confrontation she vowed to stop writing and withdrew into desolation at Sancerre, where her absence from the literary scene was keenly felt.Despite her fame, Dinah faced jealousy and opposition among local rivals. She had hoped to support Monsieur de Clagny’s political ambitions, but his electoral defeat left her disillusioned. These troubles masked a deeper dissatisfaction and intensified her longing for freedom from marriage.At the Chateau d’Anzy, Dinah contemplated her future and the possibility of meeting influential figures. She admired Lousteau, a journalist noted for his charm with women, and sought to attract him to Sancerre; efforts to engage another notable, Bianchon, at first met indifference. When Lousteau and Bianchon finally arrived—initially unrecognized—Dinah, eager to impress, invited them to her chateau and played the gracious hostess. In conversation she aired frustrations with provincial life: its monotony, its stifling limitation of creativity and individuality, and the centralization of culture in Paris. Her frankness about the narrow range of experiences in the provinces, especially for women, intrigued her guests, who appreciated her intelligence and wit.As the evening progressed, Dinah’s charm and vulnerability captivated Lousteau, who found her both fascinating and exasperating; their dynamic hinted at a possible deeper connection as she navigated attraction alongside ambition. In a lighter moment she asked for autographs—Bianchon and Lousteau obliged, leaving signatures in her album as a tangible link to the literary world and the recognition she craved.Dinah’s journey illustrates the struggle of a woman caught between societal expectations and the pursuit of personal fulfillment. Her interactions with Lousteau and Bianchon catalyze self-discovery as she grapples with identity as a writer and a woman in a restrictive provincial environment, revealing the tension between ambition and domesticity.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################At five the next morning, the household at the Chateau d’Anzy was stirring. Young Monsieur de la Baudraye had organized a day’s sport to display the twelve hundred acres he intended to reclaim—a costly project promising handsome profits—and the Parisians in his company were awake for the shoot. Gatien Boirouge asked Monsieur Gravier why the Public Prosecutor had not joined them; Gravier said the prosecutor had claimed court duty, but Gatien suspected otherwise—rumor had it Monsieur de Clagny had taken his place at the bench. Conversation turned into mockery and suspicion: Lousteau and Bianchon, two Parisian wits, derided provincial life and Gatien’s anxieties, teasing that the whole affair was merely vanity and gossip.At a copse Monsieur Gravier left the prominent men and Gatien with a keeper to pick a ravine path, while Bianchon and Lousteau lingered. They debated whether to wait for Gravier, but Gatien, convinced Gravier was already at breakfast with Madame de la Baudraye and Monsieur de Clagny, fretted: if they were together, might the two watch one another until an opportunity arose? He feared Clagny might be seductive: if he secured a high legal post, his faults might be hidden by eloquence; and since Madame de la Baudraye was ambitious for Paris, she might be taken by the idea. Lousteau and Bianchon treated such provincial intrigues with amused disdain, but Gatien insisted he could read the town’s women as well as anyone—there were too few of them for secrets to survive.As they planned, Lousteau proposed a prank: at dinner they would tell lurid tales of betrayed wives and vengeful husbands—scenes of burning or mutilation—to watch Dinah and de Clagny, thinking one of them might betray emotion. Bianchon agreed: theatrical stories might reveal an expression. Gatien favored entrapment; a Parisian editor’s wife once frightened her spouse with tales of lovers boiled alive, and simple theatrical shocks could unmask a relation. They resolved to keep stern faces and be diplomatic by day at the hunt, then provocative by night.Dinner found Lousteau in his element. He presented an album with melancholic verses signed by himself—an elegy that appeared freshly written and won Madame de la Baudraye’s sympathetic regard. Monsieur de Clagny, seeing her expression, reacted with thinly veiled dislike toward the affected poet, and took refuge in backgammon. Madame de la Baudraye, busy with embroidery, declared she stitched for her husband’s charity box. Lousteau taunted gently; she replied that her embroidery was for Monsieur de la Baudraye, and that if she ever married, she hoped her needlework would still be for him twelve years on. Wry jests and barbs about adultery and morality began to fly: the Public Prosecutor criticized Parisian writers for their easy treatment of illicit love; Lousteau defended literature by appealing to classic poets and symbol, provoking Clagny further.The conversation widened into paradoxes: if one judged morality by stern courts, history itself seemed to show authorities condemning pioneers (Socrates, Jesus) and persecuting what they cannot assimilate. Lousteau teased that many institutions—religion and state—are fragile in the face of new ideas. Clagny angrily called this blasphemy, and the company touched nerves about justice, passion, and revenge. Monsieur Gravier, half-joking, said he’d side with the betrayed husband if he were a writer; Lousteau mused on the sublimity of a trusting husband and questioned the balance between trust and jealousy. Madame de la Baudraye’s quiet remarks—sympathizing with victims who might have been tormented into crime—stirred unease: human justice was poor at judging the private tragedies behind closed doors.With the groundwork laid, Lousteau spun a story he’d heard from Charles Nodier: a young Chevalier imprisoned under suspicious circumstances, favored by the jailer’s wife but later subjected to a trap when the commandant grew jealous. The prisoner feigned escape and then, by wits and disguise, walked out past his captors. The tale, harmless in itself, failed to provoke any telltale reaction from Clagny or Dinah. Bianchon then told La Grande Bretèche—the Spanish tale of a grandee walled into a cupboard to die of starvation by a jealous husband—stunning the guests. Monsieur Gravier followed with a Spanish adventure involving a surgeon-major who, after an illicit delivery and a series of betrayals and murders, found himself killed by a husband holding a telltale arm. The cumulative effect was chilling; the company discussed the reality of such crimes in France, and Clagny reminded them that the Gazette des Tribunaux regularly recounted horrors comparable to romance.The Parisians—Lousteau, Bianchon, Etienne, the doctor, the tax receiver—watched Dinah and de Clagny closely. The guests hypothesized that if one of them were guilty of passion, these grim tales would elicit a reaction. The evening closed with the bachelors holding a council in the corridors: Gravier briefed on the aim to probe Dinah’s virtue; Lousteau warned that serenity can mask cunning. Gravier suggested a simple country trick: affixing hairs in wax on the doors of Madame de la Baudraye and the Public Prosecutor to detect nocturnal comings and goings. That night, the doctor, Lousteau, the receiver, and Gatien crept barefoot to press hairs into wax seals on the doors and agreed to inspect them at five.At dawn they found the seals unbroken—same wax, same hairs. Lousteau declared they had been chasing a will-o’-the-wisp and admitted the matter was more complex than they’d supposed. The discovery puzzled Gravier and Gatien, who exchanged baffled looks. Lousteau privately told Bianchon that if Dinah were truly virtuous she deserved the effort it required and compared the situation to storming a fortress. With a changed tone—less mockery and more admiration—Lousteau sought Dinah in the garden.Their conversation was half contrived: she had asked him to study provincial life at La Baudraye; he cut to the point, asserting that Gatien’s gossip had misrepresented him as adoring her and that her silence at dinner had been an unguarded indiscretion. He confessed he had plotted the evening’s stories to rouse some emotion and test both her and Clagny. He reassured her that her innocence had been proved—if she had been taken by Clagny, he would lose respect for her; he professed a taste for a certain perfection and disdain for cold, taciturn men. Dinah, embodying an ideal of superior womanhood, replied she believed Lousteau sought pleasure but not love, and that true love often comes late; she showed sympathy for his vulnerability, and for an instant the Parisian’s bravado softened.Lousteau’s frankness and the pair’s interchange delighted Dinah. He and Bianchon spent the day entertaining her with anecdotes and witty sayings—an intellectual feast that was to her the Ana of the century—while Lousteau flattered her by belittling a local celebrity and suggesting she could rival him. Flattered, Dinah warmed to the Parisians’ company; Clagny, Gravier, and Gatien looked on and perceived a more affectionate bearing from her.Word of the evening's promise had cascaded through the countryside; by sundown sixteen people had assembled at Anzy—coaches and hired horses bringing curious neighbors. Dinah prepared the drawing-room with the pride of a hostess staging a triumph. Lousteau, Bianchon, and Dinah exchanged knowing looks as the provincial notables arrived, resplendent in their ancestral finery, elaborately imaginary caps, and the usual local airs. The Presidente Boirouge sought medical advice from Bianchon (“English malady” by tea), and rumors circulated that Dinah had summoned him about why she bore no children—another spark for scandal-minded imaginations.A footman brought letters and a packet of proofs; Lousteau left a sheaf for Bianchon and, curious, opened a wrapping only to find an inferior romance used as packing: “Olympia, or Roman Revenge.” Spotting the comic potential, Lousteau read aloud fragments—brigands in caverns, Rinaldo and Lamberti, a Duchess’s glove, Cardinal Borborigano—then the page broke off. With Antoine-like mock erudition he teased the text and invented commentary: amateurish local color, a Duchesses’ guilt, a cavaliere, invented plots. He explained the printer's contrivances—waste sheets, single-sided proofs—and joked about literary mechanics and the author’s likely pretensions. Bianchon joined, both of them turning the fragments into a lively performance.The audience, from old grandees to curious young women, drank in the reading and the sardonic critique. Lousteau’s mock-critique and theatrical improvisation transformed a tattered romance into an amusing spectacle. Dinah, pleased by both the attention and the wit, enjoyed the interplay; the room buzzed with amusement, speculation, and the subtle electricity of social performance. Thus the evening, intended as both entertainment and a test of character, became a stage on which Parisian cleverness dazzled provincial complacency—while the mystery of the sealed doors remained, unresolved, for the men who had come to spy on hearts and reputations.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################For three days Dinah’s imagination was stirred by the talk of her two Parisian visitors. Lousteau watched her growing distraction, waiting for the moment she would be wholly captivated. Dinah wanted to show La Baudraye to her guests, and a charade began when Bianchon’s papers were left behind in Anzy. Gatien rode off to fetch them, while Madame Piedefer went shopping, leaving Dinah alone with Lousteau and Bianchon. Lousteau sat beside her; Bianchon rode backward. They mocked the local men and debated the unhappy circumstances of her life, leaving Dinah defensive.Crossing the Loire, Bianchon told her plainly she had shut herself against love but now needed it. Dinah, taken aback, asked if he prescribed love; he warned that her present way of living would make her unattractive. Lousteau half-jokingly defended him as offering good counsel.At Cosne a crowd gathered near the old carriage bearing Louis XIV’s arms. Dinah insisted they disembark; she took Bianchon’s arm and walked briskly along the Loire. Bianchon suggested Lousteau loved her but was frivolous and hard to hold; he advised befriending Lousteau, since the young man could bring beauty and happiness. Under Bianchon’s scrutiny Dinah felt vulnerable and uncertain about competing with Lousteau’s past lovers.Their coachman announced Gatien’s return. Bianchon blessed them as they entered town. Lousteau took Dinah’s hand and declared his love, but she resisted, asking if she should give herself to a man who regarded her as a passing pleasure. Gatien’s arrival complicated things: Lousteau whispered that she should pretend resistance to preserve his honor and, in a reckless moment, crushed her muslin dress, leaving her disheveled.Gatien, oblivious to the tension, rode up, and Lousteau tried to distract him. Dinah urged Gatien to fetch Bianchon’s papers from the inn; he complied reluctantly. Lousteau then urged them to hurry back to La Baudraye, claiming Dinah was unwell. Humiliated, Dinah retreated inside, overwhelmed, and Lousteau followed, begging forgiveness.Madame Piedefer appeared, anxious at Dinah’s distress. Hearing Gatien’s horse, Dinah fled to her room. Outside, Lousteau and Gatien confronted one another; Gatien accused trickery, which Lousteau shrugged off, suggesting Dinah had mocked Gatien. Dinah changed into a velvet gown; when she rejoined the men, Gatien awkwardly commented on her dress. Lousteau explained it as a response to the cool air; Dinah blushed and her mother glared. Conversation turned to Bianchon, whom Lousteau praised, and Dinah, weighed down by events, stayed silent.Intent on winning her, Lousteau declared he wished to stay in Sancerre to be near her and swore he had left Parisian vices behind. On the road back to Anzy his charm began to pierce Dinah’s reserve; she laughed, a sign her feelings were shifting. During dinner he was buoyant, sometimes lost in thought, savoring the happiness he imagined with Dinah. After coffee the women left the men to stroll in the gardens; Lousteau seized the moment, and the next day, though he soon had to return to Paris, they exchanged pledges. Dinah accompanied him to Cosne, disregarding proprieties, and back at La Baudraye praised him for showing her what true love felt like. Her three admirers, including Monsieur de Clagny, were left to reckon with their feelings; Dinah took Clagny as a confidant, lavishing affection while he remained devoted despite her new happiness.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Back in Paris, Lousteau quickly forgot the happy days at the Chateau d’Anzy. He lived by his pen in an age when writing—after the rise of the bourgeoisie—was almost servitude: constant production, or the appearance of it. He wrote daily for a newspaper and several literary magazines but lacked artistic integrity, ranking among Paris’s literary hacks. Deprived of literary pretensions and advancement, he had become a journalist.Still, his life was not unpleasant. He mixed with blue-stockings, aspiring actresses, publishers, and authors, enjoying a lifestyle that required little beyond paying rent. He kept theatre boxes, and sales of the books he reviewed covered minor expenses. His social life was busy, and his writing bought that ease. Yet after ten years he remained as financially constrained as when he arrived, still believing a monthly income of five hundred francs would make him rich.Lousteau lived in well-furnished ground-floor rooms in the Rue des Martyrs rented for twelve hundred francs. His finances peaked and dipped—January, April, July, and October were especially tight—because he spent heavily on cabs, meals, and gifts for lovers, leaving him perpetually broke despite earning seven to eight hundred francs a month.Occasionally weary of this literary life, he would joke with friends like Nathan and Bixiou about marriage and romance. He had a mistress, Madame Schontz, proud to have a writer as her lover; she lived nearby and liked teasing friends about their relationships. This bohemian existence would later affect Dinah: he could laugh about his entanglements, including Dinah, whom he had almost forgotten.When Bixiou asked about Sancerre, Lousteau recounted three timid provincial men who’d failed to pursue a local beauty. He received a long, affectionate letter from Dinah, which he read and burned, dismissing her feelings; a week later another arrived and he discarded it unread. Madame Schontz noticed the scented paper, opened hampers Dinah had sent—game and flowers—and suggested they host a dinner. Lousteau sent a clever but insincere letter he believed would charm Dinah. By December her letters were piling unread in a drawer.Then Madame Schontz told him of a marriage opportunity. A young woman had fallen into trouble; her previous lover had died, and her father, a notary, wanted to marry her quickly, offering a substantial dowry. The notary sought a respectable man to restore the daughter’s position. Madame Schontz urged Lousteau to consider it, stressing the financial benefits and a comfortable life, while warning about a strict mother-in-law who would scrutinize his past. She insisted marriage would free him and secure him.Hesitant at first, Lousteau agreed to meet the notary and family. He flattered the notary, recalling his own father and presenting himself as ambitious. The notary liked him, and Lousteau was introduced to the Cardot family as a prospective son-in-law. At his first dinner the atmosphere seemed dull, but he noticed Félicie, the notary’s daughter: shy, affectionate, grateful that he would marry her. Madame Cardot regarded him as a man with promise and proper morals.Buoyed by this reception, Lousteau felt a new purpose. He imagined stability—a charming wife, respectable in-laws, and the financial security the marriage promised—and believed this new chapter would open greater ambitions and opportunities.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Cardot, having borrowed the illustrated Gil Blas to inspect the prints, went unexpectedly with her daughter Félicie to Lousteau’s bachelor rooms to learn about her future son-in-law. Her sister-in-law Madame Camusot had already been uneasy about the match after hearing unflattering reports from her husband, a Supreme Court Councillor. On a January day in 1837 they took a cab; the porter was away, but his daughter handed them the key after Madame Cardot slipped her a gold coin.Around noon Lousteau, returning from breakfast, was startled to see Dinah, his former lover, arrive in a coach and reveal she was a mother. Overwhelmed, he tried to dismiss her, but she insisted on staying. The child, Pamela, sensed the awkwardness and announced that his mother-in-law was inside; Lousteau found Madame Cardot and Félicie hiding. Dinah declared her love; Lousteau, fearing complications, nonetheless began to imagine using her to escape the arranged marriage he faced.Madame Cardot openly disapproved and left with Félicie, while Dinah, trusting Lousteau’s reassurances and high-mindedness, remained. She was charmed by the apparent elegance of his rooms, not noticing their underlying poverty. Pamela borrowed dinnerware from Madame Schontz, who learned of Lousteau’s predicament and warned him that Cardot would not pursue the match. Lousteau, however, focused on his new life with Dinah, enjoying their intimacy and the prospect of avoiding a wealthy marriage.When his friend Bixiou arrived, he challenged Lousteau’s choice, arguing that provincial women often fail to adapt to Paris life and that marriage was meant to secure legitimacy and stability. Lousteau dismissed these cautions, insisting he preferred Dinah to any fortune. Nevertheless, Madame Schontz later told him Cardot had arranged Félicie’s marriage to another man, extinguishing his prospects of a respectable alliance.Having lost a stable future, Lousteau grew more entangled with Dinah, who believed in their love and became dependent on him. They settled into a domestic life where Lousteau kept her entertained and dependent; the passionate affair gradually devolved into the very provincial romance he had once mocked. In the end both were caught in a cycle of dependency and illusion, unprepared for the hardships their unconventional union would bring.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This winter in Paris did for Madame de la Baudraye what October had been in Sancerre. Etienne, eager to introduce Dinah to Parisian life, took her to the theater, where she at first felt self-conscious and feared recognition from acquaintances. Lousteau, vain and ambitious, stripped away her provincial modesty by taking her to fashionable dressmakers and introducing her to society. Dinah quickly adapted to the city's vibrant life, immersing herself in its culture and entertainments.A newspaper item about Monsieur le Baron de Clagny’s promotion recalled Sancerre to Dinah, and she teased Lousteau about lingering attachments. Their passion remained fresh: they exchanged extravagant vows and promises of devotion, even as Lousteau privately entertained the idea of deceiving her when he grew tired, seeing her as a useful asset.Dinah, however, preserved dignity and ambition. More than a charming companion, she was intelligent and well read, able to hold her own among sophisticated women. Lousteau recognized her worth, imagining her as both muse and partner.When Dinah showed she was pregnant, she prepared to withdraw from public life but first attended the premiere of a play by Nathan with mixed excitement and anxiety. The evening soured when she sat beside Anna Grossetete, an old acquaintance, and Lousteau abandoned her to mingle. Exposed to scrutiny, Dinah felt humiliated and uneasy. Clagny visited at the theater to warn about the harm to her reputation from her liaison with Lousteau. Dinah remained resolute about her happiness and the sacrifices she had made for love, though Clagny’s visit provoked nostalgia and regret.After the child’s birth, Dinah devoted herself to motherhood and running the household, determined to support Lousteau’s career. She pawned jewelry to help with finances, believing her sacrifices would secure a better life. Lousteau’s financial irresponsibility and laziness soon became clear; he failed to write steadily and accumulated debts, leaving Dinah to bear the burden.Her love drove her to sustain him, but as she worked tirelessly, Lousteau grew indifferent and dependent. A second child increased her responsibilities while his debts and detachment deepened. Seeking comfort with friends and other women, he withdrew from domestic duties. Dinah’s sacrifices began to feel one-sided; she questioned whether their relationship could endure.Tensions rose, with Dinah’s jealousy and insecurity surfacing. Attempts to confront Lousteau about his behavior and their finances often provoked conflict. She struggled to maintain dignity while managing the complexities of their love.The turning point came when her estranged husband, Monsieur de la Baudraye, reappeared seeking a power of attorney to manage an inheritance. Forced to face her past, Dinah was aided by Clagny—now Attorney-General—who negotiated an allowance from her husband in exchange for her cooperation, protecting her interests.Confronting betrayal and loss, Dinah reached a crossroads. Lousteau’s lack of ambition and commitment made her realize her sacrifices might not be returned. The refuge of love had become a burden. Facing motherhood and the consequences of her choices, Dinah emerged determined to reclaim her identity and seek a future defined by mutual respect and commitment rather than mere passion.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################From that day on Etienne lived in luxury, and Dinah could hold her own among Paris’s best-dressed women. Lousteau, however, pretended ruin among his friends, blaming Madame de la Baudraye and declaring himself helpless before Dinah’s supposed desperate love; yet he remained unfaithful. Monsieur de Clagny, distressed by Dinah’s disgrace, warned her of betrayal; she answered, “I know it.” When Clagny asked if she still loved Lousteau, she cried, “I would lose my soul for you!”—a confession that left him speechless and in tears.Dinah slowly saw Lousteau’s true nature. She described him to her mother as a poet: lazy, pleasure-seeking, but someone she could not hate because she felt responsible, believing she was his only support. Her mother lamented the hell she endured; Dinah replied, “I will be a mother to him!” She made compromises to live with her shame, feeling its weight mainly when confronted by others, and resolved to emulate Madame de Pompadour to keep Lousteau’s affection. His insincerity and staged devotion further degraded her dignity, yet she clung to hope that his love might return and took a twisted comfort in being his plaything.She often rehearsed the tragedy Le Dernier Jour d’un condamné, imagining their separation, but every word or gesture from Lousteau pulled her back. She even contemplated suicide while walking in her garden, but her devotion endured. Guided by the romance of Adolphe, she vowed not to become like Ellenore—refusing to display pain in tears or reproaches.Meanwhile Monsieur de la Baudraye tried to restore his fortune by manipulating his wife’s inheritance—reducing its apparent value and pocketing the difference—and investing in land for returns. He convinced himself he owed Dinah nothing for now and planned to restore her fortune later. Ambitious, he imagined a noble life in Sancerre, seeking a title and social prominence.By early 1842 Dinah felt like Lousteau’s reserve, sacrificing herself for his comfort. She dressed in mourning as a sign of remorse; her mother worried and sought to intervene. The reality of their situation proved far from literary drama: it was marked by fatigue and disillusionment.With Clagny’s help, Madame Piedefer urged Dinah to consider life apart from Lousteau, suggesting her husband might allow her to keep the children and live in Paris. Dinah let Clagny conduct negotiations; he assured her her husband would support a respectable return to society.Unknown to Dinah, Monsieur de la Baudraye had been investing in a luxurious Paris residence to polish his image, aiming for a peerage and a well-furnished home. He wrote to Dinah inviting her to live with him in Paris and promising financial support for their children’s education.Now a Countess, Dinah felt the weight of title and expectation, preparing for social prominence that contrasted with earlier struggles. In May 1842 she paid off her debts and invited Lousteau to dinner—an event signaling a decisive change. When he arrived, he was struck by her transformation. Dinah told him of her new status and declared their separation: she still cared but insisted on a calm parting. Lousteau, feeling betrayed, accused her of ruining his life; she stood firm, refusing blame. Their final exchange mixed sorrow and defiance. Dinah left both liberated and heartbroken.In her new life she transformed her home into a model of comfort, spending more than her husband expected. Monsieur de la Baudraye, back in Paris, admired her efforts but remained unaware of the emotional turmoil beneath her composed exterior. Though charming and fashionable, Dinah felt a void, missing the passion and excitement of the past. She struggled to connect with other women; Clagny tried to introduce her to influential figures, but she remained unfulfilled.After a theater evening Dinah confided in Clagny about lingering feelings; he then revealed Lousteau was living with another woman. This news provoked conflicting emotions and pushed her to immerse herself in society, seeking success and recognition to fill the gap.In March 1843 Dinah was appointed to collect donations for a charitable cause—a turning point for her public image. Her efforts were recognized and she regained social standing, though emotional scars remained and she often reflected on her choices. Summers found her in Sancerre, where her husband doted on their children; in June 1844 he proudly presented them to a cousin, showcasing the family’s new status.Dinah maintained composure through these shifts, balancing roles as mother, Countess, and a woman shaped by sacrifice. Yet she continued to be torn between love and ambition, public success and private longing—an unresolved tension that defines her journey.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Across France there were many Chevaliers de Valois; one in Alençon is the subject of this tale. Though sharing the name, these chevaliers mostly did not know one another; they accepted the Bourbon monarchy, acknowledging Henri IV’s succession because the Valois line had no male heir. The Alençon chevalier, like the others, was an elderly gentleman, tall and thin, whose youth in Paris and romantic pursuits had been interrupted by the Revolution.Well regarded among the local aristocracy, he was known for manners, wit, and anecdotes from Louis XV’s reign. A regular at social gatherings, his charm and discretion made him a favorite. At fifty-eight he claimed to be fifty, preserving a youthful appearance despite age. He had a prominent nose and a pale complexion that reddened on one side—his excuse for such flush, and for his voracious appetite, was a “hot liver.”Though seemingly frail, the chevalier remained vigorous and passionate; his face bore marks of a life fully lived. He groomed himself carefully, projecting an elegance that belied his years. His clothes were worn but always clean, reflecting his commitment to aristocratic standards. Living modestly in a rented room above Madame Lardot’s laundry, he dined out daily and subsisted on a small annuity. Beloved in Alençon for his card-playing and amiability, he was a master of conversation who engaged all ranks and whose presence was sought at events. Despite his sociability, curiosity surrounded his private life, including persistent rumors of a secret marriage to a woman named Césarine. Financially precarious, he nevertheless maintained dignity and the reputation of a gentleman by navigating provincial life with poise.One spring morning the chevalier was interrupted by Suzanne, a young girl from Madame Lardot’s laundry. She brought news that might bring both pleasure and pain, though she feigned indifference to his rumored link with Césarine. Ambitious and clever, Suzanne aspired to leave Alençon for Paris, convinced she could better herself there. The chevalier, struck by her charm and drive, encouraged her with a paternal tone and advised courting Monsieur du Bousquier, a wealthy bachelor who had shown interest. He understood the social mechanics by which beauty and wit might secure a favorable match.Du Bousquier, in contrast, was a man of dubious repute: once wealthy and influential, his past as a purveyor during the Revolution had tarnished him. Now back in Alençon, he hoped to marry Mademoiselle Cormon, a rich old maid, to restore his fortunes. His brusque opportunism contrasts with the chevalier’s elegances; their rivalry over Mademoiselle Cormon is subtle but intense. As the story unfolds, Suzanne’s ambitions and the machinations of both men intertwine, revealing an intricate dance of desire, ambition, and societal expectation in provincial life, and reflecting broader themes of class, gender, and the pursuit of happiness in a changing world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Madame Granson, a widow on a modest pension and modest property, struggled to support her son Athanase, who added a small salary from the mayor’s office. Their provincial home was simple and poor, shaping their lives and morals. Athanase, a pale, introspective twenty-three-year-old, secretly loved his cousin Mademoiselle Cormon, whom he believed to be wealthy. His passion was complicated by social expectations and the potential ridicule of loving an older woman.What began as a practical hope to secure a better life for himself and his mother became genuine affection. He admired Mademoiselle Cormon from afar, drawn to her yet constrained by his circumstances; his talent and potential went unnoticed in a setting where ambition often collided with harsh realities.Madame Granson, aware of his feelings, perceived the advantages of a match despite Athanase’s lack of fortune and the cousin’s age. She remained hopeful and patient, waiting for the right moment to act, while Athanase’s thoughts were consumed by fantasies of a future together despite seemingly insurmountable barriers.The arrival of Suzanne, a young woman from a poorer background, changed the dynamic. She felt an immediate connection to Athanase, sensing his struggles and aspirations, but her own ambitions led her toward deception as a way to escape poverty. After a brief meeting with Madame Granson, Suzanne left town with money and new clothes; the community lamented her departure.Preparing for a dinner with Mademoiselle Cormon, Athanase was urged by his mother to present himself well, unaware of her full plans. The evening promised to be pivotal as the fates of Athanase, Mademoiselle Cormon, and Suzanne began to unfold, each driven by desire and social pressure.Suzanne’s departure for Paris marked a turning point as she sought to redefine her life away from constraints. Athanase remained in Alençon, facing unfulfilled dreams and the burden of his love, their lives continuing to intertwine within the pressures of provincial society.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In many second-class French prefectures there exists a salon that gathers influential—but not elite—members of the community. Its hosts are locally prominent and invited to events, yet they do not mix with the highest echelons, who keep their distance and communicate by formal invitation.This salon, where lesser nobility, clergy, and magistrates convene, wields considerable local influence. Its members are indifferent to luxury and fashion, concerned with practical matters such as land acquisition. They resist outside influences—particularly from Paris—and their gossip often assumes the weight of judgment. Young women seldom appear; when they do it is usually to seek validation.In Alençon the upper classes gathered at the house of an old maid, Mademoiselle Rose-Marie-Victoire Cormon, whose fortune was coveted by relatives and two old bachelors. She lived with her uncle, a former grand-vicar; her family had long served the nobility and, through marriages with noble families, acquired a semblance of aristocracy despite essentially middle-class origins.Mademoiselle Cormon’s house, dating from Henri IV, provoked envy by its prime location and careful upkeep. A courtyard with a flowerbed signaled her meticulousness and insistence on cleanliness; the mansion’s robust architecture and decorative elements recalled its historical standing. Inside, the rooms were arranged for reception: a salon hung with family portraits and a dining room exuding provincial charm. The salon was a social hub—card games and local conversations took place there. Her circle numbered about 150 people, mostly mature, rarely traveling and long rooted in provincial life.Wednesdays were special: she hosted dinners that drew a larger assemblage. The women dressed modestly and the atmosphere was familiar and routine. Conversations centered on local gossip, land disputes, and judgments about aristocratic behavior, reflecting the community’s concerns and values.Despite her wealth and standing, Mademoiselle Cormon remained unmarried. She wished to marry a nobleman, but political upheaval from 1788 to 1815 made suitable matches scarce: many eligible men were too old or too young, or lacked the qualities she sought. Fear of being married for her fortune led her to set subtle tests for suitors to expose mercenary intentions.As she aged, her longing for love intensified, yet she failed to recognize some genuine affections, including that of Athanase Granson. Her innocence and naivety caused missed opportunities; over time her character stiffened and the weight of social expectation pressed on her. Though pious and virtuous, she became a figure of local ridicule—her unmarried condition turned into a proverb. She lived modestly, shunning ostentation, but her fortune did not spare her from scrutiny. The occasional return of disbanded soldiers offered hope, but none met her standards. Her yearning for companionship and motherhood turned into despair as she confronted the realities of her situation.Mademoiselle Cormon’s story illuminates the complexities of love and social expectation, and the particular challenges faced by women whose lives were constrained by rigid class conventions.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################To complete the picture of Mademoiselle Cormon’s household, we must include her and the Abbé de Sponde and their loyal servants: Jacquelin, Josette, and Mariette, who ensured the comfort of uncle and niece.Jacquelin, a short, stout man with a ruddy complexion, had served twenty-two years, doing everything from waiting at table to grooming the family mare Pénélope. He was in love with Josette, a thirty-year-old maid whom Mademoiselle would dismiss if she married him; the couple quietly saved their wages, hoping Mademoiselle’s marriage would free them to wed. Josette, plump and practical, was said to rule her mistress. Mariette, the cook, had been with the family fifteen years and prepared the local delicacies.Pénélope, the old brown-bay mare, attracted affection and conversation: childless, Mademoiselle Cormon directed maternal instincts toward the animal. The servants and the mare maintained a routine reflecting long-standing roles, while Mademoiselle grew increasingly difficult to please. Her idle mind fixed on trivial matters—household linens, the timing of Pénélope’s meals—turning minor disputes into a sense of purpose. Her life revolved around the household, her uncle, and religious duties, which magnified everyday minutiae. Concerned about her health, she obsessed over small ailments and diet.Each season she visited her estate at Prébaudet to oversee apple trees and prepare for harvest, marking the occasion with a farewell dinner that was a notable Alençon event. On the day of her departure the town buzzed with gossip about suitors, especially M. du Bousquier, whom she had rejected. The farewell dinner was full of social maneuvering; Mademoiselle tried to engage guests but often struggled to find the right words, her lack of wit amusing sharper guests.As the evening turned to local politics and du Bousquier’s rumored involvement with a young woman named Suzanne, Mademoiselle’s attempted defense of him produced a remark that unexpectedly drew laughter and admiration, showing she could contribute to the evening’s entertainment, if unintentionally. The dinner closed with banter about du Bousquier’s character, revealing a town divided. Her innocence and naivety were plain as she navigated social complexities; the evening left her contemplating her role, desires, and the evolving ties of love, ambition, and expectation in Alençon.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The next day Mademoiselle Cormon, packed into her old carriole with Josette, set off for Prebaudet, not knowing an event would hasten her marriage. The morning after her arrival, while breakfasting, Jacquelin delivered a letter from her uncle, the Abbé de Sponde: Monsieur de Troisville, a grandson of one of the abbé’s friends, wished to stay in Alençon and needed hospitality. Alarmed by the urgency, she returned to town immediately to help her uncle receive the guest.Despite the rain she insisted on a quick departure, driven by excitement and the prospect of marriage. Their arrival provoked town gossip; Josette and Jacquelin managed the household while Mademoiselle Cormon prepared for the viscount. News of his impending arrival spread, and she fretted about her home’s old-fashioned decor and her appearance, even sacrificing prized liqueurs to make a better impression.When Monsieur de Troisville arrived he was greeted by the abbé and Mademoiselle Cormon. He looked distinguished and she felt an immediate attraction. Their dinner conversation flowed: the viscount praised her home and the region, making her feel noticed and desirable.The evening changed when the Chevalier de Valois and other curious guests arrived. During introductions the viscount casually mentioned he was married, devastating Mademoiselle Cormon, who fainted. Du Bousquier, a local suitor, caught her and carried her to her room while townspeople watched and speculated.As she recovered she realized her hopes for marriage were dashed. News of her fainting and the viscount’s marital status swept Alençon, met with laughter and sympathy. Feeling the need to restore her standing, and with both Du Bousquier and the Chevalier seeking her favor, she accepted Du Bousquier’s proposal on the condition that he agree to a public falsehood about their engagement, which he did. As they prepared to announce it the Chevalier arrived, unaware of the plan. The evening closed with Mademoiselle Cormon feeling a mix of triumph and uncertainty as the town buzzed about her engagement, marking a new chapter.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The news of Mademoiselle Cormon’s marriage devastated Athanase Granson, though he concealed his turmoil. He first heard it at Chief Justice du Ronceret’s house while his mother played cards. Athanase had long pinned his hopes on Mademoiselle Cormon; the announcement felt catastrophic. Shaped by an Imperial education that bred fatalism, he drifted into indifference and despair. His mother noticed his pallor but assumed he was merely preoccupied with work.After the marriage was announced, Athanase wandered the picturesque Sarthe region, finding a bitter solace in its overlooked beauty. He withdrew from politics and society, becoming a figure of little interest to townspeople who were unaware of the depth of his suffering. His mother worried and speculated he might be contemplating a major project, not grasping the real cause of his withdrawal.Suzanne, inspired by romantic tales and adventure, returned to Alençon intending to help Athanase financially, though they never crossed paths. The town buzzed with gossip about Mademoiselle Cormon’s match, with conflicting reports about its legitimacy. Eventually the marriage contract was signed and the wedding took place amid ominous signs—a priest accidentally read from the De Profundis—setting an uneasy tone.The marriage of Mademoiselle Cormon and du Bousquier lacked celebration; the couple soon retreated to Prebaudet. Du Bousquier, intent on modernizing the Cormon estate, began renovations that alarmed locals who feared losing their provincial charm. Athanase, consumed by grief, ultimately took his own life, leaving a letter his mother misread as evidence of newfound independence rather than the despair it expressed.Athanase’s suicide shocked Alençon. Madame Granson was left to mourn privately, grappling with the full weight of her loss that others barely comprehended. The Chevalier de Valois revealed Athanase’s unrequited love for Mademoiselle Cormon, deepening the town’s sense of tragedy. Madame Granson’s grief was profound; she alone understood the true nature of her son’s struggles and bore them in silence.Madame du Bousquier returned to Alençon and encountered Madame Granson in a meeting heavy with unspoken words. Madame Granson sought solace in the church and implored the rector for a discreet burial to avoid scandal; the ceremony was conducted quietly, reflecting the community’s desire to contain the episode.As time passed, Madame du Bousquier’s marriage became both gossip and cautionary tale. Though initially appearing content, she found herself powerless and unfulfilled. Du Bousquier, outwardly successful and politically ambitious, proved a tyrant at home. The marriage brought social advantage but no children, and her longing for motherhood deepened her pain.The Chevalier de Valois, embittered by the match, sought revenge against du Bousquier, whose conduct made him a figure of ridicule. Public mockery of Mademoiselle Cormon’s misfortune gave way to sympathy for Madame du Bousquier as the true hardships of her life became known. The chevalier’s schemes only widened the rift between du Bousquier and the local aristocracy, intensifying animosity rather than restoring honor.Madame du Bousquier lived amid contradictions: she performed the duties of a devoted wife while inwardly resenting her husband’s oppression. She clung to duty even as she mourned the life she might have had. The Chevalier’s death, coinciding with sweeping political changes in France, marked an end of an era. Suzanne, honoring the past, purchased a small memento from the chevalier’s estate—a symbol of lost connections and vanished possibilities. Ultimately, Madame du Bousquier’s life illustrates the complexities of love, duty, and social expectation: pitied and admired, she endures the consequences of choices made within constraining social limits.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################DEDICATIONTo Baron Von Hammer-Purgstall, Member of the Aulic Council, Author of the History of the Ottoman Empire.Dear Baron, your encouragement for my “History of French Manners in the Nineteenth Century” led me to associate your name with my work. As a representative of studious Germany, your approval lends credibility to my efforts. Your dedication to research has sustained me in my labor on modern civilization. I hope this token of respect finds you well in Dobling, and reminds you of your sincere admirer and friend.DE BALZAC.THE COLLECTION OF ANTIQUITIESIn a small French town stands the house called the Hotel d’Esgrignon, home of the old Marquis Charles Marie Victor Ange Carol. The name began as a jest but, over time, suited a family proud of its ancient Northman lineage. Provincial nobles who never bowed to kings or commoners, the d’Esgrignons kept a stubborn dignity: marriages without dowries, refusals of titles, and a local reverence that persisted despite neglect by court and state.The Revolution imperiled them. The Marquis, respected locally, avoided emigration but was forced into hiding; family lands were sold. Mlle. d’Esgrignon managed, through the steward Chesnel, to save some property. After the Terror, the Marquis returned to a ruined castle; the old Hotel d’Esgrignon had been demolished. Chesnel used the family’s last funds to buy a new dwelling, wryly naming it the Hotel d’Esgrignon.Around 1800 émigrés returned, including the Baron de Nouastre and his daughter, whom the Marquis married. She died in childbirth, leaving a son to bear the name; grief compounded the Marquis’s reduced circumstances. When a local contractor proposed to Mlle. d’Esgrignon — an offer she and her brother deemed insulting — a rift opened with Chesnel, who had negotiated on the contractor’s behalf.Chesnel, formally a notary, was more than a servant: confidant, manager, and protector of the family’s interests. The shifting dynamics troubled him. The Marquis regarded Chesnel as a loyal companion, while Chesnel wrestled with the strain of fidelity to a declining house. Politically the era divided nobles between Bonapartists and monarchists; the d’Esgrignons remained staunchly royalist.By 1804 many émigrés had returned, yet the family’s fortunes did not revive. The Marquis became a local emblem of old nobility, presiding over a salon mockingly called the “Collection of Antiquities,” a gathering for those clinging to the past. As the bourgeoisie rose, figures like du Croisier sought to undermine the d’Esgrignons; rivalry intensified and political life in the town grew polarized.By 1822 the family’s position was precarious. The elderly Marquis faced an uncertain role in a changing society: the Restoration brought little relief, finances were strained, and their life remained modest. Still, the d’Esgrignons clung to their ideals. The Collection of Antiquities stood as a testament to a bygone era and to the enduring legacy of the family.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The Marquis was oblivious to managing his estate, and the idea that the d’Esgrignons could regain their former glory struck others as absurd. People pinned their hopes on Victurnien, the handsome heir, marrying into nobility to restore the family's prestige. In truth the d’Esgrignons lacked the political connections and finances to reclaim status, and their continuity had been broken by events of the past forty years.Only Chesnel, the notary, perceived the futility of such hopes. He understood the social transformations wrought by the Revolution and the altered political landscape; despite his loyalty, he could not ignore the family’s predicament. He tried gently to persuade the Marquis that adaptation was necessary, but the old noble remained convinced that fate or divine intervention would restore their fortunes.Victurnien, adored by his father and aunt, was a spoiled youth who had never faced real opposition. His indulgent upbringing left him selfish and reckless. The Chevalier, a relic of the old order, inflamed his extravagance with romantic tales of past grandeur, reinforcing Victurnien’s sense of superiority. Surrounded by flattery and exempt from ordinary constraints, he came to regard himself as above the common people, deepening his moral and practical flaws.When he entered society, the gap between his upbringing and modern realities became apparent. His behavior alienated townspeople, and escapades produced legal troubles Chesnel had to manage discreetly. Encouraged by companions who enjoyed his company, his arrogance hardened into a life of excess as he assumed lineage alone would shield him from consequences.Du Croisier, a rival, sought to exploit Victurnien’s weaknesses and hasten the d’Esgrignons’ ruin. Meanwhile, Victurnien’s vanity and appetite for pleasure obscured danger: he drifted into gambling and frivolity, sustained by Chesnel’s financial sacrifices, which he accepted as his due. Sent to Paris with high expectations, he quickly found the court far less welcoming than imagined; the nobility he encountered were mostly self-interested and indifferent to reviving the d’Esgrignon name.Undeterred, he adopted the extravagant lifestyle of Parisian elites. His charm and looks opened doors, and he became a fixture in high society, especially captivated by the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse. Enchanted by her world and beauty, he fell intensely in love, blind to her debts and convinced their passion was pure.As his Parisian pleasures mounted, so did his debts. Friends who enjoyed his company egged his spending, entertained by the prospect of his fall. Chesnel, ever loyal, continued to prop him up financially but grew increasingly alarmed. Despite warnings, Victurnien remained absorbed in fantasy, oblivious to reality. His tension between noble pretensions and dire finances would precipitate his downfall, illustrating the dangers of vanity, excess, and the illusion of inherited status in a changing world.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################By the end of 1823 the Kellers had advanced Victurnien two hundred thousand francs, without Chesnel or Mlle. Armande knowing. Victurnien had also received two thousand crowns from Chesnel to conceal his sources and wrote deceitful letters to his father and aunt, who remained unaware of the disaster looming over their noble house. Only du Croisier knew; he delighted not merely in ruining the d’Esgrignons but in dishonoring them, sensing his revenge as Victurnien’s debts mounted.Du Croisier’s first move targeted Chesnel, who lived modestly in the Rue du Bercail. One afternoon, after dinner, Chesnel’s housekeeper announced du Croisier, and the old lawyer’s heart sank. Du Croisier produced a bill for two hundred and twenty-seven thousand francs, demanding payment or threatening to expose Victurnien’s debts. Overwhelmed, Chesnel realized he could raise the money only by mortgaging his lands. Du Croisier seized the opportunity, proposed to buy Chesnel’s property—and completed the purchase within two days. Stripped of his lands, Chesnel was left with only his practice and his house, consumed by worry for Victurnien’s future.Chesnel dreaded telling Mlle. Armande about the family’s financial ruin. She had just received a charming, hypocritical letter from Victurnien describing his travels in Italy with the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse and remained enchanted and oblivious. Chesnel’s arrival shattered her reverie: all was lost, and Victurnien risked bringing dishonor on their house. Devastated, she realized they had given him too much freedom.The Marquis, having read Victurnien’s letter, remained unaware of the crisis. Mlle. Armande and Chesnel shared a silent understanding of their concern for the young Count; they discussed intervening to save him from his reckless lifestyle, fearing he would be left destitute. Mlle. Armande resolved to go to Paris to rescue Victurnien, masking the trip as a medical consultation to avoid gossip. Chesnel gave her his last bag of louis, and she set off, determined to confront the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse.Du Croisier meanwhile had planned his revenge to ensure Victurnien would be cut off from further support. He wrote to Victurnien, informing him that the Kellers would advance no more money without security, timing the letter to coincide with Victurnien’s growing desperation. Until then living in luxury and buoyed by occasional gambling wins, Victurnien suddenly faced the reality of his debts. In despair he briefly considered suicide but rejected it. Instead he thought of fleeing with the Duchess and forging a bill of exchange to finance their escape; after she assured him money could be found and agreed to flee, he presented a bill for three hundred thousand francs to the Kellers, who advanced the money unaware of the deceit. Du Croisier, learning of the transaction, realized his plan for revenge was bearing fruit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################When Victurnien received his money, he handed it to Mme. de Maufrigneuse, who locked it away and persuaded him to make one more visit to the Opera. He almost confessed everything to Chesnel but, despite misgivings, lingered at the Duchess’s request, held by her enchanting look.The next day he returned to the Hotel de Maufrigneuse intending to flee, only to find the Duchess resolved to remain. She thought it more remarkable to face the storm than to run, and Victurnien, torn between admiration and despair, understood the depth of his love for her. After their quarrel Diane’s coldness left him distraught; he begged forgiveness but received only an angelic indifference that intensified his anguish and clouded his judgment.Compounding his emotional turmoil came a graver discovery: a warrant had been issued for his arrest. Josephin urged him to go to the Hotel du Bon la Fontaine, where Mlle. Armande waited. Reeling, Victurnien clung to his determined aunt as they discussed hiding and the possibility of slipping into Chesnel’s house unnoticed. In executing their plan he unwittingly walked into a trap.Chesnel, meanwhile, was preparing to sell his practice to a rival notary, M. Lepressoir, who hoped to profit from Victurnien’s misfortune. Learning of the young man’s plight devastated Chesnel; fearing for Victurnien’s future, he even considered taking the blame for the forgery himself. He resolved to confront Victurnien’s creditor, du Croisier, and negotiate a settlement.Du Croisier, however, reveled in his ascendancy over the d’Esgrignon family, treating the affair as an opportunity to assert dominance and avenge aristocratic slights. At a gathering at his house news of Victurnien’s arrest spread, and guests debated the implications and the looming trial. Realizing the stakes, Chesnel sought to influence the examining magistrate, M. Camusot, by appealing to his ambitions and connections, and he intensified efforts to enlist Mme de Maufrigneuse’s aid—she, initially unaware of the forgery’s extent, felt compelled to help once she grasped Victurnien’s love and the consequences he faced.Negotiations with du Croisier proved thorny: he demanded not only money but political favors and social recognition—bargains Chesnel could scarcely meet. In desperation Chesnel offered a large sum to have charges withdrawn, but du Croisier’s pride and ambition resisted compromise. As the trial approached both sides hardened. Chesnel even contemplated lying under oath to save Victurnien, recognizing that the d’Esgrignon family’s honor and future lay on the line.The legal battle thus became emblematic of broader tensions between aristocracy and bourgeoisie: Victurnien’s fate would decide more than one man’s destiny. Throughout, Chesnel’s unwavering loyalty and willingness to navigate the treacherous provincial politics underscored the complex interplay of love, honor, and ambition in a society where social standing could determine one’s fate.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Blondet, a capable judge, was overshadowed by his passion for gardening—especially pelargoniums—which consumed his time and ambition. His marriage to a much younger woman, who had an affair with the local prefect, further obscured his career. Although he had a legitimate son, Joseph, Blondet favored his illegitimate son, Émile, whom he eventually sent to Paris with scant support after his wife's death.Blondet’s modest brick house and neat garden reflected his horticultural devotion. He planned to leave his estate to Joseph, having disinherited Émile, whom he regarded with disdain. Émile, however, prospered in Paris, aided by his mother’s family and the patronage of Mlle. de Troisville, which brought literary recognition—achievements of which Blondet remained unaware, absorbed in legal duties and frugality enforced by his maid.Joseph was groomed for the bench and spent time with the Blandureau family, while Michu, a young lawyer, navigated social circles and often outshone older judges. Meanwhile the public prosecutor, a shrewd politician, became entangled in a plot against the d’Esgrignons, resulting in a forgery charge against the young noble Victurnien d’Esgrignon.The President of the Tribunal, seeking personal advantage, manipulated events to favor du Croisier, a rival of the d’Esgrignons; influenced by his wife, he pushed the prosecutor to pursue the case despite political risk. As the trial neared, the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse intervened, providing funds to secure Victurnien’s release. Her efforts, with Chesnel the family notary’s support, aimed to restore the d’Esgrignon name. The trial ultimately exonerated Victurnien and exposed du Croisier’s machinations.Although the d’Esgrignons endured social scrutiny, their honor was preserved. Victurnien’s future remained uncertain as he struggled with his past and familial expectations. The narrative reflects on shifting nobility and enduring loyalty, embodied by figures like Chesnel, and examines ambition, familial allegiance, and the complexities of social standing in a changing France.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################Produced by John Bickers, and DagnyTHE LILY OF THE VALLEYBy Honore De BalzacTranslated by Katharine Prescott WormeleyDEDICATIONTo Monsieur J. B. Nacquart, Member of the Royal Academy of Medicine.Dear Doctor--Here is one of the most carefully hewn stones in the second course of the foundation of a literary edifice which I have slowly and laboriously constructed. I wish to inscribe your name upon it, as much to thank the man whose science once saved me as to honor the friend of my daily life.De Balzac.THE LILY OF THE VALLEYENVOIFelix de Vandenesse to Madame la Comtesse Natalie de Manerville:I yield to your wishes. It is the privilege of the women whom we love more than they love us to make the men who love them ignore the ordinary rules of common-sense. To smooth the frown upon their brow, to soften the pout upon their lips, what obstacles we miraculously overcome! We shed our blood, we risk our future!You exact the history of my past life; here it is. But remember this, Natalie; in obeying you I crush under foot a reluctance hitherto unconquerable. Why are you jealous of the sudden reveries which overtake me in the midst of our happiness? Why show the pretty anger of a petted woman when silence grasps me? Could you not play upon the contradictions of my character without inquiring into the causes of them? Are there secrets in your heart which seek absolution through a knowledge of mine? Ah! Natalie, you have guessed mine; and it is better you should know the whole truth. Yes, my life is shadowed by a phantom; a word evokes it; it hovers vaguely above me and about me; within my soul are solemn memories, buried in its depths like those marine productions seen in calmest weather and which the storms of ocean cast in fragments on the shore.The mental labor which the expression of ideas necessitates has revived the old, old feelings which give me so much pain when they come suddenly; and if in this confession of my past they break forth in a way that wounds you, remember that you threatened to punish me if I did not obey your wishes, and do not, therefore, punish my obedience. I would that this, my confidence, might increase your love.Until we meet,Felix.CHAPTER I. TWO CHILDHOODSTo what genius fed on tears shall we some day owe that most touching of all elegies—the tale of tortures borne silently by souls whose tender roots find stony ground in the domestic soil? The true history of a childhood stunted by neglect and coldness would move a heart to pity more surely than any public tragedy. Such was my childhood.From infancy I experienced a strange mixture of neglect and occasional scrutiny. Put to nurse in the country and forgotten for three years, I returned to a parental roof where indifference predominated; even servants pitied me. My brother and sisters, encouraged by a fawning habit and fear of our mother’s temper, often joined in persecuting me. The compact of childhood that protects and shelters one another was annulled in my case. Frequently punished for my brother’s faults and forbidden to prove my innocence, I learned distrust. These wrongs did not make me cringing or vicious; rather they hardened my character. Constant expectation of new blows bred a sullen resignation and a premature pride that tempered the ill effects of my upbringing.Sometimes my mother spoke of educating me herself; the mere thought chilled me, and I blessed the neglect that let me wander in the garden, watch insects, and stare at the sky. One evening, crouched under a fig-tree and engrossed in a star, I was falsely accused by our governess, Mademoiselle Caroline, of mischief with a garden faucet and severely punished.
