The star, however, remained mine; forbidden pleasures become more desired, and I kept the image of that glowing point through my boyhood.My brother Charles, five years older, was the household favorite: handsome, robust, and indulged. I, puny and sickly, was sent at five as a day pupil to a town school, with only a scanty lunch. The contrast between my hunger and my companions’ plenty wounded me deeply. Fellow pupils taunted me over rillons and dainties I did not possess; one cruel trick—the withdrawal of an offered slice—left me ashamed and resolute. I was struck with stones once; my mother heard of it and only lamented my supposed dangerous temperament.Rejected by family and schoolmates, distrust of myself grew. At twelve I was transferred to Pont-le-Voy, an Oratorian school where dull boys languished. For eight years I lived a pariah’s life, the small allowance of three francs a month barely buying the necessities for study; I could not afford games or toys and was excluded from playground life unless I humiliated myself to flatter the rich. I took refuge under a tree with borrowed books and thought much. When I won prizes for theme and translation and my parents did not appear in the theatre, I burst into tears, burned my crowns, and felt the deeper ache of being forsaken.The sense of injustice drove me inward and toward religion. At first communion I sought consolation in prayer and martyrology; my young zeal fed sublime visions that strengthened my imagination and made me sensitive to the inner life of things—qualities that would later temper my talent. My father, uneasy about the Oratorians, moved me to a Paris institution, the Lepitre Academy, at fifteen. There, as before, neglect continued: little pocket-money, discipline, and social restrictions. Monsieur Lepitre, given to Bourbon sympathies and old acquaintanceships, contrived a small subvention but not enough.In the Lepitre house the porter Doisy, a smuggler of comforts for pupils, set up breakfasts and small luxuries for the well-to-do; resisting his blandishments for long was heroic for a hungry boy. My parents’ arrival in Paris once promised escape: my brother had planned to take us all to dinner in the Palais-Royal and to a theatre. But I owed a hundred francs to Doisy; my mother, infuriated, refused indulgence and reproached me with future ruin. Sent back into Lepitre’s charge, I lost the dinner and the play—the first meeting with my mother after twelve years apart ended in reproach.At nineteen, studying at my own room under Lepitre’s guardianship and constrained by a lack of money and strict supervision, I grew desperate for Parisian life. My aunt, the Marquise de Listomère, on the Ile Saint-Louis, was ceremonious and cold; she would not offer money and made me feel ashamed of my youth. Several attempts to slip away to the Palais-Royal were thwarted by unlucky coincidences: once Monsieur Lepitre’s coach appeared, another time my mother unexpectedly arrived, and a third time my mother’s presence on a post-chaise arrested me. My family’s anxieties, political as well as moral, kept me under restraint.Study became both refuge and peril; absorbed in books, I delayed the normal stirrings of youth. In Tours, my mother’s coldness crushed me; on a journey homewards I threw myself at her feet, begged for affection, and was mocked as playing at “comedy.” At Blois I once went to the bridge to throw myself into the Loire but could not bring myself to commit suicide. Returned to the parental house, I found only the slight mercies of my sisters and was given poor clothing and scant regard. My mother’s ironical speeches and punctilious sense of duty made affection a painful, unreliable thing.Yet life at Tours contained public excitement. The Duc d’Angouleme passed in triumph; Touraine celebrated, and I longed to attend a ball given in his honor. Despite my mother’s mockery about my request, secret preparations were made: a bottle-blue coat, silk stockings, and a frilled shirt for the first time in my life. The ball overwhelmed me: heat, music, a crush of people, the military music, and cries for the duke. The spectacle disgusted me at first, but then a strange ambition stirred—an appetite for glory, for that power over the masses which enthralled men.Amid the crowd I saw a woman who changed me. Misjudged as a sleepy lad, I ventured to throw myself on a pair of shoulders I thought seemed like a mother’s; she cried out, turned, and rebuked me with a single word, “Monsieur!” Tears sprang to my eyes, and the look that followed—noble, angry, forgiving—awoke in me a passion I had never known. The woman, moving with queenly grace, took with her a purity and brightness that transformed my life: my star had become flesh and blood. From then on I saw the sky bluer and felt an irresistible longing to seek her everywhere in Touraine.My mother, seeing new melancholy in me, sent me for a few weeks to Frapesle, a chateau on the Indre, hoping the country would restore my health. For a young man newly in love but ignorant of the object’s name or station, this was an opportunity. Walking the country, I examined every house, each old tower promising that she might be there. The landscape—Charlemagne moors, woods, the valley of the Indre and its river—enchanted me. In that region, beauty is so intimate to the air that my soul felt in tune with it.From a walnut-tree beneath which I later loved to pause, I first saw the castle that housed the woman I sought. That white spot among the vines, the cambric fluttering in an arbor, was the evidence. I told myself, “If that woman lives upon this earth, she is here.” The castle, Clochegourde, belonged to the Comte de Mortsauf and his wife, a Mademoiselle de Lenoncourt—an ancient house, proud and secluded, their fortune limited, their habits fixed. My host, Monsieur de Chessel, who knew the count and countess, proposed we cross the river and call there; his teasing reading of my enthusiasm revealed how transparent my feelings had become.Clochegourde was modest but tasteful: five windows on the façade, terraced gardens, acacias, vines, and a neat arrangement of outbuildings. From the road the ground-floor seemed an upper story; a little torret and carved crest over the portico testified to lineage. The house fit into the valley as a jewel in green, and the approach quickened my heart with foreboding and delight.We were announced shortly after the bell for breakfast; the count was at Azay but the countess, whose voice I had heard at the ball, welcomed us. At sight of her my agitation nearly betrayed me; she returned to her embroidery and spoke with a calm dignity that both concealed and revealed feeling. Monsieur de Chessel explained my fatigue from walking and my being a Touraine native newly returned. Madame de Mortsauf offered me hospitality with gentle courtesy; her glance, severe at first, softened when she saw my distress. She gave me restoratives, words of comfort, and, after consultation with Monsieur de Chessel, invited us to dine. That invitation—accepted—seemed a promise: if I pleased her, my introducer might remain in the count’s good graces; it implied faith in my power to charm.Madame de Mortsauf, though often reserved, possessed a voice and manner that entranced me. At dinner she spoke of harvests and local affairs; at first it seemed she shut me out, but I soon discovered the charm of listening to her. The cadence of her speech, the briefness of her glances, the rare but luminous intonations, moved me more than any artifice. I would prolong conversations and accept reproach merely to hear the melody of her voice and to drink the subtle air of her presence.Her appearance—her white, rounded shoulders, the gauze that veiled her bosom, the braids of hair above a soft throat—had impressed me at the ball; up close, in everyday dress, she retained that luminous appeal. Her face could not be adequately described; it required an artist who could paint the inward fires and restraint that made her expression unique. Her hair was fair and soft; her brow broad and thoughtful; her eyes green tinged with brown, usually wan but capable of sudden vivid gleams when moved by sorrow or joy. A Grecian nose and graceful lips gave her face spiritual beauty; her figure, well-developed yet youthful, combined strength with an unusual softness. She seemed at once a mother and a maiden—grave from suffering, yet childlike in feeling. Her movements, her rare glances, her simplicity of dress and demeanor formed a charm that made men dream: she revealed, without ambition, a virginal purity and a moral elevation that made others thoughtful.On that morning she wore a rose-colored striped gown; her hair twisted simply and held with a comb. Beyond physical features, it was the constant emanation of her soul—her tenderness to family, her cheerfulness in serenity, her resignation amid storms—that impressed me most. Clochegourde’s salon, panelled and modestly ornamented, matched her spirit: gray paint, porcelain vases with Cape heather, cambric curtains, covered upholstery, an embroidery frame. From its windows the valley unrolled—Pont-de-Ruan, chateau d’Azay, Sache—images that would nourish my ideas and transform my mind. Many of my thoughts in science and politics seemed to find their first life there, in that composed, reflective atmosphere. The salon’s quiet regularity deepened the impression of a domestic epic lived in a small and noble sphere.Madame de Mortsauf sat with the air of a woman who does not need to attract; she charmed by naturalness and by withholding as much as by giving. If I had encountered her first amid household scenes rather than at a ball, I might have worshipped with tears and renunciation. Having already tasted a stolen kiss, my longing shifted to other desires—I wanted the full companionship of that house, the river, the vines, the nights under the stars. I felt suddenly willing to seek any task to win that vision of delight.When Monsieur de Mortsauf returned, my curiosity about him vied with fear that he might obstruct my new hopes. A little girl, Madeleine, entered—fragile and pale—and this sight brought a cloud to the mother’s brow. Madeleine, though thin and wan, still preserved a spirit; Jacques, her brother, similarly weak, followed the count. The children’s frailty suggested a family sorrow; Madame de Mortsauf’s health and spirit seemed to bear the cost. Monsieur de Mortsauf himself, about forty-five but aged by care, offered a polite, uneasy courtesy. For a while his manner concealed more than it revealed, but the family’s domestic picture—motherly care, frail children, the reserve of the house—revealed a life of tenderness restrained by trial.As the visit continued, I grew more attentive to the count’s attitudes. Though outwardly civil, he was awkwardly uneasy, the look of a man whose distrust springs from an inability to understand rather than malice; yet his treatment toward me warmed when he learned of my name and family connections. There was a respectful recognition of lineage: my father had served the Bourbons, and such devotion could not be disregarded by a man of old family like Mortsauf. The count’s complexity hinted at tragedies and virtues entangled by history—a man whose public reserve protected a private tenderness.Thus began a sequence of days at Clochegourde that would shape my life. The valley, the house, the maternal presence, and that first vivid encounter set in motion a passion that combined gratitude, reverence, and the consuming hunger of a first love. My lonely childhood had prepared me to adore and to worship with an intensity that only such neglect can generate. The domestic world of the Mortsaufs, humble and dignified, provided the soil into which my youthful devotion sank its roots.I learned quickly the subtleties of Madame de Mortsauf’s manner: the deliberate economy of her speech, the weight of her silences, the glances scarce as jewels. Her conversation, even on trivial matters, carried an undertone of feeling that betrayed a depth of soul. In the days that followed, her benevolence and her austere tenderness drew me into a state where admiration and self-sacrifice mingled. I noted the simplicity of the household, the English neatness of the salon, the embroidery, the backgammon board, the garden, the interlaced paths. Each small thing seemed to speak of a moral discipline and an inner warmth.The more I saw of the family life of Clochegourde, the clearer it became that passion would not be required to kindle respect; here was a woman whose very humanity compelled love. The contrast between their modest means and the noble air of their names made an impression on me: pride without display, ancestral dignity now humbled by circumstance. This image of noble poverty added to my reverence.In the strangeness of my own past—neglected by my mother, punished unjustly, driven inward by study and prayer—the spectacle of such domestic devotion, of a woman whose little acts were full of unostentatious heroism, opened me like a flower. It is not only beauty that wins the heart, but constancy, sacrifice, and the gentle influence of a soul that turns all small duties into holy acts. Thus Clochegourde, its valley, and above all its countess, became the scene and the cause of a passion that would shape my destiny.Our visit ended that day with impressions that all the words in my vocabulary could not fully render. Madame de Mortsauf’s presence, the music of her voice, her discreet tenderness for the children, and the quiet dignity of the home remained with me. I left with a mingled feeling of hope and helplessness—hope because I had enjoyed an attentive hour in her presence; helplessness because I was too young, too delicate in situation, and too ignorant of the ways of the world to know how to win her friendship beyond the simple worship of a distant admirer.Thus closes the account of my early years to the point where my life after twenty begins under influences both tender and decisive. From the neglect of my family to the sanctuary of the valley, my character was formed—partly by solitude and hard study, partly by the sudden appearance of a woman whose moral and physical beauty made of her my guiding star. The meeting at the ball and at Clochegourde introduced an affection that was equal parts devotion and pain, opening the way to all that followed.[Text ends here in the manuscript as provided.]############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Though ten years of emigration and farm life had altered his physique, the count still showed traces of nobility. Even the most ardent liberal would have acknowledged his chivalric loyalty and steadfast convictions: a man devoted to a cause, honest in his political views, yet ignorant of France’s actual affairs. He was one of those upright souls who, while unable to serve his party, might inadvertently harm it—willing to sacrifice his life rather than his money.At dinner I noticed the count’s flaccid cheeks and the furtive glances he cast at his children, revealing a burdened mind and a frail constitution he had passed to them. Though he might blame himself, he refused to be judged by others. His harsh demeanor, born of self-awareness of his faults, lacked grace and heightened the misery he had brought upon his family. When his wife returned with the children, an atmosphere of unhappiness settled over the house.After the conversation shifted, the count introduced me to his wife and shared details about my family I had not known. He asked my age; when I answered the countess expressed surprise at her daughter’s age, perhaps thinking me younger. Her maternal instincts sparked hope that her children might thrive; she scrutinized me, and some barriers between us dissolved. She had many questions but remained largely silent.“If study has made you ill,” she said, “the air of our valley will soon restore you.”“Modern education harms children,” the count remarked. “We burden them with mathematics and sciences, aging them prematurely. You must stay and rest here,” he urged. “You are overwhelmed by the avalanche of ideas. What future will universal education have unless we return it to religious hands?” His words echoed later when he refused to support a talented candidate: “I shall always distrust men of talent.”He then proposed a tour of the gardens.“Monsieur—” his wife interjected.“What is it, my dear?” he replied, revealing his desire to control his home.“Monsieur de Vandenesse walked from Tours this morning, and Monsieur de Chessel has already taken him on foot over Frapesle.”“Very imprudent of you,” the count said to me, shaking his head.I quickly realized his royalism’s rigidity required careful navigation. The manservant announced dinner. Monsieur de Chessel offered his arm to Madame de Mortsauf, while the count took mine, and we went to the dining room.The dining room, with its white tiled floor and varnished paper adorned with floral designs, was charming though unpretentious. Dinner was plentiful but modest: family silver, mismatched china, flowers on the table. I found joy in these quaint details, feeling contentment that blinded me to the obstacles of country life. I sat beside her, serving her wine, and felt a profound connection. That kiss had bound us in a secret that filled me with both joy and shame. I endeavored to please the count, lavishing affection on the dogs and trying to engage the children, whom I hoped would accept me as one of their own. I sensed any sign of gloom would jeopardize my place in their lives.Dinner passed in a haze of happiness: I reveled in being under her roof, ignoring her apparent coldness and the count’s polite indifference. Love, like life, has its adolescence, and I was content in my feelings. I made several foolish remarks, driven by passion, but no one noticed their cause; even she was oblivious to love. The rest of my visit felt dreamlike as I returned across the Indre, enchanted by moonlight and nature’s sounds. That evening marked a turning point; I felt the weight of past struggles clash with the joy I had just experienced.Approaching Frapesle, I glanced at Clochegourde and saw a small boat tied to a tree—Monsieur de Mortsauf’s fishing boat.“Well,” said Monsieur de Chessel, once we were out of earshot, “I needn’t ask if you found those shoulders; I must congratulate you on the reception Monsieur de Mortsauf gave you. You stepped into his heart at once.”His words lifted my spirits, though I sensed underlying bitterness.“How do you mean?” I asked.“He has never received anyone so well,” he replied.I admitted my surprise.Monsieur de Chessel, whose real name was Durand, had abandoned his father’s name—a successful merchant during the Revolution—to adopt an old parliamentary name. Ambition led him to claim a count’s title under Louis XVIII, a title his children enjoyed without understanding the sacrifices it cost him. His character bore pettiness and envy; his ambition, recently rekindled by royal favor, made him ostentatious yet kindly toward me, providing a peace I had longed for. His interest in my affairs felt paternal, a contrast to the neglect I was used to; I was grateful for his hospitality.The owners of Frapesle were intertwined with the dawn of my happiness, and I cherished those memories. Monsieur de Chessel’s ostentation annoyed some neighbors—fine horses, lavish entertaining—while the Comte de Mortsauf, with limited means, struggled to maintain his estate. Their interactions were polite but lacked intimacy.Jealousy was not the sole cause of the Count de Mortsauf’s isolation. His education had been superficial, and he emigrated just as his learning should have deepened. Long idleness followed, and hopes for a restored monarchy kept him from seeking work; the trials of life eroded his spirit. His natural gaiety faded into moroseness; he fell ill with a condition inclined to hypochondria. Hidden love affairs further deteriorated his health and prospects.After twelve years of hardship he returned to France under a decree allowing emigrants back. Crossing the Rhine, he cried “France! France!” like a child calling for its mother. Once wealthy, he returned impoverished, stripped of authority and influence, burdened by his name. His unyielding opinions and past left him vulnerable to ridicule in a society that often mocked weakness. He eventually settled in Maine on a forgotten farm that offered some stability.The Lenoncourt family, having lost their fortune, welcomed him; he stayed with them while preparing a home. Mademoiselle de Lenoncourt, rather than rejecting him, accepted his proposal and lived with her aunt, the Duchesse de Verneuil, who guided her life. Madame de Verneuil, a member of a devout society, profoundly influenced the countess, who remained devout. After her aunt’s death, Madame de Mortsauf received visits from Saint-Martin, who corrected his last works at Clochegourde. The duchess had endured trials and left Clochegourde to her niece, but her sudden death cast a shadow over the early years of the marriage.Monsieur de Mortsauf found some solace in the valley, but the birth of his first child, Jacques, brought despair: the doctor pronounced the child unviable, a truth the count concealed from his wife. Subsequent births confirmed his fears, deepening his sense of failure. The countess, sensing her husband’s turmoil, devoted herself to his care, striving to reconcile him with himself. She accepted his miserliness and mistrust, guiding him toward what was right, often at the expense of her own happiness. She resolved to remain at Clochegourde, fearing that leaving would harm their children. Her efforts masked the count’s incapacity, allowing him to find some peace in her presence.This brief history also illuminated Monsieur de Chessel, who, despite worldly knowledge, could not fully grasp Clochegourde’s complexities. My own understanding deepened as I spent time there and felt the weight of unspoken truths. One evening I found Madame de Mortsauf watching a sunset, and I sensed a shared sorrow.“Some days are hard to bear,” I remarked.“You read my soul,” she replied, surprised.“We touch at many points,” I said, recognizing our shared experiences of joy and sorrow.She shuddered, and I felt compelled to express my understanding of our mutual suffering.“Ah! My childhood was like a long illness—”“I hear Madeleine coughing,” she interrupted, and left abruptly.The countess welcomed my visits, seeing in me an amusement for her husband. I often played backgammon with the count, enduring his harshness and criticism. Despite his tyranny, the countess’s presence softened my hardships.As I grew better at the game, the count’s temper flared when I began to win. I learned to balance our matches—letting him win at first, then evening the score. The countess understood my strategy and thanked me with a grateful gaze that filled me with joy.One evening, after an intense game, the count’s mood shifted dramatically into rage. I left the room and waited in the garden. When she joined me, she revealed her husband’s condition and asked me to forget the incident.“Promise me to bury the recollection of that scene,” she implored. “I don’t ask you to swear it; just give me your word of honor.”“I will do all you wish,” I replied, “but I cannot change my opinion of him.”She acknowledged my feelings and urged me to understand his struggles.“Perhaps for my sake, you will forget what he said to you today,” she suggested. “He will wish to undo the wrong he did.”“Five days without seeing you?” I lamented.“Do not put such warmth into your manner of speaking to me,” she cautioned.We walked in silence; she eventually said, “It is late; we will part.”I wished to kiss her hand; she hesitated before allowing it.“Never take it unless I give it to you,” she warned. “Leave me my freedom.”As I departed I felt a profound connection, pledging my love in silence.The next day I reflected on our bond, feeling purpose and commitment to her. I recognized her sacrifices and strength. Our shared sufferings had forged a connection beyond friendship, binding us in a deep understanding of each other’s pain and resilience.In the days that followed, small moments deepened my affection. I was determined to support her, to be a source of strength, and to honor the bond we had formed. Navigating my feelings, I understood our connection was rare and precious, something I would cherish and protect. The love I felt for Madame de Mortsauf was more than passion; it was a commitment to stand by her, to share her struggles, and to celebrate the beauty of our shared existence.Beneath the stars I vowed to honor her spirit and nurture the love that had blossomed between us—an affection meant to endure through life’s trials.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################This scene took place on a Tuesday. I had waited until Sunday and did not cross the river. During those five days much occurred at Clochegourde. The count received his brevet as general of brigade, the cross of Saint-Louis, and a pension of four thousand francs. The Duc de Lenoncourt-Givry, made peer of France, regained two forests, resumed his place at court, and his wife reclaimed unsold property that had once been imperial crown lands. Consequently, the Comtesse de Mortsauf became an heiress. Her mother arrived at Clochegourde, bringing a hundred thousand francs saved at Givry—the amount of her dowry, which the count had never demanded despite his poverty. With his own savings the count bought two neighboring estates yielding about nine thousand francs a year. His son would inherit the grandfather’s peerage; the count now saw a way to entail the estate upon him without injuring Madeleine, and the Duc de Lenoncourt would likely help arrange a good marriage.These events soothed the count’s troubled mind. The presence of the Duchesse de Lenoncourt at Clochegourde was significant for the neighborhood. I reflected gloomily on her caste spirit, which my love for Henriette had previously hidden beneath noble sentiments. Who was I—poor, insignificant, with no future but my courage and faculties? On Sunday morning, in the private chapel where I sat with Monsieur and Madame de Chessel and the Abbé de Quelus, I glanced at the duchess’s pew and saw the duchess and her daughter, the count and his children, praying. Henriette’s large straw hat did not tremble; her unconsciousness of my presence bound me to her more than our past had. She prayed with such abandonment she seemed a religious statue, which moved me deeply.By village custom vespers followed mass. After church, Madame de Chessel suggested going to Frapesle rather than cross the Indre twice in the heat. Monsieur de Chessel offered his arm to the duchess, Madame de Chessel took the count’s, and I offered mine to the countess, feeling her arm against my side for the first time. As we walked through the woods of Sache, where light filtered through foliage, sensations and proud thoughts overwhelmed me, making my heart beat violently.“What is the matter?” she asked after a moment.“I have heard of your good fortune,” I said. “Will your new dignities change you and lessen your friendship?”“Change me!” she cried. “Oh, fie! Another such idea and I shall—not despise you, but forget you forever.”I looked at her with ecstasy.“We profit by new laws which we have neither made nor sought,” she said. “We are neither place-hunters nor beggars; neither Monsieur de Mortsauf nor I can leave Clochegourde. By my advice he has declined the command at the Maison Rouge. We are content my father should have it. This forced modesty has benefited our son. The king, whose household my father joins, will favor Jacques’ education. He must represent both Lenoncourt and Mortsauf. I can have no ambition except for him; he must live and be made worthy. Who will keep him safe in Paris? My friend,” she said, her voice breaking, “be some day his guardian and sponsor. Go to Paris; if your family will not support you, ours—especially my mother, who rules society—will help you. Put the strength of your desires into a noble ambition—”“I understand,” I interrupted. “Ambition will be my mistress. I do not need it to be wholly yours. No, I will not be rewarded for my obedience here by favors there. I will go and make my own way. From you, I would accept everything; from others, nothing.”“Child!” she murmured, pleased.“Besides, I have taken my vows,” I continued. “I am resolved to bind myself to you by ties that can never be broken.”She trembled and stopped.“What do you mean?” she asked.“First tell me how you wish me to love you.”“Love me as my aunt loved me,” she answered. “I gave you her rights when I permitted you to call me by her chosen name.”“Then I must love without hope and with absolute devotion. I will enter a seminary, become a priest, and educate your son. Jacques shall be myself in his own form; political conceptions, energy, patience—I will give him all. I will live near you; my love, enclosed in religion, can never be suspected of evil. I will consume myself in the flame and love you with a purified love.”She turned pale. “Félix, do not bind yourself in a way that might be an obstacle. I should die of grief. Do you think despairing love a life’s vocation? Wait for life’s trials. Marry neither the Church nor a woman—marry not at all—I forbid it. Remain free. You are twenty-one—My God! can I have mistaken him? I thought two months sufficed to know some souls.”“What hope have you?” I cried.“Accept our help, rise in life, make your way and your fortune, and you shall know my hope. And,” she added as if whispering, “never release the hand you are holding at this moment.”She bent to my ear as she said it.“Madeleine!” I exclaimed. “Never!”We neared a wooden gate into the park of Frapesle. Suddenly an idea—the count’s death—flashed through me, and I said, “I understand you.”“I am glad of it,” she answered, as though surprised at my thought.Her purity drew tears of admiration that mingled with the bitterness of passion. I thought she did not love me enough even to wish me liberty; love that recoils from crime seemed limited. She did not love me, I thought. To hide my pain, I kissed Madeleine’s hair. “I am afraid of your mother,” I said.“So am I,” she answered, playful. “Don’t forget to call her Madame la duchesse and speak to her in the third person. Young people have lost polite manners; you must learn them for my sake. It is good taste to respect women, no matter their age, and to recognize social distinctions. The respect shown to established superiority guarantees what is due to you. You have drunk the milk of the Revolution in your academy; but as you advance, you will find crude principles of liberty powerless to create happiness. Before considering what an aristocracy ought to be, my common sense tells me societies can exist only through a hierarchy. Be on our side—especially now,” she added, laughing, “when it triumphs.”I was touched by her combination of political feeling and affection. In defending her husband she had foreseen criticisms I might make on seeing the servility he showed her mother. Monsieur de Mortsauf, king in his castle, had a certain dignity; yet I was astonished at the obsequious distance he placed between himself and the duchess. Humiliated by his degradation, I understood the martyrdom of generous women yoked to petty men. I was respectful to the duchess because of my youth; where others saw only a duchess, I saw Henriette’s mother, and that gave sanctity to my homage.At Frapesle the count presented me to the duchess, who examined me coldly. Madame de Lenoncourt was fifty-six, wonderfully preserved, erect and imposing, with a hard face. Her courtly manner reminded me of my own mother. I behaved well; as I passed the countess she said softly, “You are perfect.”The count took my hand. “You are not angry with me, Félix, are you? If I was hasty, pardon an old soldier. We shall probably stay for dinner, and I invite you on Thursday, the evening before the duchess leaves. I must go to Tours tomorrow. Don’t neglect Clochegourde. My mother-in-law is an acquaintance I advise you to cultivate. Her salon will set the tone for the faubourg St. Germain. She has all the traditions of the great world and immense social knowledge.”The count’s changed circumstances showed in his softened manner. The duchess was not patronizing; Monsieur and Madame de Chessel accepted the invitation. The duchess questioned me about my family and, hearing that the Vandenesse in diplomacy was my brother, became almost affectionate. She told me my great-aunt, the old Marquise de Listomere, was a Grandlieu, and that by a provision of the Charter my father was once more Marquis de Vandenesse.“I am but one thing, the serf of Clochegourde,” I said to the countess.The Restoration’s transformation bewildered our generation. To me, the revolution meant nothing; the least word from Madame de Mortsauf was the sole event of importance. I was ignorant of privy councils and politics; my sole ambition was to love Henriette better than Petrarch loved Laura. This indifference made the duchess take me for a child. A large company assembled at Frapesle, and to a young man unused to the world, seeing the woman he loves admired by all is intoxicating. The count, delighted, seemed almost young; his wife hoped for change; I amused myself with Madeleine, whose clever observations made others laugh. It was a happy day. A word, a hope awakened in the morning and illuminated nature. Seeing me joyous, Henriette was joyful too.“That happiness smiling on my gray and cloudy life seems good,” she said to me the next day.I spent that day at Clochegourde. The count left early for Tours. A serious disagreement arose between mother and daughter. The duchess wanted the countess to move to Paris, where she promised a place at court and where the count might accept a position. Henriette, hiding her husband’s incapacity and her own sufferings, opposed leaving Clochegourde because its air and sky calmed his excitements and soothed her children’s health. She had persuaded him to go to Tours to transact business so as to hide his condition from the duchess. I alone knew Clochegourde’s dark secret.Henriette saw that the duchess cared little for Jacques and Madeleine—a painful discovery. The duchess brooked no opposition; she used sweetness, then cold severity, then sarcasm, to force compliance. In those ten days Henriette endured all the contentions a young woman must face to establish independence. That mother had nothing in common with her daughter; she could not even imagine the difficulties that forced Henriette to remain at Clochegourde. Families hide internal dissensions; behind scenes almost all contain deep wounds that lessen natural affections. Tortured and wounded, Henriette loved the one who did not strike her, who would build a shield around her. I was happy when she rested upon my heart; she told me of her troubles. Day by day I learned more fully what she meant by “Love me as my aunt loved me.”“Have you no ambition?” the duchess asked me at dinner.“Madame,” I replied, “I have enough in me to conquer the world; but I am only twenty-one, and I am all alone.”She looked at her daughter in astonishment. The duchess’s visit meant constraint. The countess begged me to be cautious. The great Thursday was a day of wearisome ceremonial. But the duchess returned to court pomps and Clochegourde recovered its order.My quarrel with the count had made me more at home; I could go there at all times. I clung like a climbing plant to the beautiful soul that had opened the world of shared emotions. Our fraternal marriage, founded on trust, became surer; Henriette enfolded me with maternal care, while my love for her burned like hot irons when away. I loved her with a double love—seraphic in her presence, searing when absent. At my age I was not yet man enough to torture her, who feared catastrophe to her children and sat beside them when her husband allowed her a little rest. The mere sound of ardor shook her; she needed veiled love, support mingled with tenderness—that which she gave to others. This situation brought delightful languor and secret immolation. Her conscience and self-devotion awed me; the piety that was the bond of her virtues filled the air with spiritual incense. She gave me only the back of her hand to kiss, never the palm, as though drawing the line of sensual emotions. No two souls ever clasped each other with such ardor yet no earthly tie bound us. Later I understood why that sufficed: in youth we love the whole woman, her children and interests, as our own.I was now one of the household and knew infinite sweetness—the privilege of being the mistress’s friend in a strange family. Servants treated me well; the children divined my affection and were discreet. True passions bloom more charmingly in barren soil.But I also saw the full extent of Monsieur de Mortsauf’s failings. He scolded perpetually about nothing, complained of imaginary evils, and had an inward dissatisfaction that demanded victims. On walks he selected the way and was bored whichever way we went; at home he blamed others. He found domineering pleasure in touching his wife’s sensitive chords. Sometimes he fell into gloomy silence that alarmed her and brought tender attentions. Like petted children, he let her serve him as she did Jacques and Madeleine. I discovered his petty tyranny in household details and realized how it stifled the family, hindered management, and retarded improvement of the estate. The countess had been noble in describing these daily struggles. My admiration for her rose higher than my love; I yielded to be a voluntary victim to come nearer her. She allowed me a place beside her and permission to share her sorrows; eager to die in her arena, I became a repentant apostate seeking heaven with my brethren.“Were it not for you, I must have succumbed under this life,” Henriette said when the count had been more venomous than usual.He had gone to bed. Henriette and I stayed under the acacias with the children playing in the setting sun. Our few words revealed mutual thoughts; when language failed our silence served, and our souls seemed to enter one another. We luxuriated in pensive languor and asked by a look, “Among all our days on earth, will there be one for us?”Despite the evening’s poetry, desire crept through me. After three months of repression I was unable to accept my assigned fate. I took Henriette’s hand and caressed it. She at once withdrew it; tears from my eyes fell and she offered her hand to my lips.“You must know,” she said, “that this will cause me grief. A friendship that asks such a favor is dangerous.”Then I lost control; I reproached her, spoke of my sufferings and of a slight alleviation I dared ask. I told her that at my age the soul had a sex and that I could meet death but not silence my lips. She forced me to silence with a proud glance. Ever since the gate of Frapesle, when I attributed to her the hope that our happiness might spring from a grave, I had shunned the thought of staining her soul. She said she could never be wholly mine; if I could not be to her somewhat as her old confessor—less than a lover yet more than a brother—she must never see me again. She could die and offer her sufferings to God.“I gave you,” she said, “more than I ought, so that nothing might be left to take, and I am punished.”I calmed her, promised never to cause pain, and to love her as an old man loves his youngest child.The next day I came early; there were no flowers in her gray salon. I rushed into the fields and vineyards to make two bouquets; as I gathered them it occurred to me that color and foliage have meanings, and like melody awaken memory. I enlisted Jacques and Madeleine and arranged two bouquets on the portico steps to convey a sentiment—white roses and lilies, with blue flowers intermingled, symbolizing two purities: the child and the martyr. The countess discerned it inwardly and gave me a poignant glance that was reward enough. I pressed the theories of Père Castel into service and recovered a lost science: the language of flowers. What charm in expressing our sensations through these daughters of the sun! I communed with the flora of the fields, seeking varieties that would complete my messages.Twice a week I pursued this poetic enterprise, walking long distances to find flowers, initiating myself into pleasures unknown to the man of science—a poet studying spirit rather than form. Nature offered scenes whose significance rose to the highest moral conceptions: heather with dew like diamonds, a mossy rock-clad corner like a savage cry, anemone pulsatilla like my pure idol alone in her valley, sheets of water with floating worlds of life, a cottage garden as the image of humble existence, or a forest path like a cathedral nave. I gathered such images and poured them into bouquets.In September and October no bouquet cost less than three hours’ search. These floral symphonies expressed what no declaration of love could; my baffled desires found expression as Beethoven found in notes. In their presence, Madame de Mortsauf was my Henriette. She looked at them constantly; they nourished her spirit. These neutral pleasures soothed me: abstinence brings deadly exhaustion, and a few crumbs of heavenly comfort keep one from disaster. Sometimes I found her standing before the bouquets lost in agitation; she would lift her head and murmur, “Ah, how beautiful!”Throughout the month I often saw her at the window at my arrival. If I was late, she wandered on the terrace and told me she had come to meet me and tend her youngest child.Backgammon had ended. The count’s purchases engaged him inspecting his new properties. He was neither arrogant nor offensive, and our walks together were mutual joys. On one occasion the count, wishing to know what we talked about, asked; Henriette answered in words with a double meaning that satisfied him. Madeleine laughed. Our spiritual union continued.The days passed with new joys. The grape harvest—the vintage—began in late September. It is easier to gather grapes than to mow wheat. The vintage brings abundance and hilarity: coopers and carts, girls and panniers. Classes are equal for the day; everyone shares the harvest. Jacques and Madeleine, who had never seen a vintage, were full of delight. We went to Villaines for baskets; none of us were to eat too many grapes. Jacques made me swear to stay at Clochegourde. The day in the vineyard was glorious: the children’s chatter, their racing about, the mother laughing with the joy of youth; she passed a hand round my neck and tapped my cheek, “You are melting away”—the only caress she ever gave me. I filled my basket and emptied it into the vat, moving slowly in regulated motion that kept my spirit free. I found the pleasure of external labor that regulates passion; I learned monastic discipline.For the first time in long weeks the count was neither surly nor cruel. His son’s rosy face rejoiced him. He had promised a dance in front of Clochegourde to celebrate the Bourbons’ return. As we returned, the countess leaned on my arm and whispered, “You bring us happiness.”To me, burdened with knowledge of her sleepless nights and cares, those words were more than any other woman could give.“The terrible monotony of my life is broken; all things are radiant with hope,” she said. “Oh, never leave me! Be the elder son, the protector of the younger.”This is not romantic; in youth passion restrained by obstacles can fill the crater of a volcano with pure water.We had another fête. The countess, wishing to accustom her children to practical life, gave Jacques the produce of the walnut trees and Madeleine that of the chestnuts. Gathering chestnuts and walnuts, seeing the children take pride in their small granaries, made charming scenes of innocent happiness amid autumn’s fading colors. Jacques’ walnuts suffered from rain; I advised him to hold back and sell later. His father urged prudence; the mother rejoiced to see Jacques listen. These homely scenes attached me ever more closely to Henriette.I learned how the countess had, over ten years, changed cultivation methods—rotating crops and dividing the estate into four farms on share leases. She induced the count to cultivate a model farm and rebuild two farmhouses to relet for money rents afterward. Fearing to die before her husband, she sought to secure his income and her children’s property. Fruit trees planted in the last ten years were in full bearing; hedges and elms grew well. With the new purchases and farming system, Clochegourde—divided into four farms—could bring in forty thousand francs a year, not counting vineyards and woodlands. The new road to Chinon would make tenants plentiful. The countess wished to invest fifteen thousand francs in each new estate to improve houses so they would yield better rents; this required thirty thousand francs she and her husband argued about. The fear “If I die tomorrow, what will become of them?” drove her. The count objected, but common sense carried the day. That day we went to the Cassine and the Rhetorière to decide on buildings; she spoke gently, confident of success. A carrier wanted a large farm on the new route; another honest man was ready to take la Baude; the soil would attract tenants. In two years Clochegourde might yield twenty-four thousand francs. Gravelotte in Maine was rented for seven thousand a year; his pension was four thousand. Later additions might let her go to Paris and arrange Jacques’ education.With what feeling she uttered “Paris!” I knew she wished not to be separated from her friend. I told her I would finish my education and work to be Jacques’ tutor. She looked grave.“No, Félix,” she said. “That cannot be, any more than your priesthood. I thank you as a mother, but as a woman who loves you, I can never allow you to be the victim of your attachment. Such a position would be a social discredit to you. My father would never forgive your taking such a humble place; he would refuse you his protection. You must accept society as it is; never fly in its face. This rash proposal of—”“Love,” I whispered.“No, charity,” she said, controlling tears. “This wild idea enlightens me as to your character; your heart will be your bane. I shall claim the right to teach you sometimes. Let my woman’s eye see for you. From the solitudes of Clochegourde I will share in your successes. As to a tutor, do not fear; we shall find some good old abbé, my father will devote sums to the boy’s education. Jacques is my pride; he is eleven years old.”At the Cassine Jacques, Madeleine, and I followed her like children. I left to speak with Martineau and saw how beloved she was. A laborer told me she was good and knew our hardships and treated the people well. I gave that man all the money I had.A pony arrived for Jacques; the father, an excellent horseman, would accustom him gradually. The morning of the first lesson, with Madeleine shouting and all the household clustered round, was a maternal festival. Jacques wore a blue collar embroidered by his mother and rode fearlessly. The count smiled for the first time in months; the mother burst into tears and leaned on my arm, “I think I have never suffered. Do not leave us today.” The child jumped into her arms; I and Madeleine arranged bouquets for dinner. She said, “The fifteenth of October is certainly a great day for me. Jacques has taken his first riding lesson, and I have just set the last stitch in my furniture cover.”“Then, Blanche,” the count laughed, “I must pay you for it.”He offered her his arm; an open carriage her father had sent awaited with two English horses. As we returned, she whispered anxiously, “I am too happy; happiness is like illness—it overwhelms me; I fear it may vanish.”I loved her so passionately I was jealous. She asked me what my eyes said; I told her. She was more touched than by any gift, then poured comfort into my heart. “Love me as my aunt loved me,” she said. “If I take your life, must I not be grateful?”“It was time I finished my tapestry,” she added. “Men find occupations a resource against troubles; we women need physical exercise to control inward sufferings. Each stitch rocked my thoughts; to every stitch I confided my secrets. While doing my last chair I thought much of you. What you put into your bouquets I have said in my embroidery.”Dinner was lovely. Jacques offered his garland to his mother with charming grace. In the afternoon we played backgammon; the count was charming. They accompanied me to Frapesle in the tranquil twilight. It was a day of days for the poor woman.Soon the riding lessons became a source of contention. The countess feared the father's harsh reprimands; Jacques grew thin and wore dark circles. I advised him to tell his father when tired, but the expedient failed, and the old huntsman took over. Angry reproaches resumed. Then the cost of the Cassine and Rhetorière buildings exceeded estimates by half. The news, given first to Monsieur de Mortsauf instead of his wife, became the ground of a quarrel that grew bitter until the count’s malady demanded its arrearages.That day I had gone from Frapesle to search for flowers with Madeleine. Returning, I could not find her and heard cries in the house. Madeleine ran out: “The general,” she cried, “is scolding mamma; go and defend her.”I reached the salon without being seen. Monsieur de Mortsauf was in violent tirade.“Never marry, Félix,” he said. “A woman is led by the devil; the most virtuous would invent evil if it did not exist; they are all vile.”Arguments flew. He harked back to old troubles, cursed the new farming system, declared he could have been twice as rich if he had managed Clochegourde. He sprang around, displacing furniture; then stopped, moaned his marrow was on fire, that his wife had ruined him. The countess smiled and looked upward.“Yes, Blanche,” he cried, “you are my executioner; you are killing me; you are a monster of hypocrisy. She is smiling! Do you know why she smiles, Félix?”I kept silence.“That woman denies me all happiness; she is no more to me than to you, and yet she pretends to be my wife! She fulfills none of the duties of laws, human and divine; she lies to God and man. She puts all her art into keeping me away; she has made me mad through privations; she is killing me by degrees, and she thinks herself a saint and takes the sacrament every month!”Henriette wept bitterly, answering only, “Monsieur! monsieur! monsieur!”The count’s words made me burn with shame for him and stirred my heart because they struck at chastity and delicacy—the warp and woof of first love.“She is virgin at my expense,” cried the count.At this, the countess cried, “Monsieur!”“And what mean you by your imperious ‘Monsieur!’?” he shouted. “Am I not your master? Must I teach you that I am?”He came toward her, thrusting forward his white wolf’s head; his yellow eyes looked savage. Henriette slid from her chair to avoid a blow, lay at full length, and lost consciousness. The count stopped bewildered. I took her in my arms; the count opened the door of her bedroom. We laid her, still dressed, on the bed. When she revived she motioned to unfasten her belt; the count found scissors; I made her breathe salts and she opened her eyes. The count left the room, ashamed. Two hours passed in silence. Henriette’s hand lay in mine; she pressed it but could not speak. Once she rose on her elbow and whispered, “Unhappy man!—ah! if you did but know—” then sank back. The memory of past sufferings and the present shock threw her into convulsions I calmed by an instinctive magnetism. I smoothed her hair—the first time I touched it—and sat looking at the simple room, its chintz curtains, its crucifix, her aunt’s portrait, the children’s drawings with locks of hair. What renunciations of luxury for such a woman—the chamber of a married nun, filled with holy resignation. Painful as the sight was, it exposed hidden, irreparable woe.When the children and Manette came in I left. The count waited for me; he seemed to seek me as mediator. He caught my hands, exclaiming, “Stay, stay with us, Félix!”“Unfortunately,” I said, “Monsieur de Chessel has a party, and my absence would cause remark. But after dinner I will return.”He left the house with me and took me to the lower gate.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As I read the letter I felt a maternal warmth beneath my fingers that contrasted sharply with my own mother's coldness; no wonder the countess had forbidden me to open it in Touraine, fearing I might break down. Soon after I reencountered my brother Charles, haughty and distant. His condescension and lectures on social matters I already knew made me awkward; only my deep love kept me from feeling foolish. Childhood sorrows and my solitude had sharpened my perception of the affections around me, and since returning from Clochegourde I could compare experiences and see more clearly what genuine attachment meant.I often visited the Duchesse de Lenoncourt, where Henriette's name was rarely spoken. Only the old duke, whose welcome relieved my shyness, seemed aware of our bond. Just as I began enjoying society again, the events of March 20th forced a change. On Henriette's advice I followed my brother to Ghent, accompanying the Duc de Lenoncourt. The duke's amiability became protection when he perceived my loyalty to the Bourbons; he introduced me to the king, who valued my steadfastness in trying times. A letter from Madame de Mortsauf arrived telling me of Jacques' illness; her distress was clear and I felt compelled to help. Setting aside any rivalry with my brother, I resolved to leave the court and serve my true sovereign as a reliable messenger — a mission I embraced, eager to return to Clochegourde.After executing the king's orders in France I had to evade Bonapartist authorities, traveling through Vendée until I reached Clochegourde. Jacques was better; the count welcomed me, and in the salon I met Henriette. Her surprise and joy at seeing me were immediate. She prepared my room, the count saw to my food, and at dinner we learned of the disasters at Waterloo and Napoleon's flight. My concern, however, centered on Henriette. She had even built an ice-house for me; her effort to provide iced water showed a tender thoughtfulness. During the following week she regained health and spirit, and absence had only intensified my love — her beauty and devotion impressed me anew.Henriette spoke plainly of long nights at Jacques' bedside, her prayers and exhausted vigilance. Yet the count's harshness cut through her reflections; she wondered why her words never pleased him and bore the emotional toll. Still she held firm to duty, urging her children to respect their father despite the strain. She described their agricultural success and spoke with a practical wisdom, and she had a remarkable knack for foreseeing matters concerning her children, which she attributed to divine intervention. I admired her counsel and felt gratitude for her guidance.She encouraged me to pursue opportunities in Paris, stressing the importance of being available to the king. Our conversations deepened our bond: we shared hopes, fears, and a growing sense of belonging. But the count's presence and the tension in their marriage were always present. One day she confided her fear that the count's behavior threatened her children's well-being; torn between family duty and personal happiness, she hesitated to assert herself. I urged firmness, yet she feared the consequences.When the count's health declined, Henriette and I alternated in caring for him. That shared duty drew us closer; I took comfort in supporting her. His slow recovery, however, brought back erratic, difficult behavior, testing her patience and authority. Still she put her children's needs first, often at cost to her own happiness. Between trials we found moments of intimacy and mutual support that strengthened our connection, though always shadowed by the count's influence.Eventually I returned to Paris, absorbed in responsibilities but faithful to Henriette. Our correspondence sustained us; her letters revealed both love and the burden she bore. News of Jacques' relapse later reached me and the pain in her words was palpable; I felt powerless yet determined to remain devoted. Throughout, Henriette's resilience and maternal devotion shone: she navigated obligations with courage, frequently sacrificing personal happiness for her family. My loyalty grew with her suffering; our love remained a rare, guiding light I cherished and hoped would endure.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################During a deeply emotional period I met a remarkable woman at the Élysée-Bourbon salons: immensely wealthy, of noble lineage and married to a distinguished British peer. Her beauty, grace, and wit made her the idol of Parisian society; she ruled with a firm yet charming demeanor.The English, distant and reserved, live by strict laws and rigid virtues, producing an Englishwoman who, though delicate and charming, is trapped in hypocrisy and compelled to maintain appearances; her passion is intense but constrained. You may envy these women, but they are complex and convinced that love alone suffices.My liaison with the Marchioness of Dudley became notorious. At a time when my better feelings were subdued, I was drawn to her; my fidelity to Madame de Mortsauf only increased her desire. Lady Dudley craved the chase, and my resistance inflamed her passion. Her remark about my relationship with Madame de Mortsauf—“I am wearied to death of these turtle-dove sighings”—should have warned me.Men have fewer means to resist women than women have to escape men. Social codes keep men from appearing harsh; women are expected to be coy. Though I loved Madame de Mortsauf, I was not immune to Lady Dudley’s beauty and rank. Her attention in society stirred me; she often hinted she would be my mistress. After one beguiling evening she made her intentions clear, provoking scandal in the British aristocracy.Seemingly fragile, Lady Dudley possessed a strong will. Her physical courage was remarkable—she could outpace any horse—and she pursued her desires relentlessly. By contrast, Madame de Mortsauf was nurturing and expansive, drawing others into her warmth; Lady Dudley was intense and consuming. English customs reflect a materialist bent, valuing comfort and order often at the expense of spiritual depth. Lady Dudley embodied that materialism: compelling physically but leaving an emptiness I did not feel with Madame de Mortsauf, whose love was spiritual and boundless.Lady Dudley’s jealousy was masked by charm; she sought to erase Madame de Mortsauf from my heart. I continued to write to Madame de Mortsauf, but my letters went unanswered, filling me with dread. When I finally returned to Clochegourde, a coldness revealed the harm my infidelity had caused.Madame de Mortsauf’s demeanor had altered; she was distant and sorrowful, her beauty dimmed by grief. I felt the weight of my betrayal as I watched her withdraw, pride and pain evident. The family gathered around me, but I perceived her retreat. The count, unaware of the turmoil, welcomed me warmly, while Madame de Mortsauf remained aloof.Witnessing her, I realized the depth of my wrongdoing. I had brought her suffering, and my heart ached for the love I had jeopardized. The count, whose health was fragile, expressed concern for his wife and confessed his fears, sharing the emotional toll he bore. Madame de Mortsauf’s health seemed delicate, and I was tormented by the thought that I was its cause. I longed to make amends, but she remained guarded; our conversations were strained, full of unspoken words.As the count’s condition declined, I felt helpless watching its effect on Madame de Mortsauf. Devoted to her children, she sought solace in them, yet shadows of despair crept in. I was torn between love for her and my entanglement with Lady Dudley, whose presence still loomed.In desperation I sought Madame de Mortsauf’s forgiveness, but she rebuffed me, insisting I had caused her too much pain. Left to grapple with guilt, I felt the weight of my actions pressing down on me. The tension grew and distance widened. Torn between two women who represented different aspects of love—Lady Dudley’s passionate, material allure and Madame de Mortsauf’s nurturing devotion—I was forced to confront the true nature of love and sacrifice; my desires had duties attached, and the harm I caused those I cared for weighed heavily on me.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################I know nothing more destructive than the wit of an Englishwoman; it carries that pompous certainty with which the British mask their prejudices. French wit is lace, adorning life’s joys and quarrels; English wit is corrosive, stripping flesh until only bone remains. Arabella wielded her power like a sultan, cutting down rivals with her words.“My angel,” she said, “I can speak morality too. I’ve questioned whether my love for you is a crime, and I find it both natural and pious. God created beauty to inspire adoration. The real crime would be not to love you. That other woman insults you by comparing you to men; the laws of morality do not apply to you, for you are above them. Am I not drawing closer to divine love by loving you? Will God punish a woman for seeking the divine? My love is a weakness, while that woman’s is strength. Don’t frown; I do not wish her ill. I adore the morality that has freed you for me—because you are mine forever, aren’t you?”“Yes.”“Forever and ever?”“Yes.”“Ah! I have found favor in my lord! I alone understand his worth! You say she knows how to cultivate her estate. I leave that to farmers; I cultivate your heart.”I try to recall this intoxicating babble to convey Arabella and the events that followed. Her passion, mingled with those words, sought to obliterate the impressions left by Henriette’s dignified love with impetuous affection. Arabella’s fierce attack displayed fear and admiration for her rival. The next morning she was tearful, lamenting a sleepless night.“What troubles you?” I asked.“I fear my love will ruin me,” she replied. “I have given all. That woman possesses something you still desire. If you prefer her, forget me; I will not trouble you. I will go far and die, like a plant deprived of sunlight.”She forced declarations from me and, filled with joy, dried her eyes. “Go back to her; I do not want to owe you to the force of my love, but to your own will. If you return, I shall know you love me as much as I love you, which I always doubted.”She persuaded me to return to Clochegourde. Under her influence the falsity of my position did not strike me. Had I refused to return, Lady Dudley would have found in Henriette a triumph. Arabella, confident of me, would have taken me to Paris; but going to Clochegourde now insulted Madame de Mortsauf. Would any loving woman forgive crimes against love? Unless she were an angel, a loving woman would rather see her lover die than know him happy with another. Thus my leaving Clochegourde for the Grenadiere was fatal to my true love and benefited the transient affection that held me. Lady Dudley had calculated a skilful plan.When I met the countess that morning she was pale and depressed, like one who had not slept. Generous perception revealed the nature of actions the world ignores but lofty spirits call criminal. Like a child playing near a precipice, I felt we were separated by a universe. A wail rose within us, echoing Good Friday’s “Consummatum est.” Henriette’s illusions were shattered; her heart had endured its passion. She refused to look at me, denying me the light that had shone on my life for six years. I felt the wrong of bringing into this calm roof, where pleasure was unknown, a face that had known joy. Had I let Lady Dudley depart alone and returned to Clochegourde, perhaps Henriette would not have resolved to treat me as a brother. But now she paid me ostentatious attentions—humiliating, like caring for a sick man.“You were out walking early,” said the count. “I hope you have brought back a good appetite, you whose stomach is not yet destroyed.”That remark, which made Henriette smile, completed my sense of ridicule. Day and night I could not be at Clochegourde and Saint-Cyr. By day, the transition from lover to brother seemed like an illness. I was ashamed; I cursed the pleasure Lady Dudley gave me; I wished Henriette would demand my blood. I could not tear her rival apart before Henriette, for she avoided speaking of Arabella. Had I mentioned her, Henriette would have despised me. After five years of delightful intercourse, we had nothing to say; our words had no connection with our thoughts; we hid intolerable pain from each other.Henriette feigned cheerfulness, but she was sad. She called herself my sister, and we had no common ground, remaining in constrained silence. She increased my misery by pretending to be the only victim.“I suffer more than you,” I said once.“How so?” she asked haughtily.“Because I am the one to blame.”Her manner grew so cold that I resolved to leave Clochegourde. That evening on the terrace I bade farewell. As I took my horse, she said, “Let us walk down the avenue together, alone.” At the grove marking the boundary, she stopped.“Farewell, my friend,” she said, resting her head on my chest and wrapping her arms around me. “Farewell, we shall never meet again. God has given me the sad power to see the future. Do you remember the terror when you first returned, so young, so handsome? I saw you turn your back on me as you do now. Once again, I have seen into the future. Friend, we speak for the last time. I can hardly say a few words; part of me speaks. Death has seized something in me. You have taken the mother from her children; I ask you to take her place. You can; Jacques and Madeleine love you, as if you had always made them suffer.”“Die!” I cried, terrified by the fire in her eyes. “Henriette, I command you to live. Swear to me you will send for Origet and obey him in everything.”“Would you oppose God’s mercy?” she cried, misreading me.“You do not love me enough to obey blindly, as that miserable Lady Dudley does?”“Yes, yes, I will do all you ask,” she cried, goaded by jealousy.“Then I stay,” I said, kissing her eyelids.Frightened, she escaped my arms and walked home without looking back. I followed; she was weeping and praying. We reached the lawn; I respectfully kissed her hand. This submission touched her.“I am yours—forever, and as you will,” I said; “for I love you as your aunt loved you.”She trembled and wrung my hand.“One look,” I said, “one last of our old looks! The woman who gives herself wholly,” I cried, “gives less of life and soul than I have now received. Henriette, thou art my best-beloved—my only love.”“I shall live!” she said; “but cure yourself as well.”That look erased Arabella’s memory. Thus I was the plaything of two irreconcilable passions, influenced alternately. I loved an angel and a demon; two women equally beautiful—one adorned with virtues we decry through hatred of our imperfections, the other with vices we deify through selfishness. Returning down the avenue, seeing Madame de Mortsauf surrounded by her children waving handkerchiefs, I felt a transient pride in being arbiter of two destinies; proud of inspiring such passion that death must come to whichever I abandoned. That conceit punished me doubly.I do not know what urged me to remain with Arabella and await the moment the count’s death might give me Henriette, for she would ever love me. Her harshness, tears, remorse, and Christian resignation testified to a feeling that could not be erased. Walking down that avenue, I was no longer twenty-five; I was fifty. A man passes quickly from youth to middle age. Perhaps evil thoughts came from the Tuileries, from the king’s cabinet. Who could resist the polluting spirit of Louis XVIII?Back in Paris, Arabella and I grew intimate. We soon abandoned conventional restrictions I had tried to keep—the comedies and concealments that renew desire. Young passions, like youth, raze their forests instead of cutting timber. Arabella adopted none of the bourgeois strategies; she wished to exhibit me to all Paris as her “sposo.” She used her powers of seduction to keep me under her roof, not content with whispered scandal. Seeing her happy in an imprudence that frankly admitted her position, how could I not believe her love?Once plunged into the comforts of our illegal union, despair seized me; I saw my life bound to a course defying Henriette’s ideas and advice. From then on I lived in a rage like consumptives who, knowing death near, cannot bear scrutiny. There was no corner of my heart where I could escape suffering; an avenging spirit filled me with forbidden thoughts. My letters to Henriette depicted this moral malady and did her infinite harm. “At the cost of so many treasures lost, I wished you to be at least happy,” she wrote in the only answer I received. But I was not happy. Happiness is absolute; it admits no comparisons. Once the first ardor fades, comparisons arise. All great passions first check character’s asperities; later, when lovers are accustomed, moral physiognomies reappear and natural antipathies surface. This period began with me. Less blinded, I undertook a critical examination of Lady Dudley that harmed her.First, she lacked the qualities of mind that distinguish Frenchwomen. When a Frenchwoman loves, she metamorphoses: coquetry adorns love; she abandons dangerous vanity and claims no merit but loving well. She espouses her lover’s interests and acquires experience in a day; heedless and prodigal by nature, she manages with grace. She becomes mother, adviser, doctor, giving transformations a grace that reveals infinite love. She combines other nations’ special qualities and unifies them with wit that enlivens and varies love, relieving monotony. The Frenchwoman loves always, in public or solitude; in public she speaks by silence or looks; if speech and look fail, she finds other means. The Englishwoman, by contrast, makes her love bend to the world. Educated to icy manners, she opens and shuts her heart with a British mechanism. Passionate when no eye sees her, she is cold before the world. A lover doubts his empire seeing her immobility, her aloof countenance, the calm voice with which she leaves her boudoir. Hypocrisy becomes indifference; she has forgotten all.A woman who can lay aside her love like a garment may be thought capable of changing it. What tempests in a man’s heart when he sees a woman take and drop her love like embroidery. These women, too completely mistress of themselves, never wholly belong to you; society’s influence prevents you reigning supreme. Where a Frenchwoman comforts with a look, an Englishwoman’s silence irritates and frets. They are so on their dignity that fashion often reigns over pleasures. An Englishwoman forces everything into form; Protestantism’s doubt and reasoning kill belief; it is the death of art and love. Passionate hearts flee worldliness.You can understand my shock when I found Lady Dudley could not live without the world, and the English system of two lives was natural to her. When she loved, she loved madly—no woman could compare; but when the curtain fell, she banished memory of it. In public she never answered a look or smile; she was ambassadress-like, folding her phrases and elbows; her composure outraged my heart. She degraded love to a need rather than raising it to an ideal. She expressed neither fear nor regret nor desire; then, at a given hour, tenderness reappeared like a sudden fire.Which of the two should I believe? By a thousand pin-pricks I felt the infinite differences between Henriette and Arabella. Madame de Mortsauf’s smallest movements—the sound of her gown, her lowered eyelids, her musical voice—were a continual caress, a consistent thought. She did not halve her soul for two atmospheres. Arabella’s mind existed for the world and sarcasm; she loved to rend and bite to satisfy craving. Madame de Mortsauf would have hidden her happiness; Lady Dudley chose to exhibit hers to all Paris yet kept an impenetrable English mask in public. This mixture of ostentation and dignity, love and coldness, wounded me. When I complained, she replied with triple sting, mingling boasts of love with cutting English sarcasms. In opposition she made amusement of hurting me, kneading me like dough. She caricatured my ideas, even daring to ask if I wished her to kiss me at the opera before all Paris. Despite passion, she was never meditative, self-contained, or reverent like Henriette; she was insatiable for excitement, noise, and celebrity; everything concerned herself, not me.Henriette’s letter you have read—proof of how this virtuous woman obeyed the genius of a Frenchwoman—shows how she had studied my prospects, political relations, moral conquests, and with what ardor she took hold of my life in permissible directions. Lady Dudley affected the reticence of an acquaintance. She never inquired into my affairs as a man. Prodigal for herself without being generous, she separated self-interest and love too decidedly. I knew, without proving it, that to save me a pang, Henriette would have sought for me what she would never seek for herself. In any great misfortune I would have gone to Henriette for counsel; I would sooner have been dragged to prison than say a word to Lady Dudley.The contrast extended to outward things. In France luxury expresses the man; it reproduces his ideas and gives attention value between lovers. English luxury, at first alluring, proved mechanical. The thousand attentions at Clochegourde Arabella considered servants’ business; each had a duty as if horses. She never attached to servants; the death of the best would not affect her; money could replace him. Her selfishness was candid and created laughter. Crimson draperies covered an iron nature. The siren of amorous folly could make a young man forget the hard Englishwoman; only gradually I discovered the stony rock where my seeds were wasted. Madame de Mortsauf had seen this at a glance. She was right; Arabella’s love became intolerable to me. I have since remarked that many women who ride well have little tenderness. Like Amazons, they lack a breast; their hearts are hard in some direction.When weariness took possession of me, when I comprehended the sanctity true feeling imparts to love, when memories of Clochegourde brought the fragrance of roses, the warmth of the terrace, and the warble of nightingales, I received a blow whose echo still wakes in my heart.I was working in the king’s cabinet when the Duc de Lenoncourt entered. The king asked news of the countess. I raised my head hastily; the king, offended, gave me the look that precedes harsh words.“Sire, my poor daughter is dying,” replied the duke.“Will the king deign to grant me leave of absence?” I cried, tears in my eyes.“Go, my lord,” he answered, withholding reprimand in satire.More courtier than father, the duke asked no leave but got into the carriage with the king. I left without bidding Lady Dudley goodbye, leaving a note that I was sent on a mission by the king. At the Croix de Berny I met his Majesty returning. He threw me an ironic look seeming to say: “If you mean to be anything in politics, come back; don’t parley with the dead.” The pompous equipages rolled away to cries of “Vive le Roi!” It seemed the court had driven over the dead body of Madame de Mortsauf with utter insensibility. Though the duke was an excellent man, he would no doubt play whist with Monsieur after the king retired. The duchess had long ago given her daughter the first stab by writing of Lady Dudley.My hurried journey was like a ruined gambler’s dream; I despaired at receiving no news. Had the confessor excluded me? I accused everyone. Beyond Tours, on the poplar-bordered road to Poncher, I met Monsieur Origet. He guessed I was going to Clochegourde and told me: “I doubt if you find her living. She is dying a frightful death—of inanition. When she called me last June, no medical power could control the disease; she had the symptoms Monsieur de Mortsauf knows, for he thinks he has them. Madame la comtesse was not in a transient condition; her disease had reached a point where science is useless; it is the incurable result of grief. Her physical condition is produced by inertia of an organ as necessary as the heart. Grief has done the work of a dagger. Don’t deceive yourself; she is dying of hidden grief.”“Hidden!” I exclaimed.“No,” he said, looking at me, “and since she was so seriously attacked, Monsieur de Mortsauf ceased to torment her. I am no longer needed; Monsieur Deslandes is all-sufficient; nothing can be done; her sufferings are dreadful. Young, beautiful, and rich, to die emaciated—for she dies of hunger! During the last forty days the stomach has rejected all nourishment.”Origet pressed my hand with respect. “Courage, monsieur,” he said, lifting his eyes.These words were like poisoned arrows. I sprang into the carriage and promised a reward if I arrived in time.Despite impatience, the distance seemed short as bitter reflections crowded my soul. Dying of grief, yet her children were well? Then she died through me! My conscience arraigned me. What weakness in human justice, which avenges open deeds but forgives murderers of the soul! How many victims—Madame de Beauseant, the Duchesse de Langeais, Lady Brandon—our period teems with such events. Who does not remember the young woman who poisoned herself from jealousy? Society and science are accomplices in crimes without assizes. The world declares no one dies of grief; our scientific nomenclature provides labels that serve as passports to the coffin. Is there some law we do not know? Must the century pitilessly strew earth with corpses? My God! do I belong to the race of tigers?Remorse gripped me as I entered Clochegourde’s avenue on a damp October morning. Jacques, Madeleine, and the Abbe Dominis knelt at the foot of a wooden cross. I sprang from the carriage toward them, my heart aching. The old huntsman stood apart.I kissed Jacques and Madeleine, who gave a cold look. The abbe rose; I took him by the arm.“Is she still alive?” I implored. He bowed sadly and said it might be better to wait; she was greatly changed. “It is necessary to prepare her for this interview, or it might increase her suffering—death would be a blessed release.”He told me how for six months the countess had suffered more despite Origet’s care. The doctor had come every evening for two months. “To heal the body, the heart must first be healed,” the doctor had said. As the illness increased, the countess’s words grew bitter. She called on earth to keep her, then repented. Such alternations rend the heart. “She falls, but each time she stumbles she rises higher toward heaven,” he said.I cried, “Surely this pure lily will flower in heaven?”“You left her still a flower,” he answered. “You will find her consumed, purified, pure as a diamond buried in ashes.”The count, white-headed, hurried out, declaring, “She was right!
