He is here! ‘Felix, Felix, Felix has come!’ she kept crying. My dear friend, death is here. Why did it not take a poor madman like me?”I walked toward the house but the Abbe Birotteau stopped me. “Madame la comtesse begs you will not enter yet.” Inside, servants moved, all dumb with grief. Manette searched for Madeleine; we paced the lawn. I saw the dear “mignonne” gathering autumn flowers. Overcome, I sat, broken.“Poor Felix,” said the count, “she forbade me to write to you. She knew how much you loved her.”All was gloom. The servants wept; work was left undone; the place seemed uninhabited. The count, speaking in bursts, blamed and then wept for his wife, mixing despair with accusations. Madeleine told me her mother was ready; the Abbe Birotteau accompanied me. He warned, “I have done all to prevent this meeting. The salvation of this saint required it. I shall be present to protect you against yourself and perhaps against her. Respect her weakness. She is dying of hunger and thirst. Since morning she suffers feverish irritation. Pray God she die not in a moment of doubt.”We reached the chamber. Henriette sat in white, before the fireplace, flowers about her. The abbe’s amazement at the room’s restored tenderness showed she had prepared to receive me. Her shrunken face had the greenish pallor of a magnolia; hollow eyes shone; her forehead kept proportion; inward fires issued despite waxy features. The smile on her whitened lips resembled death’s grin. Her robe showed emaciation. She knew she was changed; thought filled her with bitterness. No longer the sublime Madame de Mortsauf, she was a nameless thing struggling against annihilation. I took her dry, burning hand to kiss. Her discolored lips tried a smile—the forced smile of one seeking to hide irony, expectation, or fury.“Ah, my poor Felix, this is death,” she said, “and you do not like death; odious death. This is the end of love; I knew it would be so. Lady Dudley will never see you thus surprised. Ah! why have I so longed for you, Felix? You have come at last, and I reward your devotion by the same horrible sight that made the Comte de Rance a Trappist. I, who hoped to remain beautiful and noble in your memory, destroy your illusions! True love cannot calculate. But stay; do not go, stay. Monsieur Origet said I was better this morning; I shall recover. Your looks will bring me back to life. When I regain strength and nourishment, I shall be beautiful again. I am scarcely thirty-five; there are many years of happiness before me—we will leave Clochegourde and go to Italy.”Tears filled my eyes; I turned to the window. She put her burning lips to my ear: “My anguish has been in not seeing thee! Did you not bid me live? I will live; I will ride on horseback; I will know life, Paris, fêtes, pleasures!”Those tones pictured a lifetime’s battles, the anguish of true love lost. The countess rose with the motion of a child. The confessor fell on his knees and prayed. She flung an arm around my neck, kissed me violently, and said, “You shall not escape me now!” She fainted; I laid her on the bed. “You carried me thus before,” she murmured.Deslandes entered, surprised at the decorations. “We must suffer much to die,” she said faintly. The doctor felt a pulse and murmured that it was the forty-second day since she had eaten or drunk. The Abbe Birotteau took me to the garden. “Let us leave her to the doctor,” he said; “they will wrap her in opium. Well, you have heard her—if indeed it is she.”“No,” I said, “it is not she.”Stupefied, I hid in a punt to be alone. I tried to detach myself from my life—torture like that which afflicts adulterers. My life was a failure. Despair suggested to die beside her or bury myself at Meilleraye among Trappists. I looked at the windows where Henriette was dying, fancying I saw the light burning the night I betrothed my soul to hers. Ought I not to have followed the simple life she created, keeping faithful while I worked in the world? Had she not bidden me become great to be saved from shameful passions? Chastity! Love as Arabella understood it disgusted me.Madeleine walked slowly and sat on a bench. When I approached, she rose and returned to the house, hating me; she fled from her mother’s murderer. At the portico Jacques sat, expressionless. Young persons carrying death are often unmoved at funerals. I longed to question Jacques.“You know, Jacques,” I said, “that in me you have a most devoted brother.”“Your friendship is useless; I shall follow my mother,” he replied with a sullen look.He showed me his handkerchief stained with blood.They each had a fatal secret. The brother and sister avoided each other. Henriette lay low; all was ruins at Clochegourde.“Madame is asleep,” Manette said, happy that the countess was out of pain. Small joys matter then. “Monsieur Deslandes ordered the flowers taken away; they excited Madame’s nerves,” Manette added. She thought the flowers caused delirium.I returned as the sunset gilded Azay’s roofs. All was calm and pure. A soft light lit the bed where my Henriette lay wrapped in opium. The body seemed annihilated; the soul reigned. Blanche and Henriette, two sublime faces of one woman, reappeared. The abbes sat at the bed. The count stood stupefied. I took a seat on the sofa. We exchanged looks of admiration mingled with tears. The Abbe Dominis and I communicated in signs: yes, angels were watching. Her face cleared; the green of suffering gave way to pure white pallor; she seemed to embrace the divine.Madeleine knelt, adoring, crying “My mother!” Jacques smiled; he would follow her. “She is entering the haven,” said the Abbe Birotteau. The Angelus rang; Nature’s poetry joined the sacred hour. We burst into tears. Even servants knelt and prayed. The doctor signaled that this sleep was the hour without pain; it was time for the last sacraments. At nine o’clock she awoke quietly with gentle eyes, and we beheld her in former beauty.“Mother! you are too beautiful to die—life and health are coming back to you!” cried Madeleine.“Dear daughter, I shall live—in thee,” she answered, smiling.They embraced. The count kissed his wife. She colored when she saw me.“Dear Felix,” she said, “this is, I think, the only grief I have ever caused you. Forget all I may have said—I, a poor creature beside myself.” She held out her hand; I took and kissed it. “As in the old days, Felix?”We left the room during the last confession. I approached Madeleine. In the presence of others she could not escape civility; but, like her mother, she looked at no one.“Dear Madeleine,” I said softly, “what have you against me? Why such coldness before death, which should be reconcile us?”“I hear in my heart what my mother says,” she replied, with a look like Ingres’ “Mother of God.”“And you condemn me when your mother absolves me—if indeed I am guilty.”“You, you—always yourself!” Her voice revealed determined hatred, implacable as Corsican judgment.An hour passed in silence. The Abbe Birotteau returned after receiving the countess’s confession. We found her sitting up, dressed in the long white garment to be her shroud. She had had her letters burned—sacrifice she had delayed until this hour. She smiled with eyes moist and inward lucidity; she saw the promised land.“Dear Felix,” she said, pressing my hand, “stay with us. You must be present at my last scene; be not absent from the least painful among many things in which you are concerned.”She made a sign and the door closed. She knelt before the astonished count, then laid her head on his knees.“Though I have been a faithful wife to you,” she said faintly, “I have sometimes failed in my duty. I have given a friendship outside my family more affectionate care than to you. Perhaps I have irritated you by comparisons. I have had a deep friendship which no one—not even he who has been its object—has fully known. Though virtuous by human laws and an irreproachable wife, other thoughts, voluntary or involuntary, have crossed my mind, and in this hour I fear I welcomed them too warmly. But having loved you and continued submissive, I now pray for your blessing with a clean heart. I shall die without a bitter thought if I can hear a tender word for your Blanche, for the mother of your children—if you forgive her those things she did not forgive herself till reassured by the great tribunal which pardons all.”“Blanche, Blanche!” cried the broken man, shedding tears on her head. “Would you kill me?” He raised and kissed her solemnly. “Have I no forgiveness to ask? Have I never been harsh?”“Perhaps,” she said. “But dear friend, indulge a dying woman’s weakness; tranquillize me. Will you grant that I leave to our friend here that pledge of my affection?” She showed a letter on the mantel. “He is now my adopted son, and that is all. The heart makes bequests; my last wishes impose a duty on that dear Felix. If you do not wish to marry Madeleine, at least seek my soul’s repose by making Monsieur de Mortsauf as happy as he can be. Farewell, long farewell like that I gave last night to our valley, where I shall rest and where you will often—will you not?—return.Henriette.”I fell into abyssal reflections as the depths of her life were lighted by this expiring flame. My clouds of egotism rolled away. She had suffered as much as I—more, for she was dead. She believed others would be kind to her friend; she was blind to her daughter’s enmity. That last proof of tenderness pained me. Poor Henriette wished to give me Clochegourde and her daughter.From that dread day when I first followed my noble Henriette to the grave, the sun has been less warm, nights more gloomy, movement less agile, thought more dull. Some departed we bury in earth; others are buried within us, their memory mingling with our heartbeats; they are in us by a metempsychosis of love. A soul is within my soul. When some good thing is done by me, that soul acts and speaks. All that is good within me issues from that grave; sarcasm and bitterness are mine. Natalie, when next my eyes are darkened or my lips silent, do not ask, “Of what are you thinking?”I ceased writing for days; these memories were too bitter. I owe you an account of events that followed; they need few words. Henriette’s letter set a star of hope before me. To live at Clochegourde with Madeleine, consecrating my life to her, seemed a rescue. But it was necessary to know her true feelings. As I bade the count farewell, we walked the terrace. He spoke of his loss but soon of the future; he feared his daughter, who lacked her mother’s gentleness. Madeleine’s firm character alarmed him; he foresaw a struggle between father and daughter that would end his days in bitterness. His son was to leave; his daughter marry; what son-in-law might she have? Though talking of dying, he feared being alone without sympathy.Madeleine could see me from the terrace but would not come down. I seized a moment to beg the count to call her, saying I had a last wish of her mother to convey. The count fetched her and left us on the terrace alone.“Dear Madeleine,” I said, “if I speak to you, it should be here where your mother listened to me. I know your thoughts; but are you not condemning me without knowledge of facts? My life and happiness are bound up in this place; you know that, yet you seek to banish me by coldness instead of brotherly affection that death should have strengthened. If my mother’s name still has power, in her name I beg you never to return me to Clochegourde so long as you are in it. The sight of me causes a repugnance I cannot express, which I shall never overcome.”She stopped me.“Monsieur,” she said, voice trembling, “I know all your thoughts; but I shall not change my feelings. I would rather fling myself into the Indre than ally myself to you. If my mother’s name has power, in her name I beg you never to return while I am here. The sight of you causes repugnance I cannot overcome.”She bowed with dignity and returned to the house without looking back. The searching eye of that young girl had discovered her mother’s secrets; her hatred of the man she fancied fatal to her mother may have been increased by a sense of complicity.All before me was chaos. Madeleine hated me without considering cause or victim. The edifice of my happiness fell in ruins. I alone knew the life of that unknown woman; none had fathomed her heart. Strange truth: how many families have their Henriette! How many noble feelings leave earth with no historian to measure their depths! Mothers know their children little; so with husbands, lovers, brothers. When Madeleine disappeared, I left with a broken heart. Bidding farewell to my host at Sache, I started for Paris, following the right bank of the Indre.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY. MIDDLE OF THE STORY.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################At the story’s outset the Stanhope press and ink-distributing roller were not yet common in provincial shops. In Angoulême, linked to Parisian typography by its paper mills, only wooden presses were used. “The press groans” was literal: leather ink-balls still applied the ink, and the pressman inked by hand. This old mechanism, cherished by Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, is central to the tale.Séchard, formerly a journeyman pressman, survived the Requisition of 1793 and became the sole worker after his master’s death. Illiterate, he nevertheless obtained a master printer’s license from a Representative of the People and bought the business at a bargain. He partnered with the noble M. le Comte de Maucombe, who helped print decrees while avoiding official attention.By 1795, after the Terror, Séchard needed a compositor and hired an Abbé who later became a Bishop. Although uneducated, Séchard learned to run the business, developing a shrewd grasp of costs and profits. His avarice grew as he perceived wealth in practical measures: buying paper when prices fell, becoming his own landlord, and acquiring the old printing house.He had one son, David, whom he sent to grammar school hoping for a competent successor. David excelled, and despite Séchard’s scorn for book learning he insisted his son economize while studying in Paris; David finished his education without costing his father.The printing house held a regional monopoly on official printing, but the Cointet Brothers sought a second license, threatening Séchard’s position. Confident in David’s abilities, Séchard anticipated retirement, believing his son would counter the competition.Now in his seventies, Séchard had developed a taste for drink since his wife’s death. His appearance matched his habits: a bloated face, cunning gaze, three-cornered hat, and old-fashioned clothing befitting his character.When David returned, Séchard welcomed him with feigned warmth, eager to discuss business. Despite David’s plea to postpone serious talk, Séchard pressed on, treating his son as both heir and potential commercial rival.The printing house, established since Louis XIV’s reign, was a large room with a glass door to the workshop; customers often drifted in, distracted by the process. The yard with vines and the kitchen, where inky runoff mixed with household waste, gave the place a notorious air. The cramped, unchanged living quarters above—sparse dining room, the same old bedroom furniture—mirrored the old printer’s stinginess and relentless pursuit of profit.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################The sitting-room had been partly modernized by the late Mme. Séchard, with powder-blue wainscoting and Oriental paper. Furniture was sparse—lyre-backed chairs with blue leather cushions. The arched windows overlooking the Place du Mûrier were bare, and the room lacked a clock, sconces, or a mirror, for Mme. Séchard had died before finishing her plans. Jérôme-Nicolas Séchard, who valued only improvements that paid, left it as it was.Jérôme-Nicolas brought his son David into the room and pointed to a valuation of the printing plant on the table. David read the inventory aloud, noting the old presses and their valuation. He scoffed at the presses, calling them fit only for firewood, and dropped the paper. Séchard retorted angrily and led him downstairs, demonstrating with surprising agility that the presses worked well. He challenged David to name an English press that could match his, boasting they could earn enough without a foreman and dismissing newer machinery as ruinous to type. David remained skeptical, especially about the cost assigned to the type.Séchard defended the type—cast at M. Vaflard’s foundry—insisting that after twelve years’ wear it was still nearly new. David noted the price was high compared to competitors, but Séchard stood firm. The inventory totaled thirty thousand francs, including license and goodwill. David, recalling his father had once bought the business for ten thousand francs, found the asking price exorbitant and asked whether he could afford it. Séchard, who preferred heated argument to silence, pressed on, arguing the license alone was invaluable, bringing income from advertisements and other work. Attached to the valuation was a partnership deed: Séchard would rent the premises to David for twelve hundred francs a year and reserve a room for himself.Cornered, David realized he could accept the deal or walk away. He computed the license and goodwill’s worth and, thinking he might manage to pay the debt, accepted, surprising his father. The next day Séchard had the apprentice move David’s belongings to the attic, leaving him three empty rooms and no funds for wages. When David asked for financial help, Séchard refused, claiming he had already paid his share. Humiliated, David discovered he was alone—his father intended to profit. He mentioned his mother’s fortune, but Séchard dismissed it as mere beauty and intelligence; claiming any legal claim would be long and costly. Accepting his situation, David resolved to work hard and build his future.Séchard then mentioned a “treasure” he was leaving: Marion, a capable country girl who managed many tasks in the printing house. Séchard departed for his vineyard in Marsac, where he fretted over the thirty thousand francs and periodically visited to check the business, suspecting David was not managing well as the Cointet Brothers gained ground.David, indifferent to politics, struggled for work. The Cointets, aligning with the clergy, began monopolizing the market while David’s business dwindled; they smeared him as a Liberal and untrustworthy due to his father’s reputation. As the Cointets prospered, they offered to buy the printing house. Séchard, fearing the loss of their connection to the Liberal clientele, initially urged David to keep the paper and even negotiated what he framed as a protective price of sixty thousand francs for the business. Ultimately, however, David was forced to sell the paper to the Cointets, marking the decline of the Séchard establishment. Séchard, more detached, visited less often though he retained some feeling for the old shop.David’s situation worsened when Lucien Chardon, an old school friend reduced to poverty after his father’s death, came seeking work. David offered him a position as a printer’s reader to help. Their friendship deepened; Lucien dreamt of being a poet, David of studying natural science. They discussed Lucien’s father’s schemes for refining sugar and making paper, which intrigued David and suggested other ventures.Their ambitions, however, distracted them from the pressing needs of the shop. While they indulged plans and conversation, the Cointet Brothers consolidated their advantage. Séchard & Son scraped by, earning only a fraction of what was needed; the Cointets tolerated the losing competitor to avoid facing serious rival. The printing house’s condition reflected years of neglect: repairs undone, maintenance ignored. The building grew dilapidated, the workshop cluttered and grimy, a tangible sign of the establishment’s decline and the household’s strained fortunes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################One day in May 1821, David and Lucien stood by the window awaiting the men’s departure for dinner. Uncomfortable in the office, David led Lucien outside to sit under the vines, where sunlight highlighted their contrasts. David was robust and fighter-built, with a swarthy, weathered face and an air of melancholy shaped by humble origins. Lucien was graceful and beautiful, with a refined profile and striking blue eyes; delicate in appearance but ambitious and sharp, able to view society pragmatically and even entertain dubious means to advance.Both were low in social standing but nourished higher ideals, making them bitter when reality intruded. Lucien, impetuous and adventurous, often carried the lead in their dynamic; David, stronger but more introspective and cautious, admired Lucien and usually yielded to his charm, seeing himself as the more ordinary man. For three years their friendship was sustained by literature and art: they read voraciously, worked tirelessly, and dreamed of fame as a way out of hardship.One day David brought a new volume of André de Chénier’s poetry. The verses moved them, offering solace and kinship with the poet’s struggles; they imagined the promise of transcending their circumstances. Their reverie was interrupted by Cérizet, an apprentice who brought a stranger wanting a monograph printed. Lucien eagerly engaged the author, who mentioned Mme. de Bargeton—a woman Lucien admired. He blushed and thanked the author, then confessed to David his passionate love. David cautioned him about social barriers, but Lucien hoped love could bridge them and vowed not to visit Mme. de Bargeton without David’s welcome, a pledge that revealed both loyalty and pride.Walking through Angoulême, Lucien reflected on the gulf between his station and Mme. de Bargeton’s. The city, perched on a crag, was divided between the upper town of nobility and the lower town of commerce. That split bred tension: the upper class clung to old values, resisted outsiders and modernizing reforms, and looked down on merchants and tradespeople; the bourgeoisie resented this disdain. Despite government efforts to modernize, the upper town remained stagnant and exclusive. This rigid hierarchy and mutual animosity promised future social upheaval as the rising middle class pressed for recognition.In that climate, Lucien’s ambition to enter Mme. de Bargeton’s world was a daunting challenge. His origins as a druggist’s son marked him an outsider, yet his literary talent and charm, and Mme. de Bargeton’s artistic inclinations, gave him hope. David’s mixture of admiration and concern captured the complexity of their friendship: he understood Lucien’s depth of feeling but saw the obstacles clearly. Lucien’s determination to bridge class and his pledge to include David in any future visits encapsulated their bond as the story of love and ambition unfolded against a society of entrenched divisions and shifting fortunes.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################M. de Bargeton descended from a Bordeaux alderman ennobled under Louis XIII, but the family declined after his grandfather, “the Waster,” squandered its wealth. By 1789 the current Bargeton kept only entailed lands and a house in Angoulême with modest rents. In 1805 he married Mlle. Marie-Louise-Anaïs de Nègrepelisse, from a once-prominent family; her father lived frugally on his wife’s estate, farming and distilling brandy.During the Revolution Abbé Niollant instructed Naïs in languages and music, nurturing her independence and a certain haughty superiority. Her father’s thrift and provincial isolation reinforced this outlook; she resisted marriage, valuing autonomy over submission to a man.Seeking a match who would not challenge her intellect, her father chose M. de Bargeton: well bred but limited in mind. Naïs accepted, intending to pursue social ambitions within the constraints of the union. Despite the age gap (she thirty-six, he fifty-eight), the match seemed mutually advantageous.Settled in Angoulême, they waited for Naïs’s inheritance and a hoped-for move to Paris. Instead, modest income and petty provincial life stifled her intellectual yearnings and produced theatrical affectations; emotions that might once have been noble became exaggerated and trivialized.Her life settled into unfulfilled hopes and increased longing for love. The rise of the Empire and the fame of military heroes revived romantic dreams; she fell for a young officer whose death in battle left her heartbroken, intensifying loss and regret.Years of solitude hardened her dignity into affectation—until M. du Châtelet arrived. Charming, ambitious, and disruptive, he used music to ingratiate himself, seeing in Naïs a potentially wealthy widow to further his aims. His outsider presence shocked the local elite who defended her salon as high society. Despite scandal, she welcomed him, drawn to the excitement he brought; his arrival altered her social world and exposed the tensions of ambition, love, and provincial expectation.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Political opinion was verbose and exaggerated; the Quotidienne showed only lukewarm loyalty, and Louis XVIII was sneered at as a Jacobin. Society’s women were awkwardly dressed and ill-spoken, their appearances and conversation incomplete. In the countryside a noble spirit and old breeding masked many deficiencies, and loyalty to the House of Bourbon was a proud badge, giving the provincials a lofty sense of worth.Châtelet found solace in company largely because of his interest in Mme. de Bargeton. Many women, flattered by him, saw in Châtelet qualities lacking in their husbands; all aspired to be near Imperial Highness. To keep his position he tolerated much insolence, currying favor with the clergy and indulging the queen of Angoulême’s petty tastes while sharing new literature and poetry. Mme. de Bargeton, enchanted by the Bourbon revival but longing for Parisian genius, was told by Châtelet of a promising young poet in Angoulême.Lucien, the unnamed poet, was unaware of the storm around him. Praised by his headmaster, he anticipated a welcome at the Hôtel de Bargeton. His sister Éve secretly supported him, saving to buy him new shoes and clothes and coaching him on aristocratic manners; she left him with pride and anxiety about his future.At the Hôtel de Bargeton Lucien found the house less grand than imagined, but Mme. de Bargeton—Naïs—embodied the poetry he loved. She admired his beauty and diffidence; their meeting brimmed with mutual attraction. Lucien, enchanted, overlooked her faded airs and felt a promise of future intimacy.Sensing a rival, Châtelet sought to assert influence over Lucien, offering unsolicited advice about local society and declaring his own claims on Naïs. Despite Châtelet’s intimidation, Lucien persisted in visiting Naïs, initially flattered by noble condescension but later growing resentful of it.Naïs flattered and cultivated Lucien, treating him as a project—sometimes patroness, sometimes tender confidante. His first love swelled into deeper attachment as she addressed him more intimately and encouraged his literary ambitions, introducing him to music and books and exposing her vulnerabilities. One evening she gazed at a portrait of a dead lover, provoking Lucien’s jealousy; nevertheless he declared his feelings boldly and their relationship advanced.When their liaison became public the town buzzed with shock and gossip. Châtelet, feeling threatened, tried to undermine Lucien. Mme. de Bargeton defended her protégé, arguing—wittily—that genius can arise from humble origins and countering the nobles’ disdain.To showcase him she hosted salons and urged Lucien to adopt the noble name Rubempré, promising courtly patronage. The idea exhilarated him; under Naïs’s influence his aspirations widened and some earlier egalitarian ideals gave way to social ambition. He wrote Naïs a passionate letter expressing his hopes and asking her help for his friend David, revealing his inward conflict: torn between love and recognition, he struggled to reconcile artistic sincerity with the pursuit of social success.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################As Lucien descended from Angoulême he worried whether the great lady was angry and if she would receive David. He perceived the vast gulf between a queen and her favorite and knew David could not easily cross that social terrain. He did not realize that receiving David could ruin Mme. de Bargeton: mingling with commoners would bring her ostracism. While many sins are pardoned for the powerful, breaching social law by associating with lower classes was unforgivable.Lucien’s aristocratic instinct sensed danger. He understood that noble bearing does not always mean noble feeling; many notable men lacked the required social polish. David had not the graces Lucien possessed, and Lucien feared the ridicule his friend would face. After a poetic reverie he shifted to worldly wisdom, regretting his impulsive letter to Mme. de Bargeton and foreseeing the harsh realities of success and the sacrifices it required. He longed for simpler pleasures and felt it nobler to win by his own efforts than through a woman’s favor. Inspired by Napoleon’s rise from humble origins, Lucien resolved to pursue ambition without patronage, though he felt ashamed of his father’s shop as an obstacle.Back home, Lucien was greeted by his father, M. Postel, who reminisced about Lucien’s grandfather’s achievements; the legacy pricked Lucien’s resentment. Searching eagerly he found Mme. de Bargeton’s reply among the shop’s items: she offered to help David but revealed prejudice toward lower social standing. Lucien read it as a triumph, convinced David would shine in her circle.Eve noticed his elation and hoped for his happiness; Lucien reassured her they would all be happy, though Eve sensed his priorities shifting. David, feeling unworthy of the elevation Lucien sought, decided to decline Mme. de Bargeton’s invitation. He affirmed his commitment to his own path as a printer and emphasized the social differences between them.David’s humility and loyalty moved Lucien, who reflected on the sacrifices of those who supported him. Yet ambition began to eclipse gratitude, focusing him more on advancement than on friends.Preparing for his first public reading, Lucien agreed with David to choose a different poem—David feared the biblical piece would not please the audience. Meanwhile David and Eve shared a quiet moment that hinted at mutual affection.At the reading Lucien arrived early and encountered M. de Bargeton, socially awkward and ineffectual in conversation. His limited intellect and reliance on superficial pleasantries underscored the social dynamics Lucien must navigate. The evening, marked by Lucien’s ambition and the complex relationships around him, set the stage for conflicts and aspirations to come.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################There was talk of nominating the mute gentleman for deputy, but Lucien had only briefly visited and had not yet fathomed him. M. de Bargeton, reclining and silent, observed everything with a cool reserve that awed Lucien; imaginative, Lucien saw him as a sphinx and treated him deferentially.“I am the first comer,” Lucien said, bowing more than usual.“That is natural enough,” M. de Bargeton replied, a remark Lucien took as a sly barb. It made him self-conscious about his appearance. When Lucien asked why people from farther away came earlier, Bargeton shrugged and fell back into silence. Lucien tried to make conversation—guessing Mme. de Bargeton was dressing, fretting over the room’s preparations and his own attire, even lingering by a Japanese jar to find a topic. Bargeton’s curt answers and intent scrutiny unnerved him.The old servant then announced “M. du Châtelet.” The Baron entered with easy confidence, inspecting Lucien’s clothes and deepening his discomfort. Sixte du Châtelet, dressed with the faded air of a once-stylish beau, dismissed Lucien’s poetry as mere vaudeville. Mme. de Bargeton appeared, elaborately dressed and theatrically charming; Châtelet’s compliments delighted her, while she scarcely acknowledged Lucien, preferring Châtelet’s company.Guests arrived in a parade of types: the dignified Bishop and his Vicar-General; Mme. de Chandour and her eccentric husband—Amélie posing like a rival queen; the solemn M. de Saintot (Astolphe) with his austere wife Élisa, pretending scientific airs; M. de Bartas, craving praise for his singing; the artist Alexandre de Brébian and his wife; fashion-obsessed Lolotte and Fifine and their dowdy husbands. The Comte de Senonches, haughty, came with Zéphirine, tall and striking despite ill health; Francis, a devoted family friend who ran their household, appeared unusually attentive to Zéphirine and provoked speculation. The wealthy Marquis de Pimentel and his wife arrived with the Baron and Baroness de Rastignac, drawing envy as Mme. de Bargeton lavished them with attention.The Prefect and the General came last, followed by a nervous country gentleman who awkwardly brought a treatise on silkworms and sought to interest anyone who would listen. Mme. and Mlle. du Brossard, noble but impoverished, hovered about, pressing social connections with thinly veiled desperation; M. de Séverac, a childless widower, talked about silkworm nurseries while Mme. du Brossard promoted her daughter’s talents.Throughout the evening Lucien felt increasingly scrutinized and diminished. Most addressed him as M. Chardon rather than M. de Rubempré, a slight that stung his pride. The assembly seemed more engrossed in its own interplay of flattery and rivalry than in his art, leaving him isolated.When he read, Lucien’s excitement mingled with anxiety. He announced that he would read poems by the newly discovered André de Chénier; the audience misinterpreted this as sparing his feelings. He began with Le Malade, which elicited mild applause, but his reading of L’Aveugle fell flat—listeners failed to engage and impatience showed. Sweating under the pressure, Lucien drew courage from a supportive glance by Louise and continued with Chénier’s Iambes, which finally captured some attention though many still missed the meaning.During a break, Zéphirine sent Francis to inquire after the poems, and the women took to whispering about Lucien’s origins and work. Amélie’s comments about his noble name and printing background sparked gossip that complicated his social standing. Meanwhile Lucien, absorbed in the beauty of his chosen poet, remained largely unaware of the mounting tension and misunderstanding that surrounded his reading.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Lucien tried to immortalize his love for Mme. de Bargeton with an ode he fancied rivaled Chénier’s. Proudly presenting it under her protective gaze, he felt secure while she privately fretted for him. The audience, however, was unimpressed: the Baron dismissed the poem as youthful, ornate but empty. The women pretended admiration; their indifference and sarcastic whispers about Lucien’s ambitions were palpable.Amid the mockery, Francis, a shrewd observer, aimed to undermine Lucien by spreading rumors that his mother influenced his verse. The Bishop, unknowingly complicit, praised Lucien, but the compliment only intensified the aristocrats’ scorn. Overwhelmed and out of place, Lucien misidentified people and felt the weight of their disdain.As evening shifted to games and music, Mme. de Bargeton, stung by her peers’ contempt for Lucien, withdrew with him to her boudoir. She offered consolation, urging him to endure for future greatness. Romanticizing suffering as the road to immortality, she encouraged him to turn pain into profound work.Meanwhile, Lucien’s sister Eve and her lover David debated Lucien’s ambitions and the risks of his liaison with Mme. de Bargeton. David feared Lucien was unsuited to high society and might be ruined; Eve remained hopeful they could support him until he succeeded. Their talk turned to themselves when David proposed. Eve hesitated about their unequal social standings but accepted, moved by love and the desire to help Lucien. David vowed to provide for Lucien so he wouldn’t be alone.On the way home, Lucien, frustrated, vowed to win Mme. de Bargeton’s love and rise above the aristocrats. Hearing of Eve’s engagement felt like a setback—he feared their marriage would complicate his plans. David sought his father’s blessing but met skepticism: his father, focused on status and finances, disparaged Eve. David refused to yield, asserting his love and intention to support their future.Disheartened by his father’s refusal, David resolved to build a life with Eve despite hardship. Lucien remained torn between lofty aspirations and the harsh realities of his social standing.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################In Angoulême, the Bishop’s epigram and Mme. de Bargeton’s rejoinder made Lucien a local celebrity. Walking through Beaulieu, he was envied by young men and fed by flattering remarks. Seeking a private moment with Louise, he told her of his sister Eve’s impending marriage, hoping for consolation. Louise greeted him with passionate affection, praised his reading, and promised to make a poetic refuge for him despite surrounding envy.Bolstered by family and friends, Lucien’s self-regard grew. He teasingly asked Louise to be his Beatrice; she answered with promises of future love and urged him toward greatness. He confided about David and Eve’s attachment, and Louise reassured him their bond transcended family ties. When M. de Bargeton arrived, Lucien hesitated, but Louise invited him to dinner and a reading, strengthening his sense of belonging.Adopting the name M. de Rubempré, Lucien enjoyed the comforts of Louise’s relationship and M. de Bargeton’s patronage and expected his literary breakthrough. Eve and David postponed their wedding to support him, charmed by his persuasion, though David feared Lucien might change with status. Their intimacy drew Châtelet’s attention; he watched for anything to exploit, intent on exposing them. In the small town, even innocent interactions sparked gossip and misinterpretation, and Louise felt the pressure of social convention.Determined to declare his feelings, Lucien was thwarted by the constraints of provincial life; Louise considered fleeing those restraints. Châtelet manipulated perception, sowing doubt. An emotional outburst by Lucien, witnessed by Stanislas, was misread and became the seed of rumor. Gossip spread and exaggerated tales circulated; Châtelet seized the moment, while Stanislas enjoyed the drama.Alarmed, Mme. de Bargeton defended her honor and pressed her husband to confront Stanislas. Motivated by her distress, M. de Bargeton—though insecure—resolved to challenge Stanislas. Approaching his house with careful modesty, he ultimately won respect at the tense gathering. The scene was set for a confrontation that would further entangle lives and expose the complexities of love, honor, and social expectation in their small town.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################Châtelet grasped the gravity of a late-night visit with M. de Bargeton already abed and suspected Naïs had instigated it. Using his household authority, he asked Bargeton whether he wished to speak with Stanislas; the old gentleman, relieved to have a mediator, was led to Amélie’s bedroom, bracing for a confrontation that might leave Mme. de Bargeton a widow and further divide her from Lucien, the alleged cause of the trouble.Châtelet warned Stanislas that Bargeton would confront him about remarks involving Naïs and counseled decorum. When they returned, Bargeton demanded Stanislas retract statements about Mme. de Bargeton and M. de Rubempré. Cornered, Stanislas accepted a duel and chose pistols.News of the duel shifted the atmosphere among the guests; opinions split over who was to blame. Châtelet remained composed while Stanislas was shaken. Amélie, sensing the tension, grew unwell and was taken to her room. The men debated the consequences, some recalling Bargeton’s former skill with firearms.The next morning Mme. Chardon arrived at breakfast alarmed: Bargeton had nearly killed Stanislas, who had accused him of compromising behavior with Mme. de Bargeton. The duel’s grim detail—Stanislas shot in the neck—left him injured. Lucien vehemently defended Mme. de Bargeton’s innocence as he absorbed the news.Shortly after, Lucien received a note from Louise warning him to avoid her home because of the duel’s fallout and suggesting he spend the day with her, hinting at emotional upheaval. David, preparing for his own marriage, remarked that Lucien’s frequent visits to Mme. de Bargeton might need curbing.Compelled to visit Mme. de Bargeton, Lucien found her at breakfast with her husband and M. de Nègrepelisse, who scrutinized him to gauge the nature of his relationship with her daughter. Afterwards Louise confided she planned to go to Paris to secure a political post for Bargeton; their leaving Angoulême was meant to limit the duel’s repercussions.Louise urged Lucien to join her in Paris, praising the capital’s opportunities and intellectual life over provincial stagnation. Lucien was drawn to that life and to Louise, but his sister Eve’s impending wedding and family obligations weighed on him. When he hesitated, Louise dismissed his reservations and pressed him to choose their future over a family event. After a heated exchange she fainted; Lucien apologized and, desperate, promised discretion and to be ready to depart with her the next night.At home, Lucien’s family—Eve and their mother—expressed sorrow at his likely departure, and financial strains from wedding expenses emerged. David, overhearing, offered to help despite pressure on his own marriage and agreed to endorse a loan for Lucien, demonstrating loyalty though increasing Lucien’s guilt.Preparing to go, Lucien felt the pain of severing family ties. He and David arranged to send his belongings to Paris discreetly while Châtelet, mistrustful of Mme. de Bargeton’s departure, dispatched someone to follow her. Early the next morning Lucien set out with David, who pretended to be on business; as Mme. de Bargeton’s carriage approached, Lucien was filled with a mix of excitement and dread about the journey ahead.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############################################################MIDDLE OF THE STORY — the narrative's central section, the tale's core.############################################################# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ###################################################################################################################### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########################################################
</story>
